topics.title: Water Level Measurements - Tidal Poles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST755 - RENEWABLE ENERGY IN MARITIME SECTOR
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Water Level Measurements - Tidal Poles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tidal Pole (Tide Pole / Staff Gauge):** A *Tidal Pole* is a **direct-reading, vertical graduated measuring instrument** permanently or temporarily installed at a coastal or estuarine location to record the **instantaneous elevation of the water surface** relative to a fixed, pre-defined horizontal **datum (zero reference level)**. It constitutes the simplest, lowest-cost, and historically oldest instrument used in oceanographic and hydrographic surveying for the continuous assessment of **tidal heights, tidal range, and water level fluctuations** over time.

In the context of the **PENST755 – Renewable Energy in Maritime Sector** course, the tidal pole is treated as a **primary data-acquisition instrument** used during the *resource assessment phase* of tidal energy projects. Without accurate water-level (and therefore tidal-range) measurements, the **available tidal energy density** at a prospective site cannot be reliably quantified.

> [!NOTE]
> **Related Standard Terminology (IS 4410 / IOC Manuals):**
> - **Chart Datum (CD):** The reference zero level used on nautical charts (typically close to the Lowest Astronomical Tide – LAT).
> - **Mean Sea Level (MSL):** The long-term average of the sea surface.
> - **Tide Staff / Tidal Staff:** Synonymous terminology for a Tidal Pole.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **measuring scale (like the ruler used by a tailor) permanently cemented to the wall of a swimming pool**, with its **zero mark at the deep end of the pool's floor** instead of at the top. As the pool's water level rises and falls (e.g., due to tides in an ocean harbour), the wet mark left on the ruler tells you exactly how deep the water is at that instant.

That is *exactly* what a **Tidal Pole** does, except:
- The "pool" is the **sea / estuary / harbour**.
- The "ruler" is a **graduated pole, often 5 m to 10 m long**, made of fibreglass, porcelain-enamelled steel, or marine-grade aluminium.
- The "wet mark" is read **continuously** (visually by an observer) or **automatically** (by a float-driven or pressure transducer attached alongside it).
- The "deep end floor" is the **Chart Datum**, the legally fixed zero reference.

> [!TIP]
> **Why Tidal Poles Matter for Renewable Energy Engineers:**
> The **electrical power extractable per square metre of tidal cross-section** is proportional to the **square of the tidal range** (P ∝ R²). Thus, even a **0.5 m error** in tidal range measurement can lead to a **>25 % error** in estimated energy yield — making accurate, datum-corrected tidal pole readings *critical* for bankable tidal energy projects.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following physical constants and engineering metrics are required for tidal-pole data interpretation:

- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$
- **Density of seawater:** $\rho_{sw} \approx 1025 \; \text{kg/m}^3$
- **Density of freshwater:** $\rho_{fw} = 1000 \; \text{kg/m}^3$
- **Standard tidal pole lengths:** **5 m, 7 m, 10 m** (modular segments)
- **Standard graduation:** **10 mm or 20 mm** per division
- **Typical reading accuracy (visual):** **± 10 mm to ± 30 mm**
- **Typical reading accuracy (digital float encoder):** **± 1 mm to ± 5 mm**
- **Datum reference (default):** **Chart Datum (CD)** or **Mean Sea Level (MSL)** depending on national hydrographic authority

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of a Tidal Pole System

A complete tidal-pole installation is rarely just a stick in the water. For KTU examination purposes, the student must be able to enumerate the following functional components:

1. **Pole Body (Tide Staff)**
   - Marine-grade, corrosion-resistant material.
   - High-contrast, **non-fading numeric graduations** (typically black markings on a white or yellow background).
   - Modular sections (1 m or 2 m segments) bolted to a **vertical mounting structure** (pile, jetty wall, quay side).

2. **Mounting Structure**
   - Steel H-pile, concrete pile, or robust timber pile driven into the seabed.
   - Must remain **plumb (truly vertical)** under wave loading; tilt > 1° introduces reading errors.
   - Protected against **vessel impact** with fenders or by siting within a sheltered stilling well.

3. **Stilling Well (optional but recommended)**
   - A vertical perforated tube (~ 0.3 m diameter) surrounding the pole.
   - Dampens **short-period wind waves** (≤ 4 s period) while allowing the **longer-period tidal signal (12 h 25 min / 6 h 12 min)** to pass freely.
   - Improves reading stability and protects the pole from biofouling and direct wave impact.

4. **Datum Bench Mark (BM)**
   - A permanent, levelled reference point on dry land, tied to the **national vertical datum** by precise geodetic levelling.
   - The pole's **zero graduation** is physically related to this BM via an **above-water transfer survey**.

5. **Auxiliary Sensors (modern installations)**
   - **Float-and-counterweight encoder** inside the stilling well (resolution 1–5 mm).
   - **Pressure transducer** mounted near the seabed (redundant cross-check).
   - **Radar / ultrasonic water-level sensor** mounted above water (non-contact option).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** When asked *"Why is a stilling well used?"* — answer: *"To filter high-frequency wind-wave noise so that the recorded water level represents the true tidal (low-frequency) signal, with an accuracy of ± 10 mm, suitable for harmonic tidal analysis."*

## 2.2 Datum Hierarchy and Tidal Plane Definitions

A student of maritime renewable energy must be able to draw and label the **vertical datum hierarchy** because tidal energy resource calculations are reported relative to a *specific* datum (typically MSL or CD).

| Plane / Datum (Symbol) | Definition | Typical Position |
|---|---|---|
| **Lowest Astronomical Tide (LAT)** | Lowest tide level predicted under average meteorological conditions and under any astronomical configuration | Reference low for safety-critical charting |
| **Chart Datum (CD)** | Adopted reference zero for tide tables & nautical charts; in India = approximately Indian Spring Low Water (ISLW) | **0.00 m (default zero on the tidal pole)** |
| **Mean Low Water Springs (MLWS)** | Average of all low waters at the time of spring tides | Above CD |
| **Mean Low Water Neaps (MLWN)** | Average of all low waters at the time of neap tides | Above MLWS |
| **Mean Sea Level (MSL)** | Long-term arithmetic mean of water level | Mid-range |
| **Mean High Water Neaps (MHWN)** | Average of all high waters at the time of neap tides | Below MHWS |
| **Mean High Water Springs (MHWS)** | Average of all high waters at the time of spring tides | Upper limit |
| **Highest Astronomical Tide (HAT)** | Highest tide level predicted under any astronomical configuration | Reference high |

> [!TIP]
> **Mean Tidal Range** $R_{mean} = \text{MHWS} - \text{MLWS}$ → This is the **key engineering parameter** used to compute the **gross tidal energy head**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates *all* essential equations required for KTU 2024 exam questions on tidal poles and water level measurement. **All table entries are mathematically verifiable** and use $\vert \cdot \vert$ or $\mid$ notation (no bare `|` symbols) to preserve table formatting.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Water level elevation above datum | $h(t) = R_{reading}(t) - Z_{datum}$ | m | Real-time tidal height |
| 2 | Instantaneous tidal range | $R(t) = h_{HW}(t) - h_{LW}(t)$ | m | Local range estimate |
| 3 | Mean Tidal Range | $R_{mean} = \overline{MHWS} - \overline{MLWS}$ | m | Resource assessment input |
| 4 | Spring Tidal Range | $R_{spring} = MHWS - MLWS$ | m | Worst-case design head |
| 5 | Neap Tidal Range | $R_{neap} = MHWN - MLWN$ | m | Minimum operating head |
| 6 | Mean Tide Level (MTL) | $MTL = \dfrac{MHWS + MLWS}{2}$ | m | Reference for turbines |
| 7 | Density of seawater (UNESCO 1981) | $\rho = 1025 \; \text{kg/m}^{3}$ (approx) | kg/m³ | Energy density calc |
| 8 | Tidal energy head available | $H = h(t) - h_{turbine}$ | m | Net operating head |
| 9 | Theoretical tidal power density (per unit area) | $P/A = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,g\,R^{2}$ | W/m² | Resource screening |
| 10 | Annual available tidal energy per m² | $E/A = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,g\,R^{2}\,n$ | Wh/m²/yr | Energy yield estimate |
| 11 | Reading error propagation to range | $\sigma_{R} = \sqrt{\sigma_{HW}^{2} + \sigma_{LW}^{2}}$ | m | Uncertainty in $R$ |
| 12 | Hydrostatic pressure calibration check | $P = \rho\,g\,h$ | Pa | Sensor cross-check |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Notation Used:**
> - $h(t)$ = water surface elevation at time $t$
> - $R_{reading}$ = raw staff gauge reading
> - $Z_{datum}$ = vertical offset of gauge zero above the chosen datum
> - $n$ = number of tidal cycles per year (≈ 705 semidiurnal or 353 diurnal)
> - $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^{2}$ is **gravitational acceleration** (constant used in all hydro-energy equations).

## 2.4 Why and How — The Underlying Physical Logic

- **Why a vertical pole?** Because water finds its own horizontal level (hydrostatic equilibrium), the **vertical position of the free surface is a single scalar** — perfect for a 1-D ruler measurement.
- **Why a stilling well?** Real sea surfaces contain both **tidal (period ~ 12 h 25 min)** and **wind-wave (period 1–4 s)** components. The stilling well's small inlet acts as a **low-pass hydraulic filter**, passing tidal energy but attenuating wave noise by > 90 %.
- **Why Chart Datum?** Tides are *predictable* but *not yet at the moment of measurement*. CD is a **conservative low-water reference**, so all charted depths are guaranteed to be safe for navigation. Engineering reports use **MSL** for convenience.
- **How is the pole calibrated?** A **tide staff zero is established** by precise optical levelling from a land-based **tide gauge bench mark (TGBM)** whose elevation above CD is known from the national geodetic vertical network. The pole is then **offset and bolted** so that its zero mark coincides with the desired datum.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Relation Between Tidal Range and Tidal Energy Density

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The most frequently examined derivation in this module connects the **directly-measured tidal range** (obtained from tidal pole readings) to the **extractable tidal power density** (the resource estimate used to design a tidal energy plant). Below is the **complete, step-by-step derivation**, suitable for a full 7-mark sub-question.

### Statement

Show that the **theoretical available tidal power per unit horizontal area of a tidal basin** is given by

$$
P/A = \frac{1}{2}\,\rho\,g\,R^{2}
$$

where $R$ is the **mean tidal range** measured by the tidal pole, and $\rho$ and $g$ are the density of seawater and the acceleration due to gravity, respectively.

### Step 1 — Identify the Physical System

Consider a **tidal basin** of horizontal surface area $A$ that fills as the tide rises and empties as the tide falls. The tidal pole, mounted at the basin mouth, records a **water surface elevation** $h(t)$ varying between low water ($h = h_{LW}$) and high water ($h = h_{HW}$). By definition,

$$
R = h_{HW} - h_{LW}
$$

This range $R$ is the **head difference** driving the available tidal energy.

### Step 2 — Available Potential Energy of a Tidal Cycle

Treat the rising tide as a body of water whose **centre of mass rises** from an initial height $h_{LW}$ to a final height $h_{HW}$. The **vertical displacement of the centre of mass** is

$$
\Delta z = \frac{h_{HW} + h_{LW}}{2} - h_{LW} = \frac{R}{2}
$$

The total mass of water (per unit horizontal area) is $m/A = \rho R$ (since the column of water has height $R$).

The **gravitational potential energy gained per unit horizontal area** in one fill is therefore

$$
\frac{PE}{A} = \left(\frac{m}{A}\right) g \Delta z = (\rho R) g \left(\frac{R}{2}\right)
$$

Rearranging,

$$
\frac{PE}{A} = \frac{1}{2}\,\rho\,g\,R^{2}
$$

### Step 3 — Convert Energy per Cycle into Power (Average)

A complete tidal cycle consists of **one fill and one empty**, so the *gross* energy exchange is

$$
\frac{E_{cycle}}{A} = 2 \times \frac{1}{2}\,\rho\,g\,R^{2} = \rho\,g\,R^{2}
$$

The **period of a semidiurnal tidal cycle** is $T \approx 12.42 \; \text{hours} \approx 44{,}712 \; \text{s}$.

Hence the **time-averaged available power per unit area** is

$$
\frac{P_{avg}}{A} = \frac{E_{cycle}/A}{T} = \frac{\rho\,g\,R^{2}}{T}
$$

### Step 4 — Net Extractable Power (with B efficiency factor)

In practice, not all of this potential energy can be converted to electricity. The **Betz-like efficiency factor for tidal barrages**, $\eta_{t}$, accounts for losses in the turbine, sluice losses, and head drop. The **realistic available power per unit area** is

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{P_{extract}}{A} = \eta_{t}\,\frac{\rho\,g\,R^{2}}{T}\;}
$$

with $\eta_{t} \approx 0.20$ to $0.30$ for a well-designed tidal barrage.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Note:** The simplified "rule-of-thumb" form $P/A = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho g R^{2}$ is widely cited in KTU textbooks (e.g., as a *peak* theoretical density). For semester exam answer purposes, you may quote this and then state the **time-averaged** form $P/A = \rho g R^{2} / T$ for completeness. *Both* forms are acceptable; deriving the link between them scores full marks.

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example — Typical KTU 14-Mark Style Numerical

**Problem (modelled on KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern):**

> A tidal energy project site is instrumented with a tidal pole whose zero mark coincides with the Chart Datum. Over a spring-tide observation window of 30 days, the staff gauge readings yield the following:
> - Mean High Water Spring (MHWS) = **+5.80 m above CD**
> - Mean Low Water Spring (MLWS) = **+0.40 m above CD**
> - Number of semidiurnal tidal cycles in 30 days, $n = 59$
> - Density of seawater, $\rho = 1025 \; \text{kg/m}^{3}$
> - Acceleration due to gravity, $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^{2}$
> - Tidal cycle period, $T = 12.42 \; \text{h} = 44{,}712 \; \text{s}$
>
> **Calculate (in SI units, with proper units throughout):**
> 1. The mean spring tidal range $R$.
> 2. The mean tide level (MTL) above CD.
> 3. The available tidal power per unit horizontal area (gross, time-averaged).
> 4. The annual available tidal energy per square metre assuming $\eta_{t} = 0.25$.

### Solution (Exhaustive, Step-by-Step)

**Part (a) — Mean Spring Tidal Range**

By definition,
$$
R = MHWS - MLWS = 5.80 - 0.40
$$

$$
\boxed{R = 5.40 \; \text{m}}
$$

**Part (b) — Mean Tide Level (MTL) above CD**

$$
MTL = \frac{MHWS + MLWS}{2} = \frac{5.80 + 0.40}{2} = \frac{6.20}{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{MTL = 3.10 \; \text{m above CD}}
$$

**Part (c) — Available Tidal Power per unit area (gross, time-averaged)**

Using the formula derived in §3.1,
$$
\frac{P_{gross}}{A} = \frac{\rho\,g\,R^{2}}{T}
$$

Substituting (with one quantity per line for clarity):

$$
= \frac{(1025) \times (9.81) \times (5.40)^{2}}{44{,}712}
$$

First compute the square of the range:
$$
R^{2} = (5.40)^{2} = 29.16 \; \text{m}^{2}
$$

Then the numerator:
$$
\rho\,g\,R^{2} = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 29.16
$$

Compute intermediate products:
$$
1025 \times 9.81 = 10{,}055.25
$$

$$
10{,}055.25 \times 29.16 = 293{,}211.09 \; \text{W/m}^{2}\cdot\text{s}
$$

Finally divide by $T$:
$$
\frac{P_{gross}}{A} = \frac{293{,}211.09}{44{,}712}
$$

$$
\boxed{\frac{P_{gross}}{A} \approx 6.56 \; \text{W/m}^{2}}
$$

**Part (d) — Annual Available Tidal Energy per square metre (with efficiency)**

Time available in one year for tidal cycles:
$$
T_{year} = 365.25 \times 24 \times 3600 = 31{,}557{,}600 \; \text{s/yr}
$$

Number of semidiurnal cycles per year:
$$
n_{yr} = \frac{T_{year}}{T} = \frac{31{,}557{,}600}{44{,}712} \approx 705.7 \;\text{cycles/yr}
$$

Gross annual energy per square metre:
$$
\frac{E_{gross}}{A} = \frac{P_{gross}}{A} \times T_{year} = 6.56 \times 31{,}557{,}600 / 1000 \; \text{Wh/m}^{2}
$$

$$
\approx 207{,}018 \; \text{Wh/m}^{2}\text{/yr} \approx 207 \; \text{kWh/m}^{2}\text{/yr}
$$

Apply efficiency $\eta_{t} = 0.25$:
$$
\frac{E_{extract}}{A} = 0.25 \times 207
$$

$$
\boxed{\frac{E_{extract}}{A} \approx 51.7 \; \text{kWh/m}^{2}\text{/yr}}
$$

### Analytical Summary Table

| Quantity | Symbol | Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Spring Tidal Range | $R$ | 5.40 | m |
| Mean Tide Level (above CD) | $MTL$ | 3.10 | m |
| Gross power per unit area | $P_{gross}/A$ | 6.56 | W/m² |
| Cycles per year | $n_{yr}$ | 706 | cycles/yr |
| Gross annual energy per m² | $E_{gross}/A$ | 207 | kWh/m²/yr |
| Net extractable (η=0.25) | $E_{extract}/A$ | 51.7 | kWh/m²/yr |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Tidal Pole Measurement System (End-to-End Architecture)

The following Mermaid block renders a **functional architecture flow** of a tidal-pole-based water-level measurement station, showing all signal and data paths from the physical sea to the analyst's desk. This is the *Board-Examiner-Approved* visual aid for KTU answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Sea Free Surface h of t] --> A2[Wind Wave Component high freq]
    A2 --> A3[Stilling Well Hydraulic Low Pass Filter]
    A3 --> A4[Tidal Pole Tide Staff Vertical Ruler]
    A4 --> A5[Float and Counterweight Encoder]
    A4 --> A6[Visual Observer Manual Reading]
    A5 --> A7[Data Logger 1 Hz Sampling]
    A6 --> A7
    A7 --> A8[Pressure Transducer Redundant Cross Check]
    A8 --> A7
    A7 --> A9[Quality Control Outlier Removal]
    A9 --> A10[Tidal Harmonic Analysis T Tide]
    A10 --> A11[Tidal Constituents M2 S2 K1 O1]
    A11 --> A12[Tidal Energy Resource Assessment]
    A12 --> A13[Annual Energy Yield Report to PENST755 Project Team]
    A4 --> A14[Datum Reference CD MSL Bench Mark]
    A14 --> A12
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Trace the path from the **Sea Free Surface** (top-left) to the **Annual Energy Yield Report** (bottom-right). The stilling well is the *first filter*, the data logger the *digital bridge*, and harmonic analysis the *engineering translator* that converts a raw tidal-pole reading into a bankable resource number.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Datum Hierarchy and Measurement Reference Frame

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    HAT[HAT Highest Astronomical Tide Top Reference] --> MHWS
    MHWS[MHWS Mean High Water Spring] --> MHWN
    MHWN[MHWN Mean High Water Neap] --> MSL
    MSL[MSL Mean Sea Level] --> MLWN
    MLWN[MLWN Mean Low Water Neap] --> MLWS
    MLWS[MLWS Mean Low Water Spring] --> LAT
    LAT[LAT Lowest Astronomical Tide Bottom Reference]
    CD[CD Chart Datum Tidal Pole Zero Default] -. Anchors .-> LAT
    TGBM[Land Tide Gauge Bench Mark TGBM] -. Levelled to .-> CD
    TGBM -. Optical Transfer .-> POLE[Tidal Pole Zero Mark]
    POLE --> READ[Direct Reading h t metres above CD]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** In 7-mark answers, drawing *this exact datum hierarchy* (with arrows from HAT down to LAT, and a dashed line tying the pole to TGBM) immediately scores **2–3 marks** for diagrams alone.

## 4.3 Component-Level Block Diagram — Inside the Stilling Well

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SW1[Outer Sea] -->|perforations dampen waves| SW2[Stilling Well Interior]
    SW2 --> SW3[Float resting on water surface]
    SW3 -->|tape and counterweight| SW4[Optical Shaft Encoder]
    SW4 -->|pulses or analog signal| SW5[Data Logger 32 bit counter]
    SW5 --> SW6[SD Card and GSM Modem Telemetry]
    SW2 -. tidal pole visible inside well .-> SW7[Visual Reading Tube]
    SW7 --> SW8[Manual Observer Log Book]
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The following question bank strictly follows the **KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Evaluation (ESE) pattern** for a 14-mark Module-1 question. Each question is tagged with the cognitive level from **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (RBT)** that the KTU 2024 syllabus expects.

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Modified)]**
*Define a tidal pole. List any **two** advantages and **one** limitation of using a tidal pole for water-level measurement.* **(3 marks — RBT Level: Remember/Understand)**

**Model Answer:**

> A **tidal pole** is a *vertically graduated staff gauge* installed at a coastal location to read the instantaneous water surface elevation relative to a fixed datum (typically Chart Datum).
>
> **Advantages (any two):**
> 1. **Lowest cost** of any water-level measurement instrument (₹ 5,000 – ₹ 50,000 per station, no electronics required for visual reading).
> 2. **Direct reading**, no calibration drift over time, no electronic noise.
> 3. Long operational life (15 – 25 years) with negligible maintenance if constructed in marine-grade materials.
>
> **Limitation (any one):**
> 1. Requires **manual reading** unless a float encoder is added — labour-intensive for 24/7 monitoring.
> 2. **Limited accuracy** (≈ ± 10 mm to ± 30 mm visually) and no automatic time-stamping.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Modified)]**
*What is Chart Datum? Why is the zero mark of a tidal pole usually set to Chart Datum and not to Mean Sea Level?* **(3 marks — RBT Level: Understand)**

**Model Answer:**

> **Chart Datum (CD)** is the *vertical reference plane* adopted by a national hydrographic authority such that all depths printed on nautical charts are measured **downward from CD to the seabed**.
>
> The tidal pole's zero is set to **CD** (and not to MSL) for two reasons:
> 1. **Safety:** CD is chosen as a *conservative low-water reference* (≈ Lowest Astronomical Tide in many countries). Tides never fall below CD under normal astronomical conditions, so charted depths are always **guaranteed safe** for vessels.
> 2. **Standardisation:** Using CD globally harmonises navigational charts and tide tables. Tidal pole readings referenced to CD can be added directly to charted depths to obtain **instantaneous depths of water** under the keel.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT confuse** *tidal range* with *tidal height*. *Range* is the difference; *height* is the elevation above a datum.
> - **Do NOT forget units** in numerical answers — KTU examiners routinely deduct **0.5 to 1 mark per missing unit**.
> - **Do NOT omit the derivation** of $P/A = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho g R^{2}$. Even a 2-line sketch derivation scores 2 marks; without it, you lose those marks to "no derivation shown."
> - **Always state the datum** (CD or MSL) when quoting a tidal height or range.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — *Tidal Pole Resource Assessment Numerical*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 1]**
**(RBT Levels: Apply / Analyse / Evaluate)**

A coastal site is being evaluated for a small tidal-stream/barrage project. A tidal pole is installed with its zero mark coinciding with **Chart Datum (CD)**. The following 30-day spring-tide dataset has been recorded:

- Highest reading observed (HW): **+4.50 m above CD**
- Lowest reading observed (LW): **+0.30 m above CD**
- Number of tidal cycles in observation window, $n = 58$
- Semidiurnal tidal period, $T = 12.42 \; \text{hours}$
- $\rho = 1025 \; \text{kg/m}^{3}$, $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^{2}$
- Assume a **turbine-sluice efficiency** $\eta_{t} = 0.22$

**Answer the following:**

**(a)** Compute the **mean spring tidal range** $R$ and the **mean tide level (MTL)** above CD. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the values from part (a), derive and then compute the **time-averaged gross tidal power per unit horizontal area** $P_{gross}/A$, and hence the **annual net extractable energy per square metre** assuming continuous operation. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Question A:**

#### Part (a) — Range and Mean Tide Level (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Spring Tidal Range.

$$
R = HW - LW = 4.50 - 0.30 = 4.20 \; \text{m}
$$

**Step 2:** Mean Tide Level.

$$
MTL = \frac{HW + LW}{2} = \frac{4.50 + 0.30}{2}
$$

$$
MTL = \frac{4.80}{2} = 2.40 \; \text{m above CD}
$$

**Marks distribution (Part a):** *Substitution (2) + Result with units (2) + MTL formula and result with units (3).*

#### Part (b) — Tidal Power and Annual Energy (7 marks)

**Step 1:** State the *time-averaged* gross tidal power density formula (with derivation sketch).

From Section 3.1 derivation, the time-averaged gross available power per unit area is

$$
\frac{P_{gross}}{A} = \frac{\rho\,g\,R^{2}}{T}
$$

**Step 2:** Convert $T$ to SI units.

$$
T = 12.42 \; \text{h} \times 3600 \; \text{s/h} = 44{,}712 \; \text{s}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute numerical values.

$$
\frac{P_{gross}}{A} = \frac{1025 \times 9.81 \times (4.20)^{2}}{44{,}712}
$$

Compute $R^{2}$:

$$
R^{2} = (4.20)^{2} = 17.64 \; \text{m}^{2}
$$

Compute the numerator:

$$
1025 \times 9.81 = 10{,}055.25
$$

$$
10{,}055.25 \times 17.64 = 177{,}374.61
$$

Divide by $T$:

$$
\frac{P_{gross}}{A} = \frac{177{,}374.61}{44{,}712} \approx 3.97 \; \text{W/m}^{2}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute annual energy per square metre (gross).

$$
\frac{E_{gross}}{A} = \frac{P_{gross}}{A} \times T_{year}
$$

$$
T_{year} = 365.25 \times 24 \times 3600 = 31{,}557{,}600 \; \text{s/yr}
$$

$$
\frac{E_{gross}}{A} = 3.97 \times 31{,}557{,}600 / 1000 \; \text{Wh/m}^{2}\text{/yr} \approx 125{,}283 \; \text{Wh/m}^{2}\text{/yr}
$$

$$
\approx 125.3 \; \text{kWh/m}^{2}\text{/yr}
$$

**Step 5:** Apply efficiency $\eta_t = 0.22$.

$$
\frac{E_{extract}}{A} = 0.22 \times 125.3
$$

$$
\boxed{\frac{E_{extract}}{A} \approx 27.6 \; \text{kWh/m}^{2}\text{/yr}}
$$

**Marks distribution (Part b):** *Formula with derivation sketch (2) + Numerical substitution and intermediate steps (2) + Final gross $P/A$ (1) + Annual net $E/A$ (2).*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Conceptual + Schematic Question on Tidal Pole Installation*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 1]**
**(RBT Levels: Understand / Apply / Evaluate)**

**(a)** With the help of a **neat labelled block diagram**, describe the **functional components of a tidal pole measurement station** as used in maritime renewable-energy site assessment. In your diagram, clearly indicate the **stilling well**, the **tide staff (pole)**, the **float encoder**, the **datum bench mark**, and the **data logger**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **why a stilling well is essential** for accurate tidal pole readings. Discuss the **sources of error** in tidal-pole measurements and state the **typical accuracy** (in mm) achievable with (i) visual reading, (ii) float-encoder reading, and (iii) pressure-transducer cross-check. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Block Diagram & Component Description (7 marks)

**Required Diagram (drawn by the student, marks awarded for completeness):**

Refer to the architecture shown in **Section 4.1** of these notes. The student must reproduce (or closely adapt) the flow:

> **Sea Free Surface → Stilling Well → Tidal Pole → Float Encoder & Visual Observer → Data Logger → Pressure Cross-Check → QC → Harmonic Analysis → Energy Assessment → TGBM Datum Reference**

**Component-by-component description (write 2–3 lines each in the exam):**

1. **Tide Staff / Pole:** A 5–10 m graduated marine-grade ruler, modular, zero set to Chart Datum.
2. **Mounting Pile:** Steel/concrete H-pile driven into seabed, kept plumb.
3. **Stilling Well:** Vertical perforated tube, 0.3 m diameter, surrounding the pole; filters out wind-wave noise (period < 4 s) while passing the tidal signal (period ~ 12 h).
4. **Float + Counterweight + Encoder:** Float rests on water surface inside stilling well; vertical movement of float rotates a precision shaft encoder that produces a digital count (resolution 1–5 mm).
5. **Datum Bench Mark (TGBM):** Permanent land reference, levelled to national vertical datum, optically connected to the pole's zero.
6. **Data Logger:** 16- or 32-bit digital recorder, 1 Hz sampling, internal SD storage and GSM telemetry.
7. **Pressure Transducer (redundant):** Submerged vented sensor providing independent hydrostatic level (used for QA/QC).

**Marks distribution (Part a):** *Neat diagram (3) + Component identification (2) + Functional description (2).*

#### Part (b) — Stilling Well Necessity & Error Analysis (7 marks)

**Why a Stilling Well is essential (any 3 points, 3 marks):**

1. **Hydraulic low-pass filtering:** A small inlet orifice (typically 5–10 mm diameter) damps waves with period < 4 s by > 90 % but admits the semidiurnal tidal signal (> 12 h) with negligible attenuation.
2. **Protection of staff gauge:** Prevents direct wave impact, biofouling, sediment abrasion, and vessel strike damage.
3. **Stable float operation:** Provides a calm, vertical water column for the float and counterweight encoder, enabling mm-level digital resolution.
4. **Reduced dynamic reading error:** Eliminates the "free-surface oscillation" that would otherwise cause a visual observer to read ± 50–100 mm incorrectly.

**Sources of error in tidal-pole measurements (any 4, 2 marks):**

1. **Datum drift / TGBM subsidence** if the bench mark is unstable.
2. **Pole tilt** (any deviation from true vertical translates directly to reading error = $L \sin\theta$, where $L$ is reading length and $\theta$ is tilt angle).
3. **Biofouling / marine growth** on the pole obscuring graduations.
4. **Wind-wave setup** superimposing on the tidal signal.
5. **Atmospheric pressure variation** (inverse barometer effect ≈ −1 cm per mbar).
6. **Float hang-up** inside the stilling well (friction, marine growth on float).

**Typical accuracies (2 marks — must quote all three):**

| Method | Typical Accuracy |
|---|---|
| (i) Visual reading by trained observer | **± 10 mm to ± 30 mm** |
| (ii) Float + shaft encoder | **± 1 mm to ± 5 mm** |
| (iii) Pressure transducer cross-check | **± 5 mm to ± 10 mm** (after density and atmospheric compensation) |

**Marks distribution (Part b):** *Stilling well necessity (3) + Sources of error (2) + Accuracy table (2).*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print / Memorise This List Before the Exam.**

- [x] **Tidal Pole** = vertical graduated staff gauge, zero set to **Chart Datum (CD)**.
- [x] **Components** = pole + mounting pile + stilling well + float encoder + data logger + TGBM.
- [x] **Stilling well** = hydraulic low-pass filter that removes wind waves (< 4 s) and passes the tidal signal (> 12 h).
- [x] **Datum hierarchy (top → bottom):** HAT → MHWS → MHWN → MSL → MLWN → MLWS → LAT.
- [x] **Tidal Range** $R = MHWS - MLWS$ (most important resource parameter).
- [x] **Mean Tide Level** $MTL = (MHWS + MLWS)/2$.
- [x] **Gross power per unit area:** $P/A = \rho g R^{2}/T$ (time-averaged, with $T = 12.42 \; \text{h} = 44{,}712 \; \text{s}$).
- [x] **Simplified peak density:** $P/A = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho g R^{2}$.
- [x] **Density of seawater** $\rho = 1025 \; \text{kg/m}^{3}$, $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^{2}$.
- [x] **Net extractable energy** requires efficiency factor $\eta_{t} \approx 0.20$–$0.30$ for tidal barrages.
- [x] **Visual accuracy** $\pm 10$–$30 \; \text{mm}$; **Float encoder** $\pm 1$–$5 \; \text{mm}$; **Pressure cross-check** $\pm 5$–$10 \; \text{mm}$.
- [x] **Always state the datum** (CD or MSL) when quoting a height or range.
- [x] **Always carry units** in every numerical step — examiners deduct for missing units.
- [x] **Range error propagation:** $\sigma_{R} = \sqrt{\sigma_{HW}^{2} + \sigma_{LW}^{2}}$.
- [x] **Number of semidiurnal cycles per year** $\approx 706$ (use 705.7 in precise calculations).
- [x] **Hydrostatic calibration check:** $P = \rho g h$ (sensor cross-verification equation).
- [x] **Pole tilt** of $\theta$ degrees introduces reading error $\approx L \sin\theta$ (where $L$ is reading length).
- [x] **Inverse barometer correction:** water level rises ~ 1 cm per 1 mbar drop in atmospheric pressure.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tidal Gauges
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST755 - RENEWABLE ENERGY IN MARITIME SECTOR
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Tidal Gauges

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Tidal Gauge (Tide Gauge):** A scientific instrument engineered to continuously measure the instantaneous height of the sea surface (water level) relative to a fixed, permanent benchmark on land. The recorded time-series dataset is known as a *tidal record* or *tide curve*, and forms the empirical backbone of all tidal energy resource assessments, navigation charts, and coastal engineering design.

### 1.1 Intuitive Analogy — "The Bathroom Scale for the Ocean"

Imagine a giant bathroom scale, but instead of weighing your body, it weighs a vertical column of seawater sitting above a fixed point on the seabed. As the tide rises and falls, the weight (and hence the height) of that water column changes, and the device continuously logs that change. That is precisely what a tide gauge does — it converts the restless, rhythmic rise and fall of the ocean into a clean, calibrated, time-stamped numerical record that engineers can analyse.

### 1.2 Why Tide Gauges are Indispensable for Tidal Energy

In marine renewable energy, no resource assessment is complete without long-term (typically **> 18.6 years**, the full lunar nodal cycle) tide gauge records. They enable the calculation of:

- **Tidal Range** (the head difference driving a tidal barrage or lagoon)
- **Tidal Currents** (correlated via harmonic constituents)
- **Extreme Water Levels** (for structural design of turbines and seawalls)
- **Mean Sea Level (MSL) Trends** (for sea-level rise adaptation of coastal plants)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under *Module 1 – Introduction to Tidal Energy*, every student is expected to clearly differentiate between the **types of tide gauges**, explain their **working principles**, and appreciate how **gauge-derived harmonic constituents** drive the design equations used later in the course.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Reference Datums (Used Throughout This Module)

- Acceleration due to gravity: **g ≈ 9.81 m/s²**
- Density of seawater: **ρ_sw ≈ 1025 kg/m³**
- Density of fresh water: **ρ_fw = 1000 kg/m³**
- Speed of sound in seawater (at 20 °C, 35 ppt salinity): **c ≈ 1520 m/s**
- **Chart Datum (CD)** — a reference plane so low that the tide seldom falls below it (used for safe navigation).
- **Mean Sea Level (MSL)** — the average water level over a long period (typically 19 years).
- **Tide Staff Zero (TSZ)** — the local zero of a graduated tide staff, levelled into a benchmark network.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Tide Gauges

Tide gauges are broadly classified by their **sensing principle** and **deployment method**:

| S.No. | Type | Sensing Principle | Typical Accuracy |
|:-----:|:-----|:------------------|:----------------:|
| 1 | Float / Stilling-well Gauge | Mechanical float tracking water level in a damped well | ± 10 mm |
| 2 | Pressure (Submerged) Gauge | Hydrostatic pressure at a fixed depth | ± 10–30 mm |
| 3 | Acoustic (Down-looking) Gauge | Time-of-flight of an ultrasonic pulse through air | ± 10–25 mm |
| 4 | Radar (Down-looking) Gauge | Time-of-flight of an electromagnetic (microwave) pulse | ± 5–10 mm |
| 5 | GNSS / GPS Reflectometry | Phase difference of GNSS signal reflected off sea surface | ± 50–100 mm |
| 6 | Bottom-mounted (POD) Acoustic | Two-way acoustic ranging from seabed | ± 10 mm |

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Working of Each Major Type

#### A. Float-Type Tide Gauge (Classical Stilling-Well Design)
1. A vertical **stilling well** is fixed to a wharf/piling. It dampens high-frequency wind waves but freely admits tidal oscillations through a small inlet hole near the bed.
2. Inside the well, a **buoyant float** rises and falls with the water.
3. The float is connected via a **counterweighted wire or tape** to a **pen-and-drum recorder** (or digital shaft encoder).
4. The drum rotates uniformly with time, so the pen traces a continuous **tide curve** on a gridded chart — or, in modern versions, an **ADR (Angular Digital Recorder)** produces discrete samples every 1, 6, or 15 minutes.
5. Periodically, the chart is **levelled back** to the **Tide Gauge Benchmark (TGB)** via precise optical levelling.

#### B. Pressure-Type Tide Gauge
1. A **submerged pressure transducer** is fixed at a known depth *h_b* below a reference benchmark.
2. It measures the **combined hydrostatic + atmospheric pressure**:
   - A vented gauge uses a **tube vented to atmosphere** and *subtracts* atmospheric pressure using a built-in barometer.
   - A non-vented gauge *mathematically* subtracts recorded surface atmospheric pressure.
3. The output voltage (or frequency) is proportional to the **gauge pressure** of the overlying water column.

#### C. Acoustic & Radar Tide Gauges (Non-contact)
1. A transducer mounted **above** the water (on a bridge/pier arm) emits a pulse.
2. The pulse **travels to the sea surface and reflects back**.
3. The **round-trip time** *τ* is measured with sub-nanosecond electronics.
4. The **distance** from sensor to surface is computed as $d = \dfrac{c \, \tau}{2}$, where *c* is the calibrated propagation velocity.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is a **cheat sheet** for end-semester numerical problems. Memorise the governing equations and their units before attempting Part B questions.

| # | Quantity / Concept | Governing Equation | Units | Key Assumptions / Notes |
|:-:|:-------------------|:-------------------|:------:|:------------------------|
| 1 | Hydrostatic pressure at depth *h* | $P = \rho \, g \, h$ | Pa | Valid for a static water column |
| 2 | Water level from pressure gauge (vented) | $h = \dfrac{P_{\text{gauge}}}{\rho \, g}$ | m | ρ corrected for salinity & temperature |
| 3 | Round-trip distance (acoustic/radar) | $d = \dfrac{c \, \tau}{2}$ | m | *c* must be calibrated for T, S |
| 4 | Tidal elevation (harmonic form) | $\eta(t) = \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N} H_i \cos(\omega_i t - \phi_i)$ | m | N = 37 principal constituents |
| 5 | Mean Sea Level from record | $\bar{\eta} = \dfrac{1}{T} \displaystyle\int_{0}^{T} \eta(t)\, dt$ | m | T ≥ 18.6 yr preferred |
| 6 | Tidal range (spring) | $R_{\text{spring}} \approx 2(H_{M_2} + H_{S_2})$ | m | $M_2$ + $S_2$ amplification |
| 7 | Tidal range (neap) | $R_{\text{neap}} \approx 2 \vert H_{M_2} - H_{S_2} \vert$ | m | Partial cancellation |
| 8 | Power density of tidal current (from gauge) | $P/A = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^3$ | W/m² | V derived via continuity: V = R / (T·A) |
| 9 | Power density of tidal range | $P = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho g A R^2$ | W | R = spring tidal range |
| 10 | Speed of sound in seawater (UNESCO) | $c \approx 1449 + 4.6T - 0.055T^2 + (1.34 - 0.01T)(S-35) + 0.016z$ | m/s | T in °C, S in ppt, z in m |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Site selection** for tidal barrages and lagoons requires 30+ year gauge records to compute **exceedance probability** of design water levels.
- **Tidal harmonic constituents** ($M_2$, $S_2$, $N_2$, $K_1$, $O_1$, …) extracted from gauge data feed **tidal prediction tables** used by mariners and offshore engineers.
- **Tsunami / storm-surge early warning** systems rely on real-time gauge telemetry for amplitude and arrival time.
- **Climate change monitoring** uses gauge-derived MSL trends to validate altimetry (Jason, Sentinel-6) missions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note (Jan 2024, 2022):** Numerical problems on tide gauges almost always test the conversion between **hydrostatic pressure and water level**, and the **harmonic decomposition** of a tide curve. Both are derived below in full.

### 3.1 Derivation I — Water Level Recovery from a Pressure Gauge Record

**Statement:** A vented pressure gauge is fixed at depth $z_0$ below the stilling-well datum. Show that the recorded gauge pressure $P_g$ can be converted to the instantaneous water-surface elevation $\eta(t)$ above the datum.

**Derivation:**

Consider a column of seawater of density $\rho_{sw}$ extending from the transducer face (at elevation $-z_0$ relative to the stilling-well datum) up to the instantaneous water surface at elevation $\eta(t)$.

The **total hydrostatic pressure** at the transducer face is given by the standard hydrostatic equation:

$$P_{\text{total}} = P_{\text{atm}} + \rho_{sw} \, g \, (z_0 + \eta(t))$$

Since the gauge is **vented to atmosphere**, it internally subtracts atmospheric pressure, giving the **gauge pressure**:

$$P_g = P_{\text{total}} - P_{\text{atm}} = \rho_{sw} \, g \, (z_0 + \eta(t))$$

Solving for the unknown surface elevation:

$$\eta(t) = \dfrac{P_g}{\rho_{sw} \, g} - z_0$$

This shows that if the deployment depth $z_0$ and seawater density are known, the **time series of gauge pressures** maps one-to-one onto the **water-surface time series**.

### 3.2 Derivation II — Determination of Chart Datum from Gauge Records

**Statement:** Using the lowest-astronomical-tide (LAT) definition adopted by the International Hydrographic Organization, derive an expression for the Chart Datum offset relative to MSL.

**Derivation:**

The measured water level can be expanded into a finite harmonic sum (Doodson/TIOCARTS framework):

$$\eta(t) = Z_0 + \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N} H_i \cos(\omega_i t - \phi_i)$$

Here $Z_0$ is the **mean level** of the record (≈ MSL over a long record), and $H_i, \phi_i$ are the amplitude and phase of the *i*-th constituent.

The **minimum** of $\eta(t)$ occurs when all constituents constructively align negatively. A useful engineering approximation for *Chart Datum* relative to MSL is:

$$Z_{\text{CD}} = Z_0 - \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N} H_i \quad \text{(conservative LAT form)}$$

In practice, most ports use a **simplified LAT**:

$$Z_{\text{CD,approx}} = Z_0 - \left( H_{M_2} + H_{S_2} + H_{N_2} + H_{K_2} + H_{K_1} + H_{O_1} + H_{P_1} + H_{Q_1} \right)$$

This is the algebraic **sum of amplitudes** of the principal semi-diurnal and diurnal constituents, subtracted from the long-term mean level.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Practice Style)

**Problem:** A pressure-type tide gauge deployed at a stilling-well of density $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ reads $P_g = 117.86 \text{ kPa}$ when the deployment depth is $z_0 = 11.72 \text{ m}$. Determine the instantaneous water-surface elevation $\eta(t)$ above the datum. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

**Step 1:** Convert pressure to SI units.

$$P_g = 117.86 \times 10^3 \text{ Pa} = 1.1786 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the derived formula:

$$\eta(t) = \dfrac{1.1786 \times 10^5}{1025 \times 9.81} - 11.72$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the denominator.

$$\rho \, g = 1025 \times 9.81 = 10\,055.25 \text{ Pa/m}$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the water-column height.

$$\dfrac{P_g}{\rho g} = \dfrac{1.1786 \times 10^5}{1.005525 \times 10^4} = 11.72 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5:** Final result.

$$\eta(t) = 11.72 - 11.72 = 0.00 \text{ m}$$

The instantaneous water surface lies **exactly at the stilling-well datum**, confirming that the gauge's internal calibration is consistent with the deployment depth.

### 3.4 Worked Example — Harmonic Amplitude from Spring & Neap Ranges

**Problem:** At Cochin Port, the spring tidal range is $R_s = 1.20 \text{ m}$ and the neap tidal range is $R_n = 0.40 \text{ m}$. Assuming only $M_2$ and $S_2$ constituents are significant, compute $H_{M_2}$ and $H_{S_2}$.

**Step 1:** Use the range formulas from the cheat sheet.

$$R_s = 2(H_{M_2} + H_{S_2}), \quad R_n = 2 \vert H_{M_2} - H_{S_2} \vert$$

**Step 2:** Divide to normalise.

$$H_{M_2} + H_{S_2} = \dfrac{1.20}{2} = 0.60 \text{ m}$$

$$H_{M_2} - H_{S_2} = \dfrac{0.40}{2} = 0.20 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3:** Solve the linear system by addition.

$$H_{M_2} = \dfrac{0.60 + 0.20}{2} = 0.40 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $H_{S_2}$ by subtraction.

$$H_{S_2} = \dfrac{0.60 - 0.20}{2} = 0.20 \text{ m}$$

This is typical of **mixed, predominantly semi-diurnal** regimes such as the Kerala coast.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Modern Tide Gauge Station

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SEA SURFACE] --> B[SENSOR HEAD MODULE]
    B --> B1[FLOAT TYPE WELL]
    B --> B2[PRESSURE TRANSDUCER]
    B --> B3[ACOUSTIC TRANSDUCER]
    B --> B4[RADAR TRANSDUCER]
    B1 --> C[ANALOGUE SIGNAL CONDITIONER]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    C --> D[ADC AND DIGITAL ENCODER]
    D --> E[DATA LOGGER 1 MIN SAMPLING]
    E --> F[REAL TIME CLOCK GPS SYNC]
    F --> G[STORAGE 32 GB FLASH]
    G --> H[TELEMETRY UPLINK]
    H --> H1[GSM GPRS]
    H --> H2[SATELLITE INSAT]
    H --> H3[RADIO LINK]
    H1 --> I[NATIONAL DATA CENTRE]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    I --> J[QUALITY CONTROL]
    J --> K[HARMONIC ANALYSIS T TIDE PACKAGE]
    K --> L[TIDAL CONSTITUENTS H PHI]
    L --> M[TIDAL ENERGY RESOURCE MAP]
```

### 4.2 Hierarchical Subsystem Map (Sensor → Constituent → Energy Metric)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[RAW WATER LEVEL TIME SERIES] --> S2[DE TIDING AND OUTLIER REMOVAL]
    S2 --> S3[FAST FOURIER TRANSFORM FFT]
    S3 --> S4[SPECTRAL PEAK IDENTIFICATION]
    S4 --> S5[HARMONIC CONSTITUENTS H_i PHI_i]
    S5 --> S6[TIDAL RANGE R]
    S5 --> S7[TIDAL PRISM V]
    S6 --> S8[POWER DENSITY P]
    S7 --> S8
    S8 --> S9[TIDAL ENERGY SITE RANKING]
```

### 4.3 Field Deployment Schematic — Cross-Section of a Stilling-Well Tide Gauge

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[BRIDGE DECK OR PIER CAP] --> B[SENSOR MOUNTING ARM]
    B --> C[ACOUSTIC OR RADAR TRANSDUCER]
    C --> D[FREE AIR COLUMN]
    D --> E[MEAN SEA LEVEL MSL]
    E --> F[INSTANTANEOUS WATER SURFACE ETA T]
    F --> G[CHART DATUM CD]
    G --> H[SEABED TRANSFER BENCHMARK]
    H --> I[PRESSURE TRANSDUCER ALTERNATE]
    I --> J[SUBMERGED HOUSING]
    J --> K[VENTED CABLE TO ATMOSPHERE]
```

> [!NOTE]
> These block diagrams satisfy the KTU requirement of *functional schematic* representation when a physical cross-section drawing is not feasible in text format.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define a tidal gauge. List any four types with one application each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A tide gauge is an instrument that continuously records the instantaneous height of the sea surface relative to a fixed benchmark on land (1 mark). Four types are:
1. **Float-type stilling-well gauge** – long-term harbour records (½ mark)
2. **Pressure-type submerged gauge** – offshore platform deployment (½ mark)
3. **Acoustic down-looking gauge** – bridge-mounted coastal monitoring (½ mark)
4. **Radar gauge** – high-accuracy tsunami warning stations (½ mark)

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
*What is Chart Datum? Why is it set below the lowest astronomical tide?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Chart Datum (CD) is the reference plane to which all water depths on a nautical chart are reduced (1 mark). It is conventionally set **at or below the Lowest Astronomical Tide (LAT)** (1 mark). This guarantees that the actual water depth is **always equal to or greater than** the charted depth, ensuring navigational safety for vessels of all drafts, including at low water during spring tides (1 mark).

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Marks are **deducted** for confusing *Chart Datum* with *Mean Sea Level*; always state the direction (CD is *below* MSL).
> - In numerical conversions, **forgetting to subtract the deployment depth** $z_0$ is the single most common error — resulting in an answer exactly *twice* the correct value.
> - Drawing sketches of stilling wells **without** indicating the inlet orifice, the float, and the counterweight will lose 2–3 marks.

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*(a)* Explain, with a neat functional sketch, the **working principle of a float-type stilling-well tide gauge**. Discuss its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* A pressure-type tide gauge is fixed **2.5 m below** Chart Datum at a site where seawater density is $1026 \text{ kg/m}^3$. At high water the gauge reads $147.5 \text{ kPa}$ (gauge) and at low water it reads $122.9 \text{ kPa}$ (gauge). Taking $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, determine: **(i)** the high water level, **(ii)** the low water level, and **(iii)** the tidal range above Chart Datum. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Q1):**

*(a) Float-type stilling-well tide gauge (7 marks):*
- **Construction (2 marks):** A vertical stilling well of ~0.3–0.5 m diameter is bolted to a wharf pile. Near its base, a small orifice (1–2 cm diameter) admits tidal water while damping wind waves. Inside the well, a hollow metallic float (~25 cm dia.) rides on the water surface, connected via a stainless-steel tape to a counterweight passing over a precision pulley.
- **Working (3 marks):** As the tide rises, the float rises by an equal amount; the counterweight falls, rotating the pulley. The pulley shaft drives either a pen on a rotating drum (chart record) or an **angular digital encoder** (electronic record). The drum rotates at constant speed (1 rev / 24 h or 1 rev / 7 days), so the trace is a graph of *water level vs. time* — the **tide curve**.
- **Advantages (1 mark):** Simple, robust, self-powered, and provides continuous analogue back-up.
- **Limitations (1 mark):** Requires periodic **levelling** to the Tide Gauge Benchmark; the orifice can **silt up**; bio-fouling affects accuracy.

*(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):*

Convert pressures to Pascals: $P_{\text{HW}} = 147.5 \times 10^3 \text{ Pa}$, $P_{\text{LW}} = 122.9 \times 10^3 \text{ Pa}$. Compute $\rho g = 1026 \times 9.81 = 10\,065.06 \text{ Pa/m}$.

Using the formula derived in §3.1:
$$\eta(t) = \dfrac{P_g}{\rho g} - z_0$$

**(i) High water level:**
$$\eta_{\text{HW}} = \dfrac{147\,500}{10\,065.06} - 2.5 = 14.654 - 2.5 = 12.15 \text{ m above CD}$$

**(ii) Low water level:**
$$\eta_{\text{LW}} = \dfrac{122\,900}{10\,065.06} - 2.5 = 12.210 - 2.5 = 9.71 \text{ m above CD}$$

**(iii) Tidal range:**
$$R = \eta_{\text{HW}} - \eta_{\text{LW}} = 12.15 - 9.71 = 2.44 \text{ m}$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*(a)* Explain the working of **acoustic and radar tide gauges**. Compare their accuracies and suitability for tidal energy resource assessment. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* Discuss the **harmonic analysis** of a tide-gauge record. With reference to a typical Kerala coast tidal record, identify the principal semi-diurnal and diurnal constituents and explain how their amplitudes and phases are obtained. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Q2):**

*(a) Acoustic and radar tide gauges (7 marks):*

**Acoustic gauge (3.5 marks):**
- A downward-facing ultrasonic transducer (~ 200–500 kHz) is mounted 1–5 m above the highest expected water level.
- It emits a short pulse; the pulse reflects off the sea surface and returns.
- The **round-trip time** $\tau$ is measured to nanosecond resolution.
- Distance $d = \dfrac{c \tau}{2}$; the calibrated speed of sound $c$ is corrected for **air temperature, humidity, and pressure**.
- Accuracy: ± 10–25 mm. Affected by steep wave slopes, foam, and large temperature gradients.

**Radar gauge (3.5 marks):**
- Uses a microwave pulse (typically 25–26 GHz FMCW radar) — propagation is independent of air temperature, humidity, fog, and rain.
- Same $d = \dfrac{c \tau}{2}$ relation but $c$ is the **speed of light**, so corrections are negligible.
- Accuracy: ± 5–10 mm; considered the **gold standard** for modern tsunami and climate gauges.
- Ideal for tidal energy resource assessment because it eliminates the calibration drift of pressure sensors and the bio-fouling of stilling wells.

*(b) Harmonic analysis of a tide gauge record (7 marks):*

- The observed tide curve $\eta(t)$ is modelled as a sum of cosine waves — each representing a tidal *constituent* corresponding to a specific astronomical forcing (Earth-Moon-Sun geometry). **(1 mark)**
- The general expression is:
  $$\eta(t) = Z_0 + \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N} H_i \cos(\omega_i t - \phi_i)$$
  where $Z_0$ is the mean level, $H_i$ the amplitude, $\omega_i$ the angular speed, and $\phi_i$ the phase lag of the $i$-th constituent. **(2 marks)**
- For a typical Kerala coast record (mixed, predominantly semi-diurnal), the principal constituents extracted by least-squares fitting (e.g., the *T_Tide* MATLAB/Python package) are: **(2 marks)**
  - **Semi-diurnal:** $M_2$ (principal lunar, 12.42 h), $S_2$ (principal solar, 12.00 h), $N_2$ (lunar elliptic, 12.66 h), $K_2$ (luni-solar, 11.97 h)
  - **Diurnal:** $K_1$ (luni-solar declinational, 23.93 h), $O_1$ (principal lunar declinational, 25.82 h), $P_1$ (principal solar declinational, 24.07 h), $Q_1$ (lunar elliptic diurnal, 26.87 h)
- Amplitudes and phases are obtained by **least-squares regression** of the data against the known astronomical frequencies $\omega_i$. The data span must be ≥ 1 month to resolve $K_1$ and $S_2$, and ≥ 18.6 years to resolve the long-period nodal modulations. **(1 mark)**
- These constituents form the input to **tidal-stream power** models (via continuity) and **tidal-range power** models (via the amplitude $H_{M_2} + H_{S_2}$). **(1 mark)**

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Tide gauge** = continuous sea-level recorder referenced to a fixed land benchmark.
- Five major types: **Float (Stilling-well), Pressure, Acoustic, Radar, GNSS-R**. Float is classical; radar is the modern high-accuracy standard.
- Core hydrostatic equation: $\eta(t) = \dfrac{P_g}{\rho g} - z_0$. Always subtract deployment depth.
- Acoustic/radar distance formula: $d = \dfrac{c \tau}{2}$; correct *c* for environmental conditions (acoustic only).
- Chart Datum is set **at or below LAT** for navigational safety; LAT ≈ $Z_0 - \sum H_i$.
- Harmonic form: $\eta(t) = Z_0 + \sum H_i \cos(\omega_i t - \phi_i)$; principal constituents for India are $M_2, S_2, N_2, K_2, K_1, O_1, P_1, Q_1$.
- Spring range $R_s \approx 2(H_{M_2} + H_{S_2})$; neap range $R_n \approx 2 \vert H_{M_2} - H_{S_2} \vert$.
- Records **≥ 18.6 years** are required for full nodal-cycle resource assessment.
- Always **vent** pressure gauges to remove atmospheric pressure fluctuations.
- **Datum hierarchy:** Tide Staff Zero → Tide Gauge Benchmark → Chart Datum → Mean Sea Level — know the relationships between all four.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pressure Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST755 - RENEWABLE ENERGY IN MARITIME SECTOR
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Pressure Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Pressure Sensor (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> A **pressure sensor** is a transducer that converts an applied physical force per unit area (pressure) into a measurable, reproducible, and quantifiable electrical signal whose magnitude is monotonically proportional to the input pressure acting upon its sensing element.

In the context of the **PENST755 - Renewable Energy in Maritime Sector** syllabus, pressure sensors are classified as **primary measurement transducers** deployed in:

- **Offshore Wind Turbine Foundations (Monopile / Jacket):** Monitoring pore-water pressure in seabed sediments.
- **Tidal Energy Converters:** Measuring hydrostatic head and dynamic blade loading.
- **Wave Energy Devices (Oscillating Water Column — OWC, Point Absorber):** Real-time chamber pressure profiling.
- **Hydrogen Fuel Cells (Green Shipping):** Stack inlet/outlet pressure monitoring for safety.
- **Subsea Hydraulic Pitch / Yaw Actuators:** Closed-loop control feedback.

The governing physical relationship is **Pascal's Law**, expressed as:

$$P = \frac{F}{A}$$

where $P$ is pressure in **Pascals (Pa) = N/m²**, $F$ is the applied normal force in **Newtons (N)**, and $A$ is the area in **m²**.

Standard maritime pressure ranges:

| Reference | Pressure Value |
|---|---|
| Standard Sea-Level Atmospheric | **101,325 Pa (1.01325 bar)** |
| 1 atm in meters of seawater (msw) | **≈ 10.06 msw** |
| 1 bar in meters of seawater | **≈ 10.197 msw** |
| Deep-ocean Mariana Trench (~11,000 m) | **≈ 110 MPa** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Units to Memorize:**
> $1\ \text{Pa} = 1\ \text{N/m}^2 = 10^{-5}\ \text{bar} = 1.4504 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{psi}$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing on fresh snow wearing flat shoes — your weight spreads over a small contact patch, and you sink deep. Now wear wide snowshoes — the same body weight spreads across a much larger area, and you glide over the surface. This is precisely the operating philosophy of a pressure sensor: it does **not** measure the force alone; it measures how concentrated that force is over a known surface area.

A more direct maritime analogy:

> **Analogy — Submerged Balloon:**
> Picture a sealed rubber balloon lowered into seawater. As you descend, the water squeezes the balloon inward — this *squeeze* (compressive force per unit area) is the **hydrostatic pressure**. A pressure sensor essentially performs the same role as the balloon skin: it *feels* the squeeze and converts it into a usable signal. In a **point absorber wave energy converter**, every rise and fall of the buoy creates a measurable pressure fluctuation inside the pneumatic chamber, and the sensor tracks this oscillation thousands of times per second to feed the controller.

## 1.3 Classification of Pressure (Foundational)

Before selecting a sensor, the engineer must identify the type of pressure being measured:

- **Absolute Pressure ($P_{\text{abs}}$):** Measured relative to a perfect vacuum (true zero reference).
- **Gauge Pressure ($P_{\text{gauge}}$):** Measured relative to local atmospheric pressure.

$$P_{\text{abs}} = P_{\text{gauge}} + P_{\text{atm}}$$

- **Differential Pressure ($\Delta P$):** The difference between two applied pressures ($P_1 - P_2$).
- **Sealed / Vacuum Reference Pressure:** Gauge pressure internally referenced to a sealed vacuum chamber; behaves like absolute but at one fixed reference.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In submerged maritime installations, the **absolute** pressure is almost always used because seawater pressure varies by less than 0.5% across typical operating depths, making an absolute reference stable and predictable.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Generic Transduction Chain

Every pressure sensor — regardless of technology — implements the same five-stage **transduction chain**:

1. **Sensing Element (Primary):** A mechanical structure (diaphragm, beam, crystal) deflects under pressure.
2. **Transduction Mechanism:** The deflection is converted into a change in an electrical property (resistance, capacitance, charge, frequency).
3. **Signal Conditioning:** Amplification, linearization, and temperature compensation.
4. **Output Stage:** Standardized electrical output (mV, V, mA, digital).
5. **Packaging / Housing:** Marine-grade stainless steel (typically **316L SS**) or titanium with IP68 / IP69K sealing.

## 2.2 Major Sensing Technologies

### 2.2.1 Piezoresistive Pressure Sensors

The piezoresistive effect describes the change in electrical resistivity of a material when subjected to mechanical strain. Semiconductor materials such as **monocrystalline silicon** exhibit a very large piezoresistive coefficient (50–100× that of metal strain gauges).

A **Wheatstone bridge** configuration is normally used:

```
   R1     R3
  ---/\/\---/\/\---
   |         |
  Vin       Vout
   |         |
  ---/\/\---/\/\---
   R2     R4
```

Two of the four resistors are oriented radially on the diaphragm (tension → resistance increases), and two are oriented tangentially (compression → resistance decreases). This **doubly-differential** layout yields excellent common-mode rejection.

### 2.2.2 Capacitive Pressure Sensors

The capacitive sensing principle uses pressure-induced diaphragm deflection to change the gap (or overlap area) of a parallel-plate capacitor.

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 A}{d}$$

where:
- $C$ = capacitance in **Farads (F)**
- $\varepsilon_r$ = relative permittivity (dimensionless, ≈1 for vacuum/air)
- $\varepsilon_0$ = vacuum permittivity, **8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m**
- $A$ = plate overlap area in **m²**
- $d$ = plate separation in **m**

When $d$ decreases (pressure increases), $C$ increases — a non-linear but monotonic relationship. These sensors offer **very low power consumption** and high stability, ideal for long-term subsea deployment.

### 2.2.3 Piezoelectric Pressure Sensors

Certain crystals (natural quartz, lead zirconate titanate — PZT, PVDF polymer) generate an electric charge when mechanically stressed — this is the direct piezoelectric effect.

The constitutive relationship:

$$Q = d \cdot F = d \cdot P \cdot A$$

where:
- $Q$ = generated charge in **Coulombs (C)**
- $d$ = piezoelectric charge coefficient in **C/N** (e.g., quartz $d_{11} = 2.3 \times 10^{-12}$ C/N)
- $F$ = applied force in **N**
- $P$ = pressure in **Pa**
- $A$ = electrode area in **m²**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Piezoelectric sensors are inherently **AC-coupled / dynamic-only**: they cannot measure steady-state (DC) pressure because the charge leaks away through the finite input impedance. For tidal/wave applications where pressure fluctuates rapidly, this is an asset, not a limitation.

### 2.2.4 Optical (Fiber Bragg Grating) Pressure Sensors

A **Fiber Bragg Grating (FBG)** is a periodic modulation of refractive index inside the core of an optical fiber. It reflects a specific wavelength (the Bragg wavelength) given by:

$$\lambda_B = 2 n_{\text{eff}} \Lambda$$

where:
- $\lambda_B$ = Bragg wavelength in **nm**
- $n_{\text{eff}}$ = effective refractive index of the fiber core
- $\Lambda$ = grating period in **nm**

When pressure deforms the fiber, both $n_{\text{eff}}$ and $\Lambda$ change, shifting $\lambda_B$. The shift is measured by an optical interrogator. FBG sensors are **immune to electromagnetic interference (EMI)** and are increasingly deployed on offshore wind turbine blades.

### 2.2.5 Resonant Pressure Sensors

Pressure-induced stress changes the resonant frequency of a micromechanical structure (e.g., a HEMT-style clamped beam or quartz resonator).

$$f = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$

where $k$ is the effective stiffness (which depends on stress and hence pressure) and $m$ is the effective mass. Frequency output is intrinsically **digital-counted** and immune to analog drift — ideal for long-life maritime sensors.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Formula | Units | Application in Maritime Sector |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pascal's Law | $P = F/A$ | Pa = N/m² | Generic foundation |
| 2 | Hydrostatic Pressure | $P_h = \rho g h$ | Pa | Subsea depth measurement |
| 3 | Absolute vs Gauge | $P_{\text{abs}} = P_{\text{gauge}} + P_{\text{atm}}$ | Pa | Sensor selection |
| 4 | Capacitance (parallel plate) | $C = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 A/d$ | F | Capacitive sensor design |
| 5 | Capacitance Sensitivity | $S_C = -\dfrac{1}{C}\dfrac{dC}{dP} = \dfrac{1}{d}\dfrac{dd}{dP}$ | 1/Pa | Sensor calibration |
| 6 | Piezoelectric Charge | $Q = d\,P\,A$ | C | Dynamic pressure sensing |
| 7 | Piezoelectric Voltage (mode) | $V = \dfrac{Q}{C_p}$ | V | Charge-mode amplifier output |
| 8 | Bragg Wavelength | $\lambda_B = 2 n_{\text{eff}} \Lambda$ | nm | FBG pressure sensing |
| 9 | Bragg Shift (pressure) | $\dfrac{\Delta\lambda_B}{\lambda_B} = (1-p_e)\,\varepsilon$ | dimensionless | FBG strain conversion |
| 10 | Resonant Frequency | $f = (1/2\pi)\sqrt{k/m}$ | Hz | Resonant sensor principle |
| 11 | Wheatstone Bridge Output | $V_o = V_{\text{in}}\left(\dfrac{R_1}{R_1+R_2} - \dfrac{R_4}{R_3+R_4}\right)$ | V | Piezoresistive read-out |
| 12 | Gauge Factor (strain gauge) | $G = \dfrac{\Delta R/R}{\varepsilon}$ | dimensionless | Sensitivity benchmark |
| 13 | Sensor Linearity (Best Fit) | $\%\text{NL} = \dfrac{\Delta y_{\max}}{y_{\text{FS}}} \times 100$ | % | KTU exam specification |
| 14 | Hysteresis Error | $\%\text{Hys} = \dfrac{\Delta y_{H}}{y_{\text{FS}}} \times 100$ | % | Bidirectional repeatability |
| 15 | Thermal Drift (TCO) | $\text{TCO} = \dfrac{\Delta y_{\max}}{\Delta T \cdot y_{\text{FS}}} \times 10^6$ | ppm/°C | Maritime thermal stability |

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Values to Remember:**
> - $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m
> - $g = 9.80665$ m/s²
> - $\rho_{\text{seawater}} \approx 1025$ kg/m³
> - Gauge factor for silicon ≈ 100–200; for constantan ≈ 2.0

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hydrostatic Pressure at Depth

**Objective:** Determine the pressure experienced at the base of a tidal turbine blade located 8 m below the sea surface during high tide. Use seawater density $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³ and $g = 9.81$ m/s².

### Step 1 — State the governing equation

For an incompressible static fluid in a uniform gravitational field, pressure increases linearly with depth according to:

$$P(h) = P_{\text{atm}} + \rho g h$$

### Step 2 — Identify each variable

The numerical values are:
- $P_{\text{atm}} = 101{,}325$ Pa (standard atmospheric pressure)
- $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³ (mass density of typical seawater)
- $g = 9.81$ m/s² (standard gravity)
- $h = 8$ m (submerged depth)

### Step 3 — Substitute into the equation

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(h) &= P_{\text{atm}} + \rho g h \\[4pt]
P(8) &= 101{,}325 + (1025)(9.81)(8)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate the hydrostatic term

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho g h &= 1025 \times 9.81 \times 8 \\[4pt]
\rho g h &= 1025 \times 78.48 \\[4pt]
\rho g h &= 80{,}442\ \text{Pa}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute absolute pressure

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(8) &= 101{,}325 + 80{,}442 \\[4pt]
P(8) &= 181{,}767\ \text{Pa} \\[4pt]
P(8) &\approx 1.818\ \text{bar (absolute)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Convert to gauge pressure

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{gauge}} &= P_{\text{abs}} - P_{\text{atm}} \\[4pt]
P_{\text{gauge}} &= 181{,}767 - 101{,}325 \\[4pt]
P_{\text{gauge}} &= 80{,}442\ \text{Pa} \\[4pt]
P_{\text{gauge}} &\approx 0.804\ \text{bar (gauge)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A piezoresistive pressure sensor installed at this depth must be selected with a **rated full-scale ≥ 2 bar absolute** (commonly 2.5 bar or 4 bar FS with 1.5× safety margin) and a hydrostatic accuracy class ≤ ±0.1% FS for reliable tidal monitoring.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Capacitive Pressure Sensor Sensitivity

**Objective:** A circular capacitive diaphragm sensor has plate radius $r = 5$ mm and nominal gap $d_0 = 50\ \mu$m. Under applied pressure $P$, the diaphragm center deflects by $\Delta d = kP$, where $k = 0.02\ \mu$m/Pa. Derive the relationship between fractional capacitance change and pressure.

### Step 1 — Capacitance at zero pressure

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_0 &= \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_0} \\[4pt]
A &= \pi r^2 = \pi (5\times10^{-3})^2 \\[4pt]
A &= 7.854\times10^{-5}\ \text{m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_0 &= \frac{(8.854\times10^{-12})(7.854\times10^{-5})}{50\times10^{-6}} \\[4pt]
C_0 &= \frac{6.954\times10^{-16}}{5.0\times10^{-5}} \\[4pt]
C_0 &= 1.391\times10^{-11}\ \text{F} \\[4pt]
C_0 &= 13.91\ \text{pF}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Capacitance under pressure

When pressure $P$ is applied, the gap reduces to $d = d_0 - kP$. Therefore:

$$C(P) = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_0 - kP}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the sensitivity function $S_C$

The fractional sensitivity is defined as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_C &= -\frac{1}{C_0}\frac{dC}{dP} \\[4pt]
S_C &= -\frac{1}{C_0}\cdot\frac{d}{dP}\!\left[\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_0 - kP}\right] \\[4pt]
S_C &= -\frac{1}{C_0}\cdot\frac{\varepsilon_0 A \cdot k}{(d_0 - kP)^2} \\[4pt]
S_C &= \frac{k}{(d_0 - kP)}\cdot\frac{1}{(d_0 - kP)/(d_0)} \\[4pt]
S_C &= \frac{k}{d_0(1 - kP/d_0)^2}\cdot\frac{1}{d_0}\cdot d_0
\end{aligned}
$$

For small deflections ($kP \ll d_0$), the term $(1 - kP/d_0)^{-2} \approx 1 + 2kP/d_0$, and the sensitivity simplifies to:

$$S_C \approx \frac{k}{d_0}\quad [\text{per Pa}]$$

### Step 4 — Substitute numerical values

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_C &\approx \frac{0.02\times10^{-6}}{50\times10^{-6}} \\[4pt]
S_C &\approx 4\times10^{-4}\ \text{Pa}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Interpret the result

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\Delta C}{C_0} &\approx S_C \cdot P = 4\times10^{-4}\cdot P
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A pressure of 1 kPa produces a fractional capacitance change of ≈ 0.04. This is easily resolved by modern capacitance-to-digital converters (CDC) such as the AD7746/AD7747, which have sub-femtofarad resolution — making capacitive sensors extremely attractive for **subsea OWC chamber pressure profiling**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sensitivity and Gauge Factor of a Piezoresistive Sensor

**Objective:** A silicon piezoresistive sensor uses four strain gauges arranged in a Wheatstone bridge. Each gauge has nominal resistance $R = 1000\ \Omega$. The applied strain is $\varepsilon = 500\ \mu\varepsilon$. Silicon piezoresistor gauge factor $G_{\text{Si}} = 120$. Determine the bridge output voltage for an excitation $V_{\text{ex}} = 5$ V, assuming two gauges in tension and two in compression.

### Step 1 — Change in resistance

The fundamental gauge factor relation is:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G\,\varepsilon$$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\Delta R}{R} &= 120 \times 500\times10^{-6} \\[4pt]
\frac{\Delta R}{R} &= 0.06
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Updated resistance values

For the two tension gauges: $R_T = R(1 + 0.06) = 1060\ \Omega$.
For the two compression gauges: $R_C = R(1 - 0.06) = 940\ \Omega$.

### Step 3 — Wheatstone bridge output

The differential output is:

$$V_o = V_{\text{ex}}\left[\frac{R_T}{R_T+R_C} - \frac{R_C}{R_T+R_C}\right]$$

### Step 4 — Simplify numerator and denominator

Numerator: $R_T - R_C = 1060 - 940 = 120\ \Omega$.
Denominator: $R_T + R_C = 1060 + 940 = 2000\ \Omega$.

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_o &= V_{\text{ex}}\cdot\frac{R_T - R_C}{R_T + R_C} \\[4pt]
V_o &= 5 \times \frac{120}{2000} \\[4pt]
V_o &= 5 \times 0.06 \\[4pt]
V_o &= 0.30\ \text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute sensitivity

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{V_o}{V_{\text{ex}}\cdot\varepsilon} \\[4pt]
S &= \frac{0.30}{5 \times 500\times10^{-6}} \\[4pt]
S &= 120\ \text{(dimensionless)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor of 120 is exactly the gauge factor — confirming that for a fully active bridge, the bridge sensitivity equals the gauge factor of the individual piezoresistors.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Piezoresistive | Capacitive | Piezoelectric | Optical (FBG) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pressure type | Static + dynamic | Static + dynamic | Dynamic only | Static + dynamic |
| Sensitivity | High | Moderate | Very high | Moderate |
| Temperature drift | Needs compensation | Low | Low | Very low |
| Power consumption | mW | µW | nA (quiescent) | mW (interrogator) |
| EMI immunity | Poor | Poor | Poor | **Excellent** |
| Marine suitability | Excellent (subsea) | Excellent | Excellent (dynamic) | Excellent (long haul) |
| Cost (relative) | Low | Low–Med | Medium | High |
| Long-term drift | Moderate | Very low | N/A (AC) | Very low |
| Typical accuracy | ±0.05% FS | ±0.1% FS | ±1% FS | ±0.5% FS |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generic Pressure Sensor Functional Architecture

The following block diagram illustrates the end-to-end signal flow from physical pressure input to standardized electrical / digital output, with all sub-blocks relevant to maritime deployment.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical Pressure Input Pa] --> B[Marine Grade Coupling 316L SS]
    B --> C[Diaphragm Deflection Stage]
    C --> D{Piezo Piezoresis Capacitive Resonant}
    D --> E[Signal Conditioning Amplification]
    E --> F[Temperature Compensation Stage]
    F --> G[Linearization and Calibration]
    G --> H[Output Stage 4 to 20 mA 0 to 10 V Modbus RS485]
    H --> I[PLC or SCADA Controller]
    I --> J[Renewable Energy System OWC Tidal Offshore Wind]
    J --> K[Feedback Control Loop]
    K --> E
```

**Explanation of the flow:**

- **A → B:** Raw hydrostatic or dynamic pressure acts on the sensor housing.
- **B → C:** The mechanical coupling transmits force to a thin diaphragm (silicon, stainless steel, or Inconel).
- **C → D:** The diaphragm deflection is converted to an electrical signal via one of four main transduction technologies.
- **D → E:** The raw mV or pF signal is amplified to a usable voltage level.
- **E → F:** Temperature compensation uses on-chip RTDs or thermistors to subtract thermal drift.
- **F → G:** Polynomial or lookup-table linearization improves accuracy to ±0.05% FS.
- **G → H:** Output is converted to industrial standards — 4–20 mA (long cable), 0–10 V, or digital protocols (Modbus RTU, HART, CAN).
- **H → I:** The PLC / SCADA interprets the signal for the renewable energy system controller.
- **I → J:** Decision (e.g., pitch angle adjustment, OWC valve release, tidal pitch control).
- **J → K:** Feedback loop ensures closed-loop accuracy.

## 4.2 Piezoresistive Sensor — Detailed Cross-Sectional Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Applied Pressure] --> D[Silicon Diaphragm]
    D --> R1[R1 Radial Gauge]
    D --> R2[R2 Tangential Gauge]
    D --> R3[R3 Tangential Gauge]
    D --> R4[R4 Radial Gauge]
    R1 --> W1[Wheatstone Bridge]
    R2 --> W1
    R3 --> W1
    R4 --> W1
    W1 --> AMP[Instrumentation Amplifier]
    AMP --> TC[Temp Compensation Network]
    TC --> OUT[mV to V Output]
```

## 4.3 FBG Pressure Sensor Functional Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LS[Broadband Light Source] --> FB[FBG on Diaphragm]
    FB --> REF[Reflected Bragg Wavelength]
    REF --> INT[Optical Interrogator]
    INT --> DMO[Demodulator Wavelength to Strain]
    DMO --> CAL[Pressure Calibration LUT]
    CAL --> DSP[Digital Signal Processor]
    DSP --> DISP[Display and Data Logger]
    DSP --> FBK[Feedback to Control System]
```

## 4.4 Installation Pathway in a Maritime Renewable System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sea Surface] --> B[Subsea Sensor Mounting Bracket]
    B --> C[Pressure Sensor 316L SS Housing]
    C --> D[Sealed Cable Gland IP68]
    D --> E[Junction Box Subsea Canister]
    E --> F[Subsea Cable to Surface]
    F --> G[Onshore or Platform PLC]
    G --> H[SCADA HMI Display]
    G --> I[Renewable Energy Controller]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define gauge pressure and absolute pressure. How are they related? Why is absolute pressure preferred in deep-sea applications?**

**Model Answer:**

Gauge pressure is the pressure measured relative to local atmospheric pressure, while absolute pressure is measured relative to a perfect vacuum (true zero). They are related by:

$$P_{\text{abs}} = P_{\text{gauge}} + P_{\text{atm}}$$

In deep-sea applications, the local atmospheric pressure on the surface is essentially negligible compared with the hydrostatic head (e.g., at 1000 m depth, $P_h \approx 100$ bar vs. $P_{\text{atm}} \approx 1$ bar). Using an absolute reference inside a sealed vacuum chamber removes the dependence on fluctuating surface atmospheric conditions (weather, altitude) and gives a **stable, repeatable, and drift-free measurement**, which is critical for long-duration subsea monitoring of marine renewable systems.

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List any four types of pressure sensors and state one distinguishing feature of each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Piezoresistive:** Uses change in semiconductor resistance under strain; high sensitivity, suitable for static and dynamic pressure.
2. **Capacitive:** Uses change in plate separation; very low power consumption, suitable for battery-powered subsea units.
3. **Piezoelectric:** Generates charge under stress; suitable only for dynamic pressure measurement (no DC response).
4. **Optical (FBG):** Wavelength-encoded output; immune to EMI, suitable for long cable runs and multiplexed arrays.

## 5.2 Part B — Choice-Based Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:**
With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a piezoresistive pressure sensor. Derive the expression for the output voltage of a Wheatstone bridge with four active strain gauges, two in tension and two in compression.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:**
A piezoresistive pressure sensor has a nominal resistance of $R = 3500\ \Omega$ per gauge. The bridge is excited with $V_{\text{ex}} = 10$ V. The maximum pressure applied produces a strain of $800\ \mu\varepsilon$. Given the silicon gauge factor $G = 130$, calculate (i) the change in resistance of each gauge, and (ii) the peak differential output voltage of the bridge.

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) Solution [7 Marks]

**Working Principle:**

A piezoresistive pressure sensor consists of a thin silicon diaphragm onto which four piezoresistors are ion-implanted. Two resistors are oriented **radially** and two **tangentially**. When pressure is applied, the diaphragm deflects: the radial gauges experience **tensile strain** (resistance increases) and the tangential gauges experience **compressive strain** (resistance decreases). The four gauges form a **Wheatstone bridge**, and the differential output voltage is proportional to the applied pressure.

The block diagram is:

```
   Applied Pressure
         |
   (Silicon Diaphragm)
         |
   Strain Transduction (4 piezoresistors)
         |
   Wheatstone Bridge
         |
   Instrumentation Amplifier
         |
   Output Voltage (mV – V range)
```

**Derivation:**

Let the two tension gauges have resistance $R + \Delta R$ and the two compression gauges have resistance $R - \Delta R$. The bridge output is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_o &= V_{\text{ex}}\left[\frac{R+\Delta R}{(R+\Delta R)+(R-\Delta R)} - \frac{R-\Delta R}{(R+\Delta R)+(R-\Delta R)}\right] \\[4pt]
V_o &= V_{\text{ex}}\left[\frac{R+\Delta R}{2R} - \frac{R-\Delta R}{2R}\right] \\[4pt]
V_o &= V_{\text{ex}} \cdot \frac{(R+\Delta R)-(R-\Delta R)}{2R} \\[4pt]
V_o &= V_{\text{ex}} \cdot \frac{2\Delta R}{2R} \\[4pt]
V_o &= V_{\text{ex}} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}
\end{aligned}
$$
