Since $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \varepsilon$, we obtain the fundamental relation:

$$\boxed{V_o = V_{\text{ex}}\, G\, \varepsilon}$$

This shows that the bridge output voltage is **linearly proportional** to the applied strain.

#### Part (b) Solution [7 Marks]

**Given Data:**
- $R = 3500\ \Omega$ per gauge
- $V_{\text{ex}} = 10$ V
- $\varepsilon = 800\ \mu\varepsilon = 800 \times 10^{-6}$
- $G = 130$

**Step (i): Change in resistance**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\Delta R}{R} &= G \cdot \varepsilon = 130 \times 800\times10^{-6} \\[4pt]
\frac{\Delta R}{R} &= 0.104
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta R &= 0.104 \times 3500 \\[4pt]
\Delta R &= 364\ \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

So the tension gauges become $R_T = 3500 + 364 = 3864\ \Omega$ and the compression gauges become $R_C = 3500 - 364 = 3136\ \Omega$.

**Step (ii): Peak differential output voltage**

From the derived expression:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_o &= V_{\text{ex}} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} \\[4pt]
V_o &= 10 \times 0.104 \\[4pt]
V_o &= 1.04\ \text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Marks Lost Here:**
> 1. Many students forget to convert $\mu\varepsilon$ to $\varepsilon$ by multiplying by $10^{-6}$ — leading to a wrong answer 2000× too large.
> 2. Some students compute $\Delta R$ using a *single* gauge instead of the *differential* bridge expression; this is wrong for a fully active bridge.
> 3. The differential formula gives $V_o = V_{\text{ex}} \cdot \Delta R / R$ **only** when two gauges are in tension and two in compression. If the bridge has only one active gauge, the formula is $V_o = V_{\text{ex}} \cdot \Delta R/(4R)$ — a factor of 4 difference.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]`

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:**
Explain the operating principle of a Fiber Bragg Grating (FBG) pressure sensor. Derive the relationship between the Bragg wavelength shift $\Delta \lambda_B$ and the applied axial strain.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:**
An FBG pressure sensor with nominal Bragg wavelength $\lambda_B = 1550$ nm is bonded to a steel diaphragm. The effective strain-optic coefficient gives $(1-p_e) = 0.78$. If the diaphragm experiences a strain of $1000\ \mu\varepsilon$ under pressure, calculate (i) the Bragg wavelength shift and (ii) the new Bragg wavelength. Comment on the practical implications of this shift in an offshore installation.

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) Solution [7 Marks]

**Operating Principle:**

A Fiber Bragg Grating is a periodic variation of refractive index inscribed in the core of a single-mode optical fiber using UV interference lithography. It acts as a wavelength-selective mirror, reflecting the Bragg wavelength:

$$\lambda_B = 2 n_{\text{eff}} \Lambda$$

When external pressure deforms the diaphragm on which the FBG is mounted, the fiber experiences axial strain $\varepsilon$, which changes both the grating period $\Lambda$ and the refractive index $n_{\text{eff}}$ (via the strain-optic effect).

**Derivation:**

Differentiating the Bragg equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\Delta\lambda_B}{\lambda_B} &= \frac{\Delta n_{\text{eff}}}{n_{\text{eff}}} + \frac{\Delta\Lambda}{\Lambda}
\end{aligned}
$$

The relative change in grating period is purely mechanical:

$$\frac{\Delta\Lambda}{\Lambda} = \varepsilon$$

The change in refractive index is governed by the strain-optic tensor; for an isotropic fiber under axial strain, it reduces to:

$$\frac{\Delta n_{\text{eff}}}{n_{\text{eff}}} = -p_e\,\varepsilon$$

where $p_e$ is the effective strain-optic coefficient (≈ 0.22 for silica). Substituting both:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\Delta\lambda_B}{\lambda_B} &= -p_e\,\varepsilon + \varepsilon \\[4pt]
\frac{\Delta\lambda_B}{\lambda_B} &= (1 - p_e)\,\varepsilon
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **fundamental strain-wavelength relationship** for FBG sensors.

#### Part (b) Solution [7 Marks]

**Given Data:**
- $\lambda_B = 1550$ nm
- $(1 - p_e) = 0.78$
- $\varepsilon = 1000\ \mu\varepsilon = 1000 \times 10^{-6}$

**Step (i): Bragg wavelength shift**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta\lambda_B &= \lambda_B\,(1-p_e)\,\varepsilon \\[4pt]
\Delta\lambda_B &= 1550 \times 0.78 \times 1000\times10^{-6}\ \text{nm} \\[4pt]
\Delta\lambda_B &= 1550 \times 7.8\times10^{-4}\ \text{nm} \\[4pt]
\Delta\lambda_B &= 1.209\ \text{nm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (ii): New Bragg wavelength**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda_B' &= \lambda_B + \Delta\lambda_B \\[4pt]
\lambda_B' &= 1550 + 1.209 \\[4pt]
\lambda_B' &= 1551.209\ \text{nm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Practical Implications:**

A 1.2 nm shift is well within the resolution of modern FBG interrogators (typical resolution: 1 pm = 0.001 nm). This means the sensor can resolve pressure changes corresponding to **sub-µε** levels, making FBG ideal for **long-term structural health monitoring of offshore wind turbine blades and tidal turbine housings**, where many sensors can be multiplexed on a single fiber using wavelength-division multiplexing (WDM).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Marks Lost Here:**
> 1. Do **not** confuse $\Delta n / n$ with $\Delta n_{\text{eff}} / n_{\text{eff}}$. The total shift has **two** contributions: mechanical ($\varepsilon$) and strain-optic ($-p_e \varepsilon$). Missing either gives a wrong answer.
> 2. Some students forget to convert $\mu\varepsilon$ to dimensionless strain — causing an answer that is **off by a factor of $10^6$**.
> 3. The constant $(1-p_e)$ is approximately 0.78 for silica fibers; for polymer fibers it can exceed 0.9. Always state the material.
> 4. Temperature also shifts $\lambda_B$ independently; in maritime applications, a separate temperature-FBG must be used for compensation, or marks will be deducted for ignoring thermal cross-sensitivity.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Pressure** is force per unit area: $P = F/A$, measured in **Pascals (Pa)**.
- **Absolute pressure** = gauge pressure + atmospheric pressure ($P_{\text{atm}} = 101{,}325$ Pa).
- **Hydrostatic pressure** at depth $h$ in seawater: $P_h = \rho g h$, with $\rho_{\text{sw}} \approx 1025$ kg/m³.
- The **five-stage transduction chain** is: Sensing element → Transduction → Signal conditioning → Output → Packaging.
- **Piezoresistive sensors** use silicon strain gauges in a **Wheatstone bridge**; sensitivity equals gauge factor for fully active bridge: $V_o = V_{\text{ex}}\,G\,\varepsilon$.
- **Capacitive sensors** use diaphragm-deflection-induced gap change: $C = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 A/d$; low power, ideal for subsea.
- **Piezoelectric sensors** generate charge $Q = d\,P\,A$; **dynamic only**, cannot measure steady pressure.
- **FBG sensors** shift Bragg wavelength: $\Delta\lambda_B / \lambda_B = (1-p_e)\,\varepsilon$; immune to EMI, multi-point multiplexable.
- **Resonant sensors** shift frequency $f = (1/2\pi)\sqrt{k/m}$; intrinsically digital, ideal for long-term drift-free subsea operation.
- **Gauge factor** for silicon ≈ 100–200; for constantan ≈ 2.0.
- **Marine-grade housing** is typically **316L stainless steel** or **titanium** with **IP68** sealing.
- Always convert **µε → ε** by multiplying by $10^{-6}$ before substituting into formulas.
- Bridge output formula $V_o = V_{\text{ex}}\,\Delta R / R$ is valid **only** for a fully active four-gauge bridge.
- For an FBG, the **two contributions** (mechanical + strain-optic) must always be summed.
- **Safety margin:** specify sensor full-scale at **1.5×** to **2×** the maximum expected operating pressure.
- **Calibration standards:** ISO/IEC 17025 and DNV-GL-RP-C203 for marine pressure instrumentation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Radar Sensors. Current Measurements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST755 - RENEWABLE ENERGY IN MARITIME SECTOR
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Radar Sensors. Current Measurements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Radar Sensors in the Maritime Domain

**Formal Definition:**
*RADAR* (**Ra**dio **D**etection **a**nd **R**anging) is an electromagnetic sensing system that operates by transmitting a focused pulse of microwave energy and analyzing the time, frequency, and amplitude characteristics of the returned echo to determine the range, velocity, angular position, and physical nature of distant targets. In the maritime renewable energy context, radar sensors are used for sea-state monitoring, turbine and platform proximity surveillance, wave-rider buoy tracking, and surface current vector mapping.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Under KTU 2024 (PENST755), Module 1 introduces the foundational **remote and in-situ measurement instruments** that feed oceanographic data into renewable energy resource assessments. Radar sensors form the "non-contact, long-range" tier of this instrumentation hierarchy.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Echo in a Canyon:**
Imagine shouting in a deep mountain valley. The time it takes for the echo to return tells you how far the canyon wall is. If the wall is moving toward you, the pitch of the returning shout is slightly higher. *Radar does exactly the same thing* — it "shouts" with microwaves and listens to the echo. The round-trip travel time gives distance, and a small pitch shift (the *Doppler shift*) gives velocity. In the ocean, instead of canyon walls, the echo bounces off waves, ships, wind turbine towers, and even the moving sea surface itself.

### 1.2 Ocean Current Measurement — A Working Definition

**Formal Definition:**
*Ocean Current Measurement* refers to the quantitative determination of the speed and direction of water mass motion in the marine environment, expressed as a vector quantity (typically in m/s or knots) at specified depths, using either *in-situ* (deployed-in-water) instruments or *remote* (above-water, over-the-horizon) techniques.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for Maritime Renewables:** Tidal stream turbines, floating offshore wind platforms, and OTEC (Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion) plants all require precise, real-time current data for **resource assessment, structural loading analysis, and operational decision-making**. Errors in current measurement propagate directly into revenue uncertainty in a power-purchase agreement (PPA).

**Conceptual Analogy — Measuring a River from Above:**
Picture a wide, slow river. From a bridge you can drop a colored leaf and time how long it takes to drift 10 meters — that is an *in-situ Lagrangian* measurement. Alternatively, you could shine a flashlight at the water at an angle and notice the tiny ripples reflecting back have a different color than the light you sent — that is the *Doppler / radar-based remote* measurement. Both tell you "how fast the water is moving," but one is direct and the other is optical (or microwave) inference.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$
- **Speed of sound in seawater (≈ 15 °C, 35 PSU):** $c_s \approx 1500 \text{ m/s}$
- **Standard radar carrier bands for marine use:** **X-band (8–12 GHz)** and **S-band (2–4 GHz)**
- **Standard HF Oceanographic Radar band:** ≈ **5–25 MHz**
- **Current speed unit:** m/s (SI) or knots (1 knot = 0.5144 m/s)
- **Beam-width unit:** degrees (typically 1°–5° for marine navigation radar)
- **Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF):** 500 Hz – 3 kHz for navigation radar

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Mnemonic — "X is eXtreme, S is Smooth":** X-band gives sharper resolution but is attenuated by rain; S-band penetrates rain but has coarser resolution. Marine renewable platforms near the coast often combine both.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle of a Marine Radar

A pulse marine radar operates on a five-stage signal chain:

1. **Trigger / Timing Generation:** A master clock initiates a short (≈ 50 ns – 2 µs) microwave pulse at the **Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF)**.
2. **Transmission via Duplexer:** The duplexer routes the pulse from the high-power magnetron (or solid-state amplifier) to the rotating **slotted-waveguide antenna**, which radiates a narrow horizontal beam (typical horizontal beamwidth ≈ 0.5°–2°, vertical ≈ 20°–30°).
3. **Free-Space Propagation:** The pulse travels at speed $c$ through the atmosphere over the sea surface.
4. **Scattering and Echo Reception:** Targets (waves, buoys, turbine masts) intercept the wavefront and re-radiate a small fraction of the energy back along the line of sight. The duplexer then routes this weak return to the low-noise receiver (LNA → mixer → IF amplifier).
5. **Display / Signal Processing:** The round-trip time $\Delta t$ is converted to slant range $R$. The antenna bearing gives azimuth. The received power is mapped to a Plan Position Indicator (PPI) raster.

**The "Why" Behind Each Stage:**
- The **duplexer** protects the sensitive receiver from being burned out by the megawatt-level transmit pulse (typically 4–25 kW for marine sets).
- **Narrow horizontal beamwidth** is what gives bearing resolution; wide vertical beamwidth is acceptable because the sea surface is essentially 2-D.
- **Range resolution** depends on pulse width $\tau$ via $\Delta R = c\tau/2$. A 50 ns pulse gives ≈ 7.5 m resolution.

### 2.2 Derivation of the Fundamental Range Equation

The simplest and most heavily-tested KTU relationship in radar is the range from time-of-flight.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite:** "Derive the radar range equation from first principles" is one of the most common 7-mark questions in Part B of Module 1 papers.

Start with a pulse leaving the antenna at $t = 0$. It propagates outward at speed $c$, hits a point scatterer at distance $R$, and the echo returns along the same path. The total round-trip distance is therefore $2R$ and the round-trip time is $\Delta t$. Hence:

$$
R = \frac{c \, \Delta t}{2}
$$

The factor of 2 appears *only* because the same signal traverses the path twice. Students frequently forget the factor 2 in the exam and lose 1 mark.

### 2.3 The Monostatic Radar Range Equation (Power Form)

When a target of radar cross-section $\sigma$ is illuminated by an antenna of transmit gain $G$ at wavelength $\lambda$, the power density at the target is:

$$
S_t = \frac{P_t G}{4 \pi R^2}
$$

The target re-radiates isotropically an equivalent power $P_t G \sigma / (4\pi R^2)$. The power density returning at the radar antenna aperture (effective area $A_e = G \lambda^2 / 4\pi$) is:

$$
S_r = \frac{P_t G \sigma}{(4 \pi R^2)(4 \pi R^2)} = \frac{P_t G \sigma}{(4 \pi)^2 R^4}
$$

The received power is then $P_r = S_r A_e$:

$$
\boxed{\,P_r = \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, R^4}\,}
$$

The **$R^4$ law** is the most important practical takeaway: doubling the range requires **16 times** the transmit power. This is why long-range over-the-horizon (OTH) radar and high-frequency surface-wave radar use megawatt-class transmitters.

### 2.4 Doppler Principle for Current & Velocity Measurement

If a target moves with radial velocity $v_r$ relative to the radar, the returned carrier frequency is shifted by:

$$
f_d = \frac{2 v_r f_0}{c} = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda}
$$

The factor 2 appears because the wave is *Doppler-shifted twice*: once on the way in, once on the way out. This is the cornerstone of:
- MTI (Moving Target Indication) radar for marine traffic.
- HF surface-wave radar for ocean-surface current mapping.
- Marine radar used as a non-contact wave-rider / current sensor.

### 2.5 Ocean Current Measurement — Three Architectural Families

**Family 1 — In-Situ Eulerian Point Sensors** (current meter fixed in space, water flows past).
- *Mechanical rotor-vane:* Savonius rotor + vane; cheap, robust, slow response.
- *Electromagnetic (EM) sensor:* Faraday's law — water (a conductor) flowing through a magnetic field generates a voltage $V = B \cdot \ell \cdot v$.
- *Acoustic point sensor:* Transit-time or single-beam Doppler.

**Family 2 — In-Situ Profiling Sensors**
- *ADCP (Acoustic Doppler Current Profiler):* The workhorse of the offshore wind and tidal energy industry. It transmits four (or three) oblique acoustic beams, measures the Doppler shift of backscatter from plankton and micro-bubbles, and triangulates the 3-D current vector as a function of depth.

$$
f_d = \frac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c_s}
$$

where $\theta$ is the beam angle from vertical (typically 20° or 30°).

**Family 3 — Remote / Above-Water Sensors**
- *HF Surface-Wave Radar (CODAR, WERA):* Operates in the 5–25 MHz band, exploits **Bragg scattering** from ocean gravity waves of half the radar wavelength. The two first-order Bragg peaks are shifted in frequency by the underlying surface current — enabling current mapping up to 200 km offshore without any in-water hardware.
- *Marine X-band radar as a wave-and-current sensor:* Commercial marine radars, by recording the raw intensity time-series at short range and applying 3-D FFT, can be retrofitted to estimate wave spectra and surface current fields.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mastery of the following table is sufficient to solve 95 % of numerical questions in Module 1.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Typical Units | Notes / Boundary Conditions |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|------------------------------|
| 1 | Slant range from round-trip time | $R = c\Delta t / 2$ | m | Always remember the factor 2 |
| 2 | Range resolution | $\Delta R = c\tau/2$ | m | $\tau$ = pulse width |
| 3 | Maximum unambiguous range | $R_{max} = c/(2 \cdot \text{PRF})$ | m | Set by pulse repetition interval |
| 4 | Received power (monostatic) | $P_r = P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma \,/\,(4\pi)^3 R^4$ | W | $R^4$ law |
| 5 | Antenna effective aperture | $A_e = G\lambda^2/(4\pi)$ | m² | |
| 6 | Radial Doppler frequency | $f_d = 2 v_r f_0 / c = 2 v_r / \lambda$ | Hz | Two-way shift |
| 7 | ADCP Doppler shift | $f_d = 2 f_0 v \cos\theta / c_s$ | Hz | $c_s \approx 1500$ m/s |
| 8 | Bragg resonant wavenumber | $k_B = 2 k_0 \cos\phi$ | rad/m | $\phi$ = incidence angle |
| 9 | EM current-meter voltage | $V = B \ell v$ | V | Faraday's law |
| 10 | Nyquist sampling rate | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Hz | Required for Doppler demodulation |

> [!NOTE]
> The vertical-bar symbol has been written as `\vert` in plain text to avoid breaking the markdown table. In LaTeX, use `\vert v_r \vert` for the modulus.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Offshore wind:** ADCPs are mounted in the nacelle and on the monopile to feed **SCADA** systems with hub-height current vectors used for yaw control and load reduction.
- **Tidal stream turbines:** Seabed-mounted ADCPs map the resource over a spring–neap cycle (≈ 14 days) — the standard "resource assessment window" in IEC TS 62600-201.
- **Floating PV & offshore platforms:** HF radar or X-band radar surface-current products are assimilated into metocean models to forecast mooring loads.
- **Port & harbour surveillance:** Marine navigation radar remains the IMO-mandated primary aid under SOLAS Chapter V.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Radar Range Equation

We begin with the transmitting antenna of gain $G$ and transmit power $P_t$. Consider an isotropic radiator of equal power $P_t$ for reference: the power density at distance $R$ would be $P_t / (4\pi R^2)$. An antenna of gain $G$ focuses this into a beam, so the on-axis power density is multiplied by $G$:

$$
S_t(R) = \frac{P_t G}{4 \pi R^2}
$$

This is the **illuminating power density** at the target.

**Step 1 — Scattering by the target.**
A target of *radar cross-section* $\sigma$ (units: m²) intercepts and re-radiates energy as if it were an isotropic radiator of total power $P_t G \sigma / (4 \pi R^2)$. At the radar antenna the returned power density is therefore:

$$
S_r(R) = \frac{P_t G \sigma}{(4 \pi R^2)(4 \pi R^2)} = \frac{P_t G \sigma}{(4 \pi)^2 R^4}
$$

The denominator squared reflects the *outward $R^2$ spreading* and the *return $R^2$ spreading* — together giving the famous $R^4$ law.

**Step 2 — Capture by the receiving antenna.**
The same antenna is used for transmit and receive (monostatic case). Its effective aperture is:

$$
A_e = \frac{G \lambda^2}{4 \pi}
$$

The power delivered to the receiver is the product $S_r \times A_e$:

$$
P_r = S_r \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t G \sigma}{(4 \pi)^2 R^4} \cdot \frac{G \lambda^2}{4 \pi}
$$

**Step 3 — Final form.**
Collecting terms:

$$
P_r = \frac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4 \pi)^3 R^4}
$$

This is the **monostatic radar range equation**. For a *bistatic* geometry (separate transmit and receive sites, as in some over-the-horizon radar), the denominator changes to $(4\pi)^2 R_t^2 R_r^2$ and the gain appears as $G_t G_r$ — a frequently asked KTU modification.

### 3.2 Worked Derivation — ADCP Beam Geometry and Current Vector Reconstruction

A 4-beam Janus-configured ADCP transmits four narrow acoustic beams, each inclined at angle $\theta$ from the vertical axis. Each beam measures the radial velocity $v_{r,i}$ along its own beam axis. The water-parcel velocity vector in Earth coordinates is $\vec{V} = (v_x, v_y, v_z)$. The unit vector of beam $i$ is:

$$
\hat{e}_i = (\sin\theta \cos\phi_i,\; \sin\theta \sin\phi_i,\; \cos\theta)
$$

where the four azimuths are typically $\phi_i = 0°, 90°, 180°, 270°$.

**Radial projection:**
The measured radial velocity is the dot product:

$$
v_{r,i} = \vec{V} \cdot \hat{e}_i = v_x \sin\theta \cos\phi_i + v_y \sin\theta \sin\phi_i + v_z \cos\theta
$$

**Forming the linear system:**
With four beams the system is over-determined and is solved by least squares:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} v_{r,1} \\ v_{r,2} \\ v_{r,3} \\ v_{r,4} \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
\sin\theta & 0 & \cos\theta \\
0 & \sin\theta & \cos\theta \\
-\sin\theta & 0 & \cos\theta \\
0 & -\sin\theta & \cos\theta
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} v_x \\ v_y \\ v_z \end{bmatrix}
$$

Solving (and using that the four $\cos\theta$ terms are equal, so the vertical velocity $v_z$ is the average of the four radials scaled by $1/(4\cos\theta)$):

$$
v_z = \frac{v_{r,1}+v_{r,2}+v_{r,3}+v_{r,4}}{4 \cos\theta}
$$

$$
v_x = \frac{v_{r,1}-v_{r,3}}{2 \sin\theta}, \qquad
v_y = \frac{v_{r,2}-v_{r,4}}{2 \sin\theta}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Always state that the Janus (back-scatter, 4-beam) configuration **cancels horizontal platform motion** because opposite beams see the same motion with opposite sign. This is why Janus ADCPs are used on moving buoys and vessels.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Current Sensor

| Selection Criterion | ADCP (Acoustic) | HF Radar (CODAR/WERA) | Mechanical Rotor | EM Sensor | Lagrangian Drifter |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Spatial coverage | Vertical profile, point | 2-D surface map, 200 km | Single point | Single point | Trajectory |
| Typical accuracy (speed) | ± 0.5 cm/s | ± 5 cm/s | ± 1 cm/s | ± 0.5 cm/s | ± 1 cm/s |
| Depth range | 0 – 1000 m | Surface (top 0.5 m) | 0 – 200 m | 0 – 100 m | Surface / drogued |
| Cost (relative) | High | Very High | Low | Medium | Low per unit |
| Best for tidal energy | ✔ Best | Good for resource maps | Acceptable | Good | Lagrangian resource |
| Best for offshore wind | ✔ Hub-height profile | Supplementary | Marginal | Marginal | Supplementary |
| IMO/SOLAS compliance | N/A | N/A | N/A | N/A | N/A |

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

**Question:** A marine navigation radar transmits a 0.1 µs pulse at 9.4 GHz (X-band) with peak power 25 kW and antenna gain 30 dB. A small target of RCS 10 m² is detected at a range of 12 km. Calculate (a) the range resolution, (b) the received echo power, and (c) the Doppler shift if the target approaches at 5 m/s.

**Solution:**

**(a) Range resolution.**

$$
\Delta R = \frac{c \tau}{2} = \frac{3\times 10^8 \times 0.1\times 10^{-6}}{2} = 15 \text{ m}
$$

**(b) Received power.**
Convert gain: $G_{dB} = 30 \Rightarrow G = 10^{30/10} = 1000$.
Wavelength: $\lambda = c / f_0 = 3\times 10^8 / 9.4\times 10^9 = 0.0319$ m.

$$
P_r = \frac{25\,000 \times 1000^2 \times 0.0319^2 \times 10}{(4\pi)^3 \times (12\,000)^4}
$$

Compute stepwise:

$$
G^2 = 10^6, \quad \lambda^2 \approx 1.018\times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
(4\pi)^3 \approx 1984.4
$$

$$
(12\,000)^4 = 2.0736\times 10^{16}
$$

Numerator: $25\,000 \times 10^6 \times 1.018\times 10^{-3} \times 10 = 2.545\times 10^{8}$.

$$
P_r = \frac{2.545\times 10^{8}}{1984.4 \times 2.0736\times 10^{16}} = \frac{2.545\times 10^{8}}{4.115\times 10^{19}} \approx 6.2\times 10^{-12} \text{ W} = 6.2 \text{ pW}
$$

**(c) Doppler shift.**

$$
f_d = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda} = \frac{2 \times 5}{0.0319} \approx 313.5 \text{ Hz}
$$

A 313 Hz tone — easily detectable by the receiver's MTI filter. ✔

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture — Marine Radar Signal Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TX["Transmitter Section"]
        TIMER[Master Clock and PRF Generator]
        MOD[Modulator]
        MAG[Magnetron or Solid State PA]
    end

    ANT[Rotating Slotted Waveguide Antenna]

    subgraph RX["Receiver Section"]
        DUP[Duplexer and LNA]
        MIX[Mixer and Local Oscillator]
        IF[IF Amplifier and Matched Filter]
        DET[Envelope and Doppler Detector]
        PPI[PPI Display and Signal Processor]
    ]

    TGT[Sea Surface, Buoys, Turbine Masts, Waves]

    TIMER --> MOD --> MAG --> DUP
    DUP --> ANT
    ANT -. Microwave Pulse .-> TGT
    TGT -. Backscatter Echo .-> ANT
    ANT --> DUP
    DUP --> MIX --> IF --> DET --> PPI
    TIMER -. Range Trigger .-> PPI
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** The top horizontal branch (TX) and bottom horizontal branch (RX) share the antenna via the duplexer. The vertical "down" arrows from the antenna show the two-way propagation: transmit (downward toward targets) and receive (upward from targets). The dotted line from the timer to the display provides the *synchronization* needed to compute $R = c\Delta t/2$.

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Acoustic Doppler Current Profiler

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CTRL[Onboard Controller and Timing]
    TX[Acoustic Transmitter 75 kHz to 1.2 MHz]
    XDC[Transducer Array 4 Beams Janus]
    WATER[Water Column with Scattering Particles]
    RX[Receiver and FFT Processor]
    SOLVE[Vector Solver Least Squares]
    OUT[Output Earth Frame vx vy vz versus Depth]

    CTRL --> TX
    TX --> XDC
    XDC -. Oblique Beams .-> WATER
    WATER -. Backscatter .-> XDC
    XDC --> RX
    RX --> SOLVE
    SOLVE --> OUT
    CTRL -. Synchronization .-> RX
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why four beams?** The Janus configuration has two opposite pairs. Each pair cancels the platform's own horizontal motion, leaving only the *water* velocity component. This is what allows an ADCP to be bolted to the leg of a floating wind turbine and still produce correct currents.

### 4.3 Functional Comparison — Active vs Passive Current Sensing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Active Sensors ADCP, HF Radar, Marine Radar]
    P[Passive Sensors Lagrangian Drifter, EM Sensor, Rotor]
    A -->|Transmit Signal and Listen| SCATTER[Scatterers Plankton Bubbles Bragg Waves]
    SCATTER --> A
    P -->|Direct Contact| WATER[Water Parcel Motion]
    WATER --> P
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Resource-Assessment Decision Flow for Maritime Renewables

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Project Site Identified]
    A --> B{Short Listing and Metocean Study}
    B --> C[Deploy ADCP for 30 days at Hub Height]
    B --> D[Request HF Radar Surface Product]
    C --> E[Compute Tidal Ellipse, Mean Flow, Turbulence]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Estimate AEP or Tidal Energy Density]
    F --> G{Confidence High?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Bankable Resource Statement]
    G -- No --> I[Extend Deployment to 1 Year]
    I --> C
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define the term "radar" and state two maritime applications relevant to renewable energy projects.**

**Model Answer:**
*RADAR* stands for **Ra**dio **D**etection **a**nd **R**anging. It is a sensor system that transmits microwave pulses and analyses the backscattered echoes to determine the range, bearing, and velocity of distant objects.
Two maritime renewable-energy applications are:
1. **Wave and surface-current mapping** by repurposing a standard X-band marine radar's intensity time-series.
2. **Turbine and platform proximity surveillance** for offshore wind farms, where radars detect approaching vessels before they collide with the rotor-swept zone (per COLREGs and national maritime authority regulations).

*(Expected answer length: 5–6 lines, ~60–80 words.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Differentiate between Eulerian and Lagrangian methods of current measurement, giving one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Eulerian | Lagrangian |
|---|---|---|
| Frame of reference | Sensor fixed in space; water flows past | Sensor moves with the water parcel |
| Quantity measured | Velocity at a fixed point | Trajectory of a tagged parcel |
| Example | ADCP mounted on a monopile | GPS-tracked Lagrangian drifter (e.g., CODE drifter) |

*(Expected answer length: 4–5 lines + table.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the **monostatic radar range equation** for a pulsed marine radar, starting from the definition of antenna gain. Clearly state each term. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A coastal HF oceanographic radar operates at 12 MHz. The ocean-surface current has a radial component of 0.6 m/s directly toward the radar. Calculate the **first-order Doppler shift** observed at the Bragg-scattering frequency. State the engineering significance of this measurement. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation — Monostatic Radar Range Equation**

1. Power density at range $R$ from a transmit antenna of gain $G$:
   $$ S_t = \frac{P_t G}{4\pi R^2} $$
2. Power intercepted by a target of radar cross-section $\sigma$ and re-radiated isotropically:
   $$ P_{\text{scat}} = S_t \cdot \sigma = \frac{P_t G \sigma}{4\pi R^2} $$
3. Power density of the echo back at the radar:
   $$ S_r = \frac{P_{\text{scat}}}{4\pi R^2} = \frac{P_t G \sigma}{(4\pi)^2 R^4} $$
4. Effective aperture of the same antenna: $A_e = G \lambda^2 / (4\pi)$.
5. Received power:
   $$ P_r = S_r A_e = \frac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4} $$

Each symbol: $P_t$ peak transmit power, $G$ antenna gain, $\lambda$ wavelength, $\sigma$ radar cross-section of target, $R$ range, $P_r$ received echo power. *Marks distributed: 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 2 (for final assembly) + 1 (for definitions) = 7.*

**(b) HF Radar Doppler Shift**

Wavelength: $\lambda = c / f_0 = 3\times 10^8 / 12\times 10^6 = 25$ m.

Doppler shift (two-way):

$$ f_d = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda} = \frac{2 \times 0.6}{25} = 0.048 \text{ Hz} $$

**Engineering significance:** Although only 48 mHz, this shift is **easily measurable** with FFT integration times of a few minutes. It corresponds to the radial component of the surface current that drives the first-order Bragg peak in the HF radar spectrum. By combining the radials from two or more shore sites (multistatic geometry), the **full 2-D surface current vector** is reconstructed across the coverage area, providing the metocean input required for floating wind, tidal, and offshore-PV resource assessment.

---

#### **Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of an ADCP**. Show how the four-beam Janus configuration reconstructs the 3-D current vector from radial Doppler measurements. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A tidal-energy developer deploys a bottom-mounted 600 kHz ADCP with beam angle $\theta = 30°$ in 35 m water depth. The four radial velocities recorded in one bin are:
$v_{r,1} = +0.42$ m/s, $v_{r,2} = -0.18$ m/s, $v_{r,3} = -0.46$ m/s, $v_{r,4} = +0.22$ m/s (positive = along beam away from transducer).
Compute the **east ($v_x$), north ($v_y$), and vertical ($v_z$) velocities** in that depth bin. State the magnitude and direction of the horizontal current. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) ADCP Functional Architecture (7 marks)**

Refer to the diagram drawn in **Section 4.2**. Marks allocated as:
- Block diagram (transmitter, transducer, water column, receiver, processor, output): **2 marks**.
- Janus 4-beam explanation with cancellation of platform motion: **2 marks**.
- Vector reconstruction with the 4 × 3 matrix and least-squares solution: **3 marks**.

**(b) Velocity Reconstruction**

With $\theta = 30°$: $\sin\theta = 0.5$, $\cos\theta = 0.866$.

**East velocity:**

$$ v_x = \frac{v_{r,1} - v_{r,3}}{2 \sin\theta} = \frac{0.42 - (-0.46)}{2 \times 0.5} = \frac{0.88}{1.0} = 0.88 \text{ m/s} $$

**North velocity:**

$$ v_y = \frac{v_{r,2} - v_{r,4}}{2 \sin\theta} = \frac{-0.18 - 0.22}{1.0} = -0.40 \text{ m/s} $$

**Vertical velocity:**

$$ v_z = \frac{0.42 - 0.18 - 0.46 + 0.22}{4 \times 0.866} = \frac{0}{3.464} = 0.00 \text{ m/s} $$

A zero vertical in a well-calibrated instrument indicates no significant upwelling or downwelling at that bin.

**Horizontal speed:**

$$ V_h = \sqrt{v_x^2 + v_y^2} = \sqrt{0.88^2 + 0.40^2} = \sqrt{0.7744 + 0.16} = \sqrt{0.9344} \approx 0.967 \text{ m/s} \approx 1.88 \text{ knots} $$

**Direction (bearing, measured from north):**

$$ \phi = \text{atan2}(v_x, v_y) = \text{atan2}(0.88, -0.40) \approx 180° - 65.6° = 114.4° $$

(Approximately **ESE**, i.e. flowing toward the east-southeast.)

**Engineering interpretation:** A peak ebb-flow speed of ~0.97 m/s is **above the typical cut-in speed** of most commercial tidal stream turbines (0.5–0.7 m/s) but below the rated speed of large units (≈ 2.5 m/s). The site is therefore a candidate for further resource assessment.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor 2** in $R = c\Delta t/2$ and in the Doppler formula. A 1-mark deduction per occurrence.
> 2. **Confusing $c$ (light) with $c_s$ (sound)**. Use $c$ for radar, $c_s \approx 1500$ m/s for ADCP.
> 3. **Mixing dB and linear** gain — the most common unit error. Always convert $G_{dB}$ to linear $G = 10^{G_{dB}/10}$ *before* substituting.
> 4. **Not labelling the four beam azimuths** in the ADCP diagram. Examiners allocate a full 1 mark for the azimuth angles (0°, 90°, 180°, 270°).
> 5. **Omitting the assumption "target is a point scatterer"** when stating the radar equation — examiners deduct a mark for this.
> 6. **Confusing the radar cross-section $\sigma$ with the target's physical area.** State explicitly: "RCS is an equivalent projected area, not the actual silhouette area."

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **RADAR = Radio Detection and Ranging.** A *time-of-flight* sensor for microwaves.
- The range equation is $R = c\Delta t / 2$. **Never forget the 2.**
- The power form is $P_r = P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma / (4\pi)^3 R^4$ — the **$R^4$ law**.
- Maximum unambiguous range is set by the PRF: $R_{max} = c/(2 \cdot \text{PRF})$.
- Marine radar uses **X-band (8–12 GHz)** for high resolution and **S-band (2–4 GHz)** for weather penetration.
- Doppler shift for radial velocity: $f_d = 2 v_r / \lambda$ (radar), $f_d = 2 f_0 v \cos\theta / c_s$ (ADCP).
- **ADCP = Acoustic Doppler Current Profiler.** A Janus 4-beam geometry gives 3-D currents and cancels platform motion.
- **EM current meter** uses Faraday's law $V = B\ell v$.
- **HF Oceanographic Radar** uses Bragg scattering from ocean waves of half the radar wavelength; range up to ≈ 200 km.
- **Eulerian** = fixed sensor (ADCP, EM, rotor). **Lagrangian** = moving sensor (drifter).
- For maritime renewables, ADCP at hub height is the **gold-standard** for tidal and floating-wind resource assessment; HF radar provides regional context.
- Typical accuracies: ADCP ± 0.5 cm/s, HF radar ± 5 cm/s, rotor-vane ± 1 cm/s, EM ± 0.5 cm/s.
- Key trade-off: **range vs power ($R^4$)**, **resolution vs pulse width ($\Delta R = c\tau/2$)**, **rain penetration vs resolution (S vs X band)**.
- Compliance note: Marine navigation radar is IMO/SOLAS Chapter V mandatory; current sensors are mandated by IEC TS 62600-201 for tidal resource assessment.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wave Measurements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST755 - RENEWABLE ENERGY IN MARITIME SECTOR
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Wave Measurements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Wave measurement** is the systematic acquisition of quantitative data describing the sea surface elevation $\eta(t)$ as a function of time and space, using in-situ sensors, remote-sensing platforms, or a hybrid combination. The objective is to extract statistical, spectral, and directional parameters of ocean waves that govern the design, operation, and survivability of marine renewable energy converters (WECs), offshore wind turbines, fixed/floating platforms, and coastal defence structures.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (PENST755), wave measurement is the foundational upstream activity that feeds all downstream engineering decisions: resource assessment, power-take-off (PTO) sizing, mooring design, extreme-load estimation, and grid-integration studies for ocean energy plants.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Wave measurement is not merely "reading the sea." It is a rigorous metrology exercise governed by IEC 62600-100 series and WMO (World Meteorological Organization) standards, with traceability to SI units.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine a **municipal weather station** that records wind speed every second using a cup anemometer. Engineers then statistically process those readings to design a wind turbine. Wave measurement is the **oceanic twin of this process**, except the "wind" is invisible and we must infer it from the surface displacement $\eta(t)$.

**Real-world analogy — The ECG of the Ocean:**
A wave buoy floating offshore is, functionally, an **electrocardiogram (ECG) for the sea**. Just as a cardiologist interprets the ECG trace to assess heart health, a marine engineer interprets the $\eta(t)$ time series to assess "ocean health" — energy content, extreme events, and dominant frequencies.

- **Wave height** $\rightarrow$ analogous to **blood pressure** (magnitude of effort)
- **Wave period** $\rightarrow$ analogous to **heart rate** (frequency of occurrence)
- **Direction** $\rightarrow$ analogous to **blood flow direction** (vector component)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Wave Metrics (must memorise):**
> - **Significant wave height $H_s$** — the average height of the highest one-third of waves. Empirically close to what an experienced mariner visually estimates.
> - **Peak spectral period $T_p$** — period at which the wave energy spectrum attains its maximum.
> - **Mean zero-crossing period $T_z$** — average time between successive upward zero crossings of $\eta(t)$.
> - **Peak wave direction $\theta_p$** — direction (in degrees from North) of the most energetic waves.
> - **Energy period $T_e$** — weighted average period used for WEC power capture estimation.

---

### 1.3 Why Wave Measurement Matters for KTU Engineers

The measured wave data is the **single most critical input** to any maritime renewable energy project. Without accurate measurements:

- WEC power output is mis-predicted (PTO undersizing/oversizing)
- Mooring loads are under-estimated (catastrophic failure risk)
- Maintenance schedules cannot be optimised (unplanned downtime)
- Bankable resource assessments cannot be certified (no project finance)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Hierarchy of Wave Measurement

Wave measurement operates across three hierarchical tiers:

**Tier 1 — Point Measurement (In-situ)**
A single sensor records $\eta(t)$ at one location. Devices include wave buoys, pressure transducers, and acoustic Doppler current profilers (ADCPs).

**Tier 2 — Spatial Measurement (Arrays & Remote Sensing)**
Multiple sensors or radar systems resolve the directional wave spectrum $S(f,\theta)$. Examples: directional wave buoys, HF radar, X-band marine radar.

**Tier 3 — Global Measurement (Satellites)**
Satellite altimeters (e.g., Jason-3, Sentinel-6) provide global $H_s$ datasets with $\sim$10-day revisit cycles, used for climatology and large-scale resource mapping.

### 2.2 Physical Principles of Common Sensors

| Sensor Type | Physical Principle | Quantity Measured | Strengths | Weaknesses |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Accelerometer Buoy** | Heave motion via double integration of vertical acceleration $a_z$ | $\eta(t)$ (displacement) | Robust, WMO-standard, captures full spectrum | Mooring noise, expensive |
| **Pressure Transducer** | Hydrostatic pressure $p = \rho g (d + \eta)$ | Subsurface pressure $\rightarrow \eta$ | Cheap, fixed installation | Limited to shallow water, depth-attenuation |
| **Acoustic (ADCP)** | Doppler shift of acoustic pulses | Surface orbital velocity + $\eta$ | Provides directional info | Bottom-mounted, range-limited |
| **Radar Gauge (X-band)** | Microwave backscatter from sea surface | $\eta(t)$ non-contact | No in-water hardware | Sensitive to rain, sea-spray |
| **HF Radar (CODAR/WERA)** | Bragg scattering from ocean waves | Surface current + directional spectrum | Spatially extensive (~200 km) | Coarse resolution (~1 km) |
| **Satellite Altimeter** | Range from orbit to sea surface | Significant wave height $H_s$ (only) | Global coverage | $H_s$ only, no spectrum, low revisit |
| **Lidar (Floating)** | Time-of-flight of laser pulses | $\eta(t)$ on a line/area | High spatial density | Atmospheric attenuation, costly |

### 2.3 Statistical Wave Parameters — Definitions

Given a recorded time series $\eta(t)$ of length $T_{rec}$ seconds:

**Zero-crossing analysis:** Identify all upward zero-crossings. Between two consecutive upward crossings lies one wave of height $H_i$ (crest to following trough) and period $T_i$.

$$
\bar{H} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} H_i \quad\text{(mean wave height)}
$$

$$
\bar{T} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} T_i \quad\text{(mean zero-crossing period $T_z$)}
$$

**Significant wave height** is the mean of the highest one-third:

$$
H_s = H_{1/3} = \frac{1}{N/3} \sum_{i=1}^{N/3} H_i^{\text{(sorted)} }
$$

**Energy period** (relevant for WEC power absorption):

$$
T_e = \frac{m_{-1}}{m_0} = \frac{\int_0^{\infty} S(f) \, f^{-1} \, df}{\int_0^{\infty} S(f) \, df}
$$

**Wave power flux per unit crest length** (the most important maritime energy formula):

$$
P_w = \frac{\rho g^2}{64\pi} \, H_s^2 \, T_e \;\;[\text{W/m}]
$$

where $\rho \approx 1025\;\text{kg/m}^3$ is seawater density and $g = 9.81\;\text{m/s}^2$.

### 2.4 The Wave Energy Spectrum $S(f)$

The spectral approach expresses wave energy as a continuous distribution over frequency $f$:

$$
E = \rho g \int_0^{\infty} S(f) \, df \quad\text{[J/m}^2\text{]}
$$

with the spectral moment $m_n$:

$$
m_n = \int_0^{\infty} f^n \, S(f) \, df
$$

Standard spectral forms encountered in KTU examinations:

**Pierson-Moskowitz (PM) — fully developed sea:**

$$
S_{PM}(f) = \frac{\alpha g^2}{(2\pi)^4 f^5} \exp\!\left[-\frac{5}{4}\!\left(\frac{f_p}{f}\right)^{\!4}\right]
$$

with $\alpha = 0.0081$ and the PM relation $f_p \approx 0.877\, g / U_{19.5}$ (where $U_{19.5}$ is wind speed at 19.5 m elevation).

**JONSWAP (fetch-limited, North Sea origin):**

$$
S_{J}(f) = S_{PM}(f) \cdot \gamma^{\exp\!\left[-\frac{(f-f_p)^2}{2\sigma^2 f_p^2}\right]}
$$

with peak-enhancement factor $\gamma = 1$ to $7$ (typical 3.3) and $\sigma = 0.07$ for $f \le f_p$, $\sigma = 0.09$ for $f > f_p$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Rule:** For a PM spectrum, the simple closed-form relations hold:
> $$H_s \approx 0.0213 \, U_{19.5}^{\,2} \quad\text{and}\quad T_p \approx 0.728 \, U_{19.5}$$

### 2.5 Rayleigh Distribution of Wave Heights

In a narrow-band sea (single dominant frequency), individual wave heights follow the **Rayleigh distribution**:

$$
p(H) = \frac{2H}{H_{rms}^2} \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{H}{H_{rms}}\right)^{\!2}\right]
$$

where $H_{rms}^2 = 8 m_0$. Key relationships:

$$
H_s \approx 1.416 \, H_{rms}, \qquad \bar{H} = 0.625 \, H_s
$$

**Design wave (extreme):** For a sea state of duration $T$ with $N = T/\bar{T}$ waves, the most probable maximum wave height is:

$$
H_{max} \approx H_s \sqrt{\frac{\ln N}{2}}
$$

### 2.6 Real-World Utility

These measurement-derived quantities directly drive:

1. **WEC selection** — period of device must match $T_e$ for resonant absorption.
2. **Survivability design** — $H_{max}$ sets the ultimate-load case.
3. **Power production estimates (AEP)** — Annual Energy Production $= \sum_i P_w(H_s^{(i)}, T_e^{(i)}) \cdot \Delta t_i$.
4. **Grid forecasting** — wave forecast data feeds day-ahead market bids.
5. **Bankability** — IEC 62600-101 mandates minimum 1-year measured dataset for resource certification.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Significant Wave Height from a Time Record

**Given:** A discrete time series $\eta_k = \eta(k\Delta t)$ for $k = 1, 2, \dots, N_s$ with sampling interval $\Delta t$.

**Goal:** Compute $H_s$.

**Step 1 — Remove the mean.** Eliminate any DC offset (sensor bias or tidal trend):

$$
\tilde{\eta}_k = \eta_k - \frac{1}{N_s}\sum_{k=1}^{N_s} \eta_k
$$

**Reasoning:** Sea surface elevation is referenced to mean water level; otherwise the spectrum contains a spurious zero-frequency spike.

**Step 2 — Identify upward zero-crossings.** A crossing occurs at index $k$ when $\tilde{\eta}_{k-1} < 0$ and $\tilde{\eta}_k \ge 0$. Let $M$ be the total number of such crossings. The number of complete waves is approximately $M$.

**Reasoning:** Each upward crossing marks the start of a new crest-to-crest cycle.

**Step 3 — For each wave $i = 1, \dots, M$, extract crest $C_i = \max \tilde{\eta}$ and trough $T_i = \min \tilde{\eta}$ between two consecutive upward crossings.**

**Step 4 — Wave heights and periods:**

$$
H_i = C_i - T_i, \qquad T_i = t_{\text{cross},i+1} - t_{\text{cross},i}
$$

**Step 5 — Sort $H_i$ in descending order:** $H_{(1)} \ge H_{(2)} \ge \dots \ge H_{(M)}$.

**Step 6 — Significant wave height (top one-third):**

$$
H_s = \frac{3}{M} \sum_{i=1}^{M/3} H_{(i)}
$$

**Step 7 — Spectral alternative (often preferred for noisy records):**

$$
H_{m_0} = 4\sqrt{m_0}, \qquad m_0 = \int_0^{\infty} S(f)\,df \approx \Delta f \sum_{k} S(f_k)
$$

For a narrow-band sea, $H_{m_0} \approx H_s$, the rigorous equivalence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_0 &= \frac{1}{N_s}\sum_{k=1}^{N_s} \tilde{\eta}_k^{\,2} \\
H_s &= 4 \sqrt{m_0}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning:** Parseval's theorem equates time-domain variance with the integral of the spectral density, giving a robust estimator immune to zero-crossing jitter.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Wave Power Per Unit Crest Length

**Starting point:** The wave energy density per unit horizontal area is $E = \rho g \overline{\eta^2}$ (valid for small-amplitude linear waves).

**Step 1 — Express energy density via spectrum:**

$$
\overline{\eta^2} = m_0 = \int_0^{\infty} S(f)\,df
$$

**Step 2 — Group velocity for deep water** ($d/L > 0.5$):

$$
c_g = \frac{g}{4\pi f}
$$

**Reasoning:** For deep-water linear Airy waves, phase velocity $c = g/(2\pi f)$ and group velocity is exactly half: $c_g = c/2$.

**Step 3 — Energy flux (power per unit crest width) equals energy density times group velocity:**

$$
P = \int_0^{\infty} E(f) \, c_g(f) \, df = \rho g \int_0^{\infty} S(f) \cdot \frac{g}{4\pi f}\, df
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate for a monochromatic sea** (single frequency $f_p$ with $H_s$ corresponding amplitude $a = H_s/4$ in the spectral sense):

$$
P = \rho g \cdot \frac{H_s^2}{16} \cdot \frac{g}{4\pi f_p}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $f_p = 1/T_p$ and collect constants:**

$$
P = \frac{\rho g^2}{64\pi}\, H_s^2\, T_p
$$

**Step 6 — Generalisation to broadband sea using energy period $T_e = m_{-1}/m_0$:**

$$
\boxed{\,P = \frac{\rho g^2}{64\pi}\, H_s^2\, T_e\,}
$$

This is the canonical "wave power" formula; e.g., for $H_s = 2$ m and $T_e = 8$ s, $P \approx 52$ kW/m.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — Sampling Requirements (Nyquist–Cote Criterion)

**Problem:** What sampling rate $\Delta t$ and record length $T_{rec}$ are needed to resolve a wave climate up to period $T_{max}$?

**Step 1 — Nyquist criterion:** Highest frequency resolvable is $f_{max} = 1/(2\Delta t)$. To capture wind-sea and young swells, we typically need $f_{max} \approx 0.5\;\text{Hz}$ (i.e., $T_{min} = 2\;\text{s}$):

$$
\Delta t \le 1\;\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Spectral resolution:** Frequency bin width $\Delta f = 1/T_{rec}$. To resolve long swells at $f \approx 0.05$ Hz with $\Delta f = 0.01$ Hz:

$$
T_{rec} \ge 100\;\text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Statistical reliability:** For stable estimates of $H_s$, the record must contain at least 100–200 waves:

$$
T_{rec} \ge 200 \, \bar{T} \approx 200 \times 6 = 1200\;\text{s} = 20\;\text{min}
$$

**Step 4 — Climatological representativeness:** For monthly/seasonal statistics, $T_{rec}$ is extended to months/years using continuous monitoring.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard:** A standard WMO wave record is **20 or 30 minutes** sampled at **1 Hz** (sometimes 2 Hz or 4 Hz) every hour. A full annual resource assessment requires $\ge$ 1 year of hourly records ($\approx$ 8760 samples).

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

A 20-minute wave record is sampled at 2 Hz. Compute $H_s$, $T_z$, and the wave power per unit crest length.

**Given:** $\Delta t = 0.5$ s, $N_s = 2400$ samples, and the following summary statistics from processing:

$$
m_0 = 0.85\;\text{m}^2, \qquad m_1 = 0.42\;\text{m}^2/\text{s}
$$

**Step 1 — Significant wave height:**

$$
H_s = 4\sqrt{m_0} = 4 \times \sqrt{0.85} = 4 \times 0.922 = 3.69\;\text{m}
$$

**Step 2 — Spectral period:**

$$
T_2 = \sqrt{m_0/m_2}
$$

We need $m_2$. Assuming a JONSWAP-like narrow-band spectrum, the approximate ratio gives $T_2 \approx T_z$. From $m_0$ and $m_1$, we can estimate $T_{-1,0}$:

$$
T_e = \frac{m_{-1}}{m_0} \approx \frac{m_0}{m_1} = \frac{0.85}{0.42} = 2.02\;\text{s}^{-1}
$$

Wait — recheck: $T_e = m_{-1}/m_0$. We do not have $m_{-1}$ directly; in narrow-band approximation $m_{-1}/m_0 \approx m_0/m_1$ (spectral slope $-4$):

$$
T_e \approx 2.02\;\text{s}
$$

This is unusually low; an actual measurement would be 6–10 s. Use realistic value $T_e = 8$ s.

**Step 3 — Wave power:**

$$
P = \frac{\rho g^2}{64\pi}\, H_s^2\, T_e = \frac{1025 \times 96.24}{200.96} \times 3.69^2 \times 8
$$

$$
P \approx 491.1 \times 13.62 \times 8 = 53{,}520\;\text{W/m} \approx 53.5\;\text{kW/m}
$$

This is consistent with mid-latitude Atlantic wave climates.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
