## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** Define the term **significant wave height** $H_s$ and explain why it is the most widely used parameter in maritime engineering practice. Mention its relation to $H_{m_0}$.

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Significant wave height $H_s$ is defined as the **mean height of the highest one-third of waves** observed in a sea-state record over a typical 20–30 minute sampling window. It was originally introduced by Sverdrup and Munk during WWII as the parameter most closely matching a trained mariner's visual estimate of wave height.

It is favoured because:
- It correlates strongly with wave energy: $E \propto H_s^2$.
- For a narrow-band Rayleigh-distributed sea, $H_s \approx 4\sqrt{m_0} \equiv H_{m_0}$ where $m_0$ is the zeroth spectral moment — making it directly computable from buoy spectra.

Hence $H_s \approx H_{m_0}$ for engineering practice, and is the canonical input for power, design-load, and resource calculations.

---

**Q2.** List **any three wave-measurement instruments** with their working principle and one advantage each.

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Accelerometer Wave Buoy** — measures vertical acceleration and integrates twice to obtain $\eta(t)$. *Advantage:* WMO-standard, captures full spectral information.

2. **Acoustic Doppler Current Profiler (ADCP)** — emits acoustic pulses and exploits Doppler shift on surface backscatter. *Advantage:* provides directional wave spectrum and current profile simultaneously.

3. **Satellite Altimeter** — measures the range between satellite and sea surface via a radar altimeter. *Advantage:* global coverage, ideal for resource mapping.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**(a) [7 marks]** With the aid of a clear block diagram, describe the **end-to-end working of a directional wave buoy measurement system**. Explain the role of the heave-pitch-roll sensors and how the directional spectrum $S(f,\theta)$ is obtained.

**(b) [7 marks]** A wave-measurement campaign records the following spectral moments for a sea state: $m_0 = 0.64\;\text{m}^2$, $m_1 = 0.31\;\text{m}^2/\text{s}$, $m_2 = 0.22\;\text{m}^2/\text{s}^2$. Compute (i) the significant wave height $H_s$, (ii) the spectral periods $T_z$, $T_e$, and $T_p$ (assume JONSWAP with $\gamma = 3.3$ at $f_p$), and (iii) the wave-power flux per unit crest width.

---

**Model Solution (a):**

A directional wave buoy (e.g., Datawell Directional Waverider) integrates:

- **Heave accelerometer** along the vertical axis: records $\ddot{z}_b(t)$ for the buoy; double-integration yields $\eta(t)$.
- **Pitch and roll rate sensors** (orthogonal horizontal axes): capture the buoy's tilt angle, encoding horizontal slope information $(\partial \eta / \partial x, \partial \eta / \partial y)$.
- **Magnetometer (fluxgate compass)**: provides absolute heading reference for the slope vectors.
- **GPS receiver**: gives position and absolute time-synchronisation.

The slope $\eta_x$, $\eta_y$ signals, together with the heave displacement $\eta$, are Fourier-transformed into cross-spectra:

$$
C_{\eta\eta}(f),\quad C_{\eta \eta_x}(f),\quad C_{\eta \eta_y}(f)
$$

Using the **Longuet-Higgins et al. (1961) Maximum Likelihood Method (MLM)**, the directional spreading function is:

$$
S(f,\theta) = S(f) \cdot D(f,\theta)
$$

where the normalised spreading $D(f,\theta)$ is reconstructed from the cross-spectral matrix:

$$
D(f,\theta) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\left[1 + 2\sum_{n=1}^{2}\left(a_n \cos n\theta + b_n \sin n\theta\right)\right]
$$

with Fourier coefficients $a_n, b_n$ derived from the cross-spectra. The output products are:

- Omnidirectional spectrum $S(f)$
- Peak frequency $f_p$, peak direction $\theta_p$
- Directional spread $\sigma_\theta$
- 1-D sea parameters $H_s$, $T_z$, $T_p$

**(7 marks allocation: 3 marks diagram, 2 marks MLM, 2 marks interpretation.)**

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Significant wave height:**

$$
H_s = 4\sqrt{m_0} = 4 \times \sqrt{0.64} = 4 \times 0.8 = 3.2\;\text{m}
$$

**(ii) Spectral periods:**

Zero-crossing period:

$$
T_z = \sqrt{\frac{m_0}{m_2}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.64}{0.22}} = \sqrt{2.909} = 1.706\;\text{s}
$$

Energy period:

$$
T_e = \frac{m_{-1}}{m_0}
$$

Approximating $m_{-1} \approx m_0^2 / m_1 = 0.64^2/0.31 = 1.321\;\text{m}^2\!\cdot\!\text{s}$:

$$
T_e = \frac{1.321}{0.64} = 2.064\;\text{s}
$$

Peak period (JONSWAP approximation using PM base): For a JONSWAP spectrum, the relation

$$
T_p \approx \frac{1}{f_p}, \quad f_p = 1.0\,T_e \text{(approx in JONSWAP)} \approx 0.485\;\text{Hz}
$$

so

$$
T_p \approx 2.064 \times \frac{0.7813}{0.7813} \approx 1/0.485 \approx 2.06\;\text{s}
$$

> [Note: in real sea states, $T_p > T_e > T_z$, but this numerical example uses anomalously small spectral values for pedagogy. The student is expected to follow the formal definitions.]

**(iii) Wave-power flux per unit crest length:**

$$
P = \frac{\rho g^2}{64\pi}\, H_s^2\, T_e
$$

$$
P = \frac{1025 \times 9.81^2}{64 \times 3.1416} \times 3.2^2 \times 2.064
$$

Step-by-step:
- Numerator: $1025 \times 96.2361 = 98{,}642$
- Denominator: $201.06$
- Constant factor: $98{,}642 / 201.06 = 490.6$
- $H_s^2 = 10.24$
- Multiply: $490.6 \times 10.24 \times 2.064 = 490.6 \times 21.13 = 10{,}367\;\text{W/m} \approx 10.37\;\text{kW/m}$

**(7 marks: 2 marks for $H_s$, 3 marks for periods, 2 marks for $P$.)**

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **Pierson-Moskowitz (PM) and JONSWAP** wave spectra. State their mathematical forms, the empirical parameters, and discuss why JONSWAP is preferred for fetch-limited coastal seas typical of Indian maritime conditions.

**(b) [7 marks]** A wave energy converter (WEC) is to be deployed at a site where long-term measurements yield an annual mean significant wave height of $\overline{H_s} = 1.8$ m and mean energy period $\overline{T_e} = 7.5$ s. Estimate the average wave-power flux, the annual energy production per metre of crest, and discuss two key measurement uncertainties that may affect the bankability of this resource figure.

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Pierson-Moskowitz (PM) Spectrum:**

The PM spectrum describes a **fully developed sea** — a hypothetical steady state in which wind blows long enough over a long fetch that wave growth balances dissipation.

$$
S_{PM}(f) = \frac{\alpha g^2}{(2\pi)^4 f^5}\, \exp\!\left[-\frac{5}{4}\!\left(\frac{f_p}{f}\right)^{\!4}\right]
$$

with Phillips constant $\alpha = 0.0081$ and $f_p = 0.877\, g / U_{19.5}$. The shape is fixed once $U_{19.5}$ (or equivalently $H_s$, $T_p$) is known.

**JONSWAP Spectrum:**

The **Joint North Sea Wave Project (1973)** measured fetch-limited North Sea storms and found sharper spectral peaks than PM. They introduced a multiplicative peak-enhancement factor $\gamma$:

$$
S_{J}(f) = S_{PM}(f) \cdot \gamma^{\exp\!\left[-\frac{(f-f_p)^2}{2\sigma^2 f_p^2}\right]}
$$

with $\sigma = 0.07$ for $f \le f_p$, $\sigma = 0.09$ for $f > f_p$. Typical values: $\gamma = 1$ (no peak enhancement, equivalent to PM), $\gamma = 3.3$ (mean JONSWAP), up to 7 (rare extreme storms).

**Why JONSWAP for Indian coastal seas:**

1. Indian coastal seas (Arabian Sea, Bay of Bengal) are strongly **monsoon-modulated** — fetch varies seasonally and is rarely "fully developed."
2. Tropical-cyclone-generated waves are **fetch-limited** by storm radius.
3. Coastal WEC sites near Kerala or Gujarat typically experience **young, growing seas** — the JONSWAP narrow peak matches observations.
4. The **higher peak** of JONSWAP yields higher energy-flux estimates, which directly increases the bankable resource assessment.
5. PM spectra **underestimate** $H_s$ for fetch-limited conditions, which would lead to financial under-reporting of WEC power output.

**(7 marks: 2 marks PM, 2 marks JONSWAP, 3 marks Indian context justification.)**

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Average wave-power flux per metre crest:**

$$
P = \frac{\rho g^2}{64\pi} \overline{H_s}^{\,2}\, \overline{T_e}
$$

$$
P = \frac{1025 \times 9.81^2}{64\pi} \times 1.8^2 \times 7.5
$$

Step-by-step evaluation:
- $g^2 = 96.2361\;\text{m}^2/\text{s}^4$
- $\rho g^2 = 1025 \times 96.2361 = 98{,}642\;\text{N/m}^3\!\cdot\!\text{m}$
- $64\pi = 201.06$
- Coefficient: $98{,}642 / 201.06 = 490.6\;\text{W·s/m}^4$
- $H_s^2 = 3.24\;\text{m}^2$
- $H_s^2 \cdot T_e = 3.24 \times 7.5 = 24.3\;\text{m}^2\!\cdot\!\text{s}$
- $P = 490.6 \times 24.3 = 11{,}922\;\text{W/m} \approx 11.9\;\text{kW/m}$

**(ii) Annual energy production per metre crest:**

$$
AEP = P \times 8760 \;\text{h} \times 3600\;\text{s/h} \times \text{capacity factor}
$$

Without considering WEC efficiency, the gross oceanic resource is:

$$
AEP_{gross} = 11{,}922 \;\text{W/m} \times 8760 \;\text{h} = 104{,}436\;\text{Wh/m} \approx 104.4\;\text{MWh/m/yr}
$$

For a WEC of capture-width ratio CWR $= 0.4$ and availability $0.9$:

$$
AEP_{net} = 104.4 \times 0.4 \times 0.9 \approx 37.6\;\text{MWh/m/yr}
$$

**(iii) Two key measurement uncertainties:**

1. **Sensor calibration drift** — accelerometer bias can drift by 0.1–1 % per year in harsh marine conditions, directly biasing $H_s$ by similar magnitude. For an $H_s = 1.8$ m site, a 5 % error $\rightarrow$ 9 % error in $P$ (since $P \propto H_s^2$), translating to a $\sim$10 MWh/m/yr revenue impact over the project lifetime.

2. **Sampling / spatial representativeness** — a single buoy samples one point, but the WEC will interact with waves over a fetch of $\sim$100 m. Variability across this spatial scale (especially in shallow water with refraction/diffraction) can cause 10–20 % discrepancy between measured and absorbed power. Mitigation: deploy multi-buoy arrays or HF-radar directional systems.

Additional uncertainties for KTU enrichment: tidal aliasing, biofouling on pressure sensors, mooring-induced low-frequency noise, and storm-data gaps.

**(7 marks: 2 marks P, 2 marks AEP, 3 marks uncertainty discussion.)**

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common pitfalls where students lose marks in Wave Measurements questions:**
> 1. **Mixing $H_s$, H_{1/3}, and H_{m_0}** — remember $H_s \approx H_{1/3} \approx 4\sqrt{m_0}$ only for narrow-band Rayleigh seas. Do not state them as exact equalities.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 4** in $H_s = 4\sqrt{m_0}$ — a classic mark-deductor.
> 3. **Confusing $T_z$, $T_p$, $T_e$, $T_{02}$, $T_{-1,0}$** — each is defined via a specific spectral moment ratio; do not interchange them.
> 4. **PM vs JONSWAP** — JONSWAP is not a separate spectrum but a PM modification. Examiners penalise loose language.
> 5. **Units in $P_w$** — must end in W/m (watts per metre of crest width), NOT watts or kW. Always include the dimensional check.
> 6. **Skipping the depth check** — for shallow-water sites (e.g., $d < 50$ m), deep-water formulas may not apply; mention the deep-water assumption explicitly.
> 7. **Writing only formulas without interpretation** — KTU examiners award marks for engineering context (why $H_s$ matters, why $T_e$ over $T_z$ for WEC).
> 8. **No numerical cross-check** — when you compute $P$, sanity-check: a typical Atlantic site $\sim$ 30–70 kW/m; Indian west coast $\sim$ 5–25 kW/m. If your answer is 500 kW/m, you have a factor-of-ten error.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **$H_s = 4\sqrt{m_0}$** — the most-cited KTU formula; know both its zero-crossing and spectral derivations.
- **$P = (\rho g^2 / 64\pi)\, H_s^2\, T_e$** — the maritime renewable energy equation par excellence.
- **Wave periods hierarchy:** $T_p > T_e > T_{-1,0} > T_z > T_{02}$ (typical ordering for a JONSWAP-like sea).
- **PM spectrum:** fully developed; $\alpha = 0.0081$.
- **JONSWAP:** $\gamma = 3.3$ typical; fetch-limited; Indian seas.
- **Rayleigh distribution:** $p(H) = (2H/H_{rms}^2)\, e^{-(H/H_{rms})^2}$.
- **$H_{max} \approx H_s \sqrt{\ln N / 2}$** for design wave.
- **Standard record:** 20–30 minutes, 1–4 Hz, every hour (WMO/IEC).
- **Sensor hierarchy:** Accelerometer buoy (gold standard) > ADCP > Pressure > X-band > Satellite (H_s only).
- **Directional spectra** need heave-pitch-roll buoy or HF-radar — single-point sensors cannot resolve direction.
- **Bankability** requires $\ge 1$ year of validated data per IEC 62600-101.
- **Indian context:** Arabian Sea — high wave energy during SW monsoon; Bay of Bengal — cyclone-driven extremes.
- **Always state deep-water assumption** when using $c_g = g/(4\pi f)$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tidal Energy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST755 - RENEWABLE ENERGY IN MARITIME SECTOR
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Tidal Energy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tidal Energy** is a form of **hydropower** that converts the kinetic and potential energy of natural ocean tides (the rise and fall of sea levels caused by the gravitational interaction between the **Earth**, the **Moon**, and the **Sun**) into usable electrical power. It is classified as a highly **predictable renewable energy source** because tidal cycles are governed by precise celestial mechanics rather than stochastic weather variables.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PENST755)**, tidal energy is studied as a **Marine Renewable Energy (MRE)** resource alongside wave, ocean thermal, and offshore wind. It represents one of the most **mature marine technologies**, with the first commercial plant (La Rance, France) operational since **1966**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy — "The Lunar Breath of the Ocean"**
> Imagine you are sitting on a beach with a large bucket of water. Every 6 hours and 12.5 minutes, an invisible giant (the Moon) gently lifts the water level in your bucket by half a meter, and then gently sets it back down. If you place a small turbine at the opening of the bucket, every time the water rises or falls, you can spin the turbine and generate electricity. **That is tidal energy in a nutshell.**

The Moon's gravity acts as the **primary tide-raising force** (contributing roughly **60.7%** of tidal energy), the Sun contributes about **34.5%**, and other celestial/geographic effects account for the remaining **~4.8%**. Because the Moon's orbit is known with extreme precision, we can forecast tidal energy output **centuries in advance** — a unique advantage over solar and wind.

### 1.3 Key Physical Metrics (Standardized)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlights — Standard Tidal Parameters**
> - **Tidal Range (R):** Vertical difference between high tide and low tide (typically **2 m to 10 m** for viable sites).
> - **Tidal Prism (P):** Volume of water exchanged between high and low tide within a basin. Unit: **m³**.
> - **Spring Tide:** Maximum tidal range (Sun + Moon aligned) — occurs during **Full Moon** and **New Moon**.
> - **Neap Tide:** Minimum tidal range (Sun–Moon at right angles) — occurs during **Quarter Moon** phases.
> - **Tidal Period:** Semidiurnal (~**12 h 25 min**) for most global coastlines, including Kerala.

### 1.4 Classification of Tidal Energy Technologies

| Technology | Operating Principle | Energy Captured | TRL (Technology Readiness) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tidal Barrage** | Potential energy of height difference | Potential (Head) | **9 (Commercial)** |
| **Tidal Stream** | Kinetic energy of flowing water | Kinetic (Velocity) | **8 (Pre-commercial)** |
| **Tidal Lagoon** | Enclosed artificial basin | Potential (Head) | **7 (Demonstration)** |
| **Tidal Fence** | Vertical axis rotor across channel | Kinetic (Velocity) | **5–6 (Pilot)** |
| **Dynamic Tidal Power (DTP)** | Long dam perpendicular to coast | Potential + Kinetic | **3 (Concept)** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Origin of Tides — The Equilibrium Theory

Tides are fundamentally driven by the **tide-raising force (TRF)**, which arises from the **differential gravitational pull** across the Earth's diameter. Consider the Earth–Moon system:

1. The Moon's gravity pulls the near side of Earth **more strongly** than the center, and the center **more strongly** than the far side.
2. This produces two **tidal bulges** — one facing the Moon (high tide) and one on the opposite side (also high tide).
3. The Earth's rotation relative to these bulges produces the observed **semidiurnal cycle**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The equilibrium tide is a **theoretical, frictionless** model assuming the Earth is entirely covered by deep ocean. The actual tide (**real/dynamical tide**) is modified by coastline geometry, bathymetry, and Coriolis acceleration.

### 2.2 Types of Tides (KTU High-Yield)

- **Semidiurnal Tide:** Two nearly equal high tides and two low tides per lunar day. (Kerala coast — **Gulf of Cambay-Kozhikode region**).
- **Diurnal Tide:** One high and one low tide per lunar day.
- **Mixed Semidiurnal:** Two unequal high/low tides per day (most common globally).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All equations below are EXAM-CRITICAL for KTU ESE Module 2. Memorize units and limiting assumptions.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{avg} = \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot \dfrac{R^{2}}{2T}$ | Average tidal power per unit area of basin | W/m² |
| 2 | $E_{cycle} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot R^{2}$ | Energy per tidal cycle (single basin) | J |
| 3 | $P_{inst} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot R \cdot v$ | Instantaneous power from head difference | W |
| 4 | $E_{tidal} = 0.22 \cdot \rho \cdot g^{2} \cdot A^{2} \cdot R^{2} \cdot T$ | Annual energy from barrage (with η ≈ 0.22) | J/year |
| 5 | $P_{stream} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A_{s} \cdot v^{3}$ | Tidal stream power (Betz coefficient limited) | W |
| 6 | $v_{tidal} = \sqrt{2gR}$ | Theoretical max tidal velocity (Torricelli-like) | m/s |
| 7 | $R_{S} = 2 R_{M}$ | Spring tide range (approx.) = 2 × Mean tide range | m |
| 8 | $R_{N} = \dfrac{1}{3} R_{M}$ to $\dfrac{1}{2} R_{M}$ | Neap tide range | m |
| 9 | $\eta_{max} = \dfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$ | Betz limit for kinetic turbines | dimensionless |
| 10 | $F_{tide} = \dfrac{2 G M m r}{R^{3}}$ | Tide-raising force per unit mass | N/kg |

Where:
- $\rho$ = density of seawater = **1025 kg/m³** (KTU standard value)
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration = **9.81 m/s²**
- $A$ = basin area (m²)
- $R$ = tidal range (m)
- $T$ = tidal period (s) ≈ **44712 s** for semidiurnal
- $A_{s}$ = swept area of turbine rotor (m²)
- $v$ = tidal current velocity (m/s)
- $G$ = universal gravitational constant = **6.674 × 10⁻¹¹ N·m²/kg²**
- $M$ = mass of Moon = **7.35 × 10²² kg**
- $m$ = mass of Earth = **5.97 × 10²⁴ kg**
- $r$ = radius of Earth = **6.371 × 10⁶ m**
- $R$ = Earth–Moon distance ≈ **3.84 × 10⁸ m**

> [!WARNING]
> The factor **0.22** in Formula 4 comes from integrating the cubic power cycle and accounts for the practical efficiency of a single-basin ebb-generation scheme. It is **NOT** a universal constant — it is specific to the La Rance operational model.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Grid baseload stability:** Unlike solar/wind, tidal cycles are deterministic, allowing dispatch planning with **< 1% uncertainty** in the long term.
- **Coastal protection synergy:** Tidal lagoons can double as **storm surge barriers** (e.g., Swansea Bay lagoon design).
- **Desalination co-generation:** Tidal plants can drive **reverse-osmosis** units during off-peak generation.
- **Maritime hybrid integration:** Tidal + offshore wind + wave systems share **substation, cable, and O&M infrastructure**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Average Power from a Single-Basin Tidal Barrage

**Given:** A coastal basin of surface area $A$ (m²) and uniform tidal range $R$ (m).
**Find:** Average power extracted per tidal cycle, $P_{avg}$.

#### Step 1 — Express the potential energy of water column

The water at high tide sits at an average height of $R/2$ above the turbine centerline (assuming mid-tide reference). The gravitational potential energy of this elevated mass is:

$$
E_p = m \cdot g \cdot h = (\rho \cdot A \cdot R) \cdot g \cdot \left(\dfrac{R}{2}\right)
$$

**Logic:** Mass of water above reference = density × volume = $\rho \cdot A \cdot R$. Average height of water column above reference = $R/2$ (centroid of a uniform slab).

#### Step 2 — Simplify the energy expression

$$
E_p = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot R^{2}
$$

**Logic:** Combining $\rho A R \cdot g \cdot (R/2)$ gives the half-factor in front. **This is the maximum extractable energy per filling (or emptying) of the basin.**

#### Step 3 — Account for the tidal period

Power is energy per unit time. The semidiurnal cycle repeats every $T$ seconds, so:

$$
P_{avg} = \dfrac{E_p}{T} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot R^{2}}{2T}
$$

**Logic:** For a 12 h 25 min cycle, $T = 44712$ s. If we count both flood and ebb, total energy doubles, but average power per cycle is what we report.

#### Step 4 — Numerical sanity check (La Rance scale)

Substitute La Rance parameters: $A \approx 17 \text{ km}^{2} = 1.7 \times 10^{7} \text{ m}^{2}$, $R = 8.5$ m, $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $T = 44712$ s.

$$
P_{avg} = \dfrac{(1025)(9.81)(1.7 \times 10^{7})(8.5)^{2}}{2(44712)}
$$

Compute numerator step-by-step:

$$
(1025)(9.81) = 10055.25
$$

$$
(1.7 \times 10^{7})(8.5)^{2} = (1.7 \times 10^{7})(72.25) = 1.22825 \times 10^{9}
$$

$$
\text{Numerator} = (10055.25)(1.22825 \times 10^{9}) = 1.2351 \times 10^{13}
$$

$$
P_{avg} = \dfrac{1.2351 \times 10^{13}}{89424} \approx 1.38 \times 10^{8} \text{ W} = 138 \text{ MW}
$$

**Logic:** This matches La Rance's **installed 240 MW** capacity (with multiple turbine units and head differences). The discrepancy is absorbed by efficiency, multi-basin operation, and head recovery.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final boxed result:** $\boxed{P_{avg} = \dfrac{\rho g A R^{2}}{2T}}$ — **Must be memorized for KTU.**

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Tidal Stream Power (Betz-style for Marine Current)

**Given:** A tidal channel of cross-section $A_{s}$ with current velocity $v$.
**Find:** Maximum extractable power.

#### Step 1 — Mass flow rate through the turbine

The mass of water passing the turbine per second is:

$$
\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A_{s} \cdot v
$$

**Logic:** Mass flux = density × volumetric flow rate.

#### Step 2 — Kinetic energy flux

The kinetic energy per unit mass of the water is $\frac{1}{2} v^{2}$. Therefore, the power in the undisturbed stream is:

$$
P_{stream} = \dot{m} \cdot \dfrac{v^{2}}{2} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho \cdot A_{s} \cdot v^{3}
$$

**Logic:** This is the raw hydrodynamic power of the flow. Notice the **cubic dependence on velocity** — a 2× increase in current speed gives **8× power**.

#### Step 3 — Apply Betz limit for maximum extractable fraction

No turbine can extract 100% of the kinetic energy (it would have to stop the flow). The maximum theoretical efficiency is the **Betz coefficient**:

$$
C_{P,max} = \dfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.593
$$

Thus:

$$
P_{max} = C_{P,max} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} \rho \cdot A_{s} \cdot v^{3} = \dfrac{8}{27} \rho A_{s} v^{3}
$$

**Logic:** The Betz limit is derived by minimizing kinetic energy loss upstream and ensuring mass continuity. It is a **universal result for any free-stream turbine** (wind or tidal).

> [!NOTE]
> **Final boxed result:** $\boxed{P_{stream,max} = \dfrac{8}{27} \rho A_{s} v^{3}}$ — valid for both wind and tidal kinetic systems.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Single vs. Double Basin Tidal Schemes

| Parameter | **Single Basin (Ebb Generation)** | **Double Basin** |
|---|---|---|
| **Operating Cycle** | Generates only during ebb tide (~6 h) | Continuous generation |
| **Average Output** | Low (intermittent) | Higher (smoother) |
| **Capacity Factor** | ~22% (typical) | ~25–30% |
| **Capital Cost** | Lower | Higher (extra basin) |
| **Turbine Count** | ~24–40 (La Rance model) | More (split between basins) |
| **Example Plant** | **La Rance, France (240 MW)** | Annapolis Royal, Canada (tested) |
| **Energy Loss** | 1/3 (flood phase idle) | Reduced via cross-flow between basins |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** A "single basin" does **not** mean single turbine. La Rance has **24 × 10 MW** bulb turbines. Students often confuse this — read questions carefully.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Theoretical Maximum Tidal Velocity

If a tidal range of $R$ drops through a turbine/nozzle, the exit velocity (ignoring losses) is:

$$
v_{tidal} = \sqrt{2 g R}
$$

**Logic:** Conservation of energy: potential head $gR$ converts entirely to kinetic head $v^{2}/2$. Rearranging gives the boxed formula. For $R = 8$ m: $v = \sqrt{2(9.81)(8)} = \sqrt{156.96} \approx 12.5$ m/s.

---

### 3.5 Site Selection Criteria (KTU High-Yield)

A coastal site is **viable for tidal energy** if it satisfies the following:

1. **Tidal Range $R \geq 5$ m** (commercial threshold; 7+ m preferred).
2. **Constricted bay geometry** — amplifies range via **co-oscillation** and **resonance**.
3. **Mean tidal current velocity $v \geq 2$ m/s** (for stream systems).
4. **Hard rock or sedimentary foundation** suitable for barrage construction.
5. **Minimal environmental/ecological disruption** (mangroves, fish migration corridors).
6. **Distance to grid $\leq 50$ km** for cost-effective subsea cable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Global best sites:** Bay of Fundy (Canada) — $R \approx 16$ m; Severn Estuary (UK) — $R \approx 14$ m; Gulf of Khambhat (India) — $R \approx 5–7$ m; Palk Bay (India-Tamil Nadu) — explored but lower range.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Tidal Energy Conversion Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **end-to-end functional flow** of a single-basin tidal barrage system, from celestial forcing to grid injection.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Moon Sun Gravity Field] --> A2[Tide Raising Force Differential]
    A2 --> A3[Ocean Tidal Bulge Formation]
    A3 --> A4[Tidal Range R in Coastal Basin]
    A4 --> A5{Tidal Barrage Present?}
    A5 -->|YES| A6[Basin Filling Phase Flood Tide]
    A5 -->|NO| A7[Open Sea Energy Dissipated]
    A6 --> A8[Head Difference h Buildup]
    A8 --> A9[Turbine Gate Opens Ebb Generation]
    A9 --> A10[Bulb Turbine Spins Low Head Kaplan Type]
    A10 --> A11[Rotational Mechanical Power]
    A11 --> A12[Gearbox Step Up to 750 rpm]
    A12 --> A13[Synchronous Generator AC Output]
    A13 --> A14[Step Up Transformer 11kV to 132kV]
    A14 --> A15[Subsea Cable to Onshore Substation]
    A15 --> A16[Grid Integration and Dispatch]
    A16 --> A17[End User Load Demand]
    A10 --> A18[Environmental Impact Fish Mortality Sediment]
    A18 --> A19[Regulatory Monitoring Compliance]
```

---

### 4.2 Process Flow: Single Basin Operating Modes (Mermaid State Diagram)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> WaitingTide
    WaitingTide --> FloodPhase: Rising Tide Detected
    FloodPhase --> BasinFull: Basin Reaches High Water Level
    BasinFull --> Holding: Turbines Closed Head Builds Up
    Holding --> EbbGeneration: Ebb Tide Begins Head Reaches Threshold
    EbbGeneration --> BasinEmpty: Water Level Drops to Cutoff
    BasinEmpty --> WaitingTide: Next Cycle Begins
    EbbGeneration --> SludgeRelease: Sluice Gates for Sediment Flushing
    SludgeRelease --> WaitingTide
    Holding --> EmergencyShut: Storm Surge or Grid Fault
    EmergencyShut --> WaitingTide
```

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Tidal Stream Turbine Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Tidal Current v m/s] --> B2[Blade Rotor Swept Area As]
    B2 --> B3[Variable Pitch Blades 2 to 3]
    B3 --> B4[Hub and Yaw Mechanism]
    B4 --> B5[Sealed Gearbox Multi Stage]
    B5 --> B6[Permanent Magnet Generator PMSG]
    B6 --> B7[Power Converter AC to DC to AC]
    B7 --> B8[Subsea Step Up Transformer]
    B8 --> B9[Submarine Export Cable]
    B9 --> B10[Onshore Grid Substation]
    B6 --> B11[SCADA and Condition Monitoring]
    B11 --> B12[Predictive Maintenance Trigger]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The arrows in the diagrams above are **functional dependencies** (i.e., what feeds what), not physical flow paths. In the field, tidal plants also integrate **flood-generation, two-way generation, and pumping modes**, which are expanded in advanced elective modules.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define Tidal Energy. List the three main classifications of tidal energy conversion systems with one example for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Tidal energy is the renewable energy harnessed from the natural rise and fall of ocean tides caused by the gravitational interaction of the Earth, Moon, and Sun.

The three main classifications are:
1. **Tidal Barrage** — uses a dam across an estuary to capture potential energy (e.g., **La Rance, France**).
2. **Tidal Stream** — extracts kinetic energy of moving water using submerged turbines (e.g., **SeaGen, Northern Ireland**).
3. **Tidal Lagoon** — uses an artificially enclosed coastal basin (e.g., **Swansea Bay, UK** proposed).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students write "tidal energy is the energy of waves" — this is **WRONG**. Tides and waves are **different phenomena** with different physical origins. Marks deducted: **1/3**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*State and briefly explain the difference between Spring Tide and Neap Tide.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Spring Tide:** Occurs during **Full Moon** and **New Moon** when the Sun, Earth, and Moon are aligned (syzygy). The gravitational forces of the Sun and Moon **reinforce** each other, producing the **maximum tidal range** (typically $R_S \approx 2R_M$).
- **Neap Tide:** Occurs during the **First** and **Third Quarter** Moons, when the Sun–Earth–Moon line is at a right angle (quadrature). The Sun's and Moon's pulls partially **cancel**, producing the **minimum tidal range** ($R_N \approx 0.3$ to $0.5 \cdot R_M$).

---

### 📘 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided)

> **ESE Module 2 — INTERNAL CHOICE (Pick Either Question A or Question B)**

---

### ✏️ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**(a)** With neat schematic description, explain the working principle of a **single-basin tidal barrage** with **ebb-generation** mode. Discuss the role of bulb turbines. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A tidal barrage is proposed at a coastal inlet with the following data:
- Basin area, $A = 5 \text{ km}^{2}$
- Tidal range, $R = 6 \text{ m}$
- Density of seawater, $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$
- Tidal period, $T = 44712 \text{ s}$
- Generator + turbine overall efficiency, $\eta = 0.85$

Calculate the **average useful power output** and the **energy generated per tidal cycle**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to A(a) — 7 Marks

1. **Concept (1 mark):** Single-basin ebb-generation means the basin is **filled at high tide** and the stored water is **released through turbines at low tide** to generate power.
2. **Phases (3 marks):** Describe the four phases:
   - **Filling phase (flood tide):** Turbines closed, sluice gates open, basin fills to high water level.
   - **Holding phase (high tide slack):** Water held until head difference $h$ exceeds turbine cut-in.
   - **Generation phase (ebb):** Turbines open, water flows out, kinetic energy extracted.
   - **Emptying phase (low tide slack):** Basin at low level, cycle resets.
3. **Bulb Turbines (2 marks):** These are **low-head, high-flow** Kaplan-type turbines with the generator sealed inside a watertight bulb. Used because tidal heads are small (1–10 m) but flow volumes are huge. La Rance uses 24 such units, each 10 MW.
4. **Schematic (1 mark):** Refer to the block diagram in Section 4.1.

#### Model Solution to A(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Calculate gross average power (theoretical).**

$$
P_{gross} = \dfrac{\rho g A R^{2}}{2T}
$$

Substitute values. Note $A = 5 \text{ km}^{2} = 5 \times 10^{6} \text{ m}^{2}$.

$$
P_{gross} = \dfrac{(1025)(9.81)(5 \times 10^{6})(6)^{2}}{2(44712)}
$$

Compute numerator:

$$
(1025)(9.81) = 10055.25
$$

$$
(5 \times 10^{6})(36) = 1.8 \times 10^{8}
$$

$$
\text{Numerator} = (10055.25)(1.8 \times 10^{8}) = 1.8099 \times 10^{12}
$$

$$
P_{gross} = \dfrac{1.8099 \times 10^{12}}{89424} \approx 2.024 \times 10^{7} \text{ W} = 20.24 \text{ MW}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply efficiency to get useful power.**

$$
P_{useful} = \eta \cdot P_{gross} = (0.85)(20.24) = 17.20 \text{ MW}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate energy per tidal cycle.**

$$
E_{cycle} = P_{useful} \cdot T = (17.20 \times 10^{6})(44712)
$$

$$
E_{cycle} = 7.690 \times 10^{11} \text{ J} = 769.0 \text{ GJ} \approx 213.6 \text{ MWh}
$$

**Final Answer:**
- $P_{useful} = \mathbf{17.2 \text{ MW}}$
- $E_{cycle} = \mathbf{213.6 \text{ MWh}}$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (A-b):** Students often forget to apply $\eta$ to power (some apply it only to energy). Also, do **not** convert $A$ to km² in the denominator — convert to **m²**. Marks lost: typically **1–2 per mistake**.

---

### ✏️ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2023] — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Explain the **origin of tides** using the equilibrium theory. Derive an expression for the **tide-raising force per unit mass** and discuss how the Moon and Sun contribute. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A tidal stream channel has a cross-sectional area of $A_s = 4000 \text{ m}^{2}$ and mean peak velocity of $v = 2.5 \text{ m/s}$. Calculate the **maximum extractable power** using the Betz limit. Compare with the gross hydrodynamic power. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to B(a) — 7 Marks

1. **Origin (2 marks):** Tides arise from the **differential gravitational pull** of the Moon (and Sun) across the Earth's diameter. The near side experiences a greater pull than the center, and the center experiences a greater pull than the far side — creating two tidal bulges.
2. **Equilibrium Theory Assumption (1 mark):** Earth is **fully ocean-covered**, no continents, no friction, hydrostatic equilibrium.
3. **Derivation (3 marks):** For two masses $M$ (Moon) and $m$ (Earth parcel) separated by distance $R$, the gravitational force per unit mass at Earth's surface (relative to center) is:
   - At near side: $F_n = \dfrac{GM}{(R-r)^{2}}$
   - At far side: $F_f = \dfrac{GM}{(R+r)^{2}}$
   - The differential (TRF): $F_{tide} = F_n - F_f \approx \dfrac{2GMr}{R^{3}}$
4. **Moon vs Sun (1 mark):** Moon contributes **~60.7%** (despite smaller mass) because of the cubic $R^{3}$ term in the denominator; Sun contributes **~34.5%**; others ~4.8%.

#### Model Solution to B(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Gross hydrodynamic power.**

$$
P_{gross} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho A_{s} v^{3}
$$

Substitute: $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $A_s = 4000$ m², $v = 2.5$ m/s.

$$
v^{3} = (2.5)^{3} = 15.625 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}^{3}
$$

$$
P_{gross} = (0.5)(1025)(4000)(15.625) = (0.5)(1025)(62500) = (0.5)(6.406 \times 10^{7}) = 3.203 \times 10^{7} \text{ W}
$$

$$
P_{gross} = 32.03 \text{ MW}
$$

**Step 2 — Maximum extractable power (Betz limit).**

$$
P_{max} = C_{P,max} \cdot P_{gross} = \left(\dfrac{16}{27}\right)(32.03)
$$

$$
P_{max} = (0.5926)(32.03) = 18.98 \text{ MW} \approx 19.0 \text{ MW}
$$

**Step 3 — Comparison.**

The Betz limit caps extraction at ~59.3% of gross. The remaining ~40.7% is **wake kinetic energy** that must be preserved to maintain flow continuity.

**Final Answer:**
- $P_{gross} = \mathbf{32.03 \text{ MW}}$
- $P_{max} = \mathbf{18.98 \text{ MW}}$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (B-b):** Students frequently forget that $v$ must be **cubed**, not squared. Others apply the Betz coefficient to $v$ instead of $P$. Marks lost: **2/7** for these mistakes.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST (Print before exam)**

- [x] Tidal energy = predictable marine renewable, governed by **lunar/solar gravity**.
- [x] Primary technologies: **Barrage, Stream, Lagoon, Fence, DTP**.
- [x] Tidal range $R$ — minimum commercial viability: **5 m**.
- [x] Spring tide $R_S \approx 2R_M$; Neap tide $R_N \approx 0.3$–$0.5 R_M$.
- [x] **Betz limit:** $C_{P,max} = 16/27 \approx 0.593$ — applies to **stream turbines**.
- [x] Average power formula: $P_{avg} = \dfrac{\rho g A R^{2}}{2T}$ — **memorize verbatim**.
- [x] Energy per cycle: $E_p = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho g A R^{2}$.
- [x] Stream power: $P = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho A_s v^{3}$ — **cubic velocity dependence is key**.
- [x] Standard seawater density: $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³.
- [x] La Rance = **240 MW**, 24 × 10 MW bulb turbines, France.
- [x] Bay of Fundy (Canada) = **highest global tidal range** (~16 m).
- [x] Single-basin ebb-mode capacity factor ≈ **22%**.
- [x] Double-basin improves output continuity but raises CAPEX.
- [x] TRF per unit mass: $F_{tide} = \dfrac{2GMr}{R^{3}}$.
- [x] Moon's tidal contribution (**60.7%**) > Sun's (**34.5%**) due to proximity (cubic denominator).
- [x] India — Gulf of Khambhat is the most promising tidal site (~5–7 m range).
- [x] Site selection: range, velocity, geology, grid proximity, ecology.
- [x] Torricelli-like tidal velocity: $v = \sqrt{2gR}$ (theoretical max).
- [x] **Ebb generation** = release at low tide; **Flood generation** = generate at rising tide; **Two-way** = both phases.
- [x] **Tidal stream ≠ Tidal barrage** — the former is kinetic, the latter is potential.

---

### 🎯 Last-Minute KTU Examiner Tips

1. **Always state assumptions** (steady state, uniform basin, semidiurnal cycle).
2. **Always include units** in intermediate steps — examiners award partial marks for unit consistency.
3. **Diagrams earn free marks** — even a hand-drawn block diagram is worth 1–2 marks.
4. **Define variables** before plugging in numbers — this prevents "value substitution" errors.
5. **For numerical questions**, give the final answer in a **boxed format** with **units and 2–3 significant figures**.

---

*End of Module 2 — Tidal Energy | KTU 2024 Scheme | PENST755*

topics.title: Tide Generating Forces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST755 - RENEWABLE ENERGY IN MARITIME SECTOR
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Tide Generating Forces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tide Generating Forces (TGFs)** are the *net differential gravitational forces* exerted by extraterrestrial bodies (primarily the **Moon** and the **Sun**) on different particles of the Earth's oceans and solid body, after the dominant central gravitational pull and the balancing centrifugal force of the Earth-Moon/Earth-Sun barycentric system are subtracted out. These residual forces physically distort the water envelope, producing the rhythmic rise and fall of sea level known as **tides**.

In strict Newtonian mechanics, the **Tide Generating Force** at any point on the Earth's surface is the **vector difference** between:
1. The **gravitational pull** of the disturbing body (Moon/Sun) on that point, and
2. The **centrifugal force** acting on that point due to the revolution of the Earth around the common **barycenter** (center of mass of the Earth-Moon or Earth-Sun system).

$$\vec{F}_{TGF} = \vec{F}_{gravitational} - \vec{F}_{centrifugal}$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture — "The Stretching Rubber Sheet"**

Imagine the Earth as a soft rubber ball, and the Moon as a magnet pulling at it from a distance. The Moon does **not** simply lift the entire ocean straight up — it *stretches* the water shell in two opposite bulges simultaneously. One bulge points *toward* the Moon (because the Moon's pull is strongest on the near side), and the other bulge points *away* from the Moon (because the centrifugal effect dominates on the far side). The water on the sides (perpendicular to the Earth-Moon axis) is correspondingly depressed. This stretching pattern rotates with the Earth, producing two high tides and two low tides every lunar day (~24 hours 50 minutes).

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Numerical Constants Used in Tidal Mechanics**

| Constant | Symbol | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| Mass of Earth | $M_E$ | $\mathbf{5.972 \times 10^{24} \text{ kg}}$ |
| Mass of Moon | $M_M$ | $\mathbf{7.342 \times 10^{22} \text{ kg}}$ |
| Mass of Sun | $M_S$ | $\mathbf{1.989 \times 10^{30} \text{ kg}}$ |
| Mean Earth-Moon distance | $R$ | $\mathbf{3.844 \times 10^{8} \text{ m}}$ |
| Mean Earth-Sun distance | $R_S$ | $\mathbf{1.496 \times 10^{11} \text{ m}}$ |
| Mean radius of Earth | $r$ | $\mathbf{6.371 \times 10^{6} \text{ m}}$ |
| Universal Gravitational Constant | $G$ | $\mathbf{6.674 \times 10^{-11} \text{ N·m}^2\text{/kg}^2}$ |
| Sidereal lunar day | $T_L$ | $\mathbf{23 \text{ h } 56 \text{ min}}$ |
| Synodic lunar day | $T_{syn}$ | $\mathbf{24 \text{ h } 50 \text{ min}}$ |

### 1.4 Why Tides Matter for Renewable Energy in Maritime Sector

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Relevance — Tidal Energy Conversion**
> Tide Generating Forces establish the *periodic potential energy gradient* in coastal waters. Tidal stream turbines, tidal barrages, and dynamic tidal power systems all exploit the **kinetic and potential energy** resulting from these forces. Accurate prediction of TGF is therefore the foundational step in *resource assessment* for any tidal energy project.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two-Body System — Center of Mass (Barycenter)

The Earth and Moon orbit a common **center of mass** called the **barycenter**. Every particle on Earth (including ocean water) experiences:

- A **gravitational attraction** toward the Moon (or Sun), and
- A **centrifugal force** due to Earth's revolution about the barycenter.

Both forces have nearly equal *magnitude and direction at the center of the Earth* (so they cancel), but at other points on Earth's surface, they differ slightly — producing a **net residual force** = Tide Generating Force.

### 2.2 Vector Decomposition of the Tide Generating Force

For a unit mass located at point **P** on Earth's surface, at angular position $\theta$ measured from the Earth-Moon line:

#### Step-by-step logic:
- **Step 1:** Compute gravitational acceleration of Moon at point P: $g_M = \dfrac{GM_M}{d^2}$, where $d$ is the distance from the Moon to P.
- **Step 2:** Compute centrifugal acceleration (same for *all* points on Earth, equal to that at Earth's center): $a_c = \dfrac{GM_M}{R^2}$.
- **Step 3:** Take the **vector difference** to obtain the residual — the TGF.
- **Step 4:** Resolve the residual into a **horizontal (tractive) component** (which actually moves water) and a **vertical (radial) component** (which is mostly balanced by gravity).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master These Equations for KTU Examinations**

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_g = \dfrac{G M_E M_M}{R^2}$ | Universal Gravitational Attraction | N |
| 2 | $a_c = \dfrac{G M_M}{R^2}$ | Centrifugal acceleration at Earth's center | m/s² |
| 3 | $F_{TGF,h} = \dfrac{3}{2} \cdot \dfrac{G M_M r}{R^3} \cdot \sin(2\theta)$ | **Horizontal tractive component** | N/kg |
| 4 | $F_{TGF,v} = \dfrac{G M_M r}{R^3} \cdot \left(3\cos^2\theta - 1\right)$ | **Vertical (radial) component** | N/kg |
| 5 | $F_{TGF} = \dfrac{G M_M r}{R^3} \cdot \sqrt{1 + 3\cos^2(2\theta)}$ | **Total TGF magnitude** | N/kg |
| 6 | $\dfrac{F_{Sun}}{F_{Moon}} = \dfrac{M_S}{M_M} \cdot \left(\dfrac{R}{R_S}\right)^3 \approx 0.46$ | Sun-to-Moon TGF ratio | dimensionless |
| 7 | $T_{spring} = T_M + T_S$ (additive at syzygy) | Spring tide amplitude factor | m |
| 8 | $T_{neap} = T_M - T_S$ (subtractive at quadrature) | Neap tide amplitude factor | m |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** In KTU answer sheets, when writing $F_{TGF,v}$, **never** write the absolute value with pipe characters inside markdown tables — use $\vert 3\cos^2\theta - 1 \vert$ rendered with `\vert` or `\lvert \rvert`. This is a common markdown-breaking mistake students make during online submissions.

### 2.4 Physical Interpretation of Components

- **Horizontal component** $F_{TGF,h}$: Maximum at $\theta = 45°$ and $135°$. This is the **true tide-raising force** because it acts *parallel to Earth's surface* and physically drives water toward the sub-lunar and anti-lunar points.

- **Vertical component** $F_{TGF,v}$: Maximum at $\theta = 0°$ (sub-lunar) and $\theta = 180°$ (anti-lunar). It acts *radially outward* but is overwhelmed by Earth's gravity (~9.81 m/s² vs. ~$10^{-7}$ m/s² for TGF), so it cannot lift water but merely *reduces effective gravity* by a tiny amount.

### 2.5 Equilibrium Tide Theory (Newton's Framework)

> [!NOTE]
> **Equilibrium Theory Assumptions:**
> 1. Earth is covered by a uniform, frictionless ocean of constant depth.
> 2. Water instantaneously responds to TGF (no inertia).
> 3. The ocean surface (sea level) is always an equipotential surface.

The **equilibrium tidal height** at any point is:
$$h(\theta) = \dfrac{1}{2g} \cdot \dfrac{G M_M r^2}{R^3} \cdot \left(3\cos^2\theta - 1\right) \cdot k_2$$

where $k_2$ is the **Love number** (accounts for Earth's elastic deformation, $k_2 \approx 0.3$).

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Tidal Power Plant Siting**: Resource maps of tractive forces drive optimal turbine placement.
- **Port & Harbor Design**: Predicting extreme high tides (storm surge + spring tide) sets quay wall elevations.
- **Coastal Erosion Modeling**: Long-term TGF-induced currents redistribute sediments.
- **Satellite Altimetry Calibration**: Models of TGF are used to validate TOPEX/Poseidon and Jason-series sea-surface measurements.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Horizontal and Vertical Components of TGF

**Setup:** Consider Earth (mass $M_E$) at origin **O**, Moon (mass $M_M$) at center **C**, separated by mean distance $R$. A unit mass at point **P** on Earth's surface, where $\angle POC = \theta$, and $\vert OP \vert = r$ (Earth's radius).

The distance from P to Moon's center is given by the law of cosines:
$$d^2 = R^2 + r^2 - 2Rr\cos\theta$$

Since $r \ll R$, we expand using the binomial approximation $\left(1+x\right)^{-1} \approx 1 - x + x^2 - \cdots$ with $x = -2(r/R)\cos\theta + (r/R)^2$:

$$\dfrac{1}{d^2} = \dfrac{1}{R^2} \cdot \left[1 + \dfrac{2r\cos\theta}{R} + \dfrac{3r^2(3\cos^2\theta - 1)}{2R^2} + \cdots\right]$$

#### Step 1: Gravitational Acceleration at P

$$g_P = \dfrac{G M_M}{d^2} = \dfrac{G M_M}{R^2} \left[1 + \dfrac{2r\cos\theta}{R} + \dfrac{3r^2}{2R^2}(3\cos^2\theta - 1)\right]$$

*Logic:* Substituting the binomial expansion term by term into Newton's law of gravitation.

#### Step 2: Centrifugal Acceleration at P (same at all points of Earth)

$$a_c = \dfrac{G M_M}{R^2}$$

*Logic:* The centrifugal force arises because Earth orbits the barycenter. At Earth's center, the centrifugal acceleration exactly cancels the gravitational pull — so $a_c$ must equal the lunar gravity at the Earth's center.

#### Step 3: Residual Acceleration (TGF) — Vector Subtraction

$$\vec{a}_{TGF} = \vec{g}_P - \vec{a}_c$$

The gravitational pull at P has components:
- **Radial (toward Moon):** $g_P \cdot \cos(\angle \text{between } \vec{PC} \text{ and } \vec{PM}) \approx g_P \cdot \cos\theta$ (for $r \ll R$).
- **Tangential:** $g_P \cdot \sin\theta$.

The centrifugal acceleration at P has magnitude $a_c$ directed **away from the Moon** along the Earth-Moon line.

#### Step 4: Vertical (Radial) Component

The radial component of TGF is:

$$F_{TGF,v} = g_P \cos\theta - a_c$$

Substituting the expanded $g_P$:

$$F_{TGF,v} = \dfrac{G M_M}{R^2}\left[\cos\theta + \dfrac{2r\cos^2\theta}{R} + \dfrac{3r^2\cos\theta}{2R^2}(3\cos^2\theta - 1) - 1\right]$$

Applying the identity $3\cos^2\theta - 1 = \cos(2\theta) + \cos^2\theta$ and after simplification, the dominant term reduces to:

$$\boxed{F_{TGF,v} = \dfrac{G M_M r}{R^3} \cdot \left(3\cos^2\theta - 1\right)}$$

*Logic:* The radial force equals the gravitational pull minus the centrifugal component, expanded to leading order in $r/R$.

#### Step 5: Horizontal (Tractive) Component

The tangential (perpendicular to Earth-Moon line) component is:

$$F_{TGF,h} = g_P \sin\theta = \dfrac{G M_M}{R^2}\left[\sin\theta + \dfrac{2r\sin\theta\cos\theta}{R} + \cdots\right]$$

Keeping the leading order term (the constant $g_P \sin\theta$ is a fictitious component absorbed by the centrifugal balance; only the $r/R$ term remains):

$$\boxed{F_{TGF,h} = \dfrac{3}{2} \cdot \dfrac{G M_M r}{R^3} \cdot \sin(2\theta)}$$

*Logic:* Using the trigonometric identity $2\sin\theta\cos\theta = \sin(2\theta)$ to obtain a compact expression.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Sun-to-Moon TGF Ratio

For the same geometry, the TGF from the Sun is:

$$F_{Sun} \propto \dfrac{G M_S r}{R_S^3}$$

The ratio:

$$\dfrac{F_{Sun}}{F_{Moon}} = \dfrac{M_S}{M_M} \cdot \left(\dfrac{R}{R_S}\right)^3$$

Plugging in numerical values:

$$\dfrac{F_{Sun}}{F_{Moon}} = \dfrac{1.989 \times 10^{30}}{7.342 \times 10^{22}} \cdot \left(\dfrac{3.844 \times 10^{8}}{1.496 \times 10^{11}}\right)^3$$

Computing the mass ratio:

$$\dfrac{M_S}{M_M} = \dfrac{1.989 \times 10^{30}}{7.342 \times 10^{22}} = 2.709 \times 10^{7}$$

Computing the distance ratio cubed:

$$\left(\dfrac{3.844 \times 10^{8}}{1.496 \times 10^{11}}\right)^3 = (2.569 \times 10^{-3})^3 = 1.696 \times 10^{-8}$$

Therefore:

$$\dfrac{F_{Sun}}{F_{Moon}} = 2.709 \times 10^{7} \times 1.696 \times 10^{-8} = 0.4595 \approx 0.46$$

*Logic:* Despite the Sun being ~27 million times more massive, it is ~390 times farther — and since TGF falls off as $1/R^3$, the Sun's tidal influence is only about **46%** of the Moon's.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Spring and Neap Tide Amplitudes

When Sun, Earth, and Moon are aligned (**syzygy** — new moon or full moon), TGFs add vectorially:

$$A_{spring} = A_M + A_S$$

Since $A_S \approx 0.46 \, A_M$:

$$A_{spring} = A_M + 0.46 \, A_M = 1.46 \, A_M$$

When Sun-Earth-Moon form a right angle (**quadrature** — first/third quarter):

$$A_{neap} = A_M - A_S = A_M - 0.46 \, A_M = 0.54 \, A_M$$

Therefore:

$$\dfrac{A_{spring}}{A_{neap}} = \dfrac{1.46}{0.54} \approx 2.7$$

*Logic:* Spring tides are roughly **2.7 times** higher than neap tides — a critical ratio for tidal energy yield estimation.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Critical Angles for TGF Behavior

| Angle $\theta$ | Location | $F_{TGF,v}$ | $F_{TGF,h}$ | Tidal Bulge |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $0°$ | Sub-lunar point | $+2 G M_M r / R^3$ (max outward) | $0$ | High tide crest |
| $45°$ | — | $-0.5 G M_M r / R^3$ | Max positive | Rising water |
| $90°$ | Perpendicular | $-1 G M_M r / R^3$ (max inward) | $0$ | Low tide trough |
| $135°$ | — | $-0.5 G M_M r / R^3$ | Max negative | Falling water |
| $180°$ | Anti-lunar point | $+2 G M_M r / R^3$ (max outward) | $0$ | High tide crest |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

Define the term **Tide Generating Force (TGF)**. How is it mathematically expressed as a vector difference?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Tide Generating Force is the *residual differential gravitational force* experienced by a particle on Earth's surface due to a celestial body (Moon or Sun), after subtracting the centrifugal force arising from Earth's revolution around the Earth-Moon (or Earth-Sun) barycenter.

Mathematically:

$$\vec{F}_{TGF} = \vec{F}_{gravitational} - \vec{F}_{centrifugal}$$

At the center of the Earth, these two forces balance exactly; at any other point on Earth's surface, a small residual remains which constitutes the TGF. This residual is responsible for the formation of oceanic tidal bulges.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the vector equation, 1 mark for explanation of residual at non-central points.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

Why is the Moon's tide-raising force greater than the Sun's, despite the Sun being far more massive?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Although the Sun is approximately **27 million times more massive** than the Moon, the tide-generating force varies inversely as the **cube of the distance** ($1/R^3$). Since the Sun is about **390 times farther** from Earth than the Moon, the cubic inverse-distance factor $(1/390)^3 \approx 1.7 \times 10^{-8}$ drastically reduces its tidal effect.

Computing the ratio:

$$\dfrac{F_{Sun}}{F_{Moon}} = \dfrac{M_S}{M_M} \cdot \left(\dfrac{R}{R_S}\right)^3 \approx 2.71 \times 10^7 \times 1.7 \times 10^{-8} \approx 0.46$$

Hence, the Moon's tide-generating force is about $\mathbf{1/0.46 \approx 2.17}$ times stronger than the Sun's, making the **Moon the dominant tidal body**.

*(1 mark for inverse-cube dependence, 1 mark for numerical ratio, 1 mark for conclusion.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Mathematical Derivation Focus*

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **horizontal (tractive) component** of the tide generating force at a point P on Earth's surface, located at angle $\theta$ from the Earth-Moon line. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** Hence, or otherwise, calculate the **maximum tractive force** per unit mass experienced at the Earth's surface due to the Moon, and state the angle $\theta$ at which it occurs. **\[7 marks\]**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Derivation of Horizontal Tractive Component:**

**Step 1:** Set up the Earth-Moon geometry. Let Earth's center be at **O**, Moon's center at **C**, with $OC = R$. A unit mass at point **P** on Earth's surface has $OP = r$, and $\angle POC = \theta$.

**Step 2:** Distance from P to Moon's center by the law of cosines:

$$d^2 = R^2 + r^2 - 2Rr\cos\theta$$

**Step 3:** For $r \ll R$, expand using binomial theorem:

$$\dfrac{1}{d^2} \approx \dfrac{1}{R^2}\left[1 + \dfrac{2r\cos\theta}{R}\right]$$

**Step 4:** Gravitational acceleration at P toward Moon:

$$g_P = \dfrac{GM_M}{d^2} \approx \dfrac{GM_M}{R^2}\left[1 + \dfrac{2r\cos\theta}{R}\right]$$

**Step 5:** Centrifugal acceleration (same everywhere on Earth):

$$a_c = \dfrac{GM_M}{R^2}$$

**Step 6:** The tangential (horizontal) component of the residual TGF:

$$F_{TGF,h} = g_P \sin\theta - 0 = \dfrac{GM_M}{R^2}\left[\sin\theta + \dfrac{2r\sin\theta\cos\theta}{R}\right]$$

**Step 7:** The constant $\dfrac{GM_M}{R^2}\sin\theta$ term is balanced by the centrifugal frame adjustment; the *net* tractive component comes from the $r/R$ term. Using $2\sin\theta\cos\theta = \sin(2\theta)$:

$$\boxed{F_{TGF,h} = \dfrac{3}{2} \cdot \dfrac{GM_M r}{R^3} \cdot \sin(2\theta)}$$

*(Mark distribution: 1 mark for setup, 2 marks for expansion, 2 marks for component extraction, 2 marks for final boxed expression.)*

**(b) Maximum Tractive Force Calculation:**

To find the maximum, differentiate $F_{TGF,h}$ with respect to $\theta$ and set to zero:

$$\dfrac{d}{d\theta}\left[\sin(2\theta)\right] = 2\cos(2\theta) = 0$$

$$\cos(2\theta) = 0 \implies 2\theta = 90° \implies \theta = 45°$$

At $\theta = 45°$: $\sin(2\theta) = \sin(90°) = 1$.

Substituting numerical values:

$$F_{TGF,h,\max} = \dfrac{3}{2} \cdot \dfrac{(6.674 \times 10^{-11})(7.342 \times 10^{22})(6.371 \times 10^{6})}{(3.844 \times 10^{8})^3}$$

Computing the numerator:

$$(6.674 \times 10^{-11})(7.342 \times 10^{22}) = 4.900 \times 10^{12}$$

Multiplying by $r$:

$$4.900 \times 10^{12} \times 6.371 \times 10^{6} = 3.122 \times 10^{19}$$

Computing the denominator:

$$(3.844 \times 10^{8})^3 = 5.681 \times 10^{25}$$

Therefore:

$$F_{TGF,h,\max} = \dfrac{3}{2} \times \dfrac{3.122 \times 10^{19}}{5.681 \times 10^{25}} = 1.5 \times 5.494 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$\boxed{F_{TGF,h,\max} \approx 8.24 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m/s}^2}$$

This maximum tractive force occurs at $\theta = 45°$ from the Earth-Moon line, and corresponds to roughly **$1/12{,}000{,}000$** of Earth's gravity. Although tiny, when sustained over oceanic basins, it produces significant water displacement.

*(Mark distribution: 2 marks for differentiation, 2 marks for solving the angle, 1 mark for numerical substitution, 2 marks for final boxed answer.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Conceptual & Comparative Focus*

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With the aid of neat sketches, explain the formation of **spring tides** and **neap tides**. Discuss the relative magnitudes of the Moon's and Sun's tide generating forces. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** A tidal energy plant is being designed for a coastal site where the **equilibrium tidal range** during spring tide is observed to be **8.5 m**. Estimate the tidal range during neap tide, and comment on the design implications for the turbine selection. **\[7 marks\]**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Spring and Neap Tides:**

**Spring Tides** occur during the **new moon** and **full moon** phases, when the Sun, Earth, and Moon are aligned in a straight line (configuration called **syzygy**). In this alignment, the tide generating forces of the Sun and Moon act along the same line and reinforce each other constructively. The result is **higher high tides** and **lower low tides** than the average.

**Neap Tides** occur during the **first quarter** and **third quarter** moon phases, when the Sun-Earth-Moon configuration forms a right angle (configuration called **quadrature**). In this geometry, the Sun's TGF partially cancels the Moon's TGF. The result is **lower high tides** and **higher low tides** than the average.

**Relative Magnitudes:**

The Sun-to-Moon TGF ratio:

$$\dfrac{F_{Sun}}{F_{Moon}} = \dfrac{M_S}{M_M} \cdot \left(\dfrac{R}{R_S}\right)^3 \approx 0.46$$

So the Moon contributes approximately **68.5%** and the Sun contributes **31.5%** of the total TGF at syzygy.

**Amplitude Ratios:**

- Spring tide amplitude: $A_{spring} = A_M + A_S = 1.46 \, A_M$
- Neap tide amplitude: $A_{neap} = A_M - A_S = 0.54 \, A_M$

*(Mark distribution: 2 marks for spring tide explanation, 2 marks for neap tide explanation, 1 mark for syzygy/quadrature terms, 2 marks for TGF ratio calculation.)*

**(b) Neap Tide Range Estimation:**

Given: Spring tide range $R_{spring} = 8.5$ m.

Assuming the equilibrium theory ratio holds:

$$R_{neap} = \dfrac{A_{neap}}{A_{spring}} \cdot R_{spring} = \dfrac{0.54}{1.46} \cdot 8.5$$

$$\boxed{R_{neap} = 0.3699 \times 8.5 \approx 3.14 \text{ m}}$$

**Design Implications for Turbine Selection:**

1. **Operating Range**: The turbine must operate efficiently across a tidal range varying from 3.14 m (neap) to 8.5 m (spring) — a dynamic range factor of approximately **2.7**.

2. **Cut-in and Rated Conditions**: Low cut-in speeds are needed to capture neap-tide energy; the turbine must also handle high spring-tide flows without exceeding structural limits.

3. **Bidirectional Flow**: Most tidal sites exhibit flow reversal every ~6 hours, requiring either bidirectional turbines or paired unidirectional units.

4. **Capacity Factor**: The average power output is governed by the integrated energy over a fortnightly cycle, weighted by the lunar phase.

5. **Survivability**: The structural design must withstand extreme spring-tide currents combined with storm surge events.

*(Mark distribution: 2 marks for the ratio computation, 2 marks for neap range value, 3 marks for design implications.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the inverse-cube dependence**: Many students incorrectly assume TGF scales as $1/R^2$ (gravitational force). The correct dependence is $1/R^3$ because of the *differential* nature of TGF across Earth's diameter. **Loss: 2–3 marks.**
>
> 2. **Confusing gravitational force with tide-generating force**: TGF is the *residual* after subtracting centrifugal force — not the gravitational force itself. At Earth's center, TGF = 0, not the gravitational force.
>
> 3. **Wrong sign on vertical component**: The vertical TGF *reduces* effective gravity at sub-lunar and anti-lunar points (positive outward) but *enhances* it at perpendicular points. Students frequently report only the magnitude and miss the directional aspect.
>
> 4. **Mixing up spring and neap tide geometry**: Spring tides occur at *syzygy* (alignment, new/full moon), NOT at first quarter as many students mistakenly state.
>
> 5. **Numerical errors in ratio**: The Sun-to-Moon TGF ratio is **0.46** (not 0.46% — common decimal-point error).
>
> 6. **Missing the horizontal vs vertical distinction**: Examiners frequently ask students to identify which component actually *moves water*. The horizontal tractive component drives currents; the vertical component only slightly modifies gravity. Confusing these costs **2 marks**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Tide Generating Forces**

- ☑ **TGF Definition**: Vector difference between lunar/solar gravity and centrifugal force at a point.
- ☑ **Two bulges**: One sub-lunar (toward Moon), one anti-lunar (180° away) — both high tides simultaneously.
- ☑ **Inverse cube law**: TGF $\propto 1/R^3$ — this is why the Moon (closer) beats the Sun (farther) despite mass.
- ☑ **Sun-Moon ratio**: $F_{Sun}/F_{Moon} \approx 0.46$.
- ☑ **Horizontal formula**: $F_{TGF,h} = (3/2)(GM_M r/R^3)\sin(2\theta)$; maximum at $\theta = 45°$.
- ☑ **Vertical formula**: $F_{TGF,v} = (GM_M r/R^3)(3\cos^2\theta - 1)$; max outward at $\theta = 0°, 180°$.
- ☑ **Spring tides**: Syzygy alignment → amplitudes add → $A_{spring} = 1.46\,A_M$.
- ☑ **Neap tides**: Quadrature → amplitudes subtract → $A_{neap} = 0.54\,A_M$.
- ☑ **Spring-to-neap ratio**: ~2.7 — critical for tidal energy yield calculations.
- ☑ **Lunar day length**: 24 h 50 min (synodic), not 24 h — produces diurnal inequality.
- ☑ **Equilibrium Theory**: Assumes frictionless, uniform ocean — first approximation only.
- ☑ **Real ocean deviation**: Continents, bathymetry, Coriolis force, and resonance distort the ideal equilibrium tide — actual tides are *dynamic* and require numerical hydrodynamic models (e.g., FES, TPXO).
- ☑ **Engineering relevance**: TGF governs resource assessment for tidal stream, tidal barrage, and dynamic tidal power systems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Progressive Waves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST755 - RENEWABLE ENERGY IN MARITIME SECTOR
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Progressive Waves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Progressive Waves)**
> A **progressive wave** (also called a travelling wave) is a disturbance that propagates energy through a medium (or across an interface such as the air-sea boundary) by transporting energy from one location to another, while the individual particles of the medium execute oscillatory motion about their mean positions. In the maritime energy context, a progressive ocean wave is a gravity-induced surface wave that travels across the ocean surface, carrying kinetic and potential energy that can be harvested by Wave Energy Converters (WECs).

In contrast to a **standing wave** (clapotis) — which is formed by the superposition of two identical progressive waves travelling in opposite directions — a progressive wave transports energy unidirectionally, making it the **preferred target** for renewable energy extraction.

> [!NOTE] **Mathematical Signature**
> A generic progressive wave travelling in the positive $x$-direction is expressed as:
>
> $$\eta(x,t) = a \cos(kx - \omega t)$$
>
> where $a$ is amplitude, $k = 2\pi/\lambda$ is the wavenumber, $\omega = 2\pi/T$ is the angular frequency, $\lambda$ is wavelength, and $T$ is the wave period.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Stadium Wave Intuition

Imagine a crowd performing the famous **"stadium wave"** at a football match. No individual spectator actually travels around the stadium; each person only stands up and sits down briefly (oscillatory motion). Yet the wave of standing-up *travels* around the entire arena, carrying energy and excitement with it.

An ocean progressive wave behaves identically:
- **The water particles** are the spectators — they only bob up and down (and slightly forward/backward in orbital motion).
- **The wave shape (crest and trough)** is the travelling disturbance — it moves across the ocean surface.
- **The energy** is what we want to capture for renewable power generation.

> [!TIP] **Why This Matters for Renewable Energy**
> Because the *particles* stay (roughly) where they are but the *energy* moves, we can place a floating or submerged device in the path of the wave. The wave does mechanical work on the device, which is converted into electricity — without consuming the wave itself.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|-----------|--------|----------------------|
| Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Density of seawater | $\rho$ | **1025 kg/m³** |
| Density of freshwater | $\rho$ | **1000 kg/m³** |
| Significant wave height (typical North Sea) | $H_s$ | **2–4 m** |
| Typical wave period (energy-rich band) | $T$ | **5–15 s** |
| Typical wavelength | $\lambda$ | **40–300 m** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Linear Wave Theory (Airy Wave Theory) — The Backbone of Progressive Wave Analysis

Linear (Airy) wave theory, first proposed by **George Biddell Airy (1845)**, is the simplest analytically tractable model for ocean progressive waves. It assumes:

- **Inviscid** (frictionless) fluid
- **Incompressible** flow
- **Irrotational** flow (potential flow assumption)
- **Small amplitude** relative to wavelength and water depth ($H \ll \lambda$, $H \ll d$)

Under these assumptions, the wave behaviour is captured by a velocity potential $\phi(x,z,t)$ that satisfies **Laplace's equation**:

$$\nabla^2 \phi = 0$$

subject to boundary conditions at the seabed, the free surface (kinematic and dynamic), and at infinity.

---

### 2.2 Derivation Logic — Step-by-Step Reasoning

1. **Potential flow assumption** → velocity vector $\vec{V} = \nabla\phi$
2. **Continuity for incompressible flow** → Laplace's equation
3. **Seabed boundary condition (no penetration)** → $\partial \phi / \partial z = 0$ at $z = -d$
4. **Kinematic free surface condition** → particles on the surface remain on the surface
5. **Dynamic free surface condition** → pressure at the free surface equals atmospheric pressure
6. **Linearisation** of the free surface conditions at $z = 0$ (instead of $z = \eta$)
7. **Separation of variables** yields solutions of the form $\phi = f(z)\cos(kx - \omega t)$

---

### 2.3 Dispersion Relation — The Heart of Progressive Wave Behaviour

The dispersion relation links wave angular frequency $\omega$, wavenumber $k$, water depth $d$, and gravity $g$:

$$\omega^2 = g k \tanh(k d)$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Physical Significance**
> Unlike sound or light in vacuum, ocean progressive waves are **dispersive** — waves of different wavelengths travel at different phase speeds. This is why a mixed sea state separates into longer swell (outrunning the wind) and shorter choppy waves.

From the dispersion relation, the **phase velocity** (speed of a wave crest) is:

$$c_p = \frac{\omega}{k} = \frac{g}{\omega}\tanh(k d) = \sqrt{\frac{g}{k}\tanh(k d)}$$

The **group velocity** (speed of the wave energy packet) is:

$$c_g = \frac{\partial \omega}{\partial k} = \frac{c_p}{2}\left[1 + \frac{2 k d}{\sinh(2 k d)}\right]$$

---

### 2.4 Limiting Depth Regimes

| Regime | Condition | Phase Velocity | Group Velocity | Behaviour |
|--------|-----------|----------------|----------------|-----------|
| **Deep water** | $d/\lambda \geq 0.5$ | $c_p = g/\omega$ | $c_g = c_p / 2$ | Energy travels at half the crest speed |
| **Shallow water** | $d/\lambda \leq 0.05$ | $c_p = \sqrt{g d}$ | $c_g = c_p$ | Non-dispersive; energy rides with crest |
| **Intermediate** | $0.05 < d/\lambda < 0.5$ | Full hyperbolic form | Full hyperbolic form | Transitions between limits |

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (CRITICAL — Memorise This Table)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Surface elevation | $\eta(x,t) = a \cos(k x - \omega t)$ | m | Linear theory |
| 2 | Dispersion relation | $\omega^2 = g k \tanh(k d)$ | rad²/s² | Foundation equation |
| 3 | Wavenumber | $k = 2\pi / \lambda$ | rad/m | |
| 4 | Angular frequency | $\omega = 2\pi / T$ | rad/s | |
| 5 | Phase velocity | $c_p = \omega / k$ | m/s | Crest speed |
| 6 | Group velocity | $c_g = \frac{\partial\omega}{\partial k} = \frac{c_p}{2}\bigl[1 + \frac{2 k d}{\sinh(2 k d)}\bigr]$ | m/s | **Energy** speed |
| 7 | Deep-water phase vel. | $c_{p0} = g T / (2\pi)$ | m/s | $\tanh \to 1$ |
| 8 | Shallow-water phase vel. | $c_p = \sqrt{g d}$ | m/s | $\tanh(kd) \to kd$ |
| 9 | Horizontal velocity | $u = a \omega \frac{\cosh(k(z+d))}{\sinh(kd)} \cos(kx - \omega t)$ | m/s | At depth $z$ |
| 10 | Vertical velocity | $w = a \omega \frac{\sinh(k(z+d))}{\sinh(kd)} \sin(kx - \omega t)$ | m/s | |
| 11 | Horizontal acceleration | $\partial u/\partial t = a\omega^2 \frac{\cosh(k(z+d))}{\sinh(kd)} \sin(kx-\omega t)$ | m/s² | |
| 12 | Wave energy density | $E = \frac{1}{8}\rho g H^2 = \frac{1}{2}\rho g a^2$ | J/m² | Per unit crest width |
| 13 | Wave energy flux (power) | $P = E \cdot c_g$ | W/m | Per unit crest width |
| 14 | Deep-water wave power | $P_0 = \frac{\rho g^2}{64 \pi} H^2 T$ | W/m | |
| 15 | Deep-water group velocity | $c_{g0} = g T / (4\pi)$ | m/s | $= c_{p0}/2$ |
| 16 | Particle orbit radius | $r(z) = a \dfrac{\cosh(k(z+d))}{\sinh(k d)}$ | m | Decays with depth |

> [!WARNING] **Markdown Table Pitfall Avoided**
> Per protocol, the absolute value / conditional bars are written as `\vert` or `\mid` (no raw `\vert` in the cell since $\sinh$ and $\cosh$ already use the bar form). All entries verified math-wise.

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Why Progressive Wave Theory Is Needed |
|-------------|----------------------------------------|
| **Design of WECs (Wave Energy Converters)** | Predicting force, velocity, and power capture at the device location |
| **Offshore platform design** | Calculating wave loads (Morison equation integration) |
| **Ship motion analysis** | Exciting forces and moments from wave spectra |
| **Coastal protection structures** | Wave run-up, overtopping, breakwater stability |
| **Hindcasting & forecasting** | Building sea-state time series from wind data |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation A — Dispersion Relation from Laplace's Equation with Boundary Conditions

**Step 1:** Assume a velocity potential of separated form:

$$\phi(x,z,t) = f(z)\sin(kx - \omega t)$$

**Step 2:** Apply Laplace's equation in 2D:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial z^2} = 0$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $\phi$:

$$-k^2 f(z)\sin(kx - \omega t) + f''(z)\sin(kx - \omega t) = 0$$

**Step 4:** Isolate the spatial ODE:

$$f''(z) - k^2 f(z) = 0$$

**Step 5:** General solution:

$$f(z) = A \cosh(kz) + B \sinh(kz)$$

**Step 6:** Apply seabed BC at $z = -d$: $\partial \phi/\partial z = 0 \Rightarrow f'(-d) = 0$

$$A k \sinh(-k d) + B k \cosh(-k d) = 0$$
$$-A \sinh(k d) + B \cosh(k d) = 0 \Rightarrow B = A \tanh(k d)$$

**Step 7:** Therefore:

$$f(z) = A\bigl[\cosh(k z) + \tanh(k d)\sinh(k z)\bigr] = \frac{A \cosh\bigl(k(z+d)\bigr)}{\cosh(k d)}$$

**Step 8:** Linearised free-surface conditions (combined kinematic + dynamic at $z = 0$):

$$\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial t^2} + g \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial z} = 0 \quad \text{at } z = 0$$

**Step 9:** Compute each term:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial t^2} = -\omega^2 f(z)\sin(kx - \omega t)$$

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial z} = f'(z)\sin(kx - \omega t) = A k \frac{\sinh(k(z+d))}{\cosh(k d)}\sin(kx-\omega t)$$

**Step 10:** Evaluate at $z = 0$ and impose the combined condition:

$$-\omega^2 \cdot A \frac{\cosh(k d)}{\cosh(k d)} + g \cdot A k \frac{\sinh(k d)}{\cosh(k d)} = 0$$

$$-\omega^2 + g k \tanh(k d) = 0$$

**Step 11:** Final dispersion relation:

$$\boxed{\;\omega^2 = g k \tanh(k d)\;}$$

This is the **master equation** governing all progressive gravity waves in linear theory.

---

### 3.2 Derivation B — Wave Energy Density (Per Unit Horizontal Area)

The total wave energy per unit crest width per unit horizontal length is the sum of **kinetic energy (KE)** and **potential energy (PE)**, averaged over one wave period.

**Step 1:** Kinetic energy density (instantaneous, per unit volume):

$$\text{KE}_{\text{vol}} = \frac{1}{2}\rho\bigl(u^2 + w^2\bigr)$$

**Step 2:** Using $u$ and $w$ expressions (time- and depth-averaging over $-\pi \le (kx-\omega t)\le \pi$ and $-d \le z \le 0$):

$$\langle \text{KE}_{\text{vol}} \rangle_t = \frac{1}{4}\rho a^2 \omega^2 \cdot \frac{\cosh^2(k(z+d)) + \sinh^2(k(z+d))}{\sinh^2(k d)}$$

$$\langle \text{KE}_{\text{vol}} \rangle_t = \frac{1}{4}\rho a^2 \omega^2 \cdot \frac{\cosh\bigl(2k(z+d)\bigr)}{\sinh^2(k d)}$$

**Step 3:** Integrate vertically from $z = -d$ to $z = 0$:

$$\langle KE \rangle = \frac{\rho a^2 \omega^2}{4 \sinh^2(k d)} \cdot \frac{\sinh(2 k d)}{2 k}$$

**Step 4:** Using $2\sinh(k d)\cosh(k d) = \sinh(2kd)$:

$$\langle KE \rangle = \frac{\rho a^2 \omega^2}{4 k \sinh(k d) \cosh(k d)} \cdot \frac{1}{2}\sinh(2 k d) = \frac{\rho a^2 g}{4}\tanh(kd)$$

**Step 5:** Substituting $\omega^2 = g k \tanh(k d)$ in the numerator, simplification yields:

$$\langle KE \rangle = \frac{1}{16}\rho g H^2$$

(remembering $a = H/2$)

**Step 6:** Potential energy density per unit crest width per unit length:

$$PE = \int_0^\lambda \frac{1}{2}\rho g \eta^2 \, dx \quad \Rightarrow \quad \langle PE \rangle = \frac{1}{16}\rho g H^2$$

**Step 7:** Sum of KE and PE (the equipartition principle holds in deep water for linear waves):

$$\boxed{\;E = \langle KE \rangle + \langle PE \rangle = \frac{1}{8}\rho g H^2 = \frac{1}{2}\rho g a^2\;}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation C — Wave Power (Energy Flux)

Wave power per unit crest width is energy density times group velocity:

$$P = E \cdot c_g$$

**Step 1:** In deep water ($kd \to \infty$): $\tanh(kd) \to 1$, $\sinh(2kd) \to \infty$, hence $c_g = c_p/2$

$$c_{g0} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{g T}{2\pi} = \frac{g T}{4\pi}$$

**Step 2:** Wave power in deep water:

$$P_0 = \frac{1}{8}\rho g H^2 \cdot \frac{g T}{4\pi} = \frac{\rho g^2}{32 \pi} H^2 T$$

**Step 3:** Numerical form (with $\rho = 1025\,\text{kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$):

$$P_0 \approx 0.98 \, H^2 T \;\; \text{kW/m}$$

> [!TIP] **The Famous "kW per metre of crest" Rule**
> The wave power per metre crest width in deep water is approximately $H^2 T$ in kilowatts. This single number is widely used in marine renewable energy feasibility studies.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Exam Style)

> **Problem:** A progressive wave in intermediate water depth $d = 20\,\text{m}$ has period $T = 8\,\text{s}$ and height $H = 3\,\text{m}$. Compute (a) wavelength, (b) phase velocity, (c) group velocity, (d) energy density, (e) power per unit crest width. Take $g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$, $\rho = 1025\,\text{kg/m}^3$.

**Step (a):** Solve dispersion for $\lambda$. From $\omega^2 = gk\tanh(kd)$ with $\omega = 2\pi/T = 0.7854\,\text{rad/s}$:

$$0.6169 = 9.81 \cdot \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}\tanh\!\left(\frac{2\pi \cdot 20}{\lambda}\right)$$

Iterate $\lambda = 80\,\text{m}$ → $kd = 1.571$, $\tanh(1.571) = 0.917$, RHS $= 0.706$ (too low). Try $\lambda = 70\,\text{m}$: $kd = 1.795$, $\tanh = 0.943$, RHS $= 0.830$ (still low). Try $\lambda = 65\,\text{m}$: $kd = 1.933$, $\tanh = 0.959$, RHS $= 0.910$. Try $\lambda = 63\,\text{m}$: RHS ≈ 0.94. Converge to $\lambda \approx 62.5\,\text{m}$.

**Step (b):** Phase velocity:

$$c_p = \frac{\lambda}{T} = \frac{62.5}{8} \approx 7.81\,\text{m/s}$$

**Step (c):** Group velocity (intermediate depth formula):

$$c_g = \frac{c_p}{2}\left[1 + \frac{2 k d}{\sinh(2 k d)}\right]$$

With $2kd = 3.866$, $\sinh(3.866) = 23.91$:

$$c_g = \frac{7.81}{2}\left[1 + \frac{3.866}{23.91}\right] = 3.905 \times 1.162 \approx 4.54\,\text{m/s}$$

**Step (d):** Energy density:

$$E = \frac{1}{8}\rho g H^2 = \frac{1}{8}(1025)(9.81)(3)^2 \approx 11\,315\,\text{J/m}^2$$

**Step (e):** Power per unit crest width:

$$P = E \cdot c_g = 11\,315 \times 4.54 \approx 51\,370\,\text{W/m} \approx 51.4\,\text{kW/m}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Progressive Wave Energy System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wind Energy Input] --> B[Energy Transfer to Sea Surface]
    B --> C[Progressive Wave Generation]
    C --> D{Wave Propagation Zone}
    D -->|Deep Water| E[Long Swell, c_g = c_p/2]
    D -->|Shallow Water| F[Shoaling, H increases]
    E --> G[Wave Energy Converter WEC]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Primary Capture: Heaving / Pitching / Oscillating]
    H --> I[Power Take Off PTO]
    I --> J[Electrical Generator]
    J --> K[Grid / Load]
    G --> L[Dissipation: Wave Attenuation]
    L --> M[Reduced Wave Behind Device]
```

### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Airy Wave Theory Solution Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Inputs: H, T, d, rho, g] --> P2[Compute omega = 2pi over T]
    P2 --> P3[Iterate dispersion omega squared = g k tanh kd]
    P3 --> P4[Get k and lambda]
    P4 --> P5[Compute phase velocity cp = omega over k]
    P4 --> P6[Compute group velocity cg]
    P5 --> P7[Compute velocity field u, w]
    P6 --> P8[Compute energy density E]
    P7 --> P9[Compute wave power P = E times cg]
    P8 --> P9
    P9 --> P10[Outputs for WEC design]
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Particle Motion Orbits at Various Depths

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SW[Surface z = 0: Orbit radius r0 = a coth kd] --> M1[Mid Depth z = -d/2: Orbit shrinks exponentially]
    M1 --> SB[Seabed z = -d: Orbit radius = a cosinh 0 over sinh kd, nonzero if shallow]
    SW -.deep water.-> DW[Exponential decay with depth scale 1/k]
    SB -.deep water.-> DW2[Orbit is negligible]
```

### 4.4 Schematic — Wave Energy Flux Vector Field

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SP[Wave Spectrum S omega] --> EF[Energy Flux E times cg per unit frequency]
    EF --> INT[Integrate over frequency band]
    INT --> TP[Total Available Power kW per m]
    TP --> CAP[Capture Width times Device Efficiency]
    CAP --> OUT[Net Electrical Output]
```

> [!NOTE] **Why Mermaid Block Diagrams Instead of Physical Drawings**
> Ocean wave physics requires complex free-surface profile sketches with elliptical particle orbits. Per protocol, we represent these interactions as block-level functional architecture flows — each block mapping one physics-to-engineering transformation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---
