**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

Define a **progressive wave**. How does it differ from a standing wave in terms of energy transport?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A progressive wave is a disturbance that travels through a medium, transporting energy from one location to another while the medium particles oscillate about their equilibrium positions. The surface elevation is given by $\eta(x,t) = a\cos(kx - \omega t)$.

In contrast, a standing wave (clapotis) is formed by the superposition of two identical progressive waves travelling in opposite directions; its surface elevation is $\eta = 2a\cos(kx)\cos(\omega t)$. The progressive wave carries energy at the group velocity $c_g$ in the direction of propagation, whereas a standing wave transports **no net energy** — energy oscillates between kinetic and potential forms within fixed spatial nodes and antinodes.

**Mark split:** Definition (1) + surface expression (1) + energy transport distinction (1).

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

State the **dispersion relation** for linear progressive gravity waves and explain its significance.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$\omega^2 = g k \tanh(k d)$$

The dispersion relation links the angular frequency $\omega$, wavenumber $k$, water depth $d$, and gravitational acceleration $g$. Its significance is that ocean progressive waves are **dispersive**: different frequencies propagate at different phase velocities. Consequently, a wave group spreads out as it travels, with longer-period swell outrunning shorter-period components. This determines how wave energy is distributed spatially — a critical input for siting Wave Energy Converters (WECs).

**Mark split:** Relation (1) + dispersive meaning (1) + renewable-energy implication (1).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the dispersion relation $\omega^2 = gk\tanh(kd)$ for a linear progressive wave in water of finite depth $d$. State clearly the boundary conditions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A deep-water progressive wave has height $H = 2.5\,\text{m}$ and period $T = 9\,\text{s}$. Calculate (i) wavelength, (ii) phase velocity, (iii) group velocity, (iv) energy density per unit area, and (v) wave power per metre crest width. Take $g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$ and $\rho_w = 1025\,\text{kg/m}^3$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Assume irrotational, incompressible, inviscid flow → velocity potential $\phi$ satisfies Laplace's equation:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial z^2} = 0$$

Seek separated solution $\phi = f(z)\sin(kx - \omega t)$. Substituting yields $f''(z) - k^2 f(z) = 0$, with general solution $f(z) = A\cosh(kz) + B\sinh(kz)$.

**Seabed BC:** $\partial\phi/\partial z = 0$ at $z = -d$ gives $B = A\tanh(kd)$, so:

$$\phi = \frac{A\cosh\bigl(k(z+d)\bigr)}{\cosh(kd)}\sin(kx - \omega t)$$

**Combined linearised free-surface BC** at $z = 0$:

$$\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial t^2} + g\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial z} = 0$$

Substituting and evaluating at $z=0$:

$$-\omega^2 A + gk A\tanh(kd) = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \boxed{\omega^2 = gk\tanh(kd)}$$

**Mark split:** Laplace eq. (1) + separated form (1) + seabed BC (1) + free-surface BC (1) + final algebra (2) + box & state BCs (1).

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

Deep water → $\tanh(kd) \to 1$, so $\omega^2 = gk$.

(i) $k = \omega^2 / g = (2\pi/9)^2 / 9.81 = 0.04874 / 9.81 \times 4\pi^2$ wait — compute cleanly:

$\omega = 2\pi/T = 2\pi/9 = 0.6981\,\text{rad/s}$
$k = \omega^2/g = (0.6981)^2/9.81 = 0.04968\,\text{rad/m}$
$\lambda = 2\pi/k = 2\pi/0.04968 \approx 126.5\,\text{m}$

(ii) $c_p = \omega/k = 0.6981/0.04968 \approx 14.05\,\text{m/s}$

(iii) $c_g = c_p/2 \approx 7.03\,\text{m/s}$ (deep-water rule)

(iv) $E = \tfrac{1}{8}\rho g H^2 = 0.125 \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times (2.5)^2 \approx 7857\,\text{J/m}^2$

(v) $P = E \cdot c_g = 7857 \times 7.03 \approx 55\,235\,\text{W/m} \approx 55.2\,\text{kW/m}$

**Mark split:** (i) 1.5, (ii) 1, (iii) 1, (iv) 1.5, (v) 2.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Starting from the velocity potential of a linear progressive wave, derive expressions for the horizontal and vertical velocity components at any depth $z$. Explain how particle orbits vary with depth in deep and shallow water. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the aid of a sketch (block diagram acceptable), describe a typical Oscillating Water Column (OWC) wave energy converter. Discuss how the principles of progressive wave theory inform its design and siting. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Velocity components (7 marks):**

From $\phi = \dfrac{A\cosh\bigl(k(z+d)\bigr)}{\cosh(kd)}\sin(kx - \omega t)$,

$$u = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = A k \frac{\cosh\bigl(k(z+d)\bigr)}{\cosh(kd)}\cos(kx - \omega t)$$

$$w = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial z} = A k \frac{\sinh\bigl(k(z+d)\bigr)}{\cosh(kd)}\sin(kx - \omega t)$$

Absorbing the constant $Ak/\cosh(kd)$ into an amplitude $a\omega/\sinh(kd)$ (since $A = a\omega/k \cdot \cosh(kd)/\sinh(kd)$ from the surface matching):

$$u = \frac{a\omega\cosh(k(z+d))}{\sinh(kd)}\cos(kx-\omega t)$$

$$w = \frac{a\omega\sinh(k(z+d))}{\sinh(kd)}\sin(kx-\omega t)$$

Eliminating time gives an **ellipse** in $(x,z)$:

$$\frac{u^2}{\bigl(a\omega\cosh(k(z+d))/\sinh(kd)\bigr)^2} + \frac{w^2}{\bigl(a\omega\sinh(k(z+d))/\sinh(kd)\bigr)^2} = 1$$

**Deep water** ($kd \to \infty$): $\sinh, \cosh \to \tfrac{1}{2}e^{kz}$ → orbits are **circles** of radius $a e^{kz}$, decaying exponentially with depth.

**Shallow water** ($kd \to 0$): $\cosh \to 1$, $\sinh \to k(z+d)$ → orbits are **flat ellipses**, nearly horizontal, of nearly uniform size from surface to seabed.

**Mark split:** Velocity potentials (2) + final expressions (2) + orbit geometry (2) + deep vs. shallow contrast (1).

**(b) OWC description (7 marks):**

An Oscillating Water Column is a partially submerged hollow chamber with an opening below the mean waterline. As a progressive wave passes:

1. The wave forces water up into the chamber, compressing the trapped air above.
2. The pressurised air escapes through a turbine (commonly a Wells turbine) coupled to a generator.
3. As the wave trough arrives, the air is drawn back in, again spinning the turbine.
4. The Wells turbine rotates in the **same direction** for both airflows, simplifying the PTO.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    OWC1[Incident Progressive Wave Crest] --> OWC2[Water Rises in Chamber]
    OWC2 --> OWC3[Air Compressed Above Water Column]
    OWC3 --> OWC4[Air Expelled Through Wells Turbine]
    OWC4 --> OWC5[Generator Produces Power]
    OWC6[Wave Trough Arrives] --> OWC7[Water Falls in Chamber]
    OWC7 --> OWC8[Air Drawn In Through Turbine]
    OWC8 --> OWC5
```

**Design/siting from progressive wave theory:**

- **Chamber natural period** should match dominant wave period for resonance → use $T$ from local wave climate.
- **Chamber draft** set using $d$ (intermediate depth) and $\lambda$ — a chamber too deep wastes construction material.
- **Front opening width** chosen so that wave run-up inside is maximised, governed by shoaling and wave-height transformation near the structure.
- **Survivability** uses $H_{\max}$ estimates from deep-water wave statistics.

**Mark split:** Working principle (3) + block diagram (1) + progressive-wave-theory design link (3).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Common Mark-Loss Areas (Examiner Observations)**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\tanh(kd)$ factor** when writing the dispersion relation — many students write $\omega^2 = gk$ regardless of depth. The general form must always be stated.
> 2. **Mixing up phase velocity and group velocity** — energy travels at $c_g$, not $c_p$. Power calculations require $c_g$.
> 3. **Using freshwater density** instead of seawater density $\rho = 1025\,\text{kg/m}^3$ in maritime problems — automatic 0.5 mark deduction.
> 4. **Not stating boundary conditions explicitly** in derivations — at least two BCs (seabed + free surface) must be named.
> 5. **Confusing amplitude $a$ with height $H$** — remember $H = 2a$, so $E = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho g a^2 = \tfrac{1}{8}\rho g H^2$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Progressive wave** = travelling disturbance carrying energy unidirectionally; particles oscillate but do not translate.
- **Airy/linear wave theory** assumes small amplitude and potential flow; gives $\eta(x,t) = a\cos(kx-\omega t)$.
- **Dispersion relation:** $\omega^2 = gk\tanh(kd)$ is the master equation — *must be memorised*.
- **Phase velocity** $c_p = \omega/k$ is the speed of an individual crest.
- **Group velocity** $c_g = \partial\omega/\partial k = \tfrac{c_p}{2}\bigl[1 + \tfrac{2kd}{\sinh(2kd)}\bigr]$ is the **speed of energy**.
- **Deep water:** $c_g = c_p/2$, orbits are circles decaying as $e^{kz}$.
- **Shallow water:** $c_g = c_p = \sqrt{gd}$, orbits are flat ellipses.
- **Energy density:** $E = \tfrac{1}{8}\rho g H^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho g a^2$ — KE = PE on average (equipartition).
- **Wave power:** $P = E \cdot c_g$; deep-water form $P_0 \approx 0.98\,H^2 T$ kW/m.
- **KE/PE equipartition** holds exactly for linear waves; not true in shallow water due to non-linearities.
- **Morison-type loading** on WEC structures uses the velocity and acceleration field derived from the dispersion relation.
- **WEC siting** favours intermediate-to-deep water with high $H^2 T$ values — west coasts of Ireland, Scotland, Portugal, Chile, Australia.
- **For exam:** always specify whether your answer is per unit crest width (W/m) or per unit surface area (W/m²).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cotidal Charts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST755 - RENEWABLE ENERGY IN MARITIME SECTOR
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cotidal Charts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Cotidal Chart** (also called a **Tidal Chart** or **Tidal Atlas**) is a specialized nautical and oceanographic cartographic representation that simultaneously displays two fundamental tidal parameters across a marine region or ocean basin:

1. **Cotidal Lines** — Isolines (contour lines) that connect all geographic points experiencing **High Water (HW)** at the same instant of time, referenced to a chosen principal tidal constituent (most commonly the semi-diurnal **M₂ constituent**, with period **T = 12.42 hours**).
2. **Corange Lines** — Isolines that connect all geographic points having the **same tidal range** (i.e., the same difference between successive High Water and Low Water levels), representing the **amplitude (a)** of the tidal wave at each location.

The intersection point of corange lines where the tidal range approaches **zero**, around which cotidal lines radiate in a spiral or wheel-spoke pattern, is called an **Amphidromic Point** (Greek: *amphi* = on both sides; *dromos* = running). The complete rotating pattern is termed an **Amphidromic System**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Cotidal charts are essential for the assessment, site selection, and feasibility analysis of **tidal stream energy, tidal barrage, and tidal lagoon** projects because they directly reveal the spatial distribution of tidal energy density, phase coherence, and standing wave behavior.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Spinning Top in a Bathtub" Intuition

Imagine dropping a large pebble at the centre of a **square swimming pool**. The water surface initially ripples outward in concentric circles (like dropping a stone in a pond). However, if you now **slowly rotate the pool clockwise**, something remarkable happens — the straight radial wavefronts bend, and the crests appear to **revolve around the centre** while the centre itself remains almost perfectly still.

The ocean basin behaves in an identical way:

- The **"pebble drop"** represents the astronomical tide-generating force of the Moon and Sun.
- The **"pool rotation"** represents the **Coriolis Force** (f = 2Ω sin φ), which deflects moving water to the right in the Northern Hemisphere and to the left in the Southern Hemisphere.
- The **"still centre"** is the **Amphidromic Point**, where tidal range is zero but tidal currents are strongest (energy is conserved by transforming potential energy into kinetic energy).
- The **"rotating crests"** are the **Cotidal Lines**, numbered in **lunar hours (0h, 1h, 2h … 12h)** after the Moon's transit of the Greenwich Meridian.

Thus, a cotidal chart is essentially a **"snapshot movie"** of the ocean's tidal bulge as it races around the basin periphery while pivoting around an amphidromic pivot.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Semi-diurnal lunar period (M₂) | $T_{M_2}$ | **12.4206** | hours |
| Semi-diurnal solar period (S₂) | $T_{S_2}$ | **12.0000** | hours |
| Diurnal lunar period (K₁) | $T_{K_1}$ | **23.9345** | hours |
| Gravitational acceleration | g | **9.81** | m s⁻² |
| Mean ocean depth | H | **4000** | m |
| Earth's angular velocity | Ω | $7.2921 \times 10^{-5}$ | rad s⁻¹ |
| Mean Earth radius | R | $6.371 \times 10^{6}$ | m |
| Seawater density | $\rho$ | **1025** | kg m⁻³ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why M₂ Dominates Cotidal Charts:** Although the Sun's gravity is ~177 times stronger than the Moon's, the *gradient* (tractive force) depends on $M/r^3$. Because the Moon is ~389 times closer, its tide-generating force is **2.16 times larger** than the Sun's. The principal lunar semi-diurnal constituent **M₂** therefore accounts for roughly **68%** of the total tidal variance in most ocean basins.

### 1.4 The Three Primary Components of a Cotidal Chart

A complete cotidal chart contains three superimposed information layers:

1. **Phase Information** — Cotidal lines (solid or dashed) labelled in lunar hours or degrees of phase lag ($0°$ to $360°$).
2. **Amplitude Information** — Corange lines (dotted), labelled in centimetres or metres of tidal amplitude.
3. **Rotational Sense** — An arrow or hatched symbol indicating the **direction of rotation** of the tidal wave around each amphidromic point (counter-clockwise in the **Northern Hemisphere**, clockwise in the **Southern Hemisphere**).

### 1.5 Strategic Relevance to Maritime Renewable Energy

In the context of the **PENST755 — Renewable Energy in Maritime Sector** syllabus, cotidal charts are the **master planning tool** for tidal energy engineers. They provide:

- **Resource mapping**: Identifying zones of maximum tidal amplitude (e.g., Bay of Fundy ~16 m, Bristol Channel ~14 m) for barrage / lagoon siting.
- **Phase identification**: Locating coastal points with opposing tidal phases for **tidal fence** or **dynamic tidal power** schemes.
- **Current prediction**: Indirect estimation of tidal stream velocities through the shallow-water continuity relationship $v \approx (a \omega L)/h$, where $L$ is the basin length scale.
- **Grid synchronisation**: Two basins separated by $\Delta \phi = 180°$ allow **continuous generation** if coupled via a tidal lagoon system.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Tides as Forced Long Waves

Oceanic tides are mathematically modelled as **shallow-water long waves** (wavelength $\lambda \gg$ ocean depth $h$) that are **forced** by the astronomical tide-generating potential of the Moon and Sun and **modified** by:

- **Coriolis force** (due to Earth's rotation),
- **Bathymetry** (variable ocean depth),
- **Basin geometry** (coastline shape and continental boundaries),
- **Resonance** (when natural basin period ≈ tidal forcing period).

The governing linearised equations for tide dynamics in a rotating, shallow ocean are:

$$\frac{\partial \mathbf{u}}{\partial t} + f \,\hat{\mathbf{k}} \times \mathbf{u} = -g\,\nabla \eta + \mathbf{F}_{\text{tidal}}$$

$$\frac{\partial \eta}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (h\,\mathbf{u}) = 0$$

where $\mathbf{u} = (u, v)$ is the depth-averaged horizontal velocity, $\eta(x,y,t)$ is the free-surface elevation, $h(x,y)$ is the undisturbed depth, and $f = 2\Omega \sin\phi$ is the **Coriolis parameter**.

### 2.2 Why Ocean Basins Develop Amphidromic Systems — The Physics

A purely gravitational tidal bulge (the **equilibrium tide**) would travel westward at ~400 m s⁻¹ following the Moon. However, in a real ocean basin:

1. The **Kelvin wave mechanism** dominates: the tidal wave propagates as a **boundary-trapped Kelvin wave**, hugging the coast with its crest decaying exponentially offshore over a characteristic length scale called the **Rossby Radius of Deformation**:

$$R_d = \frac{\sqrt{g\,h}}{f}$$

1. For typical mid-latitudes ($\phi = 45°$, $f \approx 1.03 \times 10^{-4}$ rad s⁻¹) and $h = 4000$ m:

$$R_d = \frac{\sqrt{9.81 \times 4000}}{1.03 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 1930 \text{ km}$$

2. When a Kelvin wave reflects off a coastline, the **incoming** and **reflected** Kelvin waves interfere constructively, producing a **standing wave rotated by Coriolis**. This is the **Poincaré–Kelvin hybrid**, and the locus of zero amplitude is the **amphidromic point**.

3. The phase of the resulting wave is mathematically given by:

$$\Phi(x,y) = \omega\,t - k\,x + \text{(rotation term due to Coriolis)}$$

The cotidal lines are simply curves of **constant $\Phi$** — hence their characteristic **spiral / hyperbolic** appearance around the amphidromic point.

### 2.3 Rules Governing Cotidal Charts (Examiner-Favourite Facts)

| Rule | Statement | Hemisphere |
|---|---|---|
| **Rotation Rule 1** | Cotidal lines rotate **counter-clockwise** around amphidromic points. | Northern |
| **Rotation Rule 2** | Cotidal lines rotate **clockwise** around amphidromic points. | Southern |
| **Equator Rule** | At $\phi = 0°$, $f = 0$ and Coriolis vanishes → cotidal lines become **straight meridional lines** (no amphidromic systems form at the equator). | Equatorial |
| **Energy Rule** | Near an amphidromic point, tidal **range → 0** but tidal **current → maximum** (potential energy converts to kinetic energy). | Universal |
| **Phase Rule** | Phase increases uniformly around an amphidromic point over one tidal cycle ($T_{M_2}$). | Universal |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Table for KTU Numerical Problems on Cotidal Charts**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi}{T}$ | Angular frequency of tidal constituent | M₂: $\omega = 1.405 \times 10^{-4}$ rad s⁻¹ |
| 2 | $c = \sqrt{g\,h}$ | Phase speed of long gravity wave | Deep ocean: ~198 m s⁻¹ |
| 3 | $\lambda = c \cdot T = T\sqrt{g\,h}$ | Tidal wavelength | M₂ in 4000 m: ~8856 km |
| 4 | $R_d = \dfrac{\sqrt{g\,h}}{f} = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | Rossby deformation radius | Determines amphidromic spacing |
| 5 | $f = 2\Omega\sin\phi$ | Coriolis parameter | At 45°N: $1.03 \times 10^{-4}$ s⁻¹ |
| 6 | $N = \dfrac{\text{Basin Width}}{\pi R_d}$ | Number of amphidromic systems | $N \approx 1, 2, 3, ...$ |
| 7 | $P_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{\rho\,g\,A\,a^2}{2\,T}$ | Average tidal power from a barrage | Used in resource assessment |
| 8 | $P_{\text{peak}} = \rho\,g\,A\,a^2\,\omega$ | Peak tidal power flux | Maximum extractable |
| 9 | $E_{\text{cycle}} = \dfrac{\rho\,g\,A\,a^2}{2}$ | Energy per tidal cycle per unit area | Lagoon design |
| 10 | $\Delta \phi = \omega \cdot \Delta t = \dfrac{2\pi\,\Delta t}{T}$ | Phase difference between two sites | Determines continuous generation potential |
| 11 | $v \approx \dfrac{a\,\omega\,L}{h}$ | Tidal stream velocity (continuity) | Tidal turbine sizing |
| 12 | $V_t = \dfrac{3}{2}\,g\,R^2 \cdot \dfrac{M}{r^3}\left(\cos^2\theta - \dfrac{1}{3}\right)$ | Equilibrium tidal potential | Astronomical forcing strength |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Formulas 7, 8, and 9 are **direct design equations** used by tidal energy engineers. The amplitude $a$ used in these formulas is read **directly from the corange lines** of a cotidal chart, making the chart an indispensable pre-engineering tool.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Cotidal charts underpin three major maritime renewable energy decisions:

1. **Tidal Stream Farms** (e.g., MeyGen, Scotland): Charts identify phase-coherent nodes where tidal currents are unidirectional and strong (typically 2–4 m s⁻¹).
2. **Tidal Barrages** (e.g., La Rance, France; Sihwa, South Korea): Charts identify coastal inlets with maximum corange values to maximise head differential.
3. **Tidal Lagoons** (e.g., Swansea Bay, UK): Charts inform the optimal wall orientation and sluice timing to maximise energy yield while minimising environmental impact.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Phase Speed and Wavelength of the Tidal Kelvin Wave

**Objective:** Starting from the linearised shallow-water equations, derive the phase speed of the long-wave tidal disturbance that forms the basis of cotidal chart patterns.

**Step 1 — Set up the linearised shallow-water equations** (neglecting advection, viscosity, and tidal forcing initially):

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} - f\,v = -g\,\frac{\partial \eta}{\partial x}$$

$$\frac{\partial v}{\partial t} + f\,u = -g\,\frac{\partial \eta}{\partial y}$$

$$\frac{\partial \eta}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (h\,u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (h\,v)}{\partial y} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Assume a straight coastline at $y = 0$, with the ocean occupying $y > 0$, and seek a Kelvin wave solution travelling along the coast in the $x$-direction.**

**Step 3 — Apply the boundary condition $v = 0$ at $y = 0$ (no flow through the coast).** This reduces the system and requires the pressure gradient to be balanced by Coriolis deflection.

**Step 4 — Assume a wave-form ansatz:**

$$u(x,y,t) = U(y)\,e^{i(kx - \omega t)}$$

$$\eta(x,y,t) = H(y)\,e^{i(kx - \omega t)}$$

with $v = 0$ everywhere.

**Step 5 — Substitute into the $x$-momentum equation:**

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = -i\omega\,U\,e^{i(kx-\omega t)} = -g\,(ik)\,H\,e^{i(kx-\omega t)}$$

Cancelling the exponential:

$$-i\omega\,U = -i\,g\,k\,H \quad \Rightarrow \quad U = \frac{g\,k}{\omega}\,H$$

**Step 6 — Substitute into the continuity equation:**

$$\frac{\partial \eta}{\partial t} + h\,\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad -i\omega\,H + h\,(ik)\,U = 0$$

$$-i\omega\,H + i\,k\,h \cdot \frac{g\,k}{\omega}\,H = 0$$

**Step 7 — Solve for the dispersion relation:**

$$-i\omega + \frac{i\,k^2\,g\,h}{\omega} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega^2 = k^2\,g\,h$$

$$\boxed{\omega = k\sqrt{g\,h}}$$

**Step 8 — Identify the phase speed:**

$$c = \frac{\omega}{k} = \sqrt{g\,h}$$

This is the **fundamental long-wave phase speed**, valid for any tidal constituent.

**Step 9 — Compute the wavelength of the M₂ tide in a typical deep ocean ($h = 4000$ m):**

$$\lambda_{M_2} = c \cdot T_{M_2} = \sqrt{9.81 \times 4000} \times (12.4206 \times 3600)$$

$$= 198.06 \text{ m s}^{-1} \times 44714 \text{ s} \approx 8.856 \times 10^{6} \text{ m} = 8856 \text{ km}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The Atlantic Ocean basin is approximately **4500 km wide**. Since $\lambda \approx 2 \times \text{basin width}$, the basin can support approximately **one full amphidromic system** in the east-west direction — and indeed the well-known **North Atlantic M₂ amphidromic system** centred near $52°\text{W}, 30°\text{N}$ is the textbook example.

### 3.2 Derivation II — Rossby Radius of Deformation and Amphidromic Spacing

**Objective:** Determine the characteristic offshore length scale over which a Kelvin wave's amplitude decays — this directly determines the spacing of cotidal lines.

**Step 1 — From the $y$-momentum equation** (with $v = 0$):

$$0 + f\,u = -g\,\frac{\partial \eta}{\partial y}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $u = \dfrac{g\,k}{\omega}\,H\,e^{i(kx-\omega t)}$ and the assumed offshore structure $H(y) = H_0\,e^{-\alpha y}$:**

$$f \cdot \frac{g\,k}{\omega}\,H_0\,e^{-\alpha y} = -g\,H_0\,(-\alpha)\,e^{-\alpha y}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify:**

$$f \cdot \frac{g\,k}{\omega} = g\,\alpha \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha = \frac{f\,k}{\omega} = \frac{f}{c}$$

**Step 4 — Recognise that $1/\alpha$ is the **Rossby Radius of Deformation**:**

$$R_d = \frac{1}{\alpha} = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{\sqrt{g\,h}}{f}$$

For $h = 4000$ m and $f = 1.03 \times 10^{-4}$ s⁻¹:

$$R_d = \frac{198.06}{1.03 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 1.923 \times 10^{6} \text{ m} \approx 1923 \text{ km}$$

**Step 5 — Amphidromic system count** in a basin of width $W$:

$$N = \frac{W}{\pi R_d}$$

For the Atlantic ($W \approx 4500$ km):

$$N = \frac{4500}{\pi \times 1923} \approx 0.745$$

Since $N$ rounds to the nearest integer, the Atlantic supports **one full amphidromic system**, consistent with observation.

### 3.3 Derivation III — Average Tidal Power from Cotidal Chart Data

**Objective:** Using amplitude $a$ (read from corange lines) and basin area $A$, derive the average extractable tidal power.

**Step 1 — The potential energy stored in a tidal column of height $a$ above mean sea level:**

$$E_{\text{potential}} = \int_0^a \rho\,g\,z\,A\,dz = \frac{1}{2}\,\rho\,g\,A\,a^2$$

**Step 2 — For a sinusoidal semi-diurnal tide, the time-averaged potential energy over a cycle is half the peak value:**

$$\langle E_{\text{potential}} \rangle = \frac{1}{4}\,\rho\,g\,A\,a^2$$

**Step 3 — The kinetic energy contribution** (using long-wave velocity $v = \dfrac{a\,\omega\,\lambda}{2\pi h}$) equals the time-averaged potential energy by **equipartition**:

$$\langle E_{\text{kinetic}} \rangle = \frac{1}{4}\,\rho\,g\,A\,a^2$$

**Step 4 — Total available tidal energy per cycle:**

$$\langle E_{\text{total}} \rangle = \frac{1}{2}\,\rho\,g\,A\,a^2$$

**Step 5 — Average power** (energy divided by tidal period $T$):

$$P_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\rho\,g\,A\,a^2}{2\,T}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical example:** $a = 4$ m, $A = 1$ km² = $10^6$ m², $T = 12.42$ h = 44712 s:

$$P_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1025 \times 9.81 \times 10^6 \times 4^2}{2 \times 44712}$$

$$= \frac{1025 \times 9.81 \times 16 \times 10^6}{89424}$$

$$= \frac{1.609 \times 10^{11}}{8.942 \times 10^4} \approx 1.80 \times 10^{6} \text{ W} = 1.80 \text{ MW}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is **theoretical gross** power. Practical extraction efficiency is limited to **~20–30%** by the **Betz limit analogy** for tidal systems, yielding **0.36–0.54 MW** actual generation from a 1 km² site with 4 m amplitude.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Cotidal Chart Information by Ocean Basin (M₂ Constituent)

| Ocean Basin | Approx. Width (km) | Mean Depth (m) | Rossby Radius $R_d$ (km) | # Amphidromic Points | Max Corange (m) | Direction of Rotation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| North Atlantic | 4500 | 4000 | 1923 | **1** (central) | 1.0 | Counter-clockwise |
| South Atlantic | 5000 | 4000 | 2200 | **1** (off Brazil) | 0.6 | Clockwise |
| North Pacific | 8000 | 4500 | 2800 | **2** | 0.8 | Counter-clockwise |
| Indian Ocean | 7500 | 3700 | 2400 | **2** | 0.9 | Counter-clockwise |
| Arctic Ocean | 3500 | 1000 | 900 | **2** | 0.3 | Counter-clockwise |
| Southern Ocean | Circumpolar | 4500 | 3100 | Continuous belt | 1.2 | Clockwise (eastward propagation) |

### 3.5 Derivation IV — Phase Lag Between Two Coastal Points

**Objective:** Compute the phase difference (in hours and degrees) between two sites separated by distance $L$ along the cotidal line.

**Step 1 — The cotidal line rotates at angular speed $\omega$ around the amphidromic point.**

**Step 2 — If two sites are separated by arc length $s$ along a circle of radius $R$ around the amphidromic point:**

$$\Delta\phi = \frac{s}{R}\,\omega\,T = \frac{2\pi\,s}{R} \text{ radians}$$

**Step 3 — In time units:**

$$\Delta t = \frac{\Delta\phi}{\omega} = \frac{s}{R}\,T$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example:** Two sites in the Bristol Channel separated by $s = 100$ km from an amphidromic point at $R = 200$ km:

$$\Delta t = \frac{100}{200} \times 12.42 = 6.21 \text{ hours}$$

This corresponds to **180° phase difference**, allowing **continuous 24-hour generation** if both sites are developed in a coupled tidal lagoon scheme.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Reading and Interpreting a Cotidal Chart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Geographic Region of Interest] --> B[Acquire Bathymetric Grid h x,y]
    B --> C[Compute Coriolis Parameter f = 2 Omega sin phi]
    C --> D[Select Tidal Constituent e.g. M2]
    D --> E[Apply Shallow Water Equations on Grid]
    E --> F[Numerical Solver: 2D Tidal Model]
    F --> G[Extract Phase Field Phi x,y,t]
    F --> H[Extract Amplitude Field a x,y]
    G --> I[Plot Cotidal Lines: Lines of Constant Phase]
    H --> J[Plot Corange Lines: Lines of Constant Amplitude]
    I --> K[Locate Amphidromic Points: a approx 0]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Identify Energy Resource Zones]
    L --> M{Engineering Decision}
    M --> M1[Tidal Stream Farm: High Velocity Nodes]
    M --> M2[Tidal Barrage: High Corange Coastlines]
    M --> M3[Tidal Lagoon: Phase Coherent Bays]
    M1 --> N[Site Optimisation and Yield Forecast]
    M2 --> N
    M3 --> N
```

### 4.2 Schematic — Anatomy of an Amphidromic System in the Northern Hemisphere

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AMPHI[Amphidromic System: Northern Hemisphere M2 Tide]
        direction TB
        P1[Amphidromic Point: a approx 0, Currents MAX] --> R1
        R1[Cotidal Line 0h] --> R2
        R2[Cotidal Line 1h] --> R3
        R3[Cotidal Line 3h] --> R4
        R4[Cotidal Line 6h] --> R5
        R5[Cotidal Line 9h] --> R6
        R6[Cotidal Line 12h] --> P1
    end
    R1 -.->|Corange: 0.2 m| C1[Outer Corange: 0.2 m]
    R3 -.->|Corange: 0.5 m| C2[Mid Corange: 0.5 m]
    R5 -.->|Corange: 0.8 m| C3[Inner Corange: 0.8 m]
    P1 --> DIR[Rotation Direction: Counter Clockwise in NH]
    P1 --> COAST[Coastline Boundary: Energy Reflects Here]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Cotidal Chart Inputs and Maritime Energy Outputs

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INPUT[Cotidal Chart Data Set] --> P1[Phase Lines in Lunar Hours]
    INPUT --> P2[Corange Lines in Metres]
    INPUT --> P3[Amphidromic Point Coordinates]
    P1 --> OUT1[Phase Difference Delta phi between Sites]
    P2 --> OUT2[Amplitude a at Site]
    P3 --> OUT3[Distance R from Amphidromic Point]
    OUT1 --> CALC[Engineering Calculator]
    OUT2 --> CALC
    OUT3 --> CALC
    CALC --> RES1[Power: P = rho g A a squared over 2T]
    CALC --> RES2[Velocity: v approx a omega L over h]
    CALC --> RES3[Phase Lag: Delta t = s over R times T]
    RES1 --> DEC[Investment Decision for Tidal Project]
    RES2 --> DEC
    RES3 --> DEC
```

### 4.4 Schematic — Cross-Basin Energy Exchange via Phase Coupling

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BASIN_A[Basin A: Phase 0 degrees at Peak]
        A1[Tidal Lagoon A1] --> A2[Turbine Generator Set A]
    end
    subgraph BASIN_B[Basin B: Phase 180 degrees at Peak]
        B1[Tidal Lagoon B1] --> B2[Turbine Generator Set B]
    end
    A2 --> TRANSMISSION[Submarine HVDC Transmission Link]
    B2 --> TRANSMISSION
    TRANSMISSION --> GRID[Continuous 24 hour Grid Supply]
    A2 -.->|Generates at 0h, 6h, 12h, 18h| GRID
    B2 -.->|Generates at 3h, 9h, 15h, 21h| GRID
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define a **cotidal chart**. What information do the **cotidal lines** and **corange lines** convey? Why is such a chart essential for tidal energy resource assessment?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A cotidal chart is a specialised map of an ocean basin or coastal region that simultaneously displays:

- **Cotidal lines** (1 mark): Curves joining points experiencing **high water at the same instant**, expressed in lunar hours or degrees of phase referenced to a principal tidal constituent (typically M₂).
- **Corange lines** (1 mark): Curves joining points of equal **tidal range** (twice the amplitude), indicating the spatial distribution of tidal wave energy.
- **Engineering relevance** (1 mark): Cotidal charts allow engineers to identify locations of maximum tidal amplitude for barrage/lagoon siting, locate amphidromic points (high kinetic energy / tidal stream zones), and assess phase differences between sites for continuous generation schemes — making them indispensable for tidal renewable energy project planning.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** What is an **amphidromic point**? State **two key features** of cotidal lines around it in the Northern Hemisphere.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An amphidromic point is a **virtual pivot** in an ocean basin where the tidal range (corange) reduces to **zero**, but tidal current velocities are maximum because potential energy is converted into kinetic energy (1 mark).

**Two key features in the Northern Hemisphere** (2 marks):

1. Cotidal lines radiate outward from the amphidromic point like the spokes of a wheel.
2. They rotate **counter-clockwise** around the amphidromic point as the tide progresses.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Amphidromic Systems and Tidal Power Estimation**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches how **amphidromic systems** are formed in ocean basins. Discuss the role of the **Coriolis force** and the **Rossby radius of deformation** in determining the number of amphidromic points in a basin. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A coastal site in the Bay of Fundy has a tidal amplitude of **a = 8 m** as read from the corange lines of the regional cotidal chart. The proposed tidal barrage has a **usable basin area A = 5 km²**. Compute (i) the **total energy per tidal cycle**, (ii) the **average power**, and (iii) the **practical extractable power** assuming a turbine–generator efficiency of **η = 25%**. Use **M₂ tidal period T = 12.42 h**, **ρ = 1025 kg m⁻³**, **g = 9.81 m s⁻²**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Amphidromic System Formation (7 marks):**

An amphidromic system is a **rotating pattern of cotidal and corange lines** that arises when a forced tidal Kelvin wave interacts with the boundaries of an enclosed or semi-enclosed ocean basin (1 mark). The physical sequence is:

1. The astronomical tide-generating force of the Moon and Sun excites a **long gravity wave** in the ocean that propagates as a **coastal Kelvin wave**, hugging the continental boundaries with the offshore amplitude decaying over the **Rossby radius of deformation** $R_d = \sqrt{gh}/f$ (2 marks).
2. When the Kelvin wave reflects off an opposing coastline, the **incoming** and **reflected** waves superpose. Because the Coriolis force deflects moving water to the right (Northern Hemisphere), the resulting interference pattern does **not** produce a stationary standing wave — instead it produces a **rotating wave pattern** (1 mark).
3. The locus where the wave amplitudes cancel is the **amphidromic point**, characterised by zero tidal range but maximum tidal current. Cotidal lines radiate outward from this point and increase in phase lag by one full tidal period ($T_{M_2} = 12.42$ h) per complete circuit (1 mark).
4. The **number of amphidromic systems** in a basin of width $W$ is approximately $N \approx W/(\pi R_d)$. The Atlantic basin (~4500 km wide, $R_d \approx 1900$ km) supports **one** dominant amphidromic system centred near $52°\text{W}, 30°\text{N}$ (1 mark).
5. Cotidal lines rotate **counter-clockwise** in the Northern Hemisphere because the Coriolis parameter $f = 2\Omega\sin\phi > 0$ deflects the wave's phase velocity vector in that sense (1 mark).

**(b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $a = 8$ m, $A = 5 \text{ km}^2 = 5 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^2$, $T = 12.42 \text{ h} = 44712 \text{ s}$, $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg m}^{-3}$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m s}^{-2}$, $\eta = 0.25$.

**(i) Total energy per tidal cycle** (1 mark):

$$E_{\text{cycle}} = \frac{1}{2}\,\rho\,g\,A\,a^2 = \frac{1}{2}(1025)(9.81)(5 \times 10^6)(8^2)$$

$$= 0.5 \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 5 \times 10^6 \times 64$$

$$= 1.609 \times 10^{12} \text{ J} = 1609 \text{ GJ}$$

**(ii) Average power** (1 mark):

$$P_{\text{avg}} = \frac{E_{\text{cycle}}}{T} = \frac{1.609 \times 10^{12}}{44712} \approx 3.598 \times 10^{7} \text{ W} \approx 36.0 \text{ MW}$$

**(iii) Practical extractable power** (1 mark):

$$P_{\text{practical}} = \eta \times P_{\text{avg}} = 0.25 \times 36.0 = 9.0 \text{ MW}$$

**Common working notes for full marks** (4 marks distributed across substitutions, unit conversions, and correct identification of efficiency):

- Always convert $A$ from km² to m² by multiplying by $10^6$.
- Always convert $T$ from hours to seconds by multiplying by 3600.
- Recognise that the formula $E = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho g A a^2$ already incorporates the sinusoidal time-average factor of 1/2 (do not divide again).
- Final answer must include correct SI units (J for energy, W for power).

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Cotidal Charts and Phase Engineering**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper, Module 2]**

**(a)** Define **cotidal lines**, **corange lines**, and **amphidromic points**. With the help of a labelled diagram, describe the structure of an amphidromic system in the Northern Hemisphere and explain why the cotidal lines rotate **counter-clockwise**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two proposed tidal lagoon sites **X** and **Y** lie along the same cotidal chart, at distances of **50 km** and **200 km**, respectively, from the nearest amphidromic point. (i) Calculate the **phase difference** $\Delta\phi$ (in degrees and hours) between the two sites for the M₂ tide. (ii) If both lagoons are coupled to the grid via a common HVDC link, comment qualitatively on the **continuity of supply** and the **engineering implications**. (iii) Estimate the **tidal stream velocity** near the amphidromic point assuming a depth $h = 30$ m and a basin length scale $L = 50$ km. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Definitions and Structure (7 marks):**

- **Cotidal lines** (1 mark): Lines joining all points that experience **high water at the same instant**. Phase is measured in lunar hours from the Moon's transit of the Greenwich Meridian, or in degrees (one lunar hour = 30°).
- **Corange lines** (1 mark): Lines joining points of equal **tidal range** (i.e., equal amplitude × 2). They form concentric closed curves around an amphidromic point.
- **Amphidromic point** (1 mark): A **nodal point of zero tidal range** from which cotidal lines radiate like wheel spokes. Tidal currents are maximum here.
- **Structure** (2 marks): An amphidromic system in the Northern Hemisphere consists of (i) a central amphidromic point, (ii) cotidal lines spaced at equal time intervals around it, (iii) corange lines forming concentric patterns with amplitude increasing outward, and (iv) a tangential arrow indicating **counter-clockwise rotation** of phase.
- **Reason for counter-clockwise rotation** (2 marks): In the Northern Hemisphere, the Coriolis force deflects moving water to the **right** of its direction of motion. A Kelvin wave travelling northward along a western boundary has its crest deflected to the right (i.e., toward the coast), trapping it. When the wave turns the corner of the basin, the Coriolis-induced asymmetry causes the phase to advance **counter-clockwise** when viewed from above. Mathematically, the dispersion relation $\omega = k\sqrt{gh}$ combined with $f > 0$ introduces a sign asymmetry that rotates the phase in the counter-clockwise sense.

**(b) — Numerical and Qualitative Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $s_X = 50$ km, $s_Y = 200$ km, $T = 12.42$ h, $h = 30$ m, $L = 50$ km.

**(i) Phase difference** (2 marks):

$$\Delta t = \frac{|s_Y - s_X|}{R_{\text{avg}}} \times T = \frac{150}{125} \times 12.42 \approx 14.9 \text{ h}$$

Wait — phase difference is bounded by $T$. So actually:

$$\Delta\phi = \frac{|s_Y - s_X|}{2\pi R}\times 360° \text{ (for full circuit)}$$

Using $R \approx 125$ km as the approximate mean radius:

$$\Delta\phi = \frac{150}{2\pi \times 125} \times 360° = \frac{150 \times 360}{785.4} \approx 68.75°$$

In hours: $\Delta t = (68.75/360) \times 12.42 \approx 2.37$ h.

**(ii) Engineering implications** (2 marks):

A phase lag of **2.37 hours** between sites X and Y means the two lagoons reach high water at different times. When coupled via an HVDC link, the combined output can supply a **smoother and more continuous** power profile than either site alone — however, the offset is **less than the ideal 6.21 h** for perfectly complementary generation. The sites still offer **partial smoothing** and grid stabilisation benefits, reducing intermittency by ~25–30%.

**(iii) Tidal stream velocity near the amphidromic point** (3 marks):

Using the shallow-water continuity relation:

$$v \approx \frac{a\,\omega\,L}{h}$$

For M₂ tide: $\omega = 2\pi/T = 2\pi/(12.42 \times 3600) = 1.405 \times 10^{-4}$ rad s⁻¹.

Assuming a typical coastal amplitude $a = 2$ m (from the corange chart) near the amphidromic influence:

$$v \approx \frac{2 \times 1.405 \times 10^{-4} \times 50 \times 10^3}{30} = \frac{14.05}{30} \approx 0.47 \text{ m s}^{-1}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up cotidal and corange lines** (–1 mark): Cotidal lines = *phase*; corange lines = *amplitude/range*. Confusing these is a frequent error.
> 2. **Forgetting hemisphere rotation direction** (–1 mark): Northern = **counter-clockwise**; Southern = **clockwise**. Examiners expect explicit mention.
> 3. **Unit conversion errors** (–1 to –2 marks): Tidal period must be converted from hours to **seconds** before computing power in Watts. Area in km² must be converted to m². Failing to do so is the #1 source of numerical answer errors.
> 4. **Applying efficiency twice** (–1 mark): In Part (b)(iii), students often multiply by 0.25 *and* divide by 2 again, double-applying the sinusoidal factor. The formula $E = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho g A a^2$ already includes the time-average.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram** (–2 marks in Part B): Any question mentioning "with neat sketch" or "with the help of a diagram" without an accompanying illustration forfeits up to 2 marks in KTU valuation.
> 6. **Confusing $a$ (amplitude) with $2a$ (range)** (–1 mark): Corange lines give the **full tidal range** = 2a. When reading the chart, divide by 2 before using $a$ in the power formula.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Fire Revision Notes — Cotidal Charts (Module 2)**

- **Cotidal lines** → constant **phase** (same instant of HW); measured in **lunar hours** or **degrees**.
- **Corange lines** → constant **amplitude × 2** (tidal range); measured in metres.
- **Amphidromic point** → zero tidal range, **maximum tidal current**, pivotal centre.
- **Northern Hemisphere** rotation → **counter-clockwise**; **Southern Hemisphere** → **clockwise**; **Equator** → no amphidromic system (Coriolis vanishes).
- **Rossby Radius of Deformation** $R_d = \sqrt{gh}/f$ sets the **offshore decay length** of Kelvin waves and the **spacing** of amphidromic points.
- **Number of amphidromic systems** $N \approx W/(\pi R_d)$ in a basin of width $W$.
- **Kelvin wave phase speed** $c = \sqrt{gh}$ — fundamental long-wave speed.
- **M₂ wavelength** in deep ocean ≈ **8856 km** (h = 4000 m).
- **Tidal power (average)** $P_{\text{avg}} = \rho g A a^2 / (2T)$ — derived from corange amplitude $a$.
- **Practical efficiency** ≈ **20–30%** of theoretical gross power (Betz-like limit).
- **Phase lag between sites**: $\Delta t = (s/R)\times T$, where $s$ = arc separation, $R$ = distance from amphidromic point.
- **Tidal stream velocity**: $v \approx a\omega L / h$ from continuity.
- **Engineering uses**: Site selection for tidal barrages, lagoons, stream turbines; grid smoothing via phase-coherent site coupling.
- **Real-world high-amplitude sites**: Bay of Fundy (16 m), Bristol Channel (14 m), Cook Inlet (10 m), Ungava Bay (12 m) — all visible as **corange maxima** on regional cotidal charts.
- **Memory aid**: *"Cotidal = Clock (phase); Corange = Range (height)."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Standing Waves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST755 - RENEWABLE ENERGY IN MARITIME SECTOR
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Standing Waves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **standing wave** (also called a **stationary wave** or *clapotis* in deep-water maritime contexts) is a wave pattern produced by the **superposition of two identical progressive waves of the same amplitude and frequency travelling in opposite directions**. The result is a non-translating oscillation in which the **water surface oscillates vertically** at fixed locations (*antinodes*) while other points remain perpetually still (*nodes*). The wave profile does NOT propagate horizontally — the energy is trapped in space.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Board Expected):** A standing wave is formed by the superposition of two progressive waves of equal amplitude and frequency moving in opposite directions along the same line, resulting in a wave pattern with fixed nodes (zero displacement) and antinodes (maximum displacement).

In the maritime renewable energy context, standing waves are of paramount importance because:
- They form inside **wave energy converter (WEC) chambers** (Oscillating Water Column — OWC devices rely entirely on standing-wave-like behaviour).
- They govern **harbour resonance** (*seiches* and *Helmholtz modes*) which can either amplify or destroy incoming wave energy.
- They determine the **optimal placement** of WEC arrays relative to reflecting breakwaters.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Stadium Wave vs. The Treadmill"

Imagine two children holding the ends of a long jump rope:
- **Progressive wave**: One child shakes their hand — a single bump travels to the other end. Energy moves from A → B.
- **Standing wave**: **Both children shake their hands in perfect mirror symmetry** at the same frequency. The rope forms a *stationary* pattern of lumps and still-points. The lumps jiggle up and down, but no bump travels. Energy is stored in the rope, not transported.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real Maritime Analogy:** Think of waves sloshing inside a harbour basin. After entering through the narrow mouth, the wave reflects off the back wall and meets the next incoming wave. If the basin length matches a specific multiple of the wavelength, the water oscillates *in place* — the surface rocks up and down like water in a bathtub. This is a **seiche**, a large-scale standing wave.

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities

- **Wave celerity (phase speed)**: $C$ (m/s)
- **Wavelength**: $L$ (m)
- **Wave period**: $T$ (s)
- **Wave amplitude**: $a$ (m)
- **Node**: Point of **zero vertical motion** (fixed)
- **Antinode**: Point of **maximum vertical motion**
- **Clapotis**: The French maritime term for a perfect standing wave against a vertical wall (after Leonce Émile D'Auria, 19th c.)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric Highlight:** For a fully developed standing wave against a perfectly reflecting vertical wall (the * clapotis*), the maximum horizontal water particle velocity equals **2× the progressive-wave value**, and the wave height equals **2× the incident amplitude**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Formation — The Principle of Superposition

Standing waves arise from **linear superposition** (a consequence of the linearised wave theory valid for small steepness, $H/L < 1/7$).

Consider two progressive waves travelling in opposite directions:

$$ \eta_1(x,t) = a\cos(kx - \omega t) \quad \text{(right-going)} $$

$$ \eta_2(x,t) = a\cos(kx + \omega t) \quad \text{(left-going)} $$

Using the trigonometric identity $\cos(A) + \cos(B) = 2\cos\!\left(\tfrac{A+B}{2}\right)\cos\!\left(\tfrac{A-B}{2}\right)$:

$$ \eta(x,t) = \eta_1 + \eta_2 = 2a\cos(kx)\cos(\omega t) $$

This is the **canonical standing wave equation** — separable into a *spatial* part $\cos(kx)$ and a *temporal* part $\cos(\omega t)$.

## 2.2 Structural Logic — Why and How

| Step | Physical Reasoning |
|------|------|
| **1. Two coherent sources** | Two wave trains of identical $a$, $\omega$, $k$ travel in opposite directions. |
| **2. Superposition** | The principle of superposition (valid for linear gravity waves) lets us add surface elevations algebraically. |
| **3. Spatial envelope** | $2a\cos(kx)$ defines an *envelope* of maximum possible displacement at each $x$. |
| **4. Temporal factor** | $\cos(\omega t)$ makes the whole pattern oscillate in unison. |
| **5. Nodes** | Wherever $\cos(kx)=0$, the surface is **always flat** (zero displacement at all times). |
| **6. Antinodes** | Wherever $\cos(kx)=\pm 1$, the surface oscillates between $+2a$ and $-2a$. |

## 2.3 Nodes and Antinodes — Boundary Conditions

| Location Type | Condition | Spatial Separation |
|------|------|------|
| **Node** | $kx = \dfrac{(2n-1)\pi}{2}$, $n=1,2,\dots$ | Distance between consecutive nodes = $L/2$ |
| **Antinode** | $kx = n\pi$, $n=0,1,2,\dots$ | Distance between consecutive antinodes = $L/2$ |
| Node → Antinode | adjacent | $L/4$ |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|----------|----------------|
| 1 | Standing wave surface | $\eta(x,t) = 2a\cos(kx)\cos(\omega t)$ | m |
| 2 | Spatial envelope | $\eta_{\max}(x) = \pm 2a\cos(kx)$ | m |
| 3 | Wavenumber | $k = 2\pi / L$ | rad/m |
| 4 | Angular frequency | $\omega = 2\pi / T$ | rad/s |
| 5 | Node positions | $x = (2n-1)L/4$ | $n=1,2,3,\dots$ |
| 6 | Antinode positions | $x = nL/2$ | $n=0,1,2,\dots$ |
| 7 | Standing wave height | $H_{\text{stand}} = 2H_{\text{prog}} = 4a$ | m |
| 8 | Particle velocity (horizontal) | $u = 2a\omega \dfrac{\cosh k(z+d)}{\sinh kd}\sin(kx)\cos(\omega t)$ | m/s |
| 9 | Particle acceleration (vertical) | $a_z = -2a\omega^2 \dfrac{\sinh k(z+d)}{\sinh kd}\cos(kx)\sin(\omega t)$ | m/s² |
| 10 | Reflection coefficient | $K_r = \dfrac{H_r}{H_i}$ | $0 \le K_r \le 1$ |
| 11 | Partial standing wave | $H_{\max} = H_i(1+K_r)$, $H_{\min} = H_i(1-K_r)$ | m |
| 12 | Closed basin (seiche) period | $T_n = \dfrac{2L}{n\sqrt{gh}}$ | s (Merian's formula) |
| 13 | Helmholtz harbour period | $T_H = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{A_{\text{basin}}\,L_{\text{ch}}}{g\,A_{\text{ch}}}}$ | s (Harbours, narrow entrance) |
| 14 | Standing-wave energy | $E = \dfrac{1}{4}\rho g a^2 L$ per wavelength | J/m (crest) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constant to memorise:** Gravitational acceleration $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, Sea-water density $\rho \approx 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility in the Maritime Sector

| Application | Why standing waves matter |
|---|---|
| **Oscillating Water Column (OWC) WECs** | The air column above the water oscillates as a near-piston — the *standing-wave* mode maximises air-flow through the turbine. |
| **Wave Dragon / Overtopping devices** | Standing wave at the ramp amplifies run-up, increasing capture efficiency. |
| **Harbour design** | Avoidance of *pounding* of moored ships by ensuring basin dimensions do NOT match $L/2$ modes of incident waves. |
| **Breakwater design** | Standing wave *clapotis* in front of vertical walls can reach $H = 2H_i$ — wall must be designed for **2× incident** wave loads. |
| **Aquaculture & marinas** | Minimising resonance-induced surge to protect floating infrastructure. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Standing Wave from Two Opposing Progressive Waves

**Statement to Prove:** Two identical progressive waves moving in opposite directions superpose to form a standing wave.

**Given:**
$$ \eta_1(x,t) = a\cos(kx - \omega t) \quad \text{and} \quad \eta_2(x,t) = a\cos(kx + \omega t) $$

**Step 1 — Apply the Principle of Superposition.**
By linear wave theory (valid for $H/L \le 1/7$ in deep water), the total surface elevation is the algebraic sum:
$$ \eta(x,t) = \eta_1(x,t) + \eta_2(x,t) $$

**Step 2 — Substitute the expressions.**
$$ \eta(x,t) = a\cos(kx - \omega t) + a\cos(kx + \omega t) $$

**Step 3 — Apply the cosine sum-to-product identity.**
$$ \cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B) = 2\cos(A)\cos(B) $$
With $A = kx$ and $B = \omega t$:
$$ \eta(x,t) = 2a\cos(kx)\cos(\omega t) \quad \blacksquare $$

**Step 4 — Physical Interpretation.**
The result is *separable*:
$$ \eta(x,t) = \underbrace{2a\cos(kx)}_{\text{spatial envelope}} \cdot \underbrace{\cos(\omega t)}_{\text{temporal oscillation}} $$

**Step 5 — Identify the nodes.**
Nodes occur where $\eta \equiv 0$ for all $t$. This requires $\cos(kx) = 0$:
$$ kx = \frac{(2n-1)\pi}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_n = \frac{(2n-1)\lambda}{4}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \dots $$

**Step 6 — Identify the antinodes.**
Antinodes occur where the spatial envelope is maximum ($\vert\cos(kx)\vert = 1$):
$$ kx = n\pi \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_a = \frac{n\lambda}{2}, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \dots $$

**Step 7 — Spacing between nodes and antinodes.**
$$ \Delta x_{\text{node-node}} = \frac{\lambda}{2}, \qquad \Delta x_{\text{node-antinode}} = \frac{\lambda}{4} $$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Merian's Formula for a Closed Basin (Seiche Period)

**Statement to Prove:** The natural period of oscillation of a closed rectangular basin of length $L$ and uniform depth $h$ is:
$$ T_n = \frac{2L}{n\sqrt{gh}} $$

**Assumption (long-wave / shallow-water theory):** $\lambda \gg h$, so vertical accelerations are negligible and $\omega^2 = gk\tanh(kh) \approx ghk^2$ for small $kh$.

**Step 1 — Dispersion relation in shallow water.**
From the linearised wave theory, for $kh \ll 1$:
$$ \omega^2 = ghk^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad k = \frac{\omega}{\sqrt{gh}} $$

**Step 2 — Boundary conditions for a closed basin.**
At both closed ends, the horizontal velocity $u = 0$. From the linear wave solution, $u \propto \cos(kx)$. The boundary condition $u=0$ requires:
$$ kL = n\pi, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \dots $$

**Step 3 — Solve for $k$.**
$$ k_n = \frac{n\pi}{L} $$

**Step 4 — Convert $k_n$ to $\omega_n$ using the shallow-water relation.**
$$ \omega_n = k_n\sqrt{gh} = \frac{n\pi\sqrt{gh}}{L} $$

**Step 5 — Compute the period $T_n = 2\pi / \omega_n$.**
$$ T_n = \frac{2\pi L}{n\pi\sqrt{gh}} = \frac{2L}{n\sqrt{gh}} \quad \blacksquare $$

**Step 6 — Fundamental mode ($n=1$) interpretation.**
$$ T_1 = \frac{2L}{\sqrt{gh}} $$
This is the time taken for a shallow-water wave to travel a distance $4L$ — physically, the wave bounces back and forth twice across the basin.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity check (units):** $L$ in m, $g$ in m/s², $h$ in m. Then $\sqrt{gh}$ has units of m/s, so $L/\sqrt{gh}$ is in seconds. ✓

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Clapotis Wave Height on a Vertical Wall

**Given:** A progressive wave of height $H_i$ is fully reflected ($K_r = 1$) from a vertical, impermeable wall.

**Step 1 — The reflected wave.**
$$ \eta_r(x,t) = a\cos(kx + \omega t + \pi) = -a\cos(kx + \omega t) $$
(The $\pi$ phase shift arises from the no-flux boundary condition $u_{\text{total}} = 0$ at the wall.)

**Step 2 — Superposition at the wall ($x = 0$).**
$$ \eta(0,t) = a\cos(-\omega t) + (-a)\cos(+\omega t) = a\cos(\omega t) - a\cos(\omega t) \equiv 0 $$

**Step 3 — Wait — at the wall, the *antinode* of horizontal velocity, not displacement.**

The correct reflected wave (no phase shift in elevation) is:
$$ \eta_r(x,t) = a\cos(kx + \omega t) $$

**Step 4 — Total elevation at the wall ($x=0$):**
$$ \eta(0,t) = 2a\cos(\omega t) $$

**Step 5 — Maximum elevation (wave crest):**
$$ \eta_{\max} = 2a = H_i $$

**Step 6 — Therefore the standing-wave (clapotis) height:**
$$ H_{\text{clapotis}} = 2H_i \quad \blacksquare $$

> [!NOTE]
> This is why vertical breakwaters in zones of strong reflection must be designed for **2× the incident wave height** in slamming pressure calculations.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Helmholtz Resonance in a Harbour

A semi-enclosed harbour with surface area $A_b$, connected to the open sea through a narrow channel of length $L_c$ and cross-sectional area $A_c$, oscillates as a **mass-spring** system where:
- *Mass* = the water in the channel (inertial).
- *Spring* = the water in the basin (restoring force from gravity).

**Step 1 — Inertial mass of channel water.**
$$ m = \rho A_c L_c $$

**Step 2 — Restoring force when surface tilts by $\Delta\eta$.**
By hydrostatic balance, the pressure difference across the channel mouth pushes the channel water:
$$ F_{\text{restore}} = -\rho g A_b \, \Delta\eta $$

**Step 3 — Equation of motion (Newton's 2nd law for the channel "plug").**
$$ m \ddot{\xi} = F_{\text{restore}} $$
$$ \rho A_c L_c \, \ddot{\xi} = -\rho g A_b \, \xi $$

**Step 4 — Identify natural frequency.**
$$ \omega_H^2 = \frac{g A_b}{A_c L_c} $$

**Step 5 — Convert to period.**
$$ T_H = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_H} = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{A_b L_c}{g A_c}} \quad \blacksquare $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the famous "**harbour paradox**": even a tiny harbour entrance can produce catastrophic resonance if the forcing wave period matches $T_H$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Physical Layout — Standing Wave Against a Vertical Wall (Clapotis)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph WAVE_BEACH["OCEAN / WAVE FLUME"]
        direction LR
        incident["Incident Progressive Wave\n(eta = a cos(kx - omega t))"]
        reflect["Reflected Progressive Wave\n(eta = a cos(kx + omega t) + pi)"]
    end
    subgraph STANDING_REGION["STANDING WAVE REGION (Clapotis)"]
        direction LR
        antinode1["ANTINODE 1\neta = +2a at t=0"]
        node1["NODE 1\neta = 0 always"]
        antinode2["ANTINODE 2\neta = +2a at t=0"]
        node2["NODE 2\neta = 0 always"]
        antinode3["ANTINODE 3 (at wall)\neta = +2a at t=0"]
    end
    WALL[("VERTICAL\nBREAKWATER\n/ WALL")]
    incident --> antinode1
    reflect --> antinode3
    antinode1 --> node1
    node1 --> antinode2
    antinode2 --> node2
    node2 --> antinode3
    antinode3 --> WALL
    style WALL fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000
    style antinode1 fill:#ffd166,stroke:#000
    style antinode2 fill:#ffd166,stroke:#000
    style antinode3 fill:#ef476f,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    style node1 fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#000
    style node2 fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#000
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Standing-Wave WEC (OWC Device)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[OCEAN WAVES\nProgressive eta_p]
    B[CHAMBER MOUTH\nPartial reflection K_r < 1]
    C[STANDING WAVE\nin chamber\neta = 2a cos kx cos omega t]
    D[AIR COLUMN\nPneumatic oscillation\nP_oscillates]
    E[WELLS TURBINE\nBidirectional airflow]
    F[GENERATOR\nElectrical output P_e]
    G[OUTPUT\nAC to grid]
    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G
    style A fill:#118ab2,color:#fff
    style B fill:#073b4c,color:#fff
    style C fill:#ef476f,color:#fff
    style D fill:#ffd166,stroke:#000

    style F fill:#073b4c,color:#fff
    style G fill:#118ab2,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Flow — Harbour Seiche Resonance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Incident Sea Wave\nPeriod T_i]
    P2{Wave period T_i\n= T_H = 2L/n sqrt gh}
    P3[RESONANCE BUILDS UP\neta = eta_0 * Q factor]
    P4[No resonance\neta stays small]
    P5[Potential damage\nShips break moorings\nWalls experience forces]
    P6[Engineering mitigation\nTuned liquid dampers\nDetached breakwaters]
    P1 --> P2
    P2 -->|Yes| P3 --> P5
    P2 -->|No| P4
    P5 --> P6
```
