
## 4.4 Spatial Profile of a Standing Wave (Time Snapshots)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    t0["t = 0\neta = +2a cos kx\nUpper envelope"]
    t1["t = T/4\neta = 0 everywhere\nFlat surface"]
    t2["t = T/2\neta = -2a cos kx\nInverted envelope"]
    t3["t = 3T/4\neta = 0 everywhere\nFlat surface"]
    t4["t = T\neta = +2a cos kx\nBack to start"]
    t0 --> t1 --> t2 --> t3 --> t4 --> t0
    style t0 fill:#118ab2,color:#fff
    style t1 fill:#06d6a0
    style t2 fill:#ef476f,color:#fff
    style t3 fill:#06d6a0
    style t4 fill:#118ab2,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** At $t=0$, the standing wave is at its *upper* extreme. At $t=T/4$, the *entire* surface is momentarily flat (every particle passing through equilibrium). At $t=T/2$, it is fully inverted. Crucially, **the nodes (where $\eta \equiv 0$) never move at any time.**
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define a standing wave. How is it formed?**

**Model Answer:**
A standing wave is a wave pattern in which the surface oscillates vertically without any net horizontal propagation. It is formed by the **linear superposition of two identical progressive waves** of equal amplitude ($a$) and angular frequency ($\omega$) travelling in **opposite directions** along the same line. Mathematically,

$$ \eta(x,t) = 2a\cos(kx)\cos(\omega t) $$

The pattern exhibits fixed *nodes* (zero displacement) and *antinodes* (maximum displacement), separated by $\lambda/4$ and $\lambda/2$ respectively. *(3 marks)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between progressive and standing waves. List any three differences.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Progressive Wave | Standing Wave |
|---|------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Energy propagates with the wave | Energy is trapped; no net transport |
| 2 | All particles oscillate with the **same** amplitude | Amplitudes vary from 0 (node) to $2a$ (antinode) |
| 3 | Phase varies with $x$ and $t$ | Phase is uniform in $x$ at any instant; only time oscillates |
| 4 | Wave profile translates | Wave profile does not translate |
| 5 | Example: ocean swells in deep water | Example: harbour seiche, OWC chamber wave |

*(3 marks — 1 for valid definition and 2 for tabular comparison points)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**A(a).** Derive the mathematical expression for a standing wave formed by the superposition of two progressive waves travelling in opposite directions. Hence obtain the positions of nodes and antinodes. **(7 marks)**

**A(b).** A closed rectangular harbour basin has length $L = 400 \text{ m}$ and uniform depth $h = 8 \text{ m}$. Using Merian's formula, calculate the natural period of the fundamental ($n=1$) seiche mode. If the dominant incoming wave has period $T = 30 \text{ s}$, comment on whether the harbour is in danger of resonance. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — A(a)

**Step 1:** Consider two progressive waves:
$$ \eta_1 = a\cos(kx - \omega t), \qquad \eta_2 = a\cos(kx + \omega t) $$

**Step 2:** Superpose:
$$ \eta = \eta_1 + \eta_2 = 2a\cos(kx)\cos(\omega t) $$

**Step 3 — Nodes:** Require $\cos(kx) = 0$:
$$ kx_n = \frac{(2n-1)\pi}{2} \Rightarrow x_n = \frac{(2n-1)\lambda}{4}, \quad n = 1,2,3,\dots $$

**Step 4 — Antinodes:** Require $\vert\cos(kx)\vert = 1$:
$$ x_a = \frac{n\lambda}{2}, \quad n = 0,1,2,\dots $$

**Step 5 — Spacing:** Node-to-node = $\lambda/2$; node-to-antinode = $\lambda/4$.

*(Full marks for showing the identity, separable form, and both position equations with physical interpretation.)*

#### Model Solution — A(b)

**Step 1 — Apply Merian's formula for the fundamental mode ($n=1$):**
$$ T_1 = \frac{2L}{\sqrt{gh}} $$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:**
$$ T_1 = \frac{2 \times 400}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 8}} = \frac{800}{\sqrt{78.48}} = \frac{800}{8.859} $$

**Step 3 — Compute:**
$$ T_1 \approx 90.30 \text{ s} $$

**Step 4 — Compare with the incident wave period:**
$$ T_{\text{incident}} = 30 \text{ s}, \qquad T_1 = 90.30 \text{ s} $$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:** Since $30 \text{ s} \neq 90.30 \text{ s}$, the **fundamental** mode is not excited. **However**, the third harmonic ($n=3$) of the harbour has period:
$$ T_3 = \frac{T_1}{3} = \frac{90.30}{3} = 30.10 \text{ s} \approx 30 \text{ s} $$

Therefore, the harbour is in **danger of third-mode resonance**. The design must include damping measures (e.g., perforated screens, TLDs) at the entrance. *(7 marks)*

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]` *(Alternative to A)*

**B(a).** Explain the phenomenon of *clapotis* formed in front of a vertical seawall. Derive an expression for the height of the clapotis in terms of the incident wave height. **(7 marks)**

**B(b).** A small fishing harbour has a basin area $A_b = 50{,}000 \text{ m}^2$ connected to the open sea by a channel of length $L_c = 80 \text{ m}$ and cross-section $A_c = 25 \text{ m}^2$. Calculate the Helmholtz resonance period. If a storm swell of period $T = 180 \text{ s}$ is expected, will resonance occur? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — B(a)

**Step 1 — Definition of Clapotis:**
Clapotis is the French maritime term for the **standing wave** formed by the complete reflection of a progressive wave from a vertical, smooth, impermeable wall.

**Step 2 — Mathematical setup:**
Incident: $\eta_i = a\cos(kx - \omega t)$  
Reflected (with $\pi$ phase reversal in velocity, none in elevation): $\eta_r = a\cos(kx + \omega t)$  
Total: $\eta = 2a\cos(kx)\cos(\omega t)$

**Step 3 — At the wall ($x = 0$):**
$$ \eta(0,t) = 2a\cos(\omega t) $$

**Step 4 — Maximum surface elevation at the wall:**
$$ \eta_{\max} = 2a = H_{\text{incident}} $$

**Step 5 — Clapotis height (crest to trough):**
$$ H_{\text{clapotis}} = 2H_{\text{incident}} $$

**Step 6 — Engineering implication:** Vertical breakwaters in highly reflective zones must be designed for **twice the incident wave height** to resist wave slamming and run-up loads.

*(7 marks — 2 for definition, 3 for derivation, 2 for engineering comment)*

#### Model Solution — B(b)

**Step 1 — Helmholtz period formula:**
$$ T_H = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{A_b L_c}{g A_c}} $$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**
$$ T_H = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{50{,}000 \times 80}{9.81 \times 25}} $$

**Step 3 — Compute the inside of the square root:**
$$ \frac{50{,}000 \times 80}{9.81 \times 25} = \frac{4{,}000{,}000}{245.25} = 16{,}310.5 \text{ s}^2 $$

**Step 4 — Take the square root:**
$$ \sqrt{16{,}310.5} = 127.71 \text{ s} $$

**Step 5 — Multiply by $2\pi$:**
$$ T_H = 2\pi \times 127.71 = 802.5 \text{ s} $$

**Step 6 — Compare with incident wave period:**
$$ T_{\text{incident}} = 180 \text{ s} \quad \text{vs} \quad T_H = 802.5 \text{ s} $$

**Step 7 — Conclusion:** Since $180 \text{ s} \ll 802.5 \text{ s}$, the harbour will **NOT** undergo Helmholtz resonance for this storm. However, long-period *tsunami-like* waves (with $T \to 800$ s) would be highly dangerous. Designers should verify other basin modes (seiche $T_n = 2L/n\sqrt{gh}$) as a check. *(7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Deductions**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in $H_{\text{clapotis}} = 2H_i$** — costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing node and antinode spacing** — remember: **node-to-node = $\lambda/2$**, **node-to-antinode = $\lambda/4$**. Confusing these is a frequent error.
> 3. **Mixing up Merian's and Helmholtz formulas** — Merian is for a *closed basin* with vertical side walls; Helmholtz is for a *narrow-entrance harbour* with a much larger basin. Using the wrong one = full mark loss on that sub-part.
> 4. **Dropping units in the final answer** — KTU board examiners *do* deduct 0.5 marks for missing units in the final numerical answer.
> 5. **Forgetting that standing-wave antinodes have $2a$ displacement**, not $a$ — read the question carefully: "in terms of incident amplitude $a$" or "in terms of incident height $H_i$".

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Standing Waves**

- [x] **Definition:** Superposition of two identical progressive waves moving in **opposite directions**.
- [x] **Canonical equation:** $\eta(x,t) = 2a\cos(kx)\cos(\omega t)$ — *separable* in space and time.
- [x] **Nodes** at $x = (2n-1)\lambda/4$, spacing $\lambda/2$.
- [x] **Antinodes** at $x = n\lambda/2$, spacing $\lambda/2$; surface oscillates between $\pm 2a$.
- [x] **Clapotis**: perfect standing wave at a vertical wall, $H_{\text{clapotis}} = 2H_i$.
- [x] **Partial standing wave** with reflection coefficient $K_r$: $H_{\max} = H_i(1+K_r)$, $H_{\min} = H_i(1-K_r)$.
- [x] **Seiche** (closed basin): $T_n = \dfrac{2L}{n\sqrt{gh}}$ — *Merian's formula* (shallow water).
- [x] **Helmholtz harbour mode:** $T_H = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{A_b L_c}{g A_c}}$ — narrow-entrance resonance.
- [x] **Particle velocity** under standing wave is $90°$ out of phase with elevation — the OPPOSITE of progressive waves.
- [x] **Energy in standing wave** is purely *potential* at antinodes and purely *kinetic* at nodes; it oscillates between the two.
- [x] **Engineering relevance:** OWC-WEC chambers, harbour resonance, vertical breakwater design, marina layout.
- [x] **Linear theory assumption:** $H/L \le 1/7$ (small steepness) for the superposition principle to hold.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-line KTU answer opener (for definition-type 3-mark questions):**
> *"A standing wave is a non-translating wave pattern produced by the superposition of two progressive waves of equal amplitude and frequency moving in opposite directions, characterised by fixed nodes and antinodes."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Resonance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST755 - RENEWABLE ENERGY IN MARITIME SECTOR
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Resonance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of the **Renewable Energy in Maritime Sector**, **Resonance** is defined as a physical phenomenon in which a marine energy harvesting system, an offshore structure, or a wave-energy converter (WEC) is driven by an external periodic excitation — typically ocean waves, wind gusts, vortex shedding, or the rotational/electrical excitation from a generator — at a frequency that coincides (or nearly coincides) with one of the natural frequencies of the system. When this occurs, the system exhibits sustained or rapidly amplifying oscillations of very large amplitude, potentially leading to structural failure, fatigue damage, power-extraction collapse, or — beneficially — maximum energy absorption in *resonant Wave Energy Converters (WECs)*.

Mathematically, for a linearised single-degree-of-freedom (SDOF) system, resonance occurs when the excitation frequency `ω` equals the undamped natural frequency `ωn`, producing a **theoretical infinite amplitude** in the absence of damping.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Focus:** For renewable maritime systems, resonance is treated as a **two-edged sword**:
> 1. **Destructive resonance** → must be *avoided* in offshore wind turbine towers, floating PV platforms, and ship hulls to prevent structural collapse.
> 2. **Constructive resonance** → must be *engineered into* resonant Wave Energy Converters (e.g., the oscillating water column, point absorber, and Archimedes Wave Swing) to maximize the captured wave power.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Child on a Swing"

Imagine a child sitting on a swing in a park near the sea-cliff. The swing is a pendulum with its own **natural frequency** (about 1 swing every 2 seconds for a child). If you give the child a tiny push once every 2 seconds — *exactly in time* with the natural sway — the swing will rise higher and higher with each push, even though your push is gentle. This is **resonance** — small periodic inputs, perfectly timed, produce enormous motion.

If you push randomly, the swing will not build up; it will stay small. The ocean behaves similarly: a wave train with a period close to the natural heave period of a buoy will pump it up and down violently, and a well-designed WEC exploits exactly this effect. Conversely, an offshore wind turbine tower with a natural sway period accidentally matching the wave period or rotor rotational frequency can be destroyed — the infamous **Tacoma Narrows Bridge collapse (1940)** being the classical engineering cautionary tale.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Vocabulary Used in the Maritime Renewable Context:**
> - **Natural Frequency (`ωn`)** — the rate at which the structure/system "wants" to oscillate freely.
> - **Excitation Frequency (`ω`)** — the rate of the external driving force (waves, wind, vortex shedding).
> - **Damping Ratio (`ζ`)** — the dissipation of energy, primarily through hydrodynamic radiation, viscous drag, and structural hysteresis.
> - **Lock-in** — when vortex shedding frequency synchronises with structural natural frequency, leading to large-amplitude cross-flow vibrations in marine risers and cables.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Acceleration due to gravity:** **g = 9.81 m/s²**
- **Density of seawater:** **ρ ≈ 1025 kg/m³**
- **Density of air (for wind resonance):** **ρ_air ≈ 1.225 kg/m³**
- **Standard significant wave period range for offshore design:** **Ts = 4 s to 20 s**
- **Standard VIV lock-in reduced velocity range:** **Ur = 1.0 to 4.0** (dimensionless)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

**Step 1 — The Equation of Motion of a Marine SDOF System**
A floating buoy, a heaving Wave Energy Converter, or a tower in surge can be idealised as a mass-spring-damper system subjected to wave excitation. The equation of motion in the time domain is:

$$m\ddot{x}(t) + c\dot{x}(t) + kx(t) = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

- `m` is the **physical mass** of the body (in kg).
- `c` is the **total damping coefficient** = structural damping + hydrodynamic (radiation + viscous) damping.
- `k` is the **hydrostatic stiffness** (e.g., for a heaving buoy, `k = ρ g A_w`, where `A_w` is the waterplane area).
- `F_0 sin(ωt)` is the **first-order wave excitation force** at circular frequency `ω`.

**Step 2 — Why "Why" of Resonance**
If the input frequency `ω` matches the system's natural frequency, every input cycle pumps energy *in phase* with the existing motion. Energy accumulates faster than damping can bleed it off, so amplitude grows. If `ω` is far from `ωn`, input energy partially cancels itself between cycles, and motion remains small.

**Step 3 — The Three Regimes of Operation**
- **Sub-resonant regime** (ω < ωn): The system behaves quasi-statically, motion is in phase with force.
- **Resonant regime** (ω ≈ ωn): Amplitude peaks, phase lag is 90°.
- **Super-resonant regime** (ω > ωn): Motion is 180° out of phase, amplitude decreases monotonically.

**Step 4 — Hydrodynamic Coupling in the Sea**
In real marine applications, the mass `m` is augmented by **added (or virtual) mass `m_a`** due to the water accelerated along with the structure. The effective inertial term becomes `(m + m_a)`, which **lowers the natural frequency** of the system below its in-air value. This is critical for ship roll/pitch and for heaving WECs.

**Step 5 — Beneficial Use: Resonant WEC Tuning**
A point-absorber WEC is tuned so that its natural heaving frequency matches the dominant wave spectral peak. The dimensionless **capture width ratio** (extracted power / power in incident wave crest) can then approach theoretical maxima of ~50% in regular waves and 30–40% in irregular seas.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **high-yield formula set** for the KTU ESE 2024 exam on this topic. Memorise units, not just symbols.

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Undamped Natural Frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m + m_a}}$ | rad/s; depends on total inertial mass |
| 2 | Natural Period | $T_n = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega_n}$ | seconds |
| 3 | Damping Ratio | $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{k(m + m_a)}}$ | dimensionless; for marine systems typically 0.02 – 0.20 |
| 4 | Damped Natural Frequency | $\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$ | rad/s; the actual ringing frequency |
| 5 | Frequency Ratio | $r = \dfrac{\omega}{\omega_n}$ | dimensionless; r = 1 ⇒ exact resonance |
| 6 | Steady-State Amplitude | $X = \dfrac{F_0 / k}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ | metres |
| 7 | Resonance Amplification Factor (Peak) | $M_{peak} = \dfrac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ | dimensionless; very large for small ζ |
| 8 | Quality Factor (Q-Factor) | $Q = \dfrac{1}{2\zeta}$ | dimensionless; energy stored / energy lost per cycle |
| 9 | Half-Power Bandwidth | $\Delta\omega = 2\zeta\omega_n$ | rad/s; width between −3 dB points |
| 10 | Hydrostatic Stiffness (heaving buoy) | $k = \rho g A_w$ | N/m; `A_w` = waterplane area, `ρ` = 1025 kg/m³ |
| 11 | Added Mass Ratio (typical buoy) | $\mu = \dfrac{m_a}{m}$ | 0.1 – 5.0 for typical buoys |
| 12 | Critical Damping | $c_c = 2\sqrt{k(m + m_a)}$ | N·s/m |
| 13 | Strouhal Number (VIV) | $St = \dfrac{f_s D}{U}$ | dimensionless; St ≈ 0.18 – 0.22 for circular cylinders |
| 14 | Lock-in Reduced Velocity | $U_r = \dfrac{U}{f_n D}$ | dimensionless; lock-in between 1.0 and 4.0 |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Offshore Wind Turbine Tower Design (NREL / DNV GL standards):** Tower first natural fore-aft and side-side frequencies must lie in a "soft-stiff" design range, typically between the 1P (rotor) and 3P (blade-passing) excitation frequencies to avoid resonance. The KTU examiner expects awareness of this 1P–3P exclusion band.
- **Floating Offshore Wind (e.g., Hywind, WindFloat):** Platform surge, heave, and pitch natural periods must be detuned from typical wave spectral peak periods (8–14 s) to limit motion and facilitate turbine access.
- **Resonant Wave Energy Converters (e.g., Pelamis, Ocean Power Technology's PowerBuoy):** The buoy/oscillating system is intentionally tuned to peak wave frequency to maximise power capture.
- **Marine Risers and Mooring Lines:** Vortex-Induced Vibration (VIV) lock-in is suppressed using helical strakes, fairings, or tuned mass dampers.
- **Ship Structural Design:** Hull girder whipping and springing resonances (high-frequency hull-plate responses) must be kept away from propeller-induced excitation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Steady-State Amplitude of a Forced SDOF Marine System

**Starting Point — Equation of Motion:**

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step (a) — Assume Steady-State Harmonic Solution**
Try a particular solution of the form:

$$x_p(t) = X \sin(\omega t - \phi)$$

Here `X` is the unknown amplitude we must find, and `φ` is the phase lag between the input force and the response.

**Step (b) — Compute Derivatives**

$$\dot{x}_p(t) = X \omega \cos(\omega t - \phi)$$

$$\ddot{x}_p(t) = -X \omega^2 \sin(\omega t - \phi)$$

**Step (c) — Substitute Back into the Equation of Motion**

$$-m X \omega^2 \sin(\omega t - \phi) + c X \omega \cos(\omega t - \phi) + k X \sin(\omega t - \phi) = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step (d) — Group the Sine and Cosine Terms**

$$X(k - m\omega^2) \sin(\omega t - \phi) + c X \omega \cos(\omega t - \phi) = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step (e) — Express the Right Side in the Same Phase Frame**
Use the trigonometric identity:

$$F_0 \sin(\omega t) = F_0 \sin(\omega t - \phi)\cos\phi + F_0 \cos(\omega t - \phi)\sin\phi$$

**Step (f) — Equate Coefficients of `sin(ωt − φ)` and `cos(ωt − φ)`**

$$X(k - m\omega^2) = F_0 \cos\phi$$

$$c X \omega = F_0 \sin\phi$$

**Step (g) — Solve for the Amplitude `X`**
Square both equations and add them:

$$X^2 \left[ (k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2 \right] = F_0^2 (\cos^2\phi + \sin^2\phi) = F_0^2$$

**Step (h) — Final Expression for Steady-State Amplitude**

$$X = \frac{F_0}{\sqrt{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}}$$

**Step (i) — Non-Dimensionalise the Result**
Divide numerator and denominator by `k`, define static deflection `X_static = F_0 / k` and frequency ratio `r = ω / ωn`. The final, exam-ready form is:

$$\boxed{\,X = \frac{X_{static}}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}\,}$$

This is the **single most important KTU result** for the topic of resonance in marine systems.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: The Resonance Peak Amplification

**Objective:** Find the value of `r` at which `X` is maximised (the resonant peak).

**Step (a) — Identify the Denominator**
The denominator to be minimised is the function:

$$D(r) = (1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2$$

**Step (b) — Differentiate with Respect to `r` and Set to Zero**

$$\frac{dD}{dr} = 2(1 - r^2)(-2r) + 2(2\zeta r)(2\zeta) = -4r(1 - r^2) + 8\zeta^2 r = 0$$

**Step (c) — Factor Out `4r`**

$$4r \left[ -(1 - r^2) + 2\zeta^2 \right] = 0$$

**Step (d) — Identify the Non-Trivial Root**
The solution `r = 0` corresponds to the static-deflection minimum, not resonance. Setting the bracket to zero:

$$1 - r^2 = 2\zeta^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad r_{peak} = \sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2}$$

**Step (e) — Substitute Back into the Amplitude Formula**
At `r = r_peak = √(1 − 2ζ²)`, the denominator becomes:

$$(1 - r_{peak}^2)^2 + (2\zeta r_{peak})^2 = (2\zeta^2)^2 + 4\zeta^2(1 - 2\zeta^2) = 4\zeta^2 - 4\zeta^4 = 4\zeta^2(1 - \zeta^2)$$

**Step (f) — Final Peak Amplification Factor**

$$\boxed{\,M_{peak} = \frac{X_{peak}}{X_{static}} = \frac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For *small* damping (ζ << 1, typical of large marine structures), this simplifies to `M_peak ≈ 1/(2ζ)`, which is the famous resonance amplification that can be 10×, 50×, or even 100× the static displacement.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Quality Factor (Q) and Energy Interpretation

**Step (a) — Definition of Q-Factor**
The Q-factor is the ratio of energy stored per cycle to energy dissipated per cycle:

$$Q = 2\pi \frac{E_{stored}}{\Delta E_{cycle}}$$

**Step (b) — Energy Stored in the Spring at Peak Displacement**
At maximum deflection `X`, all mechanical energy is potential:

$$E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} k X^2$$

**Step (c) — Energy Dissipated per Cycle**
For viscous damping, the energy lost in one complete cycle is:

$$\Delta E_{cycle} = \pi c \omega X^2$$

**Step (d) — Take the Ratio**

$$Q = 2\pi \cdot \frac{\frac{1}{2} k X^2}{\pi c \omega X^2} = \frac{k}{c \omega}$$

**Step (e) — Evaluate at Resonance (ω = ωn)**
At resonance, `ω = ωn = √(k/m)`, so:

$$Q = \frac{k}{c \omega_n} = \frac{k}{c \sqrt{k/m}} = \frac{\sqrt{km}}{c} = \frac{1}{2\zeta}$$

$$\boxed{\,Q = \frac{1}{2\zeta}\,}$$

**Interpretation:** Lower damping (ζ ↓) ⇒ higher Q ⇒ sharper, taller resonance peak. Marine cables and tall slender risers often exhibit Q in the range 10–50, leading to dramatic lock-in amplitudes.

---

### 3.4 KTU Problem Walk-Through (Sample Numerical)

**Question:** A heaving point-absorber buoy has physical mass `m = 5000 kg`, hydrodynamic added mass `m_a = 4500 kg`, hydrostatic stiffness `k = 25{,}000 N/m`, total damping `c = 1800 N·s/m`. The buoy is excited by a regular wave of height `H = 1.5 m` and period `T = 4 s`. The first-order wave excitation force amplitude is `F_0 = 60 kN`. Find:
1. The natural frequency and natural period.
2. The frequency ratio `r`.
3. The damping ratio `ζ`.
4. The steady-state heave amplitude `X`.

**Solution:**

**Part (1) — Natural frequency and period:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m + m_a}} = \sqrt{\frac{25000}{5000 + 4500}} = \sqrt{\frac{25000}{9500}}$$

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{2.6316} = 1.622 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$T_n = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_n} = \frac{2\pi}{1.622} = 3.873 \text{ s}$$

**Part (2) — Excitation frequency and frequency ratio:**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi}{T} = \frac{2\pi}{4} = 1.571 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$r = \frac{\omega}{\omega_n} = \frac{1.571}{1.622} = 0.968$$

**Part (3) — Damping ratio:**

$$\zeta = \frac{c}{2\sqrt{k(m + m_a)}} = \frac{1800}{2 \sqrt{25000 \times 9500}}$$

$$\zeta = \frac{1800}{2 \times 15406.6} = \frac{1800}{30813.2} = 0.0584$$

**Part (4) — Steady-state heave amplitude:**

$$X = \frac{F_0 / k}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

$$X = \frac{60000 / 25000}{\sqrt{(1 - 0.9372)^2 + (2 \times 0.0584 \times 0.968)^2}}$$

$$X = \frac{2.4}{\sqrt{(0.0628)^2 + (0.1131)^2}} = \frac{2.4}{\sqrt{0.00394 + 0.01280}} = \frac{2.4}{\sqrt{0.01674}}$$

$$X = \frac{2.4}{0.1294} = 18.55 \text{ m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Sanity Check:** The amplitude 18.55 m is unphysically large for a 1.5 m wave. This implies the system is **dangerously close to resonance** (r = 0.968, ζ = 5.8%). In practice, the buoy would experience non-linear slamming, stroke-end impacts, and likely power-take-off saturation. The example illustrates *exactly why KTU and real-world designers detune marine WECs deliberately away from peak resonance for survivability.*
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**A1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**
**Q: Define resonance in the context of marine renewable energy systems. Distinguish between destructive and constructive resonance with one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Resonance is the phenomenon in which a marine energy system (buoy, tower, hull, or WEC) is subjected to a periodic excitation whose frequency matches the system's natural frequency, leading to large-amplitude oscillations. **(1 mark)**
- *Destructive resonance* must be avoided: example — vortex-induced vibration lock-in of an offshore wind turbine tower leading to fatigue damage; the design ensures the tower's natural frequency lies between the 1P and 3P rotor excitation bands. **(1 mark)**
- *Constructive resonance* is deliberately exploited: example — the heaving point-absorber WEC, which is tuned so that its heave natural period equals the dominant wave period, maximising the capture width ratio. **(1 mark)**

---

**A2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
**Q: What is the quality factor (Q) of a marine oscillating system? How is it related to the damping ratio?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The quality factor `Q` is a dimensionless parameter that measures the sharpness of resonance of a marine oscillating system. It is defined as the ratio of energy stored per cycle to the energy dissipated per cycle, **Q = 2π E_stored / ΔE_cycle**. **(2 marks)**
Mathematically, it is related to the damping ratio `ζ` by the inverse relationship **Q = 1 / (2ζ)**. A small damping ratio (typical of large, lightly-damped marine structures such as spar buoys) produces a high Q-factor, which corresponds to a sharp and tall resonance peak. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]`**

**A heaving point-absorber Wave Energy Converter is to be deployed in a test site with characteristic regular wave period T = 5 s and wave excitation force amplitude F₀ = 75 kN. The system parameters are: physical mass m = 8000 kg, hydrodynamic added mass m_a = 6000 kg, hydrostatic stiffness k = 30,000 N/m, total linear damping coefficient c = 2400 N·s/m.**

**(a) [7 marks]** Determine:
- (i) the undamped natural frequency ωn and natural period Tn of the buoy.
- (ii) the damping ratio ζ.
- (iii) the frequency ratio r, and state whether the system is operating in the sub-resonant, resonant, or super-resonant regime.

**(b) [7 marks]** Calculate:
- (i) the static deflection X_static = F₀ / k.
- (ii) the steady-state heave amplitude X using the exact forced-vibration formula.
- (iii) the peak resonance amplification factor M_peak and comment on whether the system is safely detuned.

---

**Model Solution A:**

**Part (a):**

**(i) Natural frequency and period [2 marks]:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m + m_a}} = \sqrt{\frac{30000}{8000 + 6000}} = \sqrt{\frac{30000}{14000}} = \sqrt{2.1429} = 1.464 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$T_n = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_n} = \frac{2\pi}{1.464} = 4.291 \text{ s}$$

**(ii) Damping ratio [2 marks]:**

$$\zeta = \frac{c}{2\sqrt{k(m + m_a)}} = \frac{2400}{2\sqrt{30000 \times 14000}} = \frac{2400}{2 \times 20512.0} = \frac{2400}{41024.0} = 0.0585$$

**(iii) Frequency ratio and regime [3 marks]:**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi}{T} = \frac{2\pi}{5} = 1.257 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$r = \frac{\omega}{\omega_n} = \frac{1.257}{1.464} = 0.858$$

Since `r < 1`, the system operates in the **sub-resonant regime**, very close to but below the natural frequency. The excitation period (5 s) is longer than the natural period (4.29 s), so the buoy heaves with a frequency slightly higher than its own preferred rate.

---

**Part (b):**

**(i) Static deflection [2 marks]:**

$$X_{static} = \frac{F_0}{k} = \frac{75000}{30000} = 2.5 \text{ m}$$

**(ii) Steady-state amplitude [3 marks]:**

$$X = \frac{X_{static}}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

$$X = \frac{2.5}{\sqrt{(1 - 0.7362)^2 + (2 \times 0.0585 \times 0.858)^2}}$$

$$X = \frac{2.5}{\sqrt{(0.2638)^2 + (0.1004)^2}} = \frac{2.5}{\sqrt{0.0696 + 0.0101}} = \frac{2.5}{\sqrt{0.0797}}$$

$$X = \frac{2.5}{0.2823} = 8.85 \text{ m}$$

**(iii) Peak amplification factor and safety comment [2 marks]:**

$$M_{peak} = \frac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}} = \frac{1}{2 \times 0.0585 \times \sqrt{1 - 0.00342}} = \frac{1}{0.117 \times 0.9983} = 8.56$$

The actual operating amplification is `M_actual = X / X_static = 8.85 / 2.5 = 3.54`. The peak value is 8.56, so the system is operating at about **41% of the theoretical maximum amplification** — a moderately tuned WEC with a high power capture. However, the damping is low (ζ = 5.85%), and a slight increase in wave period toward 4.3 s would push the buoy into catastrophic resonance. The designer should monitor spectral changes and consider active PTO control to widen the safe operating band.

---

#### **QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]`**

**An offshore wind turbine (monopile-supported, in deep water) is being designed. The tower has an effective modal mass of m = 250,000 kg in the side-side direction, lateral stiffness k = 4.5 × 10⁶ N/m, and total damping coefficient c = 1.2 × 10⁵ N·s/m. The rotor operates at a rotational speed N = 12 rpm, producing a 1P excitation frequency. Blade-passing gives a 3P excitation at three times the rotor frequency.**

**(a) [7 marks]** Determine:
- (i) the tower's first side-side natural frequency fn (in Hz) and natural period Tn.
- (ii) the 1P and 3P excitation frequencies.
- (iii) whether the tower's natural frequency lies within the "soft-stiff" design exclusion band (i.e., between 1P and 3P). State the engineering implication.

**(b) [7 marks]** Calculate:
- (i) the damping ratio ζ and the quality factor Q of the tower.
- (ii) the theoretical peak amplification factor M_peak of the tower at resonance.
- (iii) the half-power bandwidth Δf. Comment on the vulnerability of the tower if the rotor speed varies by ±10%.

---

**Model Solution B:**

**Part (a):**

**(i) Natural frequency and period [2 marks]:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{4.5 \times 10^6}{250000}} = \sqrt{18} = 4.243 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{4.243}{2\pi} = 0.6754 \text{ Hz}$$

$$T_n = \frac{1}{f_n} = 1.481 \text{ s}$$

**(ii) 1P and 3P excitation frequencies [2 marks]:**

$$f_{1P} = \frac{N}{60} = \frac{12}{60} = 0.200 \text{ Hz}$$

$$f_{3P} = 3 \times f_{1P} = 0.600 \text{ Hz}$$

**(iii) Design exclusion check [3 marks]:**
The tower natural frequency is `f_n = 0.6754 Hz`. The 1P–3P exclusion band is **0.200 Hz to 0.600 Hz**. Since `f_n = 0.6754 Hz > 0.600 Hz`, the tower is in the **"stiff-stiff" regime** (above 3P). This is generally *not* the most desirable design location; a soft-stiff design (fn between 1P and 3P) is preferred for steel monopiles. The current design may be acceptable but is harder to achieve and may require stiffer tower sections. Engineering implication: the tower avoids resonance with rotor harmonics at the design point, but the margin to 3P is only 0.0754 Hz, which corresponds to a rotor speed variation tolerance of roughly ±2 rpm before lock-in risk emerges.

---

**Part (b):**

**(i) Damping ratio and Q-factor [2 marks]:**

$$\zeta = \frac{c}{2\sqrt{km}} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^5}{2\sqrt{4.5 \times 10^6 \times 250000}} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^5}{2 \times 1.061 \times 10^6} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^5}{2.122 \times 10^6} = 0.0566$$

$$Q = \frac{1}{2\zeta} = \frac{1}{2 \times 0.0566} = 8.83$$

**(ii) Peak amplification factor [2 marks]:**

$$M_{peak} = \frac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}} = \frac{1}{2 \times 0.0566 \times 0.9984} = 8.85$$

**(iii) Half-power bandwidth [3 marks]:**

$$\Delta\omega = 2\zeta \omega_n = 2 \times 0.0566 \times 4.243 = 0.4804 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\Delta f = \frac{\Delta\omega}{2\pi} = \frac{0.4804}{2\pi} = 0.0765 \text{ Hz}$$

The 3P excitation frequency is 0.600 Hz, and `f_n` is 0.6754 Hz — a margin of 0.0754 Hz, **which is essentially equal to the half-power bandwidth**. If the rotor speed varies by +10% (N = 13.2 rpm), `f_3P` rises to 0.660 Hz, which is now *within* the half-power bandwidth of the tower. The amplification at `f_3P` would be roughly `M ≈ 0.707 × M_peak = 6.26`, meaning the tower-top displacement could be over 6 times the static value, posing a serious fatigue and structural risk. Mitigation: install a Tuned Mass Damper (TMD), increase structural damping via composite bracing, or impose stricter rotor-speed control.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose 1–3 marks each):**
> 1. **Forgetting added mass `m_a`** — In any marine problem, the natural frequency must be computed using `m + m_a`, not `m` alone. Many students lose 1–2 marks by ignoring hydrodynamic added mass.
> 2. **Mixing up `r = ω/ωn` and `r = ωn/ω`** — Always define the frequency ratio explicitly in your answer; KTU evaluators deduct for ambiguity.
> 3. **Using `1/(2ζ)` instead of `1/(2ζ√(1−ζ²))` for the peak amplification** — The latter is exact; the former is an approximation valid only for `ζ << 0.3`. State which one you are using and justify.
> 4. **Forgetting units** — In numerical answers, write `rad/s`, `Hz`, `m`, and dimensionless `r` explicitly. Missing units cost marks.
> 5. **Confusing resonance with the 1P–3P exclusion band concept** — The 1P/3P separation is the *rotor-driven* tower design rule, distinct from the wave-frequency resonance of floating WECs. State clearly which is being analysed.
> 6. **Omitting physical interpretation** — KTU 2024 scheme rewards outcome-based answers. A pure numerical answer without an engineering comment is marked down.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Exam-Last-Night Edition):**

- ✅ **Resonance** = excitation frequency ≈ natural frequency ⇒ amplitude amplifies dramatically.
- ✅ **Natural frequency (SDOF):** `ωn = √(k / (m + m_a))` — *never forget added mass in sea*.
- ✅ **Damping ratio:** `ζ = c / (2√(k(m+m_a)))` — typical marine range 0.02 – 0.20.
- ✅ **Frequency ratio:** `r = ω / ωn` — resonance at `r = 1`.
- ✅ **Steady-state amplitude:** `X = (F₀/k) / √[(1 − r²)² + (2ζr)²]`.
- ✅ **Peak amplification:** `M_peak = 1 / (2ζ√(1 − ζ²))` ≈ `1/(2ζ)` for small damping.
- ✅ **Q-factor:** `Q = 1/(2ζ)`; high Q ⇒ sharp peak ⇒ dangerous lock-in.
- ✅ **Half-power bandwidth:** `Δf = 2ζ fn`.
- ✅ **WEC design:** *tune* to wave spectral peak to maximise capture width ratio.
- ✅ **Offshore wind tower:** avoid 1P–3P band; design "soft-stiff" (between rotor and blade-passing frequencies).
- ✅ **VIV lock-in:** Strouhal `St ≈ 0.2`; reduced velocity `Ur = U/(fn D)`; mitigate with strakes, fairings, or TMDs.
- ✅ **Real-world pitfall:** `m_a` lowers `ωn` → resonance is *easier* to hit than in air; the sea is a kind amplifier.
- ✅ **Units mantra:** `ω` in rad/s, `f` in Hz, `m` in kg, `k` in N/m, `c` in N·s/m, `ζ` and `r` dimensionless.
- ✅ **For exams:** always state the regime (sub-resonant / resonant / super-resonant) and add a one-sentence engineering comment.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Coriolis Forces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST755 - RENEWABLE ENERGY IN MARITIME SECTOR
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Coriolis Forces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coriolis Force (or Coriolis Effect):** A fictitious (pseudo / inertial) force that acts perpendicular to the direction of motion of an object within a rotating reference frame. On Earth, this force deflects moving fluids (air and water) — and therefore ocean currents, wind fields, and the energy they carry — to the **right in the Northern Hemisphere** and to the **left in the Southern Hemisphere**.

Mathematically, the Coriolis force acting on a body of mass $m$ moving with velocity $\vec{v}$ in a frame rotating with angular velocity $\vec{\Omega}$ is:

$$
\vec{F}_{cor} = -2m\left(\vec{\Omega} \times \vec{v}\right)
$$

For any engineering study involving **ocean currents, tidal streams, offshore wind, wave propagation, or floating PV platforms**, the Coriolis parameter is conventionally expressed as:

$$
f = 2\,\Omega \sin\phi
$$

where $\phi$ is the geographic latitude. The Earth’s sidereal angular velocity is **$\Omega = 7.2921 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{rad/s}$**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — the spinning merry-go-round:**
> Imagine you are standing on a slowly spinning merry-go-round and you try to walk straight across the disc from the centre to the edge. Because the disc itself is rotating, by the time you reach the rim you have drifted sideways — your path, relative to the disc, looks *curved*. To an observer standing on the ground (an inertial frame), you walked in a perfectly straight line; to you, on the spinning disc, a mysterious sideways "push" seemed to act on you. That apparent sideways push is the **Coriolis force**.
>
> The Earth is essentially a giant merry-go-round. Winds and ocean currents — the "walking person" — appear to be deflected sideways even though no real sideways force is acting on them.

## 1.3 Why Coriolis Force Matters in the Maritime Renewable-Energy Sector

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In Module 2 of *PENST755*, Coriolis force is introduced because it governs the **large-scale behaviour of every fluid-borne renewable resource at sea**:
> - It shapes **global wind belts** that drive offshore wind turbines.
> - It sets the **rotation direction of ocean gyres** that concentrate wave energy.
> - It produces **Ekman transport** that drives coastal upwelling — relevant to OTEC (Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion) sites.
> - It determines the **rotational sense of tidal eddies** exploited by tidal-stream turbines.
> - It deflects **wave direction** as swell crosses ocean basins.

Without Coriolis force, the design assumptions for offshore wind farms, tidal energy converters, OTEC plants, and wave-energy arrays would be physically incomplete.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Rotating Reference Frame — Building the Concept Step-by-Step

When a fluid parcel moves with absolute velocity $\vec{V}_a$ but we observe it from a frame rotating with angular velocity $\vec{\Omega}$ (such as the Earth), the acceleration transforms as:

$$
\left(\frac{d\vec{V}_a}{dt}\right)_{\text{inertial}} = \left(\frac{d\vec{V}_r}{dt}\right)_{\text{rot}} + 2\,\vec{\Omega}\times\vec{V}_r + \vec{\Omega}\times\left(\vec{\Omega}\times\vec{R}\right) + \frac{d\vec{\Omega}}{dt}\times\vec{R}
$$

| Term | Physical Meaning | Maritime Significance |
|------|------------------|----------------------|
| $2\,\vec{\Omega}\times\vec{V}_r$ | **Coriolis acceleration** | Deflection of winds/currents |
| $\vec{\Omega}\times(\vec{\Omega}\times\vec{R})$ | **Centrifugal acceleration** | Shape of ocean surface (geoid) |
| $\dfrac{d\vec{\Omega}}{dt}\times\vec{R}$ | **Euler acceleration** | Negligible (Earth’s rotation is steady) |

The Coriolis term always acts **perpendicular** to the relative velocity, so it does no work — it only redirects the flow.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

1. **Identify the rotating frame:** For Earth-based studies, the frame rotates with angular velocity $\vec{\Omega}$ about the polar axis.
2. **Express $\vec{\Omega}$ in local Cartesian coordinates** (East, North, Up):
   $$\vec{\Omega} = \Omega\cos\phi\,\hat{E} + \Omega\sin\phi\,\hat{U}$$
   where $\phi$ = latitude, $\hat{E}$ = local East, $\hat{U}$ = local Up.
3. **Cross the angular-velocity vector with the velocity vector** to get the Coriolis acceleration magnitude:
   $$\vert \vec{a}_{cor}\vert = 2\,\Omega\,\vert\vec{v}\vert\,\sin\phi$$
4. **Apply the right-hand rule** to get the direction:
   - Northern Hemisphere ($\phi > 0$): deflection **to the right** of motion.
   - Southern Hemisphere ($\phi < 0$): deflection **to the left** of motion.
   - Equator ($\phi = 0$): **zero Coriolis force** in the horizontal plane.
5. **Multiply by mass** to obtain the pseudo-force used in momentum equations for the marine environment.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is the **single source of truth** for numerical problems on Coriolis force in maritime renewable-energy applications. Memorise the units and the Coriolis parameter $f$ — they appear in every oceanographic and atmospheric equation (Ekman, geostrophic, Rossby).

| # | Quantity | Formula | Typical Units | Engineering Use |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|------------------|
| 1 | Coriolis force vector | $\vec{F}_{cor} = -2m\,(\vec{\Omega}\times\vec{v})$ | N (Newton) | Force on water/air parcel |
| 2 | Coriolis acceleration | $\vec{a}_{cor} = -2\,(\vec{\Omega}\times\vec{v})$ | $\text{m/s}^2$ | Acceleration of currents/winds |
| 3 | Magnitude of Coriolis acceleration | $a_{cor} = 2\,\Omega\,v\,\sin\phi$ | $\text{m/s}^2$ | Quick estimate |
| 4 | Coriolis parameter | $f = 2\,\Omega\,\sin\phi$ | $\text{rad/s}$ | Used in Ekman & Rossby equations |
| 5 | Coriolis frequency (planetary) | $f = 1.458 \times 10^{-4}\,\sin\phi$ | $\text{rad/s}$ | Numerical substitution |
| 6 | Geostrophic wind/current speed | $V_g = \dfrac{1}{\rho\,f}\,\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial n}$ | $\text{m/s}$ | Design of offshore wind farms |
| 7 | Ekman layer depth | $D_E = \pi\,\sqrt{\dfrac{2\,A_z}{f}}$ | m | Tidal & OTEC site selection |
| 8 | Rossby radius of deformation | $R_d = \dfrac{\sqrt{g\,H}}{f}$ | m | Scale of ocean gyres |
| 9 | Earth’s angular velocity | $\Omega = 7.2921 \times 10^{-5}$ | $\text{rad/s}$ | Constant for all problems |
| 10 | Inertial period | $T_i = \dfrac{2\pi}{f} = \dfrac{\pi}{\Omega\sin\phi}$ | s | Time-scale of pure inertial motion |

> **Note on notation:** Where absolute values are required (e.g., magnitude of velocity $\vert v\vert$), use $\vert \cdot \vert$ in LaTeX to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Maritime Renewable System | Role of Coriolis Force |
|---------------------------|----------------------|
| Offshore Wind Farms | Sets the prevailing wind direction, drives low-level jets that determine turbine hub-height design. |
| Tidal-Stream Turbines | Influences the rotational sense of tidal eddies — critical when siting devices between islands. |
| OTEC Plants | Drives coastal upwelling, bringing cold deep water to the surface; without it, OTEC sites would not exist. |
| Wave Energy Converters | Refracts long-period swell across ocean basins — affects wave-climate statistics. |
| Floating Solar (Floatovoltaics) | Affects surface currents that impose hydrodynamic loads on mooring lines. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering takeaway:** Any resource map for a maritime renewable project is incomplete without the Coriolis term. It is the “hidden director” that decides *where* the energy is and *which way* it is flowing.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Coriolis Force from First Principles

### Step 1 — Position Vector in the Inertial Frame
A point $P$ has an absolute (inertial-frame) position vector $\vec{r}_a$. A rotating frame with origin $O$ has its position vector $\vec{r}_r$ related by:

$$
\vec{r}_a = \vec{R} + \vec{r}_r
$$

where $\vec{R}$ is the position of the rotating origin measured from the inertial frame.

### Step 2 — First Time Derivative (Velocity)
Differentiating with respect to time and using the rotating-frame rule $\dfrac{d\hat{e}}{dt}\bigg|_{inertial} = \vec{\Omega}\times\hat{e}$:

$$
\vec{V}_a = \vec{V}_R + \vec{\Omega}\times\vec{r}_r + \vec{V}_r
$$

where $\vec{V}_r = \dfrac{d\vec{r}_r}{dt}\bigg|_{rot}$ is the velocity as seen in the rotating frame.

### Step 3 — Second Time Derivative (Acceleration)
Differentiating once more:

$$
\vec{a}_a = \vec{a}_R + \dot{\vec{\Omega}}\times\vec{r}_r + \vec{\Omega}\times(\vec{\Omega}\times\vec{r}_r) + 2\,\vec{\Omega}\times\vec{V}_r + \vec{a}_r
$$

### Step 4 — Identify the Four Apparent Terms
Rearranging for $\vec{a}_r$ (acceleration observed in the rotating frame):

$$
\vec{a}_r = \vec{a}_a - \vec{a}_R - \dot{\vec{\Omega}}\times\vec{r}_r - \vec{\Omega}\times(\vec{\Omega}\times\vec{r}_r) - 2\,\vec{\Omega}\times\vec{V}_r
$$

For the Earth, $\dot{\vec{\Omega}}\approx 0$ (Earth rotates steadily) and $\vec{a}_R = 0$ (origin fixed at Earth’s centre for global studies). So:

$$
\vec{a}_r = \vec{a}_a - \vec{\Omega}\times(\vec{\Omega}\times\vec{r}_r) - 2\,\vec{\Omega}\times\vec{V}_r
$$

### Step 5 — Apply Newton’s Second Law in the Rotating Frame
The real forces acting on a fluid parcel of mass $m$ are pressure gradient, gravity, and friction. To write $\sum \vec{F}_{real} = m\vec{a}_r$, we move the rotational terms to the left:

$$
\sum \vec{F}_{real} + \underbrace{(-m\,\vec{\Omega}\times(\vec{\Omega}\times\vec{r}_r))}_{\text{Centrifugal pseudo-force}} + \underbrace{(-2m\,\vec{\Omega}\times\vec{V}_r)}_{\text{Coriolis pseudo-force}} = m\vec{a}_r
$$

### Step 6 — Final Coriolis Force Expression
$$
\boxed{\;\vec{F}_{cor} = -2m\,(\vec{\Omega}\times\vec{V}_r)\;}
$$

This is the rigorous expression used in every oceanographic and atmospheric model for offshore renewable-energy design.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Horizontal Coriolis Term for Local East-North Coordinates

Let $\vec{V}_r = u\,\hat{E} + v\,\hat{N} + w\,\hat{U}$, with $u, v, w$ the East, North, Up components.

Then:

$$
\vec{\Omega}\times\vec{V}_r = 
\begin{vmatrix}
\hat{E} & \hat{N} & \hat{U}\\
0 & \Omega\cos\phi & \Omega\sin\phi\\
u & v & w
\end{vmatrix}
$$

Evaluating:

$$
\vec{\Omega}\times\vec{V}_r = (w\Omega\cos\phi - v\Omega\sin\phi)\,\hat{E} + (u\Omega\sin\phi - w\Omega 0)\,\hat{N} + (v\Omega 0 - u\Omega\cos\phi)\,\hat{U}
$$

Simplifying:

$$
\vec{\Omega}\times\vec{V}_r = (w\cos\phi - v\sin\phi)\,\Omega\,\hat{E} + u\,\Omega\sin\phi\,\hat{N} - u\,\Omega\cos\phi\,\hat{U}
$$

For nearly-horizontal marine flows ($w \ll u, v$), the **horizontal Coriolis acceleration** reduces to:

$$
\vec{a}_{cor,\,h} = -2\,\Omega\sin\phi\,(u\,\hat{N} - v\,\hat{E}) = f\,(v\,\hat{E} - u\,\hat{N})
$$

$$
\boxed{\;a_{cor,\,E} = +f\,v,\qquad a_{cor,\,N} = -f\,u,\qquad f = 2\Omega\sin\phi\;}
$$

These two scalar equations are exactly what appears in numerical models of offshore wind and tidal flow.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Hemispheric Behaviour

| Phenomenon | Northern Hemisphere ($\phi>0$, $f>0$) | Southern Hemisphere ($\phi<0$, $f<0$) | Maritime Renewable Relevance |
|------------|----------------------------------------|--------------------------------------|------------------------------|
| Deflection of wind | Right of motion | Left of motion | Wind-farm layout |
| Surface current vs. wind | 45° right (Ekman) | 45° left (Ekman) | Wave-energy resource |
| Ocean gyre rotation | Clockwise | Counter-clockwise | Current-energy extraction |
| Trade-wind belt | NE Trades | SE Trades | Sailing-vessel PV propulsion |
| Upwelling coast | Eastern boundary | Western boundary | OTEC nutrient supply |
| Inertial period at 30° | ~23.93 h | ~23.93 h | Floater natural period |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (For Examiner-Style Practice)

> **Problem:** A tidal-stream turbine is to be deployed at latitude $\phi = 12^\circ\,\text{N}$ in the Arabian Sea. The peak tidal velocity is $v = 1.8\ \text{m/s}$. Compute (a) the Coriolis parameter $f$, (b) the Coriolis acceleration on a 1 kg water parcel, and (c) the direction of deflection.

**Solution:**

(a) $f = 2\Omega\sin\phi = 2(7.2921\times10^{-5})\sin 12^\circ$

$$
f = 1.4584\times10^{-4}\times 0.2079 = 3.032\times10^{-5}\ \text{rad/s}
$$

(b) $a_{cor} = f\,v = (3.032\times10^{-5})(1.8) = 5.458\times10^{-5}\ \text{m/s}^2$

(c) Since $\phi > 0$, the parcel deflects to the **right** of its direction of motion.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**Define Coriolis force. Why is it classified as a fictitious force, and how does its magnitude vary with latitude?**

**Model Answer (≈ 90 words):**

> Coriolis force is the inertial (fictitious) force that appears when motion is observed from a rotating reference frame such as the Earth. It is fictitious because no physical agent pushes the fluid sideways — the deflection is purely a kinematic consequence of the rotating observer. Its magnitude is $F_{cor} = 2\,m\,\Omega\,v\,\sin\phi$, so it is **zero at the equator**, **maximum at the poles**, and reverses sign between the hemispheres.

**(3 marks — 1 mark definition, 1 mark fictitious reasoning, 1 mark latitude dependence.)**

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
**Explain the concept of the Coriolis parameter $f$. State its value at $\phi = 10^\circ$ N and comment on its importance for offshore renewable-energy systems.**

**Model Answer (≈ 95 words):**

> The Coriolis parameter $f = 2\,\Omega\sin\phi$ is the projection of the Earth’s angular velocity onto the local vertical. At $\phi = 10^\circ\,\text{N}$:
> $$f = 2(7.2921\times 10^{-5})\sin 10^\circ = 2.532\times 10^{-5}\ \text{rad/s}$$
> It enters the **Ekman layer depth** $D_E = \pi\sqrt{2A_z/f}$, the **geostrophic wind** $V_g = \frac{1}{\rho f}\frac{\partial p}{\partial n}$, and the **Rossby radius** $R_d = \frac{\sqrt{gH}}{f}$. All three are essential when siting offshore wind, tidal, OTEC, and wave-energy systems.

**(3 marks — 1 mark concept, 1 mark numerical value, 1 mark maritime relevance.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Coriolis Force: Theory & Numerical [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derive the expression for the Coriolis force acting on a fluid parcel of mass $m$ moving with velocity $\vec{v}$ in a reference frame rotating with angular velocity $\vec{\Omega}$. Show clearly how the horizontal component reduces to $a_E = fv$ and $a_N = -fu$ in local East–North coordinates.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

1. Start from the absolute-frame acceleration in a rotating frame:
   $$\vec{a}_a = \vec{a}_r + 2\vec{\Omega}\times\vec{V}_r + \vec{\Omega}\times(\vec{\Omega}\times\vec{r}_r)$$
2. Newton’s second law in rotating frame requires adding pseudo-forces:
   $$\sum \vec{F}_{real} - 2m\vec{\Omega}\times\vec{V}_r - m\vec{\Omega}\times(\vec{\Omega}\times\vec{r}_r) = m\vec{a}_r$$
3. The **Coriolis pseudo-force** is therefore:
   $$\boxed{\vec{F}_{cor} = -2m(\vec{\Omega}\times\vec{V}_r)}$$
4. Local ENU angular velocity vector:
   $$\vec{\Omega} = \Omega\cos\phi\,\hat{E} + \Omega\sin\phi\,\hat{U}$$
5. For $\vec{V}_r = u\hat{E}+v\hat{N}+w\hat{U}$, with $w\ll u,v$ for horizontal flow:
   $$\vec{a}_{cor,h} = -2\vec{\Omega}\times\vec{V}_r = f v\,\hat{E} - f u\,\hat{N}$$
6. Hence:
   $$\boxed{a_E = +f v,\quad a_N = -f u,\quad f = 2\Omega\sin\phi}$$

**(7 marks — 2 marks vector derivation, 2 marks ENU decomposition, 2 marks horizontal approximation, 1 mark final boxed result.)**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A research vessel is mapping offshore wind resources near the coast of Gujarat ($\phi = 22^\circ\,\text{N}$). A surface wind of speed $18\ \text{m/s}$ blows due South. Compute (i) the Coriolis parameter, (ii) the magnitude of the Coriolis acceleration per unit mass, and (iii) the resulting deflection direction. Comment on its relevance to the design of an offshore wind farm planned for this latitude.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

(i) Coriolis parameter:
$$
f = 2(7.2921\times 10^{-5})\sin 22^\circ = 1.4584\times 10^{-4}\times 0.3746 = 5.464\times 10^{-5}\ \text{rad/s}
$$

(ii) Magnitude of Coriolis acceleration per unit mass:
$$
a_{cor} = f\,v = (5.464\times 10^{-5})(18) = 9.84\times 10^{-4}\ \text{m/s}^2
$$

(iii) Direction: Northern Hemisphere $\Rightarrow$ deflection to the **right of motion**. Since wind blows South, the Coriolis force pushes it towards the **West**, i.e., the wind veers clockwise (backing in Southern Hemisphere).

**Engineering comment (1 mark):**
The persistent rightward deflection at $22^\circ$ N produces the **NE monsoon trade winds** during winter and the **SW monsoon** in summer, dominated geostrophically. Wind-farm designers at this latitude must use **VE V90-type directional statistics** that include this veer; ignoring it underestimates the yaw misalignment load by up to **8–12 %**.

**(7 marks — 2 marks each for (i) (ii) (iii) and 1 mark for the engineering comment.)**

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: Ekman Transport & Maritime Renewables [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the Ekman layer and Ekman transport. Show mathematically how the Coriolis force produces a net mass transport at 90° to the surface wind in deep ocean, and discuss its implications for selecting an OTEC plant site.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

1. The **Ekman layer** is the surface ocean boundary layer (≈ 10–100 m deep) in which the wind stress is balanced by the Coriolis force on the resulting current.
2. For steady, homogeneous flow, the momentum balance per unit mass in the Ekman layer is:
   $$A_z\frac{d^2 u}{dz^2} + f v = 0,\qquad A_z\frac{d^2 v}{dz^2} - f u = 0$$
3. With surface boundary condition $u(0)=U_s$, $v(0)=0$ and deep-ocean decay $u,v\to\infty=0$:
   $$u(z) = U_s e^{z/D}\cos\!\left(\tfrac{\pi z}{D} - \tfrac{\pi}{4}\right),\quad v(z) = U_s e^{z/D}\sin\!\left(\tfrac{\pi z}{D} - \tfrac{\pi}{4}\right)$$
4. **Depth-integrated (Ekman) transport** is obtained by integrating across the layer:
   $$M_E = \int_{-\infty}^{0}\rho(u,v)\,dz = \frac{\tau}{\,f\,}\,(-\hat{n})$$
   where $\hat{n}$ is the unit vector pointing 90° to the **right** of the wind in the NH. This proves the **90° deflection**.
5. **Implications for OTEC site selection:**
   - The 90° offshore Ekman transport produces **coastal upwelling** along the *eastern* boundary of ocean basins in the NH (and western boundary in the SH).
   - OTEC plants need a $\Delta T \geq 20^\circ\text{C}$ between warm surface and cold deep water within ~1000 m.
   - Hence OTEC sites are best located on **eastern boundaries in the NH** (e.g., Hawaiian coast, west coast of India during SW monsoon) because Coriolis-driven upwelling naturally supplies the cold deep water.

**(7 marks — 2 marks Ekman derivation, 2 marks 90° result, 1 mark OTEC link, 2 marks site-selection reasoning.)**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A floating tidal-energy platform is being designed for deployment at $\phi = 50^\circ\,\text{S}$. The peak tidal current speed is $2.4\ \text{m/s}$, and the eddy viscosity is $A_z = 0.10\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$. Compute (i) the Coriolis parameter, (ii) the Ekman depth $D_E$, and (iii) the inertial period of the platform. Comment on whether the inertial period resonates with any common ocean swell period.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

(i) Coriolis parameter at $50^\circ$ S:
$$
f = 2(7.2921\times 10^{-5})\sin(-50^\circ) = -1.116\times 10^{-4}\ \text{rad/s}
$$
(Magnitude $|f| = 1.116\times 10^{-4}\ \text{rad/s}$.)

(ii) Ekman depth:
$$
D_E = \pi\sqrt{\dfrac{2 A_z}{\vert f\vert}} = \pi\sqrt{\dfrac{2(0.10)}{1.116\times 10^{-4}}}
$$
$$
D_E = \pi\sqrt{1792} = \pi(42.33) \approx 133.0\ \text{m}
$$

(iii) Inertial period:
$$
T_i = \dfrac{2\pi}{\vert f\vert} = \dfrac{2\pi}{1.116\times 10^{-4}} \approx 56{,}300\ \text{s} \approx 15.6\ \text{h}
$$

**Comment on resonance (1 mark):**
The inertial period of ~15.6 h is **far longer** than typical ocean swell periods (8–18 s) and wind-sea periods (3–10 s), so there is **no risk of inertial resonance** with surface waves. However, the inertial period is comparable to **semidiurnal tidal constituents (M2 ≈ 12.42 h, S2 ≈ 12.0 h)** — engineers must therefore ensure that the platform’s natural mooring period does *not* match either $T_i$ or $M_2/S_2$, otherwise large-amplitude lateral motion may build up over successive cycles.

**(7 marks — 2 marks each for (i)(ii)(iii), 1 mark for resonance comment.)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner’s Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on Coriolis-force questions — read carefully before you write your exam!**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign:** The Coriolis force is $\vec{F} = -2m(\vec{\Omega}\times\vec{v})$. Many students drop the minus sign and end up deflecting winds to the *left* in the Northern Hemisphere — instant 2-mark penalty.
> 2. **Conflating Coriolis with centrifugal:** Centrifugal force points *outward* along the rotation axis projection, while Coriolis is *perpendicular to motion*. Mixing these up costs the full 7 marks in derivation questions.
> 3. **Wrong units of $f$:** $f$ is in rad/s, not per second. Examiners deduct marks if you write “Hz” or omit units.
> 4. **Skipping the ENU decomposition:** When the question asks for *horizontal* components $a_E = f v$, $a_N = -f u$, you must show the cross-product expansion. Writing only $f = 2\Omega\sin\phi$ without deriving the components forfeits 3–4 marks.
> 5. **Treating Coriolis as doing work:** Coriolis is always perpendicular to velocity, so it does **zero work**. Students who say it “slows down the current” lose a mark on conceptual questions.
> 6. **Forgetting latitude-dependence in numerical problems:** Always substitute $\phi$ in degrees after converting to radians in your calculator ($\sin$ accepts degrees only if your calculator is in DEG mode). Examiner reports repeatedly cite this as a top-3 mistake.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Coriolis force is fictitious** — it appears only in a rotating (non-inertial) frame.
- **Vector form:** $\vec{F}_{cor} = -2m(\vec{\Omega}\times\vec{v})$.
- **Magnitude:** $F_{cor} = 2\,m\,\Omega\,v\,\sin\phi$.
- **Coriolis parameter:** $f = 2\Omega\sin\phi$, $\Omega = 7.2921\times 10^{-5}\ \text{rad/s}$.
- **Horizontal acceleration components (ENU):** $a_E = +fv$, $a_N = -fu$.
- **Direction rule:** Right in NH, Left in SH, Zero at equator (in horizontal plane).
- **Ekman transport** is 90° to the surface wind (right in NH, left in SH).
- **Ocean gyres:** Clockwise in NH, counter-clockwise in SH.
- **Trade winds:** NE Trades in NH, SE Trades in SH.
- **Inertial period:** $T_i = \dfrac{2\pi}{\vert f\vert} = \dfrac{\pi}{\Omega\vert\sin\phi\vert}$.
- **Rossby radius:** $R_d = \dfrac{\sqrt{gH}}{f}$ — sets scale of ocean gyres.
- **Maritime renewable impact:** Affects offshore wind, tidal stream, OTEC upwelling, wave refraction, and floating-platform mooring design.
- **Work done by Coriolis:** **Zero** (always perpendicular to motion).
- **Common pitfalls:** wrong sign, wrong latitude input units, missing horizontal-component derivation, confusion with centrifugal force.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Examiner Tip:** Always state the *direction* of the Coriolis force *and* give its *magnitude*. A 3-mark question with both earns full credit; a magnitude without direction gets only 2 marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kelvin Waves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST755 - RENEWABLE ENERGY IN MARITIME SECTOR
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Kelvin Waves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Kelvin Waves

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the discipline of geophysical fluid dynamics and coastal ocean engineering, a **Kelvin Wave** is a special class of progressive gravity wave that propagates along a rigid vertical lateral boundary (such as a coastline) in a homogeneous, rotating fluid of constant depth, where the **Coriolis force** is balanced precisely by the **pressure gradient force** in the direction normal (perpendicular) to the boundary.

The defining signature of a Kelvin wave is twofold:

1. **Boundary-trapped propagation:** The wave travels parallel to the coast (or the equator) with a non-dispersive phase speed equal to the classical shallow-water gravity wave speed.
2. **Exponential cross-shore decay:** The wave amplitude decays exponentially with distance from the boundary at a characteristic scale known as the **Rossby Radius of Deformation**, $R_d$.

Mathematically, the phase speed of a Kelvin wave is given by:

$$c = \sqrt{gH}$$

where $g$ is the acceleration due to gravity ($\approx 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$) and $H$ is the mean undisturbed water depth. This speed is **independent of the wave frequency**, making the Kelvin wave a non-dispersive wave system — a critical property for tidal and tsunami coastal energy assessments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Kelvin waves are essential in the maritime renewable energy syllabus because they govern the transport of tidal energy along coastlines, directly influencing the placement and efficiency of **tidal stream turbines** and **tidal barrage systems**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy and Physical Intuition

Imagine a rectangular rotating swimming pool (a classic turntable experiment). If you disturb the water at one end, the water does not slosh back and forth uniformly across the pool. Instead, because the pool is **rotating**, the water is forced to hug the walls. A wave bump traveling along the wall appears to be "stuck" to it, even though the wall is perfectly straight and vertical.

This is precisely how a Kelvin wave behaves:

- The **Coriolis force** (an apparent force due to Earth's rotation) acts to push the water to the right of its motion in the Northern Hemisphere.
- The **coastline acts as a physical wall**, providing a pressure gradient that exactly opposes the Coriolis deflection.
- The result is a wave that slides along the coast, with its energy concentrated in a narrow strip whose width is set by the **Rossby Radius of Deformation** ($R_d$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Earth's Rotation Parameter (Coriolis Parameter):** Defined as $f = 2\Omega \sin(\phi)$, where $\Omega = 7.292 \times 10^{-5} \text{ rad/s}$ is the Earth's angular velocity and $\phi$ is the latitude. For a typical mid-latitude $\phi = 30^\circ$, $f \approx 7.29 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s}^{-1}$.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Magnitude | Engineering Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gravity | $g$ | $9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ | Sets baseline wave speed |
| Mean Ocean Depth | $H$ | $100 - 4000 \text{ m}$ | Determines phase speed $c$ |
| Coriolis Parameter (mid-latitude) | $f$ | $\sim 10^{-4} \text{ s}^{-1}$ | Sets trapping scale $R_d$ |
| Rossby Radius | $R_d = \sqrt{gH}/f$ | $30 - 3000 \text{ km}$ | Coastal energy band width |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Framework

The Kelvin wave solution emerges from the linearized shallow-water equations on an **f-plane** (constant Coriolis parameter). The logic proceeds in a sequence of physical and mathematical postulates:

### Step 1 — Establish the Governing Equations
For a thin homogeneous fluid layer of mean depth $H$, rotating with constant Coriolis parameter $f$, the linearized shallow-water equations are:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} - f v = -g \frac{\partial \eta}{\partial x} \quad \text{(x-momentum)}$$

$$\frac{\partial v}{\partial t} + f u = -g \frac{\partial \eta}{\partial y} \quad \text{(y-momentum)}$$

$$\frac{\partial \eta}{\partial t} + H \left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} \right) = 0 \quad \text{(continuity)}$$

Here $u(x,y,t)$ and $v(x,y,t)$ are the depth-averaged horizontal velocity components, and $\eta(x,y,t)$ is the free-surface elevation.

### Step 2 — Apply the Coastal Boundary Constraint
For a straight coastline along the x-axis at $y = 0$, with the ocean occupying $y > 0$, the no-normal-flow condition requires:

$$v(x, 0, t) = 0$$

This is the **rigid wall boundary condition**.

### Step 3 — Assume a Wave Form
Seek a wave-like solution propagating along the x-axis, with a cross-shore amplitude structure:

$$u, v, \eta \propto e^{i(kx - \omega t)} \cdot F(y)$$

### Step 4 — Enforce the Geostrophic Cross-Shore Balance
A Kelvin wave satisfies the **geostrophic balance** exactly in the y-direction:

$$f u = -g \frac{\partial \eta}{\partial y}$$

This balance is the heart of the Kelvin wave physics. It says that the Coriolis force (acting on the alongshore current $u$) is exactly opposed by the cross-shore slope of the free surface.

### Step 5 — Solve for the Cross-Shore Structure
The above constraint forces $v = 0$ everywhere (not just at the wall), and the cross-shore profile of $\eta$ and $u$ must be:

$$\eta(x, y, t) = \eta_0 \, e^{-y/R_d} \cos(kx - \omega t)$$

$$u(x, y, t) = \eta_0 \, \frac{g}{f} \, e^{-y/R_d} \cos(kx - \omega t)$$

The exponential decay constant $R_d = \sqrt{gH}/f$ is the **Rossby Radius of Deformation**.

### Step 6 — Extract the Dispersion Relation
Substituting the above solutions back into the governing equations yields:

$$\omega^2 = g H k^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad c = \frac{\omega}{k} = \sqrt{gH}$$

This is the famous **Kelvin wave dispersion relation** — linear, non-dispersive, and identical to a non-rotating shallow-water gravity wave.

### Step 7 — Interpret the "Why"
- The rotation does **not** change the phase speed but **does** trap the wave to the coast.
- The trapping width depends on the depth $H$ (deeper ocean $\Rightarrow$ wider trapping) and the rotation rate $f$ (stronger rotation $\Rightarrow$ narrower trapping).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** The following table compiles every equation, boundary condition, and unit you must memorize for a KTU question on Kelvin waves. The use of $\vert$ (instead of the pipe symbol) ensures the LaTeX renders correctly inside markdown tables.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Definitions & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Phase Speed | $c = \sqrt{gH}$ | $g$ in $\text{m/s}^2$, $H$ in $\text{m}$, $c$ in $\text{m/s}$ |
| 2 | Dispersion Relation | $\omega = k\sqrt{gH}$ | $\omega$ in $\text{rad/s}$, $k$ in $\text{rad/m}$ |
| 3 | Coriolis Parameter | $f = 2\Omega \sin\phi$ | $\Omega = 7.292 \times 10^{-5} \text{ rad/s}$, $\phi$ latitude |
| 4 | Rossby Radius | $R_d = \sqrt{gH}/\vert f \vert$ | $R_d$ in metres |
| 5 | Surface Elevation | $\eta = \eta_0 \, e^{-y/R_d} \cos(kx - \omega t)$ | $\eta_0$ amplitude in $\text{m}$ |
| 6 | Alongshore Velocity | $u = (g/f) \, \eta_0 \, e^{-y/R_d} \cos(kx - \omega t)$ | $u$ in $\text{m/s}$ |
| 7 | Cross-shore Velocity | $v \equiv 0$ | (Identically zero everywhere) |
| 8 | Geostrophic Balance | $f u = -g \, \partial \eta / \partial y$ | Defines the trapping mechanism |
| 9 | Coastal BC | $v(x, 0, t) = 0$ | No normal flow at the wall |
| 10 | Wave Period | $T = 2\pi / \omega$ | $T$ in seconds |
| 11 | Wavelength | $\lambda = 2\pi / k$ | $\lambda$ in metres |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Kelvin waves are not mere theoretical curiosities — they drive several marine renewable energy phenomena:

- **Tidal Energy Resource Mapping:** Tidal waves entering a basin (e.g., the Bay of Fundy, Bristol Channel) propagate as Kelvin waves, and their energy is concentrated near the coast within a band of width $R_d$. This directly guides the siting of **tidal stream turbines**.
- **Storm Surge Forecasting:** Storm surges are essentially forced Kelvin waves. Predicting the surge height $\eta$ at the coast requires the exact exponential decay model.
- **Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion (OTEC) Intake/Outlet Design:** Coastal Kelvin wave dynamics set the alongshore current speeds that determine heat exchange near OTEC plant sites.
- **Equatorial Upwelling for Offshore Wind/Bio-energy:** Equatorial Kelvin waves (a similar mechanism at the equator where $f \to 0$) drive deep nutrient-rich water upward, supporting marine biomass — relevant to **blue energy** food chains.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Coastal Kelvin Wave Solution

We begin with the linearized shallow-water equations on an f-plane:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} - f v = -g \frac{\partial \eta}{\partial x} \tag{1}$$

$$\frac{\partial v}{\partial t} + f u = -g \frac{\partial \eta}{\partial y} \tag{2}$$

$$\frac{\partial \eta}{\partial t} + H \left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} \right) = 0 \tag{3}$$

**Geometric setup:** The coastline is the line $y = 0$, and the ocean occupies the half-plane $y > 0$. The boundary condition is:

$$v(x, 0, t) = 0 \tag{4}$$

**Step 1: Enforce the geostrophic cross-shore balance.**

For a Kelvin wave, the cross-shore momentum equation (2) reduces to a geostrophic balance. We postulate that the inertial term $\partial v / \partial t$ vanishes identically, which forces $v = 0$ everywhere. Substituting $v = 0$ into (2):

$$f u = -g \frac{\partial \eta}{\partial y} \tag{5}$$

**Step 2: Reduce the x-momentum equation.**

With $v = 0$, equation (1) becomes:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = -g \frac{\partial \eta}{\partial x} \tag{6}$$

**Step 3: Reduce the continuity equation.**

With $v = 0$, equation (3) becomes:

$$\frac{\partial \eta}{\partial t} + H \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 0 \tag{7}$$

**Step 4: Differentiate (5) with respect to $y$ and (6) with respect to $t$.**

From (5):

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = -\frac{g}{f} \frac{\partial^2 \eta}{\partial y^2} \tag{8}$$

Differentiating (6) with respect to time:

$$\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2} = -g \frac{\partial^2 \eta}{\partial x \partial t} \tag{9}$$

**Step 5: Use (7) to eliminate the time derivative of $\eta$.**

From (7):

$$\frac{\partial \eta}{\partial t} = -H \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} \tag{10}$$

Substituting (10) into (9):

$$\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2} = g H \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} \tag{11}$$

**Step 6: Recognize the classical 1-D wave equation.**

Equation (11) is the standard 1-D wave equation with phase speed:

$$c = \sqrt{gH} \tag{12}$$

**Step 7: Solve the wave equation via separation of variables.**

Assume a solution of the form:

$$u(x, y, t) = U(y) \, e^{i(kx - \omega t)} \tag{13}$$

Substituting into (11) gives the dispersion relation:

$$-\omega^2 U = -gH k^2 U \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega^2 = gH k^2 \tag{14}$$

Taking the positive root (wave propagating in the $+x$ direction):

$$\omega = k \sqrt{gH} \tag{15}$$

**Step 8: Determine the cross-shore structure $U(y)$.**

Substituting the assumed form into the geostrophic balance (5):

$$f U(y) = -g \frac{d \eta_0(y)}{d y} \tag{16}$$

From the wave equation solution, $\eta$ has the same harmonic time-dependence as $u$. We write:

$$\eta(x, y, t) = \Phi(y) \, e^{i(kx - \omega t)} \tag{17}$$

Equation (7) gives:

$$-i \omega \Phi + H (i k) U = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad U = \frac{\omega}{k H} \Phi = \frac{\Phi}{\sqrt{H/g}} \tag{18}$$

Thus:

$$\eta = \sqrt{H/g} \, u \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Phi = \sqrt{H/g} \, U \tag{19}$$

Substituting into (16):

$$f U = -g \frac{d}{dy}\left[ \sqrt{H/g} \, U \right] = -g \sqrt{H/g} \, \frac{dU}{dy} = -\sqrt{gH} \, \frac{dU}{dy} \tag{20}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{dU}{dy} = -\frac{f}{\sqrt{gH}} U = -\frac{U}{R_d} \tag{21}$$

where the **Rossby Radius of Deformation** is:

$$R_d = \frac{\sqrt{gH}}{f} \tag{22}$$

**Step 9: Integrate to obtain the exponential decay.**

Equation (21) is a first-order ODE with solution:

$$U(y) = U_0 \, e^{-y/R_d} \tag{23}$$

Combined with (13) and (19), the full real-valued solutions are:

$$\boxed{\eta(x, y, t) = \eta_0 \, e^{-y/R_d} \cos(kx - \omega t)} \tag{24}$$

$$\boxed{u(x, y, t) = \frac{g}{f} \eta_0 \, e^{-y/R_d} \cos(kx - \omega t)} \tag{25}$$

$$v(x, y, t) \equiv 0 \tag{26}$$

**Step 10: Verify the boundary condition (4).**

At $y = 0$, the boundary condition requires $v = 0$. Since $v \equiv 0$ everywhere by construction, the condition is automatically satisfied. The exponential factor $e^{-y/R_d}$ at $y = 0$ equals $1$, giving maximum amplitude at the coast and decaying offshore.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Calculation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A coastal location at latitude $\phi = 30^\circ \text{ N}$ has an offshore water depth of $H = 100 \text{ m}$. A tidal Kelvin wave of amplitude $\eta_0 = 0.5 \text{ m}$ and wavelength $\lambda = 500 \text{ km}$ propagates poleward along the coast. Compute (a) the phase speed, (b) the Rossby radius, and (c) the wave amplitude at $y = 200 \text{ km}$ offshore.

**Solution:**

**(a) Phase speed:**

$$c = \sqrt{gH} = \sqrt{9.81 \times 100} = \sqrt{981} \approx 31.32 \text{ m/s}$$

**(b) Rossby radius of deformation:**

First, compute $f$:

$$f = 2\Omega \sin\phi = 2 \times 7.292 \times 10^{-5} \times \sin(30^\circ) = 1.4584 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.5$$

$$f = 7.292 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s}^{-1}$$

Now:

$$R_d = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{31.32}{7.292 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 4.295 \times 10^{5} \text{ m} \approx 429.5 \text{ km}$$

**(c) Amplitude at $y = 200 \text{ km}$:**

$$\eta(200 \text{ km}) = 0.5 \times e^{-200/429.5} = 0.5 \times e^{-0.4657}$$

$$= 0.5 \times 0.6277 \approx 0.314 \text{ m}$$

The amplitude has dropped to about 63% of its coastal value at 200 km offshore.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Kelvin Wave Physical and Mathematical Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Coastal Kelvin Wave Setup] --> setupB[Define Geometry: Coast at y=0, Ocean y>0]
    setupB --> govC[Governing Linearized Shallow Water Equations]
    govC --> bcD[Apply Boundary Condition v x,0,t = 0]
    bcD --> geostrophicE[Enforce Geostrophic Cross Shore Balance fu = -g partial eta by partial y]
    geostrophicF{v vanishes identically?}
    geostrophicF -->|Yes| trapG[Consequence: Wave is Trapped to the Coast]
    geostrophicF -->|No| exitH[Not a Kelvin Wave Solution]
    trapG --> assumpI[Assume Wave Form exp i kx - omega t times F y]
    assumpI --> dispJ[Derive Dispersion Relation omega = k sqrt gH]
    dispJ --> phaseK[Phase Speed c = sqrt gH independent of k and f]
    dispJ --> odeL[Solve Cross Shore ODE dU by dy = -U by R_d]
    odeL --> rossbyM[Define Rossby Radius R_d = sqrt gH over f]
    rossbyM --> finalN[Exponential Decay Solution: eta = eta_0 exp -y by R_d cos kx - omega t]
    finalN --> appO[Engineering Applications]
    appO --> app1[Tidal Stream Turbine Siting]
    appO --> app2[Storm Surge Forecasting]
    appO --> app3[OTEC Coastal Hydraulics]
    appO --> app4[Equatorial Upwelling for Blue Energy]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Coastal Energy Trapping Visualisation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    coastA[Coastline y=0] -->|Max amplitude eta_0| bandB[Energy Band 0 to R_d]
    bandB -->|Exponential decay exp -y by R_d| outerC[Open Ocean y greater than R_d]
    bandC[Phase Speed c = sqrt gH] --> coastalA
    coriolisD[Coriolis Force fu] -->|Balances| pressureE[Pressure Gradient g partial eta by partial y]
    pressureE --> bandB
    windF[Forcing: Wind Tidal or Storm] --> coastalA
```

## 4.3 Schematic Description (Verbal Supplement)

The diagrams above translate the mathematical content into a process flow. The first diagram shows the logical deduction chain: from the geometry setup to the governing equations, the boundary condition, the geostrophic balance, the assumption of a wave form, the dispersion relation, the cross-shore ODE, the definition of the Rossby radius, and finally the closed-form exponential-decay solution with its engineering applications. The second diagram is a stylized representation of the physical trapping mechanism — the coast at $y = 0$ experiences maximum amplitude, the energy is concentrated within a band of width $R_d$, and the Coriolis force balances the cross-shore pressure gradient. Forcing mechanisms such as wind, tidal input, or storm action inject energy into this coastal waveguide.
