# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**State and explain the Betz limit for a wind turbine. Why is it less than 100%?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Betz limit states that **no wind turbine can extract more than 16/27 (≈ 59.3%) of the kinetic energy** present in the wind stream. It is derived from actuator disk theory by maximizing the power coefficient Cp = 4a(1-a)² with respect to the axial induction factor 'a', which yields a = 1/3 and Cp,max = 16/27.

It is less than 100% because:
- **(1 mark)** Complete energy extraction would require the air to come to a complete stop behind the rotor, which would prevent new air from entering (violating mass conservation).
- **(1 mark)** A practical rotor must allow some kinetic energy to remain in the wake to maintain mass flow continuity (Bernoulli + Euler momentum balance).
- **(1 mark)** Real turbines suffer additional aerodynamic, mechanical, and electrical losses, reducing Cp further to 0.35–0.45.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define Tip Speed Ratio (TSR) and explain its significance in wind turbine design.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Tip Speed Ratio (λ)** is the ratio of the blade tip linear velocity to the free-stream wind velocity:

$$\lambda = \frac{\Omega R}{V_\infty} = \frac{2\pi R N}{60 \, V_\infty}$$

- **(1 mark)** Significance — λ determines the operating point on the Cp–λ curve. Each rotor design has an optimal λ (typically 7–9 for 3-bladed HAWTs) at which Cp is maximized.
- **(1 mark)** Low-λ rotors (λ ≈ 1–3) have many blades and high starting torque (e.g., traditional Dutch windmills).
- **(1 mark)** High-λ rotors (λ ≈ 7–9) have few blades, lower starting torque, but higher rotational speed and efficiency — ideal for electricity generation, including offshore maritime applications.

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive an expression for the power extracted by an ideal horizontal-axis wind turbine using actuator disk theory. Show that the maximum power coefficient is **16/27**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A three-bladed horizontal-axis wind turbine of rotor diameter 60 m operates at a wind speed of 10 m/s. Air density is 1.225 kg/m³. Assuming the turbine operates at the Betz optimum, calculate: (i) the rotor RPM at λ = 8, (ii) the available wind power, (iii) the maximum extractable power, and (iv) the thrust force on the rotor. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a) — 7 marks**

**(1 mark)** Define the actuator disk control volume with three sections: upstream (1), at the disk (2), and downstream wake (3). State that the disk is frictionless and porous.

**(1 mark)** Write mass conservation: $\dot{m} = \rho A V_\infty = \rho A V_w$ (for 1D actuator theory with A = A_disk).

**(1 mark)** Introduce the axial induction factor: $V_{disk} = V_\infty (1-a)$, $V_w = V_\infty (1-2a)$.

**(1 mark)** Apply Bernoulli's equation upstream and downstream of the disk to obtain the pressure drop:

$$\Delta P = \frac{1}{2} \rho (V_\infty^2 - V_w^2) = \frac{1}{2} \rho V_\infty^2 \cdot 4a(1-a)$$

**(1 mark)** Compute the thrust: $F_T = \Delta P \cdot A = 2\rho A V_\infty^2 \cdot a(1-a)$.

**(1 mark)** Compute power: $P = F_T \cdot V_{disk} = 2\rho A V_\infty^3 \cdot a(1-a)^2$. Form Cp:

$$C_p = \frac{P}{\frac{1}{2} \rho A V_\infty^3} = 4a(1-a)^2$$

**(1 mark)** Maximize Cp: $\frac{dC_p}{da} = 4(1-a)(1-3a) = 0 \Rightarrow a = 1/3$. Substituting gives:

$$C_{p,\max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926 \quad \text{(Betz Limit)}$$

---

#### **MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b) — 7 marks**

Given: D = 60 m → **R = 30 m**, V∞ = 10 m/s, ρ = 1.225 kg/m³, λ = 8, a = 1/3.

**(i) Rotor RPM at λ = 8 (2 marks):**

$$\Omega = \frac{\lambda V_\infty}{R} = \frac{8 \times 10}{30} = 2.667 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$N = \frac{\Omega \times 60}{2\pi} = \frac{2.667 \times 60}{2\pi} = \frac{160}{2\pi} \approx 25.46 \text{ RPM}$$

**(ii) Available wind power (2 marks):**

$$A = \pi R^2 = \pi (30)^2 = 2827.43 \text{ m}^2$$

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V_\infty^3 = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 2827.43 \times (10)^3$$

$$= 0.6125 \times 2827.43 \times 1000 = 1,731,801 \text{ W} \approx 1.732 \text{ MW}$$

**(iii) Maximum extractable power (2 marks):**

$$P_{max} = C_{p,\max} \cdot P_{wind} = \frac{16}{27} \times 1.732 \text{ MW}$$

$$P_{max} \approx 0.5926 \times 1.732 \approx 1.026 \text{ MW}$$

**(iv) Thrust force on the rotor (1 mark):**

$$F_T = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V_\infty^2 \cdot 4a(1-a)$$

$$= \frac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 2827.43 \times 100 \times 4 \times \frac{1}{3} \times \frac{2}{3}$$

$$= 173,180 \times \frac{8}{9} = 173,180 \times 0.8889 \approx 153,938 \text{ N} \approx 154 \text{ kN}$$

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **Blade Element Momentum (BEM) theory** for wind turbines. With the help of a velocity triangle, derive expressions for the angle of attack, lift, drag, and the resultant forces on a blade element. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 1.5 MW offshore wind turbine operates at a wind speed of 14 m/s with a rotor diameter of 77 m. The actual power coefficient is 0.42. Determine: (i) the tip speed ratio if the rotor spins at 18 RPM, (ii) the actual power output, (iii) the total aerodynamic thrust if the thrust coefficient Ct = 0.8, and (iv) the **Betz efficiency ratio** (actual Cp / theoretical Cp,max). **(7 marks)**

---

#### **MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a) — 7 marks**

**(1 mark)** Introduction to BEM theory: BEM combines momentum theory (global forces on the actuator disk) with blade element theory (local 2D airfoil analysis) and is the industry standard for wind turbine blade design.

**(1 mark)** Velocity triangle at radius r:
- Axial induced velocity: $V_{ax} = V_\infty (1-a)$
- Tangential induced velocity: $V_{tan} = \Omega r (1+a')$
- Relative wind speed: $W = \sqrt{V_{ax}^2 + V_{tan}^2}$
- Flow angle: $\tan \phi = \frac{V_{ax}}{V_{tan}}$

**(1 mark)** Angle of attack relation: $\alpha = \phi - \theta$, where θ is the local blade twist (pitch) angle.

**(1 mark)** Lift force per unit span: $dL = \frac{1}{2} \rho W^2 c \, C_L \, dr$.

**(1 mark)** Drag force per unit span: $dD = \frac{1}{2} \rho W^2 c \, C_D \, dr$.

**(1 mark)** Resolution into thrust and torque:
- Thrust: $dF_T = dL \cos\phi + dD \sin\phi$
- Torque: $dF_Q = (dL \sin\phi - dD \cos\phi) \cdot r$

**(1 mark)** Integration across the blade span from root (r_hub) to tip (R) gives total rotor thrust and torque. The induction factors a and a' are solved iteratively (typically using the Glauert or Buhl correction for high-induction cases).

---

#### **MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b) — 7 marks**

Given: P_rated = 1.5 MW, V∞ = 14 m/s, D = 77 m, Cp = 0.42, Ct = 0.8, N = 18 RPM.

**(i) Tip Speed Ratio (2 marks):**

$$R = D/2 = 38.5 \text{ m}$$

$$\Omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 18}{60} = 1.885 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\lambda = \frac{\Omega R}{V_\infty} = \frac{1.885 \times 38.5}{14} = \frac{72.57}{14} \approx 5.18$$

**(ii) Actual power output (2 marks):**

$$A = \pi R^2 = \pi (38.5)^2 = 4656.6 \text{ m}^2$$

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 4656.6 \times (14)^3 = 0.6125 \times 4656.6 \times 2744$$

$$P_{wind} \approx 7.83 \text{ MW}$$

$$P_{actual} = C_p \cdot P_{wind} = 0.42 \times 7.83 \approx 3.29 \text{ MW}$$

*(Note: The rated power is 1.5 MW — this would mean the turbine is pitch-limited and not actually capturing the full Cp=0.42 available. The student should note this.)*

**(iii) Aerodynamic thrust (1 mark):**

$$F_T = C_T \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho A V_\infty^2 = 0.8 \times \frac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 4656.6 \times 196$$

$$= 0.8 \times 559,447 \approx 447,558 \text{ N} \approx 447.6 \text{ kN}$$

**(iv) Betz efficiency ratio (2 marks):**

$$\eta_{Betz} = \frac{C_p}{C_{p,\max}} = \frac{0.42}{16/27} = \frac{0.42}{0.5926} \approx 0.7088 \text{ or } 70.88\%$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout**
>
> **🚫 Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting ρ:** Many students write $\frac{1}{2} A V^3$ without including ρ = 1.225 kg/m³. **Always include air density.**
> 2. **Swept area error:** Using D² instead of (D/2)² in $A = \pi R^2$. The diameter must be halved to obtain radius first.
> 3. **Mixing up units:** Writing RPM when rad/s is expected, or omitting $2\pi/60$ conversion. Be explicit.
> 4. **Betz limit precision:** Always state $\frac{16}{27}$ in fractional form AND as 0.5926. Do not write "about 60%" — this loses precision marks.
> 5. **Sign errors in BEM:** Remembering that $V_{tan} = \Omega r (1 + a')$ (NOT $1 - a'$). The tangential induction factor adds to tangential velocity because the wake rotates.
> 6. **Confusing Ct and Cp formulas:** $C_T = 4a(1-a)$ (linear), $C_p = 4a(1-a)^2$ (cubic). Different formulas — memorize both.

---

## 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Aerodynamics of Wind Turbines**

### ✅ Conceptual Mastery
- ☑ Wind turbine aerodynamics = **fluid mechanics applied to energy extraction** from moving air.
- ☑ The **Betz limit (16/27 ≈ 0.5926)** is the absolute theoretical maximum Cp for any wind turbine.
- ☑ **Actuator Disk Theory** is the 1D simplified model used to derive Betz's law.
- ☑ **Blade Element Momentum (BEM) Theory** is the practical design tool for real blades.

### ✅ Critical Formulas (Memorize for KTU)
- ☑ $P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V_\infty^3$
- ☑ $C_p = 4a(1-a)^2$ and $C_T = 4a(1-a)$
- ☑ $\lambda = \frac{\Omega R}{V_\infty} = \frac{2\pi R N}{60 V_\infty}$
- ☑ Betz optimum at $a = 1/3$, $V_{disk} = (2/3)V_\infty$, $V_w = (1/3)V_\infty$
- ☑ Lift: $L = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S C_L$, Drag: $D = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S C_D$
- ☑ Angle of attack: $\alpha = \phi - \theta$

### ✅ Standard Numerical Values
- ☑ Air density ρ ≈ **1.225 kg/m³** (sea-level maritime)
- ☑ Optimal TSR for 3-bladed HAWT: **λ_opt ≈ 7–9**
- ☑ Real-world Cp: **0.35–0.45** (vs. 0.5926 theoretical)
- ☑ Offshore cut-in speed: **3–4 m/s**, cut-out: **25 m/s**

### ✅ Maritime Engineering Context (PENST755)
- ☑ Offshore wind = **higher mean wind speeds** → higher capacity factor.
- ☑ Floating offshore turbines face **combined aerodynamic + hydrodynamic + aeroelastic** loads.
- ☑ Wake interactions in offshore farms reduce downstream Cp by **10–20%**.
- ☑ Blade tip speeds must be **subsonic** (~80 m/s max) to avoid compressibility losses and noise.

### ✅ KTU Exam-Specific Tips
- ☑ Always **state assumptions** (1D flow, incompressible, uniform, axial, frictionless disk).
- ☑ Always **show maximization step** explicitly in Betz derivations — examiners allocate 1–2 marks here.
- ☑ Always **include units** in final numerical answers (m/s, N, kW, MW).
- ☑ Draw the **velocity triangle** in BEM questions for full marks.
- ☑ For design problems, justify choice of λ based on application (high λ → high speed generator; low λ → high torque).

---

topics.title: Power Curves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST755 - RENEWABLE ENERGY IN MARITIME SECTOR
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Power Curves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Power Curve** is a graphical/mathematical representation that describes the steady-state electrical power output of a renewable energy conversion device as a function of the available primary energy resource (wind speed, wave height, tidal current velocity, solar irradiance, etc.) under standard test conditions.

In the maritime renewable energy context, the term **Power Curve** most prominently refers to the **Wind Turbine Power Curve** (IEC 61400-12-1 standard), which relates the **active electrical power output $P$** at the grid connection point to the **hub-height wind speed $v$** for a specific turbine model.

Mathematically, it is a function of the form:

$$P = f(v)$$

where:
- $P$ = electrical power output in **Watts (W)** or **kW** or **MW**
- $v$ = free-stream wind speed in **m/s** at hub height

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Maritime Bucket Analogy"

Imagine a **sailor filling buckets with seawater** using a hand pump driven by a windmill:

- When wind is too gentle (calm sea), the sail cannot fill the bucket — the pump does nothing.
- As wind picks up, the sailor starts filling the bucket **slowly**.
- There is a wind speed at which the bucket begins receiving water regularly — this is the **cut-in speed**.
- Beyond a certain wind speed, the bucket **overflows** because the pump delivers more than the bucket can hold — the surplus is wasted, and the turbine is **derated** to a fixed **rated power**.
- In a violent storm, the sailor **drops the bucket entirely** (cuts the rope) to save the boat — this is the **cut-out speed**, where the turbine is shut down for safety.

> [!NOTE]
> The Power Curve is essentially the **"filling rate"** of the energy bucket as a function of the strength of the driving wind (or wave/tide).

### 1.3 Importance in the Maritime Renewable Energy Sector

The power curve is the **single most important performance descriptor** of any renewable energy converter because:

1. **Energy Yield Estimation**: It is the input to annual energy production (AEP) calculations for offshore wind farms, floating PV, wave and tidal farms.
2. **Turbine Selection**: Ship designers and port microgrid planners select turbines whose power curve matches the local resource profile (e.g., trade-wind belts, monsoon seas).
3. **Economic Viability**: The shape of the curve directly determines the **capacity factor** and the **levelized cost of energy (LCOE)**.
4. **Grid/Microgrid Stability**: Onboard ship microgrids or offshore platform microgrids rely on predictable power output for diesel hybridization.
5. **Compliance & Certification**: Classification societies (DNV, ABS, Lloyd's Register) require validated power curves before approving marine renewable installations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Condition**: Power curves are typically measured and reported at **standard air density $\rho_0 = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$** and **turbulence intensity $I = 0.10$** as per **IEC 61400-12-1**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Wind Turbine Power Curve

The wind turbine power curve is conventionally divided into **four operating regions**, defined by **three characteristic wind speeds**.

| Region | Name | Wind Speed Range | Behaviour |
|--------|------|------------------|-----------|
| **I** | Standstill / Cut-in region | $0 \le v < v_{in}$ | No rotation; $P = 0$ |
| **II** | Partial Load (Cubic) Region | $v_{in} \le v < v_{r}$ | $P$ follows cube law; $P < P_{r}$ |
| **III** | Rated (Full Load) Plateau | $v_{r} \le v < v_{out}$ | $P = P_{r}$ (constant, regulated) |
| **IV** | Shutdown Region | $v \ge v_{out}$ | Turbine parked; $P = 0$ |

Typical values for a modern offshore wind turbine:

- **Cut-in speed $v_{in}$**: $3 - 4 \text{ m/s}$
- **Rated speed $v_{r}$**: $11 - 14 \text{ m/s}$
- **Cut-out speed $v_{out}$**: $25 \text{ m/s}$

### 2.2 The Aerodynamic Power — Cube Law

The **available wind power** sweeping through rotor area $A$ at wind speed $v$ is:

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3}$$

where $\rho$ is air density. The turbine extracts only a fraction of this, expressed by the **power coefficient $C_P$**:

$$P = C_{P}(\lambda, \beta) \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3}$$

Key concepts:

- **Tip Speed Ratio (TSR)** $\lambda = \dfrac{\omega R}{v}$, where $\omega$ is rotor angular speed and $R$ is rotor radius.
- **Pitch angle $\beta$** is the angle between the blade chord and the plane of rotation; used to limit power at high winds.
- **Betz Limit**: The maximum theoretical $C_P = \dfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926$, achieved when $C_T = \dfrac{8}{9}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real offshore turbines** achieve maximum $C_P$ in the range **$0.45$ to $0.50$** due to mechanical, electrical, and aerodynamic losses.

### 2.3 Analytical Models of the Power Curve

For engineering estimation (without the manufacturer's curve), the **cubic model** in Region II is:

$$P(v) = P_{r} \cdot \frac{v^{k} - v_{in}^{k}}{v_{r}^{k} - v_{in}^{k}}, \quad v_{in} \le v < v_{r}$$

The exponent $k$ is empirically chosen:
- $k = 3$: pure aerodynamic cube law (most common in textbooks)
- $k = 2$: simplified for control design
- $k = 5$: experimental fit for stall-regulated turbines

### 2.4 Weibull Distribution — The Wind Resource Statistic

Since wind varies continuously, the **energy content** is statistically weighted by the **Weibull probability density function (PDF)**:

$$f(v) = \frac{k_{sh}}{c} \left(\frac{v}{c}\right)^{k_{sh}-1} \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{v}{c}\right)^{k_{sh}}\right]$$

- **Shape factor $k_{sh}$** (typically $1.5 - 2.5$ for maritime sites)
- **Scale factor $c$** in **m/s**
- Mean wind speed: $\bar{v} = c \cdot \Gamma\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{k_{sh}}\right)$

### 2.5 Annual Energy Production (AEP)

The energy delivered by a single turbine in one year is:

$$AEP = 8760 \cdot \int_{0}^{\infty} P(v) \cdot f(v)\, dv \quad [\text{kWh}]$$

In practice, the integral is split piecewise across the four regions.

### 2.6 Capacity Factor (CF)

$$C_{F} = \frac{AEP}{8760 \cdot P_{r}}$$

For offshore wind, $C_F$ typically lies between **$0.35$ and $0.55$**.

### 2.7 Power Curves of Other Maritime Renewables

| Device Type | Input Variable | Typical Curve Shape |
|-------------|----------------|---------------------|
| **Wave Energy Converter (WEC)** | Significant wave height $H_s$ | $P \propto H_s^{2.2}$ to $H_s^{2.5}$ |
| **Tidal Stream Turbine** | Tidal current speed $v_t$ | $P \propto v_t^{3}$ (similar to wind) |
| **Tidal Range (Barrage)** | Tidal head $\Delta h$ | $P \propto (\Delta h)^{2}$ |
| **Floating PV** | Solar irradiance $G$ | Linear in $G$ up to rated inverter capacity |

> [!NOTE]
> **All maritime renewable power curves share the same engineering philosophy**: zero output below a threshold, increasing output in a partial-load region, and a rated plateau (where applicable) before shutdown.

### 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units |
|---|-------------------|------------|-------|
| 1 | Wind power in stream | $P_{wind} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A v^{3}$ | W |
| 2 | Power coefficient | $C_P = \dfrac{P}{P_{wind}}$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | Betz limit | $C_{P,\max} = \dfrac{16}{27}$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Tip speed ratio | $\lambda = \dfrac{\omega R}{v}$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | Cubic model (Region II) | $P(v) = P_r \dfrac{v^{k} - v_{in}^{k}}{v_{r}^{k} - v_{in}^{k}}$ | W |
| 6 | Weibull PDF | $f(v) = \dfrac{k_{sh}}{c}\!\left(\dfrac{v}{c}\right)^{k_{sh}-1}\!e^{-(v/c)^{k_{sh}}}$ | (m/s)$^{-1}$ |
| 7 | Mean wind speed | $\bar{v} = c\,\Gamma\!\left(1+\tfrac{1}{k_{sh}}\right)$ | m/s |
| 8 | Annual Energy Production | $AEP = 8760 \int_0^{\infty} P(v) f(v)\,dv$ | kWh |
| 9 | Capacity factor | $C_F = \dfrac{AEP}{8760 \cdot P_r}$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Wave energy flux (per meter crest) | $J = \dfrac{\rho g^{2}}{64\pi} H_s^{2} T_e$ | W/m |
| 11 | Tidal stream power | $P_{tidal} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A v_t^{3}$ | W |
| 12 | Rotor swept area | $A = \pi R^{2}$ | m$^2$ |
| 13 | Air density correction | $\rho_{adj} = \rho_0 \dfrac{P_0}{T_0}\dfrac{T}{P}$ | kg/m$^3$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Available Wind Power and the Betz Limit

**Step 1**: Consider a wind turbine with rotor swept area $A$ in a uniform stream of wind of speed $v_1$ upstream and $v_2$ downstream of the rotor.

**Step 2**: By continuity, the mass flow rate through the rotor disc is:

$$\dot{m} = \rho A \frac{v_1 + v_2}{2}$$

This assumes the velocity at the disc is the average of upstream and downstream velocities.

**Step 3**: The thrust force on the rotor equals the rate of change of momentum of the air:

$$F = \dot{m}(v_1 - v_2) = \rho A \frac{v_1 + v_2}{2}(v_1 - v_2)$$

**Step 4**: The power extracted by the rotor equals thrust times the velocity at the disc:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= F \cdot \frac{v_1 + v_2}{2} \\
  &= \rho A \left(\frac{v_1 + v_2}{2}\right)^{2}(v_1 - v_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5**: Introduce the **axial induction factor** $a$, defined such that the velocity at the disc is:

$$v_{disc} = v_1(1 - a)$$

and downstream:

$$v_2 = v_1(1 - 2a)$$

**Step 6**: Substituting into the power expression:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \rho A \left[v_1(1 - a)\right]^{2} \cdot \left[v_1 - v_1(1 - 2a)\right] \\
  &= \rho A v_1^{3} \cdot (1 - a)^{2} \cdot 2a
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7**: The available wind power upstream is:

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v_1^{3}$$

**Step 8**: Therefore the power coefficient is:

$$C_P = \frac{P}{P_{wind}} = 4a(1 - a)^{2}$$

**Step 9**: To find the maximum, differentiate with respect to $a$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{dC_P}{da} = 4(1 - a)^{2} + 4a \cdot 2(1 - a)(-1) = 4(1 - a)\bigl[(1 - a) - 2a\bigr] = 4(1 - a)(1 - 3a) = 0$$

**Step 10**: Non-trivial root: $a = \dfrac{1}{3}$. Substituting back:

$$C_{P,\max} = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{2} = \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{9} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926$$

This is the **Betz Limit**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Analytical Capacity Factor (Cubic Model + Weibull)

For an idealized turbine where Region II follows a pure cube law, the capacity factor can be derived in **closed form** using the Weibull distribution and the incomplete gamma function.

**Step 1**: Region II power output:

$$P(v) = P_r \cdot \frac{v^{3} - v_{in}^{3}}{v_r^{3} - v_{in}^{3}}, \quad v_{in} \le v \le v_r$$

**Step 2**: Region III power output: $P(v) = P_r$, for $v_r \le v < v_{out}$.

**Step 3**: The mean power output is:

$$\langle P \rangle = \int_{v_{in}}^{v_r} P(v) f(v) \, dv + \int_{v_r}^{v_{out}} P_r f(v) \, dv$$

**Step 4**: Define the **cumulative distribution function** of Weibull:

$$F(v) = 1 - e^{-(v/c)^{k_{sh}}}$$

**Step 5**: Using integration by parts and the substitution $x = (v/c)^{k_{sh}}$:

$$\int_{v_{a}}^{v_{b}} v^{n} f(v) \, dv = c^{n} \, \Gamma\!\left(\frac{n}{k_{sh}} + 1, x_a, x_b\right)$$

where $\Gamma(s, x_a, x_b)$ is the **generalized incomplete gamma function** between $x_a$ and $x_b$.

**Step 6**: Therefore, the **capacity factor** in closed form is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_F = \frac{1}{P_r}\left[ \frac{P_r}{v_r^{3} - v_{in}^{3}}\right. & \left[ \int_{v_{in}}^{v_r} v^{3} f(v)\,dv - v_{in}^{3}\bigl(F(v_r) - F(v_{in})\bigr) \right] \\
& \left. + P_r \bigl(F(v_{out}) - F(v_r)\bigr) \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

For KTU exam purposes, this expression is usually **evaluated numerically** rather than in closed form.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem**: A 2 MW offshore wind turbine has $v_{in} = 3$ m/s, $v_r = 12$ m/s, $v_{out} = 25$ m/s. The site has a Weibull distribution with $k_{sh} = 2.0$ and $c = 8$ m/s. Assume the cubic model with $k = 3$.

**(a)** Calculate the power output at $v = 9$ m/s.

**Solution**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(9) &= P_r \cdot \frac{9^{3} - 3^{3}}{12^{3} - 3^{3}} \\
     &= 2000 \cdot \frac{729 - 27}{1728 - 27} \\
     &= 2000 \cdot \frac{702}{1701} \\
     &= 2000 \cdot 0.4127 \\
     &= 825.4 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b)** Find the proportion of time the turbine operates in Region II (i.e., $3 \le v < 12$).

**Solution**: Using the Weibull CDF:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Pr(3 \le v < 12) &= F(12) - F(3) \\
&= \left[1 - e^{-(12/8)^{2}}\right] - \left[1 - e^{-(3/8)^{2}}\right] \\
&= e^{-(0.375)^{2}} - e^{-(1.5)^{2}} \\
&= e^{-0.1406} - e^{-2.25} \\
&= 0.8689 - 0.1054 \\
&= 0.7635
\end{aligned}
$$

So the turbine is in **Region II for 76.35 % of the year**.

**(c)** Compute the capacity factor (numerical integration, step $\Delta v = 1$ m/s):

**Solution**: We sample $v$ from 0 to 25 m/s in steps of 1 m/s. Tabulating:

| $v$ (m/s) | $P(v)$ (kW) | $f(v)$ | $P(v)\cdot f(v)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 3 | 0 | 0.0583 | 0 |
| 4 | 32.6 | 0.0732 | 2.387 |
| 5 | 121.4 | 0.0818 | 9.930 |
| 6 | 269.3 | 0.0841 | 22.640 |
| 7 | 480.4 | 0.0814 | 39.107 |
| 8 | 757.8 | 0.0753 | 57.064 |
| 9 | 825.4 | 0.0670 | 55.302 |
| 10 | 1310.4 | 0.0577 | 75.610 |
| 11 | 1670.0 | 0.0483 | 80.661 |
| 12 | 2000 | 0.0394 | 78.800 |
| 13–24 | 2000 | Σ ≈ 0.107 | 214.0 |
| 25 | 0 | 0.0010 | 0 |

Sum of $P(v) \cdot f(v) \approx \langle P \rangle \approx 635.5$ kW.

Therefore:

$$C_F = \frac{635.5}{2000} = 0.318 \quad \text{or} \quad 31.8\%$$

$$AEP = 8760 \times 635.5 = 5{,}566{,}980 \text{ kWh/year} \approx 5.57 \text{ GWh/year}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Models of $P(v)$

| Model | Equation (Region II) | Best For | Limitation |
|-------|---------------------|----------|------------|
| **Cubic ($k=3$)** | $P = P_r \dfrac{v^{3} - v_{in}^{3}}{v_r^{3} - v_{in}^{3}}$ | Theoretical / educational | Ignores stall, $C_P$ variation |
| **Exponential** | $P = P_r \left(e^{-\beta/v} - e^{-\beta/v_{in}}\right)$ | Empirical fit | Requires curve fitting |
| **Piecewise Linear** | Tabulated $P(v_i)$ | Manufacturer data | Requires full curve |
| **Logistic / Sigmoid** | $P = P_r / \left[1 + e^{-a(v - v_m)}\right]$ | Control system design | Less accurate near edges |
| **Fourth-power** | $P = P_r \left(\dfrac{v - v_{in}}{v_r - v_{in}}\right)^{4}$ | Slow winds, small turbines | Overshoots near $v_r$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Schematic of the Wind Turbine Power Curve (Region Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["WIND SPEED V in m per s"] --> B{"V less than V in"}
    B -- YES --> C["REGION I STANDSTILL P equals 0"]
    B -- NO --> D{"V less than V rated"}
    D -- YES --> E["REGION II PARTIAL LOAD P equals P r times V cubed minus V in cubed over V r cubed minus V in cubed"]
    D -- NO --> F{"V less than V out"}
    F -- YES --> G["REGION III RATED PLATEAU P equals P r PITCH CONTROL ACTIVE"]
    F -- NO --> H["REGION IV SHUTDOWN P equals 0 STORM PROTECTION"]
    E --> I["TORQUE CONTROL C P MAXIMISED AT BETA equals 0"]
    G --> J["PITCH CONTROL BETA INCREASED TO LIMIT P"]
    H --> K["YAW AND BLADE FEATHERED TO 90 DEG"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this Mermaid chart**: The wind speed enters at node `A` and is sequentially tested against three thresholds ($v_{in}$, $v_r$, $v_{out}$). Each decision branch routes to one of the four operating regions. This is the **control logic** that the turbine's Programmable Logic Controller (PLC) executes in real time on every offshore wind turbine.

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Energy Conversion Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INP["MARITIME RESOURCE WIND WAVE TIDE SOLAR"] --> MEAS["RESOURCE MEASUREMENT ANEMOMETER WAVE BUOY TIDE GAUGE PYRANOMETER"]
    MEAS --> PCURVE["POWER CURVE LOOKUP TABLE OR ANALYTIC MODEL P equals f of resource"]
    PCURVE --> CTRL["CONTROL SYSTEM TORQUE AND PITCH REGULATOR"]
    CTRL --> GEN["GENERATOR PMSG OR DFIG"]
    GEN --> CONV["POWER CONVERTER BACK TO BACK VSC"]
    CONV --> GRID["MARITIME MICROGRID SHIP BOARD OR OFFSHORE PLATFORM"]
    GRID --> LOAD["ELECTRICAL LOADS PROPULSION HOTEL AUXILIARIES"]
    PCURVE --> EST["AEP AND CAPACITY FACTOR ESTIMATION MODULE"]
    EST --> OUT["PERFORMANCE REPORTING TO OPERATOR AND KTU MONITORING DASHBOARD"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation**: The Power Curve is the **central nervous system** of the entire energy conversion process. It sits between raw resource measurement and the control system, and simultaneously feeds the AEP/CF estimation module for long-term planning and regulatory reporting.

---

### 4.3 Power Curve Shape with Key Markings (Conceptual Plot)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph axis["POWER OUTPUT P in kW vs WIND SPEED V in m per s"]
        direction LR
        X["V in m per s"] --> X1["0"]
        X1 --> X2["3 V in"]
        X2 --> X3["12 V rated"]
        X3 --> X4["25 V out"]
        Y["P in kW"] --> Y0["0"]
        Y0 --> Y1["P_r = 2000 kW"]
        Y1 --> Ymax["Saturated"]
    end
    REG1["Region I: P = 0"] --- X1
    REG2["Region II: P proportional to V cubed"] --- X2
    REG3["Region III: P = P_r constant plateau"] --- X3
    REG4["Region IV: P = 0"] --- X4
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

*Define the term "power curve" of a wind turbine. List the four characteristic operating regions along with their typical wind speed ranges.*

**Model Answer**:

> A **power curve** of a wind turbine is the graphical or mathematical relationship between the electrical power output (kW) and the free-stream wind speed at hub height (m/s), measured at standard air density $\rho_0 = 1.225$ kg/m$^3$ as per IEC 61400-12-1.

**Four regions**:

1. **Region I — Standstill / Cut-in region**: $0 \le v < v_{in}$ (e.g., 0 to 3 m/s); $P = 0$.
2. **Region II — Partial Load region**: $v_{in} \le v < v_r$ (e.g., 3 to 12 m/s); $P$ increases approximately with $v^{3}$.
3. **Region III — Rated / Full Load plateau**: $v_r \le v < v_{out}$ (e.g., 12 to 25 m/s); $P = P_r$, held constant by pitch control.
4. **Region IV — Shutdown region**: $v \ge v_{out}$ (e.g., $\ge 25$ m/s); $P = 0$; turbine parked.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for listing + 1 mark for typical values/regions)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

*State the Betz limit. Why is it impossible for a practical wind turbine to achieve this limit?*

**Model Answer**:

> **Betz Limit**: The maximum fraction of kinetic wind energy that an ideal, actuator-disc, frictionless wind turbine can extract is $C_{P,\max} = \dfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926$ (i.e., **59.26 %**).

A practical turbine cannot reach this limit because of:

- **Aerodynamic losses**: blade drag, tip losses, hub losses, wake rotation.
- **Mechanical losses**: gearbox friction, bearing friction.
- **Electrical losses**: generator copper losses, converter switching losses.
- **Control limitations**: inability to maintain optimal tip-speed ratio at all winds.
- **Wake interaction** in offshore wind farms.

Real offshore turbines operate at $C_P \approx 0.45 - 0.50$.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for stating Betz limit + 2 marks for reasons)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Derive the maximum power coefficient ($C_{P,\max}$) of an ideal horizontal-axis wind turbine using the actuator-disc theory. Clearly state the assumptions and the value of the axial induction factor $a$ at the optimum.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A 2.5 MW offshore wind turbine has cut-in speed 3 m/s, rated speed 12 m/s, and cut-out speed 25 m/s. Using the cubic power-curve model ($k = 3$), calculate:

1. The power output at a wind speed of 9 m/s.
2. The annual energy production (AEP) if the site has a Weibull distribution with shape factor $k_{sh} = 2.0$ and scale factor $c = 9$ m/s. Use the Weibull PDF table provided below.

**Weibull PDF values** (for $c = 9$ m/s, $k_{sh} = 2.0$):

| $v$ (m/s) | $f(v)$ (1/m/s) |
|-----------|----------------|
| 3 | 0.0611 |
| 4 | 0.0745 |
| 5 | 0.0818 |
| 6 | 0.0845 |
| 7 | 0.0834 |
| 8 | 0.0795 |
| 9 | 0.0735 |
| 10 | 0.0662 |
| 11 | 0.0580 |
| 12 | 0.0497 |
| 13 | 0.0417 |
| 14–24 | Σ ≈ 0.1061 |
| 25 | 0.0010 |

---

**Model Solution**:

**(a)** See **Section 3.1, Steps 1–10** above. The final result is:

$$C_{P,\max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926 \quad \text{at} \quad a = \frac{1}{3}$$

**Assumptions**: incompressible, steady, uniform, frictionless flow; actuator disc; no wake rotation; infinite number of blades.

*(7 marks: 4 marks derivation + 2 marks for stating assumptions + 1 mark for final numerical value)*

**(b) Part (1)**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(9) &= 2500 \cdot \frac{9^{3} - 3^{3}}{12^{3} - 3^{3}} \\
     &= 2500 \cdot \frac{729 - 27}{1728 - 27} \\
     &= 2500 \cdot \frac{702}{1701} \\
     &= 2500 \cdot 0.4127 \\
     &= 1031.7 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Part (2)**: Tabulating $P(v) \cdot f(v)$ across regions:

| $v$ (m/s) | $P(v)$ (kW) | $f(v)$ | $P(v) \cdot f(v)$ |
|-----------|-------------|--------|--------------------|
| 3 | 0 | 0.0611 | 0.00 |
| 4 | 65.2 | 0.0745 | 4.86 |
| 5 | 224.7 | 0.0818 | 18.38 |
| 6 | 488.3 | 0.0845 | 41.26 |
| 7 | 865.2 | 0.0834 | 72.16 |
| 8 | 1363.6 | 0.0795 | 108.41 |
| 9 | 1031.7 | 0.0735 | 75.83 |
| 10 | 1734.8 | 0.0662 | 114.84 |
| 11 | 2164.6 | 0.0580 | 125.55 |
| 12 | 2500 | 0.0497 | 124.25 |
| 13–24 | 2500 | 0.1061 | 265.25 |
| 25 | 0 | 0.0010 | 0.00 |

Sum $\sum P(v) f(v) \approx 950.79$ kW

Therefore:

$$\langle P \rangle = \sum P(v) f(v) \cdot \Delta v = 950.79 \times 1 = 950.79 \text{ kW}$$

$$AEP = 8760 \times 950.79 = 8{,}328{,}920 \text{ kWh/year} \approx 8.33 \text{ GWh/year}$$

$$C_F = \frac{950.79}{2500} = 0.380 \text{ or } 38.0\%$$

*(7 marks: 1 mark sub-part 1 + 4 marks sub-part 2 table + 2 marks for AEP/CF computation)*

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* With the help of a neatly labelled sketch, describe the four regions of a wind turbine power curve. Explain the physical significance of cut-in, rated, and cut-out speeds.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Discuss the power curves of the following marine renewable energy systems, comparing their input variables and curve shapes:

1. Wave Energy Converter (WEC)
2. Tidal Stream Turbine
3. Floating Offshore Solar PV

For each, state **two engineering design implications** specific to maritime deployment.

---

**Model Answer**:

**(a)** Sketch (reproduced verbally for clarity):

```
 P (kW)
   |
 P_r ---------------------        Region III
   |                     |        (Rated Plateau)
   |                  __/
   |               __/   <- Region II (Partial Load, ∝ v³)
   |           __/
   |        __/
   |     __/
   |  __/
   |_/                          Region I (Standstill)
   |_________________________   Region IV (Shutdown)
   0   v_in   v_r         v_out     V (m/s)
```

**Physical significance**:

- **Cut-in speed $v_{in}$**: The minimum wind speed at which the turbine begins to generate useful electrical power. Below this, generator torque cannot overcome friction.
- **Rated speed $v_r$**: The wind speed at which the turbine reaches its **maximum (rated) electrical power** $P_r$. Beyond this, **pitch control** (rotating blades about their axis) is activated to spill excess aerodynamic power and protect the drivetrain.
- **Cut-out speed $v_{out}$**: The safety threshold above which the turbine is **parked** (blades feathered, rotor locked, yaw parked) to prevent structural damage from extreme gusts. Typical offshore values are 25 m/s (older turbines) to 32 m/s (modern turbines with enhanced storm survival).

*(7 marks: 2 marks sketch + 3 marks for describing 4 regions + 2 marks for physical significance)*

**(b)** Comparison:

| System | Input Variable | Power Curve Shape | Engineering Implication 1 | Engineering Implication 2 |
|--------|----------------|-------------------|--------------------------|---------------------------|
| **WEC** | Significant wave height $H_s$ (m) & energy period $T_e$ (s) | $P \propto H_s^{2.2} - H_s^{2.5}$ with a startup threshold | **Survivability in storm seas**: WEC must absorb extreme wave loads (100-year return period); survival mode engages above $H_s > 6 - 8$ m. | **Slow dynamic response**: Power ramps up and down over wave groups (10–30 s); unsuitable as a sole baseload; needs storage or hybridization. |
| **Tidal Stream Turbine** | Tidal current speed $v_t$ (m/s) | $P \propto v_t^{3}$, very intermittent (semi-diurnal ebb/flood) | **Resource predictability**: Tides are deterministic — AEP estimates have < 5 % uncertainty, far better than wind. | **Bidirectional flow**: Rotor must yaw or be symmetric to handle reversing flow during ebb/flood cycles. |
| **Floating PV** | Plane-of-array irradiance $G$ (W/m²) | Linear in $G$ up to inverter rating, then flat plateau | **Motion-induced losses**: Pitching/rolling of floater reduces effective irradiance by 5–15 %; requires dynamic MPPT. | **Salt corrosion**: Aluminum frames and junction boxes must be marine-grade; regular cleaning needed due to biofouling. |

*(7 marks: 1 mark per system input variable + 1 mark per system curve shape + 1 mark per engineering implication = total 9 marks possible; capped at 7)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Power Curve Questions**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract $v_{in}^{k}$** in the numerator of the cubic model. Many students write $P = P_r \dfrac{v^{k}}{v_r^{k}}$, which over-estimates power by 30–50 % at low wind speeds.
> 2. **Using $P_r$ outside Region II and Region III**: In Region IV, the answer is **always $P = 0$**, not $P_r$. Examiners specifically look for this distinction.
> 3. **Confusing Betz limit (16/27)** with the **thrust coefficient optimum (8/9)**. They are different and appear in different derivations.
> 4. **Forgetting units** in AEP. AEP must be expressed in **kWh/year** or **MWh/year** with the 8760 h/year factor.
> 5. **Omitting the standard air density assumption** $\rho_0 = 1.225$ kg/m³ in power curve definitions. KTU examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark if this is missing.
> 6. **Using kW inconsistently**: Power curve in kW but $\rho$ in kg/m³, $A$ in m², $v$ in m/s — give $P$ in W unless you explicitly divide by 1000.
> 7. **In Region III of WEC curves**: Many students forget that WECs have a **survival / cut-out wave height** at which the device is parked. Always mention this.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition**: A **power curve** is the $P$ vs $v$ relationship for a renewable energy converter, standardized at $\rho_0 = 1.225$ kg/m³.
- **Four regions**: Standstill, Partial Load ($P \propto v^3$), Rated Plateau, Shutdown.
- **Three characteristic speeds**: **Cut-in $v_{in}$**, **Rated $v_r$**, **Cut-out $v_{out}$**.
- **Betz limit** $C_{P,\max} = 16/27 \approx 0.5926$; achieved at axial induction $a = 1/3$.
- **Cubic model (Region II)**: $P = P_r \cdot (v^{k} - v_{in}^{k})/(v_r^{k} - v_{in}^{k})$ with $k = 3$ standard.
- **Weibull PDF**: $f(v) = (k_{sh}/c)(v/c)^{k_{sh}-1} e^{-(v/c)^{k_{sh}}}$ weights the wind distribution.
- **AEP** $= 8760 \int P(v) f(v)\, dv$ in **kWh/year**.
- **Capacity factor** $C_F = AEP / (8760 \cdot P_r)$; offshore wind $\approx 0.35 - 0.55$.
- **Wave energy** power scales as $H_s^{2.2 - 2.5}$, **tidal stream** as $v_t^{3}$, **floating PV** linearly with irradiance $G$.
- **Air density correction** is mandatory for hot/cold maritime climates (e.g., Red Sea, North Sea).
- **Standard reference**: **IEC 61400-12-1** for wind, **IEC TS 62600-100** for wave, **EMEC/IECRE** for tidal.
- **For KTU numericals**: always tabulate $P(v) \cdot f(v)$, sum, and multiply by $\Delta v$ and 8760 for AEP.
- **In derivations**: state assumptions explicitly (incompressible, steady, uniform, frictionless, infinite blades) before applying the Betz equation.
- **Engineering interpretation**: power curve is the central link between resource measurement, control, generation, and AEP estimation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Assessment of Wind Energy at Site
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST755 - RENEWABLE ENERGY IN MARITIME SECTOR
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Assessment of Wind Energy at Site

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wind Energy Site Assessment** is the systematic engineering procedure of quantifying the available wind resource at a candidate location to determine its technical and economic viability for installing wind energy conversion systems (WECS). The process involves **wind resource measurement**, **statistical characterization of wind speed**, **vertical extrapolation**, **turbulence analysis**, and **annual energy production (AEP) estimation**, primarily governed by the **IEC 61400 series** of standards.

The assessment bridges atmospheric science, fluid mechanics, and electrical power engineering to produce a probabilistic model of the wind regime at the hub height of the proposed turbine.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "The River-Flow Estimation"**
> Just as a hydroelectric engineer must measure the *average flow rate and its variability* of a river to design a dam and estimate annual electricity output, a wind energy engineer must measure the *average wind speed and its variability* at a site to size the wind turbine and predict annual energy. A river that flows strongly only one month a year cannot sustain a large hydro-plant; similarly, a site with high but extremely variable wind will have a low *Capacity Factor*, even if peak speeds are impressive. The site assessment is the "gauging of the atmospheric river."

## 1.3 Core Metrics & Standard Constants

The following physical constants and standard reference values are mandated by **IEC 61400-1** for wind energy analysis:

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit |
|:----------|:------:|:--------------:|:----:|
| Standard air density | $\rho_0$ | **1.225** | $\text{kg/m}^3$ |
| Standard atmospheric pressure | $P_0$ | **101325** | $\text{Pa}$ |
| Standard temperature | $T_0$ | **288.15** | $\text{K}$ (15 °C) |
| Universal gas constant (air) | $R$ | **287.05** | $\text{J/(kg·K)}$ |
| Reference height (IEC) | $z_{\text{ref}}$ | **10** | $\text{m}$ |
| Hours per year | — | **8760** | $\text{hr}$ |
| Power-law shear exponent (offshore) | $\alpha$ | **0.10 – 0.14** | — |
| Power-law shear exponent (onshore) | $\alpha$ | **0.20 – 0.40** | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Marine/Offshore Winds are Special:** Over open water, the **surface roughness length $z_0$** is extremely low (typically 0.0002 m vs 0.03 m for grassland). This produces *lower wind shear*, *higher mean wind speeds* at hub height, and *more laminar flow*, making offshore sites significantly more energy-dense than onshore counterparts.

## 1.4 Three Pillars of Site Assessment

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A rigorous KTU-aligned wind site assessment must answer three fundamental questions:
> 1. **How strong is the wind?** (Mean wind speed $\bar{V}$ and Weibull parameters $c, k$)
> 2. **How steady is the wind?** (Diurnal/seasonal variability, turbulence intensity $I_u$)
> 3. **How much energy can be extracted?** (Annual Energy Production — AEP in **MWh/yr** or **GWh/yr**)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Wind Energy Conversion Chain

The assessment follows a logical cascade of engineering steps:

### Step 1 — Wind Speed Measurement
- Onshore: **Cup anemometers** (mechanical) and **Sonic anemometers** (ultrasonic, no moving parts).
- Offshore: **Floating LiDAR (Light Detection and Ranging)** systems are now preferred over costly met-masts.
- Measurements are taken at **multiple heights** (typically 1–3 levels: e.g., 40 m, 80 m, 120 m) to capture the vertical wind profile.
- Minimum data period: **1 full year** (per IEC 61400-1) to capture all seasonal variations.

### Step 2 — Vertical Extrapolation
Wind speed measured at one height must be extrapolated to the turbine hub height $z_h$. Two accepted models:

**Logarithmic Law (preferred for offshore):**
$$V(z) = V_{\text{ref}} \cdot \frac{\ln(z / z_0)}{\ln(z_{\text{ref}} / z_0)}$$

where $z_0$ is the aerodynamic roughness length (m).

**Power Law (preferred for onshore):**
$$V(z) = V_{\text{ref}} \cdot \left(\frac{z}{z_{\text{ref}}}\right)^{\alpha}$$

where $\alpha$ is the shear exponent. For open sea, $\alpha \approx 0.10$.

### Step 3 — Statistical Modeling (Weibull Distribution)
The Weibull distribution is the global industry standard (IEC 61400-12) for representing wind speed frequency.

$$f(v) = \frac{k}{c}\left(\frac{v}{c}\right)^{k-1} \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{v}{c}\right)^{k}\right]$$

The cumulative distribution:
$$F(v) = 1 - \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{v}{c}\right)^{k}\right]$$

- **Shape factor $k$** (dimensionless, typically 1.5–3.0): describes wind variability. Higher $k$ = more steady wind.
- **Scale factor $c$** (m/s): closely related to mean wind speed.

The **Rayleigh distribution** is a special case of Weibull with $k = 2$, often used when only mean wind speed is known.

### Step 4 — Wind Power Density
The kinetic power flux per unit swept area:
$$\frac{P}{A} = \frac{1}{2}\rho \bar{V}^{3} \quad [\text{W/m}^2]$$

For a Weibull-distributed wind regime, the **mean power density** becomes:
$$\frac{\bar{P}}{A} = \frac{1}{2}\rho \, c^{3} \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{3}{k}\right)$$

where $\Gamma$ is the Gamma function. Note the cubic dependence on $c$ — even a 10% error in wind speed causes a ~33% error in power.

### Step 5 — Capacity Factor & AEP
$$\text{CF} = \frac{E_{\text{actual}}}{P_{\text{rated}} \times 8760}$$

$$\text{AEP} = \text{CF} \times P_{\text{rated}} \times 8760 \quad [\text{Wh/yr}]$$

Typical offshore CF: **40–60%**; typical onshore CF: **25–40%**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU 2024 Examinations**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|:-:|:---------|:--------|:-----:|:------|
| 1 | Air density (ideal gas) | $\rho = P / (R T)$ | $\text{kg/m}^3$ | $P$ in Pa, $T$ in K |
| 2 | Power in wind | $P = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A V^{3}$ | $\text{W}$ | $A = \pi D^{2}/4$ |
| 3 | Wind power density | $P/A = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{3}$ | $\text{W/m}^2$ | Per unit swept area |
| 4 | Power-law profile | $V(z) = V_{\text{ref}}(z/z_{\text{ref}})^{\alpha}$ | $\text{m/s}$ | $0.10 \le \alpha \le 0.40$ |
| 5 | Log-law profile | $V(z) = V_{\text{ref}}\,\dfrac{\ln(z/z_0)}{\ln(z_{\text{ref}}/z_0)}$ | $\text{m/s}$ | $z_0$ = roughness length |
| 6 | Weibull PDF | $f(v) = \tfrac{k}{c}(v/c)^{k-1}\exp[-(v/c)^{k}]$ | $\text{s/m}$ | — |
| 7 | Weibull CDF | $F(v) = 1 - \exp[-(v/c)^{k}]$ | — | — |
| 8 | Weibull mean speed | $\bar{V} = c\,\Gamma(1+1/k)$ | $\text{m/s}$ | $\Gamma(1)=1, \Gamma(1.5)=\sqrt{\pi}/2$ |
| 9 | Weibull power density | $\bar{P}/A = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho c^{3}\Gamma(1+3/k)$ | $\text{W/m}^2$ | Key AEP equation |
| 10 | Rayleigh ($k=2$) power | $\bar{P}/A = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho \bar{V}^{3} \cdot \tfrac{3\sqrt{\pi}}{8}$ | $\text{W/m}^2$ | Uses mean speed only |
| 11 | Capacity factor | $\text{CF} = E_{\text{act}} / (P_r \cdot 8760)$ | — | $0 < \text{CF} < 1$ |
| 12 | Annual Energy Production | $\text{AEP} = \text{CF} \cdot P_r \cdot 8760$ | $\text{Wh/yr}$ | Divide by $10^6$ for MWh |
| 13 | Turbulence intensity | $I_u = \sigma_v / \bar{V}$ | — | IEC limit $\approx 0.18$ (class A) |
| 14 | Air density correction (alt) | $\rho = \rho_0 \exp(-H/8435)$ | $\text{kg/m}^3$ | $H$ = altitude in m |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the maritime sector, this assessment pipeline is the **gatekeeper of all offshore wind investments**. The North Sea, Arabian Sea (off Gujarat, Kerala coast), and Bay of Bengal have been systematically surveyed using floating LiDAR buoys. The outcome of a site assessment directly determines:
- **Turbine selection** (IEC Wind Class I/II/III)
- **Foundation type** (monopile, jacket, floating spar)
- **Cable routing** and **substation sizing**
- **Bankable financial modeling** for the project
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Weibull Mean Wind Speed from First Principles

The Weibull mean (expected value) is defined as:
$$\bar{V} = E[V] = \int_{0}^{\infty} v \, f(v) \, dv$$

**Step 1** — Substitute the Weibull PDF:
$$\bar{V} = \int_{0}^{\infty} v \cdot \frac{k}{c}\left(\frac{v}{c}\right)^{k-1} \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{v}{c}\right)^{k}\right] dv$$

**Step 2** — Pull constants out of the integral:
$$\bar{V} = \frac{k}{c^{k}} \int_{0}^{\infty} v^{k} \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{v}{c}\right)^{k}\right] dv$$

**Step 3** — Apply the substitution $u = (v/c)^{k}$, which means $v = c \, u^{1/k}$ and $dv = \tfrac{c}{k} u^{1/k - 1} du$.

The integrand $v^{k} dv$ becomes $c^{k} u \cdot \tfrac{c}{k} u^{1/k - 1} du = \tfrac{c^{k+1}}{k} u^{1/k} du$.

**Step 4** — Substituting back:
$$\bar{V} = \frac{k}{c^{k}} \cdot \frac{c^{k+1}}{k} \int_{0}^{\infty} u^{1/k} e^{-u} du = c \int_{0}^{\infty} u^{1/k} e^{-u} du$$

**Step 5** — Recognize the integral as the Gamma function $\Gamma(x) = \int_{0}^{\infty} u^{x-1} e^{-u} du$ with $x = 1 + 1/k$:
$$\boxed{\bar{V} = c \, \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{k}\right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** For $k = 1$ (exponential distribution), $\Gamma(2) = 1! = 1$, so $\bar{V} = c$. For $k = 2$ (Rayleigh), $\Gamma(1.5) = \sqrt{\pi}/2 \approx 0.886$, so $\bar{V} \approx 0.886\,c$, meaning $c \approx 1.128\,\bar{V}$. This is the textbook conversion between the Rayleigh scale factor and mean wind speed.

## 3.2 Derivation: Mean Power Density for Weibull Wind

The mean power density in a Weibull wind regime is:
$$\frac{\bar{P}}{A} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{2}\rho v^{3} \, f(v) \, dv = \frac{1}{2}\rho \int_{0}^{\infty} v^{3} \frac{k}{c}\left(\frac{v}{c}\right)^{k-1} e^{-(v/c)^{k}} dv$$

Following identical substitution mechanics as Section 3.1 (with $v^3 \to v^{k+2}$), we obtain:
$$\frac{\bar{P}}{A} = \frac{1}{2}\rho \, c^{3} \int_{0}^{\infty} u^{(3/k) + 1 - 1} e^{-u} du$$

Recognizing the Gamma function with argument $1 + 3/k$:
$$\boxed{\frac{\bar{P}}{A} = \frac{1}{2}\rho \, c^{3} \, \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{3}{k}\right)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the Gamma correction matters:** Naive use of $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho \bar{V}^{3}$ *underestimates* the true power density for typical wind sites. For $k=2$, the correction factor $\Gamma(2.5)/\Gamma(1.5)^{3} = 1.5\sqrt{\pi}/(0.886)^{3} \approx 3\pi/8 \approx 1.178$, meaning the Weibull-based power density is ~18% higher than the cube-of-mean estimate. This is a **classic KTU pitfall**.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Full Solution)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU-style):** A coastal site in Kerala has measured wind data. A 50 m mast gives a mean wind speed of **7.2 m/s** at 30 m height. The Weibull shape factor at the site is **$k = 2.1$**. A turbine with hub height **80 m** and rotor diameter **80 m** is proposed. Assume sea-surface roughness $z_0 = 0.0002$ m, standard air density 1.225 kg/m³, and a capacity factor of 0.35. Calculate: (a) the mean wind speed at hub height, (b) the Weibull scale factor $c$, (c) the mean power density, and (d) the AEP.

**Solution:**

### Part (a) — Mean wind speed at 80 m (Log-law)

Using $V(z) = V_{\text{ref}} \cdot \dfrac{\ln(z/z_0)}{\ln(z_{\text{ref}}/z_0)}$:

$$V(80) = 7.2 \cdot \frac{\ln(80/0.0002)}{\ln(30/0.0002)} = 7.2 \cdot \frac{\ln(400000)}{\ln(150000)}$$

$$= 7.2 \cdot \frac{12.899}{11.918} = 7.2 \cdot 1.0823 = \mathbf{7.79 \text{ m/s}}$$

### Part (b) — Weibull scale factor $c$

From $\bar{V} = c\,\Gamma(1+1/k)$ with $k=2.1$:
$$\Gamma(1 + 1/2.1) = \Gamma(1.4762)$$

Using the Gamma function identity: $\Gamma(1.4762) \approx 0.8854$

Therefore:
$$c = \frac{\bar{V}}{\Gamma(1+1/k)} = \frac{7.79}{0.8854} = \mathbf{8.80 \text{ m/s}}$$

### Part (c) — Mean power density

$$\frac{\bar{P}}{A} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times (8.80)^{3} \times \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{3}{2.1}\right)$$

$$\Gamma(1 + 3/2.1) = \Gamma(2.4286) \approx 1.380$$

$$= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 681.5 \times 1.380 = \mathbf{575.9 \text{ W/m}^2}$$

### Part (d) — Annual Energy Production

Swept area: $A = \pi D^{2}/4 = \pi(80)^{2}/4 = 5026.5 \text{ m}^2$

Rated power (assuming 100% Cp at this density for AEP base):
$$P_r = \frac{1}{2}\rho A V_{\text{rated}}^{3} \;\;\text{(if not given, use CF directly)}$$

Using the capacity factor approach:
$$\text{AEP} = \text{CF} \times P_r \times 8760$$

If the turbine's rated power is, say, $P_r = 2.0$ MW:
$$\text{AEP} = 0.35 \times 2{,}000{,}000 \times 8760 = 6{,}132{,}000 \text{ kWh/yr} = \mathbf{6.13 \text{ GWh/yr}}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Site Selection Criteria

| Criterion | Excellent | Marginal | Unsuitable | Why it Matters |
|:----------|:---------:|:--------:|:----------:|:---------------|
| Mean wind speed at hub | $\geq 8.5$ m/s | 6.0–8.5 m/s | $< 6.0$ m/s | Cubic dependence on energy |
| Weibull shape factor $k$ | $\geq 2.5$ | 1.8–2.5 | $< 1.8$ | Steadier wind = higher CF |
| Wind power density | $\geq 600$ W/m² | 350–600 W/m² | $< 350$ W/m² | Direct energy indicator |
| Turbulence intensity $I_u$ | $\leq 0.10$ | 0.10–0.18 | $> 0.18$ | Fatigue, IEC class A limit |
| Wind shear $\alpha$ (offshore) | $\leq 0.10$ | 0.10–0.14 | $> 0.14$ | Low shear = marine site |
| Obstacle clearance | 20D upwind | 10D | $< 10D$ | Wake-induced losses |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Wind Energy Site Assessment Workflow

The following functional architecture block diagram maps the end-to-end engineering workflow from raw measurement to bankable AEP.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Stage 1: Site Reconnaissance] --> A2[Topographic Maps, Bathymetry, GIS Screening]
    A2 --> A3[Stage 2: Permitting and Constraints Check]
    A3 --> A4[Stage 3: Wind Measurement Campaign]
    A4 --> A5[Cup Anemometer Met-Mast OR Floating LiDAR Buoy]
    A5 --> A6[10-min Average Wind Speed Time Series at 2-3 Heights]
    A6 --> A7[Stage 4: Data Quality Control]
    A7 --> A8[Removal of Icing, Sensor Fault, Tower Shadow Data]
    A8 --> A9[Stage 5: Vertical Extrapolation]
    A9 --> A10[Log Law or Power Law to Hub Height 80m to 120m]
    A10 --> A11[Stage 6: Statistical Modeling]
    A11 --> A12[Weibull Fit: Solve for k and c via WAsP or Maximum Likelihood]
    A12 --> A13[Stage 7: Wind Rose Construction]
    A13 --> A14[Directional Frequency, Energy Rose, Turbulence Rose]
    A14 --> A15[Stage 8: Power Density and AEP]
    A15 --> A16[Mean Power Density P per A, Capacity Factor, GWh per year]
    A16 --> A17[Stage 9: Wake and Loss Modeling]
    A17 --> A18[Array Layout, Wake Decay Coefficient, Gross minus Net AEP]
    A18 --> A19[Stage 10: Bankable AEP Report]
    A19 --> A20[IEC 61400 Compliant Final Output for Financing]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Assessment Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input[Input Module]
        IN1[Raw 10-min Wind Data CSV]
        IN2[Site Coordinates, Altitude, Bathymetry]
    end

    subgraph Processing[Processing Module]
        P1[QC and Filtering Engine]
        P2[Vertical Shear Calculator]
        P3[Weibull Parameter Estimator MLE]
        P4[Wind Rose Generator]

        P6[Turbulence and IEC Class Classifier]
    end

    subgraph Output[Output Module]
        O1[V mean, c, k, alpha, z0]
        O2[Directional Wind Rose Plot]

        O5[Annual Energy Production in GWh per yr]
        O6[Capacity Factor in percent]
    end

    IN1 --> P1
    IN2 --> P2
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P3 --> P5
    P1 --> P6
    P3 --> O1
    P4 --> O2
    P5 --> O3
    P6 --> O4
    P3 --> O5
    P3 --> O6
```

## 4.3 Typical Marine Wind Profile (Vertical Shear)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0[Surface z=0 m, V=0 m/s]:::sea
    L1[z=10 m: V=6.5 m/s]:::layer
    L2[z=30 m: V=7.2 m/s Measured]:::layer
    L3[z=50 m: V=7.5 m/s]:::layer
    L4[z=80 m: V=7.8 m/s Hub]:::hub
    L5[z=120 m: V=8.1 m/s]:::layer
    L6[z=200 m: V=8.5 m/s]:::layer

    L0 --> L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5 --> L6

    classDef sea fill:#1e6091,stroke:#0a2540,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef layer fill:#a8dadc,stroke:#1d3557,color:#0a2540
    classDef hub fill:#e63946,stroke:#7d060b,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The marine boundary layer exhibits a steep wind shear in the lowest 30 m and becomes nearly linear-logarithmic above. The hub at 80 m sits in the **fully-developed turbulent layer** where the Weibull model is most accurate.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define Weibull distribution parameters $k$ and $c$ in the context of wind energy site assessment. State typical ranges for offshore sites in India.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Weibull distribution** is a two-parameter statistical model used to describe the frequency distribution of wind speeds at a site. The **scale factor $c$ (m/s)** is approximately equal to (and slightly higher than) the mean wind speed; it scales the distribution along the wind-speed axis. The **shape factor $k$ (dimensionless)** controls the shape and peakedness of the distribution — higher $k$ means narrower, more concentrated wind speeds, hence steadier wind. For offshore sites along the Gujarat and Tamil Nadu coasts, typical values are **$k \approx 1.8$–$2.4$** and **$c \approx 7$–$9$ m/s**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse the *scale factor* with mean wind speed. Remember: $\bar{V} = c \cdot \Gamma(1+1/k)$, so $c \geq \bar{V}$ for all physical $k$ values.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List any three site selection criteria for offshore wind farms along the Indian coastline.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per criterion):**
1. **Mean wind speed at hub height $\geq 7$ m/s** (to achieve commercial CF > 35%).
2. **Water depth $\leq 60$ m** for fixed-bottom monopile/jacket foundations; deeper sites need floating platforms.
3. **Distance to shore $\leq 50$ km** to limit cable losses and installation cost.
4. **Low turbulence intensity** ($I_u < 0.18$, IEC Class A) — open sea typically satisfies this.
5. **Avoid shipping lanes, marine protected areas, and seismic zones**.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Recommended Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper, Module 3]**
> **(a)** Derive the expression for the **mean power density** of a wind regime that follows the Weibull probability density function. Define all variables used. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** At a proposed offshore site near Kochi, the Weibull parameters are $k = 2.0$ and $c = 8.5$ m/s. A turbine with rotor diameter 90 m, hub height 90 m, and rated power 3.0 MW is to be installed. The capacity factor is estimated as 0.42. Compute: (i) the mean wind speed, (ii) the mean wind power density, and (iii) the annual energy production in MWh. Use $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**
The power density at wind speed $v$ is $P/A = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^{3}$. The mean power density is:
$$\frac{\bar{P}}{A} = \int_{0}^{\infty}\frac{1}{2}\rho v^{3} f(v) \, dv = \frac{1}{2}\rho \int_{0}^{\infty} v^{3} \cdot \frac{k}{c}\left(\frac{v}{c}\right)^{k-1} \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{v}{c}\right)^{k}\right] dv$$

Pulling out constants: $\dfrac{k}{2\rho c^{k}} \cdot$ ... substitution $u = (v/c)^{k} \Rightarrow v = c u^{1/k}, \, dv = \tfrac{c}{k} u^{1/k - 1} du$.

$$= \frac{1}{2}\rho c^{3} \int_{0}^{\infty} u^{(3/k)} e^{-u} du = \boxed{\frac{1}{2}\rho c^{3} \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{3}{k}\right)}$$

where $\Gamma$ is the Gamma function, $c$ is the scale factor (m/s), and $k$ is the dimensionless shape factor.

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Mean wind speed** (using $k=2$, so $\Gamma(1.5) = \sqrt{\pi}/2 \approx 0.8862$):
$$\bar{V} = c \cdot \Gamma(1 + 1/k) = 8.5 \times 0.8862 = \mathbf{7.53 \text{ m/s}}$$

**(ii) Mean power density** (using $\Gamma(1 + 3/k) = \Gamma(2.5) = 1.5 \times \Gamma(1.5) = 1.5 \times 0.8862 = 1.3293$):
$$\frac{\bar{P}}{A} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times (8.5)^{3} \times 1.3293$$
$$= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 614.125 \times 1.3293$$
$$= \mathbf{500.1 \text{ W/m}^2}$$

**(iii) Annual Energy Production:**
$$\text{AEP} = \text{CF} \times P_r \times 8760 = 0.42 \times 3000 \times 8760$$
$$= 0.42 \times 26{,}280{,}000 = \mathbf{11{,}037{,}600 \text{ kWh/yr} = 11{,}037.6 \text{ MWh/yr}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Valuation Warning:** Many students compute AEP directly from $P/A \times A \times 8760$, which **double-counts the capacity factor** (since CF already accounts for the turbine's power curve, availability, and array losses). Use *either* the CF approach *or* the swept-area-times-Cp approach, **never both**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper, Module 3, Set B]**
> **(a)** Explain the **logarithmic wind profile law**. State its limitations and the conditions under which the **power law** is preferred. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Wind speed at 10 m height at a marine site is measured as 5.8 m/s. Using the power law with $\alpha = 0.12$ (open sea), determine the wind speed at 80 m and 120 m hub heights. If a turbine with diameter 100 m is installed at 80 m, compute the available wind power in kW. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Logarithmic Law (7 marks):**
The **logarithmic wind profile** is derived from the **Prandtl mixing-length theory** for atmospheric boundary-layer flow:
$$V(z) = \frac{u_{*}}{\kappa} \ln\!\left(\frac{z}{z_0}\right)$$

where $u_{*}$ is the **friction velocity**, $\kappa \approx 0.41$ is the **von Kármán constant**, and $z_0$ is the **aerodynamic roughness length** (m). Practical form using a reference measurement:
$$V(z) = V_{\text{ref}} \cdot \frac{\ln(z/z_0)}{\ln(z_{\text{ref}}/z_0)}$$

**Limitations:** Assumes (i) neutral atmospheric stability, (ii) constant roughness, (iii) horizontally homogeneous terrain — violated at coastlines with sea-land transition. **Power law is preferred** for: (a) onshore complex terrain where $z_0$ is hard to define, (b) quick engineering estimates, (c) when only two measurement heights are available.

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

Wind speed at 80 m:
$$V(80) = 5.8 \times \left(\frac{80}{10}\right)^{0.12} = 5.8 \times 8^{0.12}$$
$$8^{0.12} = e^{0.12 \ln 8} = e^{0.12 \times 2.0794} = e^{0.2495} = 1.2835$$
$$V(80) = 5.8 \times 1.2835 = \mathbf{7.44 \text{ m/s}}$$

Wind speed at 120 m:
$$V(120) = 5.8 \times \left(\frac{120}{10}\right)^{0.12} = 5.8 \times 12^{0.12}$$
$$12^{0.12} = e^{0.12 \ln 12} = e^{0.12 \times 2.4849} = e^{0.2982} = 1.3474$$
$$V(120) = 5.8 \times 1.3474 = \mathbf{7.81 \text{ m/s}}$$

Available wind power at 80 m (swept area $A = \pi(100)^{2}/4 = 7853.98$ m²):
$$P = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 7853.98 \times (7.44)^{3}$$
$$= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 7853.98 \times 411.83$$
$$= \mathbf{1{,}981{,}300 \text{ W} \approx 1981.3 \text{ kW}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall (Power Law):** Students often apply $(z/z_{\text{ref}})^{\alpha}$ but write the height ratio upside-down. Always confirm $z > z_{\text{ref}}$ for the ratio to be $> 1$ (wind speed increases with height). For open marine sites, $\alpha = 0.10$–$0.14$ is realistic; values above 0.20 indicate a shore-side or obstructed location.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Site Assessment of Wind Energy**

- **Cubic Power Law:** A 1% error in wind speed yields a ~3% error in power. Wind speed is **king** in site assessment.
- **Weibull > Rayleigh > Cube of Mean:** Always prefer the Weibull-based mean power density $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho c^{3}\Gamma(1+3/k)$ over $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho \bar{V}^{3}$.
- **Gamma Function Bookmarks (must memorize):** $\Gamma(1)=1$, $\Gamma(1.5)=\sqrt{\pi}/2 \approx 0.886$, $\Gamma(2)=1$, $\Gamma(2.5)=1.329$, $\Gamma(3)=2$.
- **Rayleigh shortcut** ($k=2$): $\bar{P}/A = \tfrac{3\sqrt{\pi}}{8} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho \bar{V}^{3} \approx 0.5947 \cdot \rho \bar{V}^{3}$.
- **Power law vs log law:** Log law is *physically rigorous*; power law is *empirical but convenient*. For offshore, log law is strongly preferred.
- **Air density correction:** Offshore marine air is denser than the standard 1.225 kg/m³ due to humidity, salt content, and lower temperature — typical offshore $\rho \approx 1.27$–$1.32$ kg/m³ boosts AEP by ~5%.
- **IEC 61400 Wind Classes:** Class I (Vref = 50 m/s, offshore), Class II (42.5), Class III (37.5) — turbulence categories A (high), B (medium), C (low).
- **One full year** of measurement data is the **minimum** for a bankable assessment (IEC 61400-12-1).
- **Capacity factor** is the single best KPI of site quality: 25–35% onshore, 40–55% offshore, up to 60% at prime sites.
- **Common pitfalls to avoid in the KTU exam hall:**
  1. Mixing up scale factor $c$ with mean wind speed $\bar{V}$.
  2. Using power-law shear $\alpha$ in the log-law equation (or vice versa).
  3. Forgetting to convert rotor diameter $D$ into swept area $A = \pi D^{2}/4$.
  4. Double-counting capacity factor and Cp.
  5. Reporting AEP in W instead of kWh or MWh (per year).
- **Maritime-specific bonus:** Marine sites have *low roughness* ($z_0 \sim 10^{-4}$ m), *low shear* ($\alpha \sim 0.10$), *high mean speeds*, and *high capacity factors* — making them the most sought-after wind resource worldwide.

> **End of Module 3 — Topic: Assessment of Wind Energy at Site**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Study with Calculation of Power Output and Capacity Factor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST755 - RENEWABLE ENERGY IN MARITIME SECTOR > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Case Study with Calculation of Power Output and Capacity Factor ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Power Output (P_out):** The actual electrical power generated by a renewable energy conversion system (e.g., offshore wind turbine, wave energy converter) at a given instant, expressed in **Watts (W)** or **Megawatts (MW)**. For wind, it is bounded by the rated power and is a function of the available resource (wind speed, wave height, tidal velocity). > [!NOTE] > **Capacity Factor (CF):** The ratio of the actual energy produced by a generating system over a period (usually one year) to the maximum possible energy that would have been produced if the system had operated at its full rated capacity continuously over the same period. It is a dimensionless quantity, expressed as a percentage. **Mathematical Expression:** $$ CF = \frac{E_{actual}}{P_{rated} \times T_{period}} $$ where $E_{actual}$ is the actual energy generated (MWh), $P_{rated}$ is the nameplate/installed capacity (MW), and $T_{period}$ is the total hours in the period (8760 hours for one year). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **water tank** representing the maximum energy a wind farm could theoretically produce if it ran at full capacity all year (8760 hours). Now imagine a **real bucket** collecting water from an intermittent tap (the wind). The capacity factor tells us how full the bucket is compared to the full tank. - **CF = 100%** → The wind blew perfectly all year (impossible in real life). - **CF = 45%** → The wind farm produced 45% of its theoretical maximum. For offshore wind in the maritime sector, **typical capacity factors range from 35% to 55%**, depending on the wind regime, turbine design, and site conditions. Offshore winds are stronger and more consistent than onshore, hence higher CF. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight for Maritime Sector:** Offshore wind farms benefit from higher wind speeds (less friction over water), lower turbulence, and better wind direction consistency. This results in capacity factors typically **10-15% higher** than comparable onshore installations. --- ### 1.3 Why Capacity Factor Matters Capacity factor is the **single most important metric** for evaluating the economic viability and performance of a renewable energy system. It directly determines: - Annual revenue generation - Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE) - Grid reliability and dispatch planning - Comparison between different renewable sources ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic Steps **Step 1: Resource Assessment** - Measure wind speed data at the site for at least one year - Fit the data to a **Weibull probability distribution** (standard for wind analysis) - Determine mean wind speed, Weibull shape parameter (k), and scale parameter (c) **Step 2: Turbine Power Curve Definition** The turbine does not produce power linearly. It follows a piecewise function: $$ P(v) = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{for } v < v_{ci} \text{ or } v > v_{co} \\ P_{rated} \times \dfrac{v^2 - v_{ci}^2}{v_{r}^2 - v_{ci}^2} & \text{for } v_{ci} \leq v \leq v_{r} \\ P_{rated} & \text{for } v_{r} < v \leq v_{co} \end{cases} $$ **Step 3: Mean Power Output Calculation** Integrate the power curve over the Weibull probability density function: $$ P_{mean} = \int_{v_{ci}}^{v_{r}} P_{rated} \cdot \frac{v^2 - v_{ci}^2}{v_{r}^2 - v_{ci}^2} \cdot f(v) \, dv + \int_{v_{r}}^{v_{co}} P_{rated} \cdot f(v) \, dv $$ **Step 4: Capacity Factor Computation** $$ CF = \frac{P_{mean}}{P_{rated}} $$ **Step 5: Annual Energy Production (AEP)** $$ AEP_{gross} = N \times P_{rated} \times CF \times 8760 $$ **Step 6: Apply System Losses** - Availability loss - Wake effect loss - Electrical transmission loss - Environmental degradation $$ AEP_{net} = AEP_{gross} \times \prod_{i} (1 - L_i) $$ **Step 7: Net Capacity Factor** $$ CF_{net} = \frac{AEP_{net}}{P_{rated,total} \times 8760} $$ ### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Step - **Resource Assessment:** Renewable energy is intermittent. We need statistical characterization. - **Power Curve:** Real turbines cannot capture all wind energy (Betz limit = 59.3%); they also have operational constraints. - **Mean Power:** Averaging over the probability distribution accounts for variability. - **Capacity Factor:** Normalizes output for fair comparison between systems of different sizes. - **Losses:** Real systems never achieve theoretical performance due to multiple inefficiencies. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Parameter / Formula | Expression | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Weibull PDF | $f(v) = \frac{k}{c}\left(\frac{v}{c}\right)^{k-1} e^{-(v/c)^k}$ | $m^{-1}s$ | k = shape, c = scale | | 2 | Mean Wind Speed | $\bar{v} = c \cdot \Gamma\left(1 + \frac{1}{k}\right)$ | $m/s$ | $\Gamma$ = Gamma function | | 3 | Mean Cube of Wind Speed | $\langle v^3 \rangle = c^3 \cdot \Gamma\left(1 + \frac{3}{k}\right)$ | $m^3/s^3$ | Used in power calc | | 4 | Available Wind Power | $P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$ | W | Per turbine | | 5 | Swept Area | $A = \pi (D/2)^2$ | $m^2$ | D = rotor diameter | | 6 | Turbine Power (cubic region) | $P(v) = P_r \cdot \frac{v^2 - v_{ci}^2}{v_r^2 - v_{ci}^2}$ | W | For $v_{ci} \leq v \leq v_r$ | | 7 | Capacity Factor | $CF = \frac{E_{actual}}{P_{rated} \times 8760}$ | Dimensionless $\vert$ % | Annual basis | | 8 | Gross AEP | $AEP_{gross} = N \cdot P_r \cdot CF \cdot 8760$ | MWh | N = # turbines | | 9 | Net AEP | $AEP_{net} = AEP_{gross} \cdot \prod (1-L_i)$ | MWh | After all losses | | 10 | Betz Limit | $C_{p,max} = 16/27 \approx 0.593$ | Dimensionless | Theoretical max | | 11 | Air Density (sea level) | $\rho = 1.225$ | $kg/m^3$ | **Standard constant** | | 12 | Hours per Year | $T = 8760$ | hours | Non-leap year | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Note for KTU Exam:** The vertical pipe $\vert$ in tables represents absolute value or "such that". In actual formulas, use modulus notation carefully. The Gamma function values you'll need: $\Gamma(1) = 1$, $\Gamma(2) = 1$, $\Gamma(3) = 2$, $\Gamma(1.5) \approx 0.886$, $\Gamma(2.5) \approx 1.329$. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Capacity factor calculations are essential for: - **Marine spatial planning:** Determining optimal offshore wind farm locations - **Financial modeling:** Bankable energy yield assessments - **Grid integration studies:** Predicting power availability - **Technology comparison:** Offshore wind vs. tidal vs. wave - **Carbon credit calculations:** Determining CO₂ avoidance - **Insurance underwriting:** Risk assessment based on resource variability ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 CASE STUDY: 150 MW Offshore Wind Farm — Capacity Factor Analysis > [!IMPORTANT] > **CASE STUDY CONTEXT:** A shipping company is evaluating investment in a 150 MW offshore wind farm in the North Sea (56°N, 3°E) located 50 km from shore at water depth of 35 m. The farm consists of 30 identical 5 MW turbines. You are the renewable energy engineer tasked with calculating the expected annual energy production and capacity factor to support the investment decision. #### 3.1.1 Given Data | Parameter | Symbol | Value | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Number of turbines | N | 30 | — | | Rated power per turbine | $P_r$ | 5 | MW | | Total installed capacity | $P_{total}$ | 150 | MW | | Rotor diameter | D | 126 | m | | Hub height | h | 90 | m | | Cut-in wind speed | $v_{ci}$ | 3 | m/s | | Rated wind speed | $v_r$ | 12 | m/s | | Cut-out wind speed | $v_{co}$ | 25 | m/s | | Mean wind speed at hub | $\bar{v}$ | 9.5 | m/s | | Weibull shape parameter | k | 2.1 | — | | Weibull scale parameter | c | 10.7 | m/s | | Air density | $\rho$ | **1.225** | $kg/m^3$ | | Availability loss | $L_{av}$ | 5 | % | | Wake losses | $L_w$ | 7 | % | | Electrical losses | $L_e$ | 3 | % | | Other losses (icing, etc.) | $L_o$ | 2 | % | --- #### 3.1.2 STEP 1: Calculate Swept Area of Rotor The swept area is the circular area swept by the rotor blades: $$ A = \pi \times \left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^2 $$ Substituting the values: $$ A = \pi \times \left(\frac{126}{2}\right)^2 $$ $$ A = \pi \times (63)^2 $$ $$ A = \pi \times 3969 $$ $$ \boxed{A = 12{,}469 \, m^2} $$ --- #### 3.1.3 STEP 2: Verify Mean Wind Speed Consistency The mean wind speed from Weibull distribution: $$ \bar{v} = c \cdot \Gamma\left(1 + \frac{1}{k}\right) $$ $$ \bar{v} = 10.7 \times \Gamma\left(1 + \frac{1}{2.1}\right) = 10.7 \times \Gamma(1.476) $$ Using $\Gamma(1.476) \approx 0.886$: $$ \bar{v} = 10.7 \times 0.886 = 9.48 \, m/s \approx 9.5 \, m/s \quad \checkmark $$ The given data is consistent. --- #### 3.1.4 STEP 3: Calculate Mean Cube of Wind Speed For Weibull distribution: $$ \langle v^3 \rangle = c^3 \cdot \Gamma\left(1 + \frac{3}{k}\right) $$ Substituting k = 2.1 and c = 10.7: $$ \langle v^3 \rangle = (10.7)^3 \times \Gamma\left(1 + \frac{3}{2.1}\right) $$ $$ \langle v^3 \rangle = 1225.04 \times \Gamma(2.429) $$ Using $\Gamma(2.429) \approx 1.484$: $$ \langle v^3 \rangle = 1225.04 \times 1.484 $$ $$ \boxed{\langle v^3 \rangle = 1818 \, m^3/s^3} $$ --- #### 3.1.5 STEP 4: Calculate Theoretical Available Wind Power The available wind power per turbine (uncapped): $$ P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A \langle v^3 \rangle $$ $$ P_{wind} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 12{,}469 \times 1818 $$ $$ P_{wind} = 0.6125 \times 12{,}469 \times 1818 $$ $$ P_{wind} = 13{,}876{,}948 \, W \approx 13.88 \, MW $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Observation:** The available wind power (13.88 MW) is greater than the rated power (5 MW). This means the turbine cannot capture all the available energy — it is limited by its design. This is why we MUST use the actual power curve. --- #### 3.1.6 STEP 5: Calculate Mean Power Output Using Turbine Power Curve For a more accurate calculation, we use the turbine power curve and integrate over the Weibull distribution. **Turbine Power Curve (approximated):** | Wind Speed (m/s) | Power Output (MW) | |---|---| | 0 to 3 | 0 | | 4 | 0.15 | | 5 | 0.40 | | 6 | 0.85 | | 7 | 1.50 | | 8 | 2.30 | | 9 | 3.20 | | 10 | 4.10 | | 11 | 4.85 | | 12 to 25 | 5.00 | **Step-by-step Calculation Using the Cubic Power Equation:** For wind speeds between cut-in (3 m/s) and rated (12 m/s), the power follows the cubic relation: $$ P(v) = P_r \cdot \frac{v^2 - v_{ci}^2}{v_r^2 - v_{ci}^2} $$ $$ P(v) = 5 \cdot \frac{v^2 - 9}{144 - 9} = 5 \cdot \frac{v^2 - 9}{135} $$ **Probability of Each Wind Speed Bin (using Weibull PDF):** The Weibull PDF gives: $$ f(v) = \frac{k}{c}\left(\frac{v}{c}\right)^{k-1} e^{-(v/c)^k} = \frac{2.1}{10.7}\left(\frac{v}{10.7}\right)^{1.1} e^{-(v/10.7)^{2.1}} $$ Calculating for key wind speeds: | v (m/s) | $f(v)$ (1/(m/s)) | P(v) (MW) | $f(v) \cdot P(v) \cdot \Delta v$ | |---|---|---|---| | 3 | 0.0676 | 0 | 0 | | 4 | 0.0782 | 0.15 | 0.0117 | | 5 | 0.0868 | 0.40 | 0.0347 | | 6 | 0.0924 | 0.85 | 0.0785 | | 7 | 0.0945 | 1.50 | 0.1418 | | 8 | 0.0929 | 2.30 | 0.2137 | | 9 | 0.0878 | 3.20 | 0.2810 | | 10 | 0.0799 | 4.10 | 0.3276 | | 11 | 0.0702 | 4.85 | 0.3405 | | 12-25 | — | 5.00 | 0.7705 | | **Sum** | | | **≈ 2.20 MW** | **Numerical Integration Result:** $$ P_{mean} = \int_0^\infty P(v) \cdot f(v) \, dv \approx 2.20 \, MW $$ (Detailed numerical integration using the formula above yields $P_{mean} \approx 2.20$ MW per turbine) --- #### 3.1.7 STEP 6: Calculate Gross Capacity Factor $$ CF_{gross} = \frac{P_{mean}}{P_r} = \frac{2.20}{5.0} $$ $$ \boxed{CF_{gross} = 0.440 = 44.0\%} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** Without considering losses, the offshore wind farm would produce 44% of its theoretical maximum output. This is consistent with typical North Sea offshore wind farms. --- #### 3.1.8 STEP 7: Calculate Gross Annual Energy Production For the entire wind farm (30 turbines): $$ AEP_{gross} = N \times P_r \times CF_{gross} \times 8760 $$ $$ AEP_{gross} = 30 \times 5 \times 0.440 \times 8760 $$ $$ AEP_{gross} = 150 \times 0.440 \times 8760 $$ $$ AEP_{gross} = 578{,}160 \, MWh $$ $$ \boxed{AEP_{gross} = 578.16 \, GWh/year} $$ --- #### 3.1.9 STEP 8: Apply System Losses The combined derating factor for all losses: $$ \eta_{losses} = (1 - L_{av}) \times (1 - L_w) \times (1 - L_e) \times (1 - L_o) $$ $$ \eta_{losses} = 0.95 \times 0.93 \times 0.97 \times 0.98 $$ Calculating step-by-step: $$ 0.95 \times 0.93 = 0.8835 $$ $$ 0.8835 \times 0.97 = 0.8570 $$ $$ 0.8570 \times 0.98 = 0.8399 $$ $$ \boxed{\eta_{losses} \approx 0.840} $$ --- #### 3.1.10 STEP 9: Calculate Net Annual Energy Production $$ AEP_{net} = AEP_{gross} \times \eta_{losses} $$ $$ AEP_{net} = 578.16 \times 0.840 $$ $$ \boxed{AEP_{net} = 485.65 \, GWh/year} $$ --- #### 3.1.11 STEP 10: Calculate Net Capacity Factor $$ CF_{net} = \frac{AEP_{net}}{P_{total} \times 8760} $$ $$ CF_{net} = \frac{485{,}650 \, MWh}{150 \, MW \times 8760 \, h} $$ $$ CF_{net} = \frac{485{,}650}{1{,}314{,}000} $$ $$ \boxed{CF_{net} = 0.3696 \approx 37.0\%} $$ --- ### 3.2 Summary of Case Study Results | Metric | Symbol | Value | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Mean Power Output | $P_{mean}$ | 2.20 | MW | | Gross Capacity Factor | $CF_{gross}$ | 44.0 | % | | Gross Annual Energy | $AEP_{gross}$ | 578.16 | GWh | | Net Annual Energy | $AEP_{net}$ | 485.65 | GWh | | **Net Capacity Factor** | $CF_{net}$ | **37.0** | **%** | | Equivalent Full Load Hours | EFLH | 3238 | h/year | **Equivalent Full Load Hours:** $$ EFLH = CF_{net} \times 8760 = 0.370 \times 8760 = 3238 \, hours/year $$ This means the wind farm effectively runs at full power for **3238 hours per year** (about 37% of the time). --- ### 3.3 Engineering Interpretation & Recommendations **Performance Assessment:** - A net CF of 37% is **good** for offshore wind (typical range: 35-50%) - Equivalent to powering approximately **55,500 households** annually (assuming 8 MWh/household/year) - CO₂ avoidance: ~400,000 tonnes/year (assuming 0.82 kg CO₂/kWh grid emission factor) **For Maritime Application:** This wind farm could potentially supply renewable electricity to: - A nearby port facility for ship-to-shore power - An offshore oil platform - Coastal hydrogen production for maritime fuel ## 4.
