KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Define **capacity factor** of a renewable energy system. State its typical range for offshore wind installations. **Model Answer:** Capacity factor (CF) is defined as the ratio of actual energy produced by a generating system over a specific period to the maximum possible energy that would have been produced if the system operated continuously at its rated capacity during the same period. $$ CF = \frac{E_{actual}}{P_{rated} \times T} $$ For offshore wind installations in the maritime sector, the typical capacity factor ranges from **35% to 55%**, depending on wind resource quality, turbine technology, and site conditions. Good offshore sites (e.g., North Sea) typically achieve 40-50%. *(3 marks: Definition 2 marks, Typical range 1 mark)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** List any **four factors** that affect the capacity factor of an offshore wind farm. **Model Answer:** 1. **Wind resource characteristics** — Mean wind speed, Weibull parameters (k, c), wind direction consistency 2. **Turbine design parameters** — Cut-in speed, rated speed, rotor diameter, hub height 3. **Wake losses** — Inter-turbine spacing and wind farm layout effects 4. **Availability and maintenance** — Downtime for scheduled and unscheduled maintenance 5. **Electrical and transmission losses** — Cable losses, transformer efficiency 6. **Environmental conditions** — Air density, turbulence intensity, icing *(Any four factors: 3 marks)* --- ### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - Students commonly **lose 2-3 marks** by forgetting to apply losses after calculating gross AEP > - **Critical mistake:** Calculating CF as $P_{mean}/P_{rated}$ instead of $AEP_{net}/(P_{rated} \times 8760)$ for NET capacity factor > - Many students **omit unit conversions** — always keep MW for power, MWh for energy, and 8760 hours for annual calculations > - **Weibull integration** errors: forgetting to multiply by $\Delta v$ in numerical integration, or using wrong Gamma function values > - **Betz limit confusion**: Students often confuse the Betz coefficient with turbine power coefficient $C_p$ --- ### **PART B - Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam - Model Question]** A shipping company plans to install a **100 MW offshore wind farm** consisting of 20 turbines, each rated at 5 MW, off the coast of Gujarat, India. The wind resource at the hub height (100 m) has been characterized by a Weibull distribution with shape parameter **k = 2.0** and scale parameter **c = 9.0 m/s**. The turbine specifications are: - Cut-in speed: 4 m/s - Rated speed: 13 m/s - Cut-out speed: 25 m/s - Rotor diameter: 130 m - Air density: 1.225 kg/m³ The estimated losses are: availability (4%), wake (6%), electrical (2%), and other (3%). **Calculate:** **(a)** [7 marks] The mean power output per turbine and the **gross capacity factor** using numerical integration of the turbine power curve with the Weibull distribution. Use wind speed bins of 1 m/s. **(b)** [7 marks] The **gross and net Annual Energy Production (AEP)** for the entire wind farm, and the **net capacity factor**. --- #### **Solution to Question A:** ### Part (a): Mean Power Output and Gross Capacity Factor **Step 1: Calculate Swept Area** $$ A = \pi \times (130/2)^2 = \pi \times 65^2 = 13{,}273 \, m^2 $$ **Step 2: Define Turbine Power Curve** For $4 \leq v \leq 13$ m/s: $$ P(v) = 5 \times \frac{v^2 - 16}{169 - 16} = 5 \times \frac{v^2 - 16}{153} $$ **Step 3: Calculate Weibull PDF at Various Wind Speeds** $$ f(v) = \frac{2.0}{9.0}\left(\frac{v}{9.0}\right)^{1.0} e^{-(v/9.0)^2} $$ | v (m/s) | $f(v)$ | P(v) (MW) | $f(v) \times P(v) \times \Delta v$ | |---|---|---|---| | 0-4 | — | 0 | 0 | | 4 | 0.0853 | 0 | 0 | | 5 | 0.0995 | 0.294 | 0.0293 | | 6 | 0.1067 | 0.784 | 0.0836 | | 7 | 0.1067 | 1.471 | 0.1569 | | 8 | 0.0995 | 2.353 | 0.2341 | | 9 | 0.0878 | 3.431 | 0.3012 | | 10 | 0.0733 | 4.706 | 0.3450 | | 11 | 0.0581 | 5.000 | 0.2905 | | 12 | 0.0431 | 5.000 | 0.2155 | | 13-25 | — | 5.000 | 0.431 | | **Total** | | | **≈ 2.09 MW** | **Detailed Calculation for v = 11 m/s:** $$ P(11) = 5 \times \frac{121 - 16}{153} = 5 \times \frac{105}{153} = 5 \times 0.686 = 3.43 \, MW $$ Wait, that's wrong. Let me recalculate: $$ P(11) = 5 \times \frac{11^2 - 4^2}{13^2 - 4^2} = 5 \times \frac{121 - 16}{169 - 16} = 5 \times \frac{105}{153} = 3.43 \, MW $$ **Corrected Power Curve Values:** | v (m/s) | $P(v)$ (MW) | |---|---| | 5 | 0.294 | | 6 | 0.784 | | 7 | 1.471 | | 8 | 2.353 | | 9 | 3.431 | | 10 | 4.706 | | 11 | 5.000 (capped at rated) | | 12 | 5.000 | | 13+ | 5.000 | **Mean Power Output:** $$ \boxed{P_{mean} \approx 2.10 \, MW} $$ **Gross Capacity Factor:** $$ CF_{gross} = \frac{2.10}{5.0} = \boxed{0.420 = 42.0\%} $$ --- ### Part (b): Annual Energy Production and Net Capacity Factor **Step 1: Gross AEP** $$ AEP_{gross} = N \times P_r \times CF_{gross} \times 8760 $$ $$ AEP_{gross} = 20 \times 5 \times 0.420 \times 8760 $$ $$ AEP_{gross} = 100 \times 0.420 \times 8760 $$ $$ \boxed{AEP_{gross} = 367{,}920 \, MWh = 367.92 \, GWh} $$ **Step 2: Combined Loss Factor** $$ \eta = (1 - 0.04) \times (1 - 0.06) \times (1 - 0.02) \times (1 - 0.03) $$ $$ \eta = 0.96 \times 0.94 \times 0.98 \times 0.97 $$ Calculating step-by-step: - $0.96 \times 0.94 = 0.9024$ - $0.9024 \times 0.98 = 0.8844$ - $0.8844 \times 0.97 = 0.8579$ $$ \boxed{\eta = 0.858} $$ **Step 3: Net AEP** $$ AEP_{net} = 367.92 \times 0.858 = \boxed{315.68 \, GWh} $$ **Step 4: Net Capacity Factor** $$ CF_{net} = \frac{AEP_{net}}{P_{total} \times 8760} = \frac{315{,}680}{100 \times 8760} = \frac{315{,}680}{876{,}000} $$ $$ \boxed{CF_{net} = 0.360 = 36.0\%} $$ --- ### **PART B - Question B (14 Marks) - Alternative** **[KTU University Exam - Model Question]** A **tidal energy farm** is being designed for installation in the Gulf of Kutch, India. The site has a **mean tidal current velocity of 1.8 m/s** with a tidal cycle of 12.4 hours. The proposed system uses **12 underwater tidal turbines**, each with: - Rotor diameter: 20 m - Power coefficient: 0.40 - Cut-in speed: 0.5 m/s - Cut-out speed: 3.5 m/s - Generator efficiency: 90% Sea water density: **1025 kg/m³**. Assume the turbines operate for 60% of the tidal cycle (effective operation time considering slack tide). **Calculate:** **(a)** [7 marks] The **available tidal power per turbine** and the **average power output per turbine**. **(b)** [7 marks] The **total annual energy generation** of the tidal farm and its **capacity factor** assuming each turbine has a rated capacity of 1 MW. --- #### **Solution to Question B:** ### Part (a): Available Tidal Power and Average Power Output **Step 1: Swept Area** $$ A = \pi \times (20/2)^2 = \pi \times 100 = 314.16 \, m^2 $$ **Step 2: Available Tidal Power** $$ P_{available} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 $$ $$ P_{available} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 314.16 \times (1.8)^3 $$ $$ P_{available} = 512.5 \times 314.16 \times 5.832 $$ $$ P_{available} = 938{,}675 \, W \approx 0.939 \, MW $$ **Step 3: Extractable Power (with $C_p$)** $$ P_{extractable} = C_p \times P_{available} = 0.40 \times 0.939 = 0.376 \, MW $$ **Step 4: Actual Power Output (with generator efficiency)** $$ P_{output} = \eta_{gen} \times P_{extractable} = 0.90 \times 0.376 $$ $$ \boxed{P_{output} = 0.338 \, MW = 338 \, kW \text{ per turbine}} $$ --- ### Part (b): Annual Energy and Capacity Factor **Step 1: Effective Operation Hours per Day** Tidal cycles per day: $24/12.4 \approx 1.94$ cycles Operation time per cycle: $12.4 \times 0.60 = 7.44$ hours Operation per day: $1.94 \times 7.44 = 14.43$ hours Operation per year: $14.43 \times 365 = 5268$ hours **Step 2: Annual Energy per Turbine** $$ E_{annual,turbine} = P_{output} \times 5268 = 0.338 \times 5268 = 1780.6 \, MWh $$ **Step 3: Total Annual Energy for Farm** $$ E_{annual,total} = 12 \times 1780.6 = 21{,}367 \, MWh $$ $$ \boxed{E_{annual,total} = 21.37 \, GWh} $$ **Step 4: Capacity Factor** Total installed capacity = $12 \times 1 = 12$ MW $$ CF = \frac{E_{annual,total}}{P_{total} \times 8760} = \frac{21{,}367}{12 \times 8760} = \frac{21{,}367}{105{,}120} $$ $$ \boxed{CF = 0.203 = 20.3\%} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Note:** Tidal capacity factors are typically lower (20-30%) than offshore wind due to the bidirectional nature of tides and required cut-out at high velocities for safety. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember **📋 High-Density Revision Checklist:** - ✅ **Capacity Factor (CF)** = Actual Energy / (Rated Power × Time) - ✅ **Standard values**: Air density = **1.225 kg/m³** (air), **1025 kg/m³** (sea water) - ✅ **Hours per year = 8760** - ✅ **Weibull PDF formula** and **Gamma function values** are essential - ✅ **Three regions of power curve**: Below cut-in (zero), cubic region (cut-in to rated), constant region (rated to cut-out) - ✅ **Mean cube of wind speed**: $\langle v^3 \rangle = c^3 \Gamma(1 + 3/k)$ - ✅ **Betz limit = 16/27 ≈ 0.593** (theoretical maximum $C_p$) - ✅ **Typical CF ranges**: Offshore wind (35-55%), Tidal (20-30%), Wave (15-25%), Onshore wind (25-40%) - ✅ **Loss categories**: Availability (3-5%), Wake (5-10%), Electrical (2-5%), Environmental (2-5%) - ✅ **Gross vs. Net AEP**: Gross = theoretical, Net = after all derating factors - ✅ **Equivalent Full Load Hours (EFLH)** = CF × 8760 **Common Exam Formulae to Memorize:** | Formula | Application | |---|---| | $P_{wind} = 0.5 \rho A v^3$ | Available wind power | | $A = \pi D^2/4$ | Swept area | | $CF = E_{actual}/(P_r \times 8760)$ | Capacity factor | | $EFLH = CF \times 8760$ | Equivalent full load hours | | $f(v) = (k/c)(v/c)^{k-1}e^{-(v/c)^k}$ | Weibull PDF | | $\bar{v} = c \Gamma(1 + 1/k)$ | Mean wind speed | **Final Exam Tips:** 1. Always state assumptions clearly 2. Show all calculation steps (partial credit) 3. Include units in every step 4. Cross-check final answers for physical reasonableness 5. For offshore wind, expect CF between 30-50% 6. Remember: Gross CF uses theoretical max; Net CF uses actual energy after losses

topics.title: Marine Spatial Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST755 - RENEWABLE ENERGY IN MARITIME SECTOR
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Marine Spatial Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Marine Spatial Planning (MSP)** is defined by UNESCO's Intergovernmental Oceanographic Commission (IOC) as a **public process of analyzing and allocating the spatial and temporal distribution of human activities in marine areas to achieve ecological, economic, and social objectives that are usually specified through a political and participatory process**.

In the context of **KTU PENST755 (Renewable Energy in Maritime Sector)**, MSP refers specifically to the **zoning, governance, and regulatory framework** that determines **where** offshore renewable energy installations (wind, tidal, wave, OTEC, and floating solar) can be deployed, **how** they co-exist with other maritime sectors (shipping, fisheries, defense, tourism), and **under what environmental and social constraints** they must operate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:** Marine Spatial Planning is a *cross-sectoral, ecosystem-based, adaptive, and participatory* planning tool that balances **Blue Economy growth** with **marine ecosystem conservation** — it is the "Zoning Master Plan" of the ocean.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the ocean as a **large, shared apartment building** (the marine space). Every tenant (sector) wants to use the rooms differently:
- One tenant (fisherman) wants the **ground floor** for anchorage and fishing.
- Another (shipping company) wants the **wide corridors** for transport routes.
- A new tenant (offshore wind developer) wants the **rooftop** (shallow shelf areas) for turbines.
- The **environmentalist** insists certain "quiet zones" (Marine Protected Areas) be kept empty.

Without a **building management committee** (MSP authority), chaos and lawsuits follow. **Marine Spatial Planning is exactly this management committee** — it draws the floor plan, sets the rules, mediates disputes, and ensures sustainable coexistence.

| Analogy Element | Real Marine Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Apartment Building | Marine / Coastal Zone |
| Tenants | Maritime Sectors (Energy, Fisheries, Shipping) |
| Floor Plan | MSP Zoning Map |
| Building Rules | MSP Regulations & Permits |
| Quiet Zones | Marine Protected Areas (MPAs) |
| Building Committee | MSP Authority / Coastal States |

## 1.3 Key Physical / Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics govern MSP decision-making and are essential for KTU numerical/descriptive questions:

- **EEZ (Exclusive Economic Zone):** **200 nautical miles** (≈ **370.4 km**) from baseline.
- **Territorial Sea:** **12 nautical miles** (≈ **22.22 km**) from baseline.
- **Baseline:** The low-water line along the coast as officially recognized.
- **1 Nautical Mile:** **1.852 km** exactly (standard ISO definition).
- **Continental Shelf:** Up to **350 nautical miles** from baseline (UNCLOS Article 76) or to the edge of the continental margin.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for Renewable Energy:** Over **80% of global offshore wind potential** lies within the **200 nautical mile EEZ** of coastal states. MSP is the legal-institutional gatekeeper for accessing this resource.

## 1.4 Origin and Evolution of MSP

- **1960s–1990s:** Single-sector management dominated (fisheries, oil & gas, shipping handled separately).
- **2002:** World Summit on Sustainable Development (WSSD) called for MSP application by **2010** in coastal states.
- **2006:** UNESCO-IOC published the **"Joint Approach to Marine Spatial Planning"** (the seminal framework).
- **2014:** European Union formally adopted the **MSP Directive (2014/89/EU)** — first regional legal binding framework.
- **2019 onwards:** India began MSP discourse under the **Ministry of Earth Sciences (MoES)** and **National Centre for Sustainable Aquaculture (NCSA)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU exam purposes, remember the **three pillars of MSP** (UNESCO-IOC):
> 1. **Ecosystem-based management**
> 2. **Spatial and temporal allocation**
> 3. **Participatory, cross-sectoral governance**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Core Principles of Marine Spatial Planning (KTU High-Yield)

MSP is built on **seven universally accepted principles** as codified by UNESCO-IOC and the EU MSP Directive. These are the most frequently tested concepts.

| # | Principle | Meaning (Simplified) | Renewable Energy Link |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Ecosystem-Based Approach (EBA)** | Manage human activities considering the entire ecosystem, not just the target species/resource | Wind farms must avoid migratory bird paths, marine mammal breeding zones |
| 2 | **Precautionary Principle** | If an activity risks harm, take preventive measures even without full scientific certainty | Mandatory EIA before commissioning offshore wind |
| 3 | **Adaptive Management** | Plans are revised periodically as new data emerges | Turbine layout revised after 5-year environmental monitoring |
| 4 | **Participatory Governance** | All stakeholders (industry, fishers, communities, scientists) are consulted | Public hearings for offshore wind project siting |
| 5 | **Spatial & Temporal Allocation** | Distribute activities in space and time to minimize conflict | Tidal turbines operate only during slack tide; shipping lanes shift seasonally |
| 6 | **Sustainability (Triple Bottom Line)** | Balance **economic, social, environmental** goals | Blue Economy + Net Zero + Coastal Livelihoods |
| 7 | **Integration Across Sectors & Scales** | Coordinate plans across local, national, regional levels | Aligning India's Sagarmala with IMO and SDG-14 |

## 2.2 The 10-Step MSP Process (UNESCO-IOC Framework)

This is the **operational backbone** of Marine Spatial Planning and a **high-yield KTU topic**.

1. **Identify the need & establish authority** — Political mandate.
2. **Define goals & objectives** — Vision statement (e.g., "30 GW offshore wind by 2030").
3. **Analyze existing conditions** — Baseline data: bathymetry, biodiversity, currents, shipping density.
4. **Engage stakeholders** — Public consultation, Indigenous & fisherfolk representation.
5. **Develop spatial zoning scenarios** — GIS-based alternative maps.
6. **Evaluate trade-offs** — Multi-Criteria Decision Analysis (MCDA).
7. **Select preferred plan & allocate space** — Formal zoning decision.
8. **Implement, monitor, enforce** — Permits, surveillance (e.g., satellite AIS, drones).
9. **Review & adapt** — 5–10 year review cycle.
10. **Communicate outcomes** — Public dashboards, marine atlases.

## 2.3 Zoning Categories in MSP (Exam Favorite)

MSP maps divide the ocean into **functional zones**. The standard zoning typology is:

| Zone Type | Symbol | Allowed Use | Renewable Energy Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Preservation / Conservation Zone** | High protection | No extraction, low-impact research only | Excluded from renewable energy |
| **Marine Protected Area (MPA)** | Strong protection | Regulated tourism, scientific study | Buffer zone for cables |
| **Renewable Energy Zone (REZ)** | Designated priority | Wind, tidal, wave, OTEC, floating PV | **Primary deployment zone** |
| **Multi-Use Zone** | Co-location permitted | Aquaculture + Wind, Tourism + Tidal | Floating wind + seaweed farming |
| **Shipping Lane / Cable Corridor** | Linear infrastructure | Vessel routes, inter-array cables | Submarine cable routing |
| **Fishing Zone** | Traditional use | Trawling, artisanal fisheries | Co-existence protocols |
| **Buffer / Transition Zone** | Mitigation | Reduced activity to absorb impacts | Sediment plume control |
| **Defence / Security Zone** | Restricted | Naval, strategic installations | Exclusion areas |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas and planning metrics are essential for KTU numerical/descriptive answers.

| # | Concept | Formula / Metric | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Nautical Mile to Kilometer | $d_{km} = d_{NM} \times 1.852$ | km | EEZ/Territorial Sea conversion |
| 2 | EEZ area (simplified rectangular model) | $A_{EEZ} = L \times W$ where $W = 200\,NM$ | km² | Approximate jurisdictional area |
| 3 | Power Density (offshore wind) | $P_{den} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 C_p$ | W/m² | Capacity per unit sea area |
| 4 | Wind Farm Capacity Factor | $CF = \frac{E_{actual}}{E_{rated} \times 8760} \times 100$ | % | Energy yield assessment |
| 5 | Land/Sea Equivalent Ratio (offshore wind) | $A_{land} \approx 10 \times A_{sea}$ for same MW | ratio | Spatial footprint comparison |
| 6 | Zoning Suitability Index (MCDA) | $SI = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \times s_i$ | dimensionless | Multi-criteria scoring |
| 7 | Setback Distance (turbine spacing) | $D_{min} = k \times D_{rotor}$ (typically $k = 5$ to $8$) | m | Spatial layout in REZ |
| 8 | Cable Length Approximation | $L_{cable} \approx \sqrt{A_{REZ}} \times 1.2$ | km | Submarine cable routing |
| 9 | Carbon Avoided (offshore wind) | $CO_{2,avoided} = E_{gen} \times EF_{coal}$ | tonnes | MSP climate justification |
| 10 | MSP Review Cycle | $T_{review} = 5$ to $10$ years | years | Adaptive management |

Where:
- $\rho$ = air density ≈ **1.225 kg/m³**
- $A$ = swept area of rotor (m²)
- $v$ = wind speed (m/s)
- $C_p$ = power coefficient (Betz limit = **0.593**, practical = 0.35–0.45)
- $w_i$ = weight of criterion *i*; $s_i$ = score of criterion *i*

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the maritime renewable energy sector, MSP serves **four critical engineering-decision functions**:

1. **Siting Decision Support:** Determines optimal turbine location (wind resource × grid proximity × seabed condition × exclusion zones).
2. **Conflict Resolution Mechanism:** Mediates spatial overlap between oil & gas platforms, fishing zones, and wind farms (e.g., North Sea, Bay of Bengal).
3. **Environmental Carrying Capacity Enforcement:** Sets cumulative impact limits (e.g., maximum % of EEZ that can be developed).
4. **Permit & Licensing Gateway:** Acts as the upstream regulatory filter before project-specific Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** MSP is **NOT a one-time map**. It is a **living, adaptive, GIS-driven governance process** — its value is in the **feedback loop**, not the static plan.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Marine Spatial Planning is a **policy-engineering hybrid discipline**. This section provides (a) a complete numerical derivation of MSP boundary conversions and wind farm spatial footprint, and (b) an analytical decision matrix for KTU exam problems.

## 3.1 Numerical Derivation: EEZ Area and Renewable Energy Zone Capacity

### Problem Setup
A coastal state has a **straight coastline of length 300 km**. Its EEZ extends **200 nautical miles** offshore. The MSP authority designates the outer **50 nautical miles** of the EEZ as a **Renewable Energy Zone (REZ)**, of which only **30%** is technically feasible (after excluding shipping lanes, MPAs, and depth > 50 m zones). The MSP rule permits offshore wind at a **spatial power density of 8 MW/km²**.

**Find:**
(a) Total EEZ area in km²
(b) REZ area in km²
(c) Technically feasible REZ area
(d) Maximum installable offshore wind capacity

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Convert nautical miles to kilometers** (using the standard factor **1 NM = 1.852 km**):

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{EEZ} &= 200 \times 1.852 \\
&= 370.4\ \text{km}
\end{aligned}
$$

The width of the REZ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{REZ} &= 50 \times 1.852 \\
&= 92.6\ \text{km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Total EEZ area** (using the simplified rectangular approximation, $A_{EEZ} = L \times W$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{EEZ} &= 300\ \text{km} \times 370.4\ \text{km} \\
&= 111{,}120\ \text{km}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the REZ area** (outer 50 NM strip of EEZ):

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{REZ} &= 300\ \text{km} \times 92.6\ \text{km} \\
&= 27{,}780\ \text{km}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Technically Feasible REZ** (apply 30% feasibility factor):

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{feas} &= A_{REZ} \times 0.30 \\
&= 27{,}780 \times 0.30 \\
&= 8{,}334\ \text{km}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Maximum Installable Wind Capacity** (multiply by spatial power density):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{max} &= A_{feas} \times P_{den} \\
&= 8{,}334\ \text{km}^2 \times 8\ \text{MW/km}^2 \\
&= 66{,}672\ \text{MW} \\
&\approx 66.7\ \text{GW}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Result Summary

| Quantity | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| EEZ Width | $W_{EEZ}$ | 370.4 km |
| REZ Width | $W_{REZ}$ | 92.6 km |
| Total EEZ Area | $A_{EEZ}$ | 111,120 km² |
| REZ Area | $A_{REZ}$ | 27,780 km² |
| Feasible REZ Area | $A_{feas}$ | 8,334 km² |
| Maximum Wind Capacity | $P_{max}$ | **66,672 MW ≈ 66.7 GW** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation for KTU:** Out of the **111,120 km²** of EEZ, only **7.5%** is theoretically allocable to wind energy. This is precisely why **MSP zoning efficiency and multi-use strategies** are critical — a poorly planned MSP can waste the most valuable ocean real estate.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Multi-Criteria Decision Analysis (MCDA) for REZ Siting

In KTU descriptive answers, students may be asked to **justify why a particular site is chosen**. The MCDA scoring formula is:

$$
SI_{site} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_i
$$

Where:
- $w_i$ = weight of criterion *i* (sum of all $w_i$ = 1)
- $s_i$ = score of criterion *i* on a 0–10 scale
- Higher $SI$ = more suitable site

### Worked Example: Comparing Three Offshore Wind Sites

| Criterion | Weight $w_i$ | Site A Score | Site B Score | Site C Score |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Wind speed potential | 0.30 | 9 | 7 | 8 |
| Grid distance (inverse) | 0.20 | 6 | 9 | 7 |
| Water depth (shallower = better) | 0.20 | 8 | 5 | 9 |
| Distance from shipping lane | 0.15 | 7 | 8 | 6 |
| Distance from MPA | 0.10 | 9 | 6 | 8 |
| Fisherfolk conflict potential (inverse) | 0.05 | 5 | 8 | 7 |
| **Suitability Index $SI$** | **1.00** | **7.65** | **7.20** | **7.65** |

**Calculation for Site A:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SI_A &= (0.30 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 6) + (0.20 \times 8) + (0.15 \times 7) + (0.10 \times 9) + (0.05 \times 5) \\
&= 2.70 + 1.20 + 1.60 + 1.05 + 0.90 + 0.25 \\
&= 7.70
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision Logic:** A and C are tied, but **Site A** has higher wind resource — typically chosen for renewable energy deployment. This is the **heart of MSP analytical reasoning** that KTU examiners expect.

## 3.3 Decision Flow for Conflict Resolution in MSP

When two sectors compete for the same sea area, MSP applies this logic:

$$
\text{Conflict Intensity} = f(D_{overlap},\ V_{activity},\ T_{simultaneity})
$$

- If conflict intensity > threshold → **spatial separation** (zoning)
- If conflict intensity = moderate → **temporal separation** (e.g., fishing closed during construction)
- If conflict intensity = low → **co-location** (multi-use, e.g., wind + aquaculture)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse *Marine Spatial Planning* with *Marine Protected Areas*. MSP is the **overall zoning master plan**; MPAs are **one specific zone** within the MSP framework.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: The MSP Process (UNESCO-IOC 10-Step Model)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Identify Need and Establish Authority] --> B[Step 2: Define Goals and Objectives]
    B --> C[Step 3: Analyze Existing Conditions and Baseline Data]
    C --> D[Step 4: Engage Stakeholders and Public Participation]
    D --> E[Step 5: Develop Spatial Zoning Scenarios GIS-based]
    E --> F[Step 6: Evaluate Trade-offs Using MCDA]
    F --> G[Step 7: Select Preferred Plan and Allocate Space]
    G --> H[Step 8: Implement Monitor and Enforce]
    H --> I[Step 9: Review and Adapt 5 to 10 Year Cycle]
    I --> J[Step 10: Communicate Outcomes to Public]
    J -.feedback loop.-> C

    style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#1e40af,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#0e7490,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#0d9488,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#15803d,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#65a30d,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#ca8a04,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff
    style I fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style J fill:#581c87,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: MSP Functional Architecture for Renewable Energy Integration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Policy Drivers] --> P2[SDG 14, Paris Agreement, Blue Economy]
    P2 --> P3[MSP Authority and Governance Body]
    P3 --> P4[Data Layer: Bathymetry, Wind, Biodiversity, Shipping]
    P4 --> P5[GIS Based Suitability Mapping]
    P5 --> P6[Zoning Output: REZ, MPA, Shipping, Fishing]
    P6 --> P7[Permit Issuance for Renewable Energy Projects]
    P7 --> P8[Project: Offshore Wind, Tidal, Wave, Floating PV]
    P8 --> P9[Monitoring: Environmental, Social, Technical]
    P9 --> P10[Adaptive Review and Plan Update]
    P10 -.feedback.-> P3

    style P1 fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style P8 fill:#15803d,color:#ffffff
    style P10 fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Zoning Hierarchy Diagram (Spatial Allocation Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Marine Space Total EEZ] --> Z1[Conservation Zone No Development]
    M --> Z2[Marine Protected Area Limited Use]
    M --> Z3[Renewable Energy Zone REZ Priority]
    M --> Z4[Multi-Use Zone Co-Location]
    M --> Z5[Shipping and Cable Corridor]
    M --> Z6[Fishing Zone Traditional]
    M --> Z7[Defence and Security Zone]

    Z3 --> R1[Offshore Wind]
    Z3 --> R2[Tidal Energy]
    Z3 --> R3[Wave Energy]
    Z3 --> R4[Floating Solar PV]
    Z3 --> R5[OTEC and Ocean Thermal]

    style M fill:#0c4a6e,color:#ffffff
    style Z1 fill:#166534,color:#ffffff
    style Z2 fill:#15803d,color:#ffffff
    style Z3 fill:#ca8a04,color:#ffffff
    style Z4 fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> These diagrams follow KTU's preference for **process-flow representation** of complex planning frameworks, replacing the need for hand-drawn schematics in the exam hall.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Marine Spatial Planning (MSP) and list its three core principles.
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Marine Spatial Planning (MSP) is a public process of analyzing and allocating the spatial and temporal distribution of human activities in marine areas to achieve ecological, economic, and social objectives, usually specified through a political and participatory process (UNESCO-IOC definition).

**Three Core Principles:**
1. **Ecosystem-based management** — considering the whole ecosystem, not isolated species or sectors.
2. **Spatial and temporal allocation** — distributing activities across space and time to minimize conflict.
3. **Participatory, cross-sectoral governance** — involving all stakeholders in decision-making.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write only "zoning of ocean" — this is **incomplete**. MSP is a **process**, not just a map. Always include the words *public, participatory, ecosystem-based, and adaptive*.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between a Marine Protected Area (MPA) and a Renewable Energy Zone (REZ) in the context of MSP.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Marine Protected Area (MPA) | Renewable Energy Zone (REZ) |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Objective** | Biodiversity conservation, ecosystem protection | Clean energy generation from offshore renewables |
| **Allowed Activities** | Limited research, regulated tourism, no extraction | Wind farms, tidal, wave, floating solar, OTEC |
| **Governance Priority** | Highest environmental protection | Highest energy production priority |
| **Typical Size** | Smaller, often < 500 km² | Larger, often thousands of km² |
| **Conflict Resolution Role** | Acts as exclusion buffer | Co-located with shipping/fishing via multi-use rules |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do NOT state that REZ is a subset of MPA or vice-versa. They are **parallel, complementary zones** within the same MSP framework.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **(Choose A or B)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **UNESCO-IOC 10-step framework** for Marine Spatial Planning, highlighting how each step integrates renewable energy siting.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A coastal state's EEZ extends **200 NM** from a **500 km** straight coastline. The MSP authority designates the **outermost 30 NM** as a Renewable Energy Zone. After excluding shipping lanes (10% loss) and deep water (20% loss), the spatial power density of offshore wind is **7 MW/km²**. Calculate the total EEZ area, REZ area, feasible area, and maximum installable capacity.

---

### Model Answer to Question A:

#### (a) UNESCO-IOC 10-Step MSP Framework with Renewable Energy Integration

| Step | Process | Renewable Energy Integration |
|---|---|---|
| 1. **Identify need & establish authority** | Political mandate, legal basis | Ministry of New & Renewable Energy (MNRE) designated as lead |
| 2. **Define goals & objectives** | Vision statement, e.g., "30 GW offshore wind by 2030" | Quantitative RE target embedded in MSP |
| 3. **Analyze baseline conditions** | Bathymetry, wind atlas, biodiversity, shipping density | Wind resource mapping, marine mammal surveys |
| 4. **Stakeholder engagement** | Fishers, coastal communities, industries, scientists | Fisherfolk consultations, Industry MoUs |
| 5. **Develop zoning scenarios** | GIS-based alternative maps | Multi-scenario REZ maps generated |
| 6. **Evaluate trade-offs (MCDA)** | Weighted scoring of alternatives | Wind resource, grid proximity, environmental sensitivity |
| 7. **Select preferred plan & allocate space** | Formal zoning decision | REZ boundaries legally notified |
| 8. **Implement, monitor, enforce** | Permits, satellite AIS, patrol vessels | Pre-construction EIA, post-commissioning bird/bat monitoring |
| 9. **Review & adapt** | 5–10 year review cycle | Capacity additions, technology upgrades incorporated |
| 10. **Communicate outcomes** | Public dashboards, marine atlases | RE progress, capacity factor, CO₂ avoided published |

#### (b) Numerical Solution

**Step 1 — Convert NM to km** ($1\,NM = 1.852\,km$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{EEZ} &= 200 \times 1.852 = 370.4\ \text{km} \\
W_{REZ} &= 30 \times 1.852 = 55.56\ \text{km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total EEZ area:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{EEZ} &= 500 \times 370.4 \\
&= 185{,}200\ \text{km}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — REZ area:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{REZ} &= 500 \times 55.56 \\
&= 27{,}780\ \text{km}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply exclusions (10% + 20% = 30% total loss):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{feas} &= 27{,}780 \times (1 - 0.30) \\
&= 27{,}780 \times 0.70 \\
&= 19{,}446\ \text{km}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Maximum installable capacity** (at 7 MW/km²):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{max} &= 19{,}446 \times 7 \\
&= 136{,}122\ \text{MW} \\
&\approx 136.1\ \text{GW}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Results Table:

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| EEZ Area | 185,200 km² |
| REZ Area | 27,780 km² |
| Feasible REZ Area | 19,446 km² |
| Maximum Wind Capacity | **136,122 MW ≈ 136.1 GW** |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **role of Marine Spatial Planning in accelerating the Blue Economy**, with special reference to the integration of **offshore wind, tidal, wave, and floating solar PV** in India's maritime sector.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Using a labeled diagram, explain the **different zoning categories** used in Marine Spatial Planning. Mention how **multi-use zones** can enhance renewable energy deployment.

#### Model Answer Outline for Question B:

**(a)** MSP accelerates Blue Economy by:
- **De-risking investment** through clear regulatory pathways.
- **Reducing conflict** between fisheries, oil & gas, shipping, and renewables.
- **Optimizing spatial capital** by co-locating wind farms with aquaculture (e.g., seaweed, mussel farming).
- **Supporting India's targets:** 30 GW offshore wind by 2030 (MNRE).
- **Linking to Sagarmala, PM-SAGY, and National Blue Economy Policy.**

**(b)** Zoning diagram: Refer to **Section 4.3** of these notes. Multi-use zones increase revenue per km², reduce visual footprint, and support **fisherfolk livelihood diversification** — a key KTU high-yield point.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Always show unit conversions** in numerical answers — losing 1 mark for forgetting "1 NM = 1.852 km" is common.
> 2. **Do not confuse MSP with MPA** — these are **different concepts** with different objectives.
> 3. In long answers, **always include at least one example** (e.g., North Sea, Gulf of Kutch, Sagarmala) for higher marks.
> 4. MSP is a **process** — avoid framing it as a "one-time plan."
> 5. The **three pillars (ecological, economic, social)** must be mentioned to score full marks in definition questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **MSP Definition (UNESCO-IOC):** Public, participatory, ecosystem-based allocation of marine activities in space and time.
- [ ] **Three Pillars:** Ecological + Economic + Social objectives.
- [ ] **Three Core Principles:** Ecosystem-based, Spatial-Temporal, Participatory.
- [ ] **MSP is a process, not a map** — emphasize the **10-step UNESCO-IOC cycle**.
- [ ] **1 Nautical Mile = 1.852 km** (ISO standard, must memorize).
- [ ] **EEZ = 200 NM; Territorial Sea = 12 NM; Continental Shelf ≤ 350 NM** (UNCLOS).
- [ ] **Zoning Types:** Conservation, MPA, REZ, Multi-Use, Shipping/Cable, Fishing, Defence, Buffer.
- [ ] **REZ** is the **primary zone** for offshore wind, tidal, wave, floating PV, OTEC.
- [ ] **MCDA formula:** $SI = \sum w_i \cdot s_i$ — used for site suitability ranking.
- [ ] **Spatial power density of offshore wind:** typically **5–10 MW/km²**.
- [ ] **Wind farm capacity factor:** typically **35–55%** for offshore.
- [ ] **Adaptive management cycle:** **5–10 years** per review.
- [ ] **EU MSP Directive (2014/89/EU):** world's first binding regional MSP law.
- [ ] **India context:** Sagarmala Programme, MNRE 30 GW offshore wind target, Blue Economy Policy 2021.
- [ ] **MSP vs MPA:** MSP = master plan; MPA = one zone type within the MSP.
- [ ] **Co-location / Multi-use** increases revenue, reduces conflict, enhances social acceptance.
- [ ] **Precautionary Principle** mandates EIA before any RE installation in MSP-governed waters.
- [ ] **Key Global Examples:** North Sea (UK-Germany-Denmark-Netherlands), Gulf of Maine (USA-Canada), Baltic Sea, Bay of Bengal (India-Bangladesh-Myanmar).

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Tip:** When a 14-mark question is asked on MSP, structure your answer as: **Definition (2 marks) → Principles (3 marks) → Process/Steps (4 marks) → Renewable Energy Integration (3 marks) → Indian Context with Example (2 marks)**. This format has consistently scored full marks in past KTU evaluations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Closed and Open Cycle, Environmental Impacts of OTEC, Salinity Gradients
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST755 - RENEWABLE ENERGY IN MARITIME SECTOR
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Closed and Open Cycle, Environmental Impacts of OTEC, Salinity Gradients

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion (OTEC) — Refresher

**OTEC (Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion)** is a marine renewable energy technology that exploits the **temperature differential (ΔT)** between the warm **surface seawater** (sun-heated tropical upper layer, ~25–30 °C) and the **cold deep seawater** (below the thermocline, ~4–7 °C at depths of 800–1000 m). This temperature gradient is the thermal "head" that drives thermodynamic cycles producing electricity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Highlight:** For OTEC plants to be thermodynamically and economically feasible, a **minimum ΔT of 20 °C** between surface and deep ocean water is mandatory. This restricts commercially viable OTEC to **tropical latitudes between ~20° N and 20° S.**

---

## 1.2 Closed Cycle OTEC (CC-OTEC) — The "Refrigerator-in-Reverse"

### 📌 Formal Definition
A **Closed Cycle OTEC** system uses a **secondary working fluid** (a low-boiling-point refrigerant such as **ammonia (NH₃)**, **R-134a**, or **R-22**) confined within a sealed Rankine cycle loop. Warm surface seawater indirectly transfers heat to this working fluid inside an **evaporator (heat exchanger)**, vaporizing it. The high-pressure vapor expands through a **turbine** coupled to a generator, then is condensed back to liquid by cold deep seawater inside a **condenser**.

### 🍕 Intuitive Analogy — "The Tea-Kettle on a Fridge"
Imagine you boil a small amount of highly volatile liquid inside a sealed kettle (the evaporator) using hot tropical seawater piped over its surface — just like steam rising from a kettle. The rising vapor turns a small turbine wheel (the turbine-generator). The exhaust vapor is then cooled by pumping icy deep-sea water over the outside of the condenser coils — as if your kettle were sitting inside a refrigerator. The vapor condenses back to liquid and gets recycled. **Nothing escapes; the working fluid is constantly reused** — hence "closed."

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** The working fluid never contacts seawater directly. Heat transfer happens **across metal walls of heat exchangers**, making heat-exchanger design (plate or shell-and-tube) the single most critical cost driver in CC-OTEC.

---

## 1.3 Open Cycle OTEC (OC-OTEC) — "The Seawater Itself Works"

### 📌 Formal Definition
An **Open Cycle OTEC** system uses **warm surface seawater as the working fluid itself**. The seawater is flash-evaporated in a **partial vacuum chamber** (operating at pressures below the saturation pressure at warm-water temperature), and the resulting low-pressure steam drives a **low-pressure turbine**. The expanded steam is condensed by direct contact with cold deep seawater, producing **fresh potable water** as a valuable byproduct (hence the popular name **"OTEC-DESAL"** plants).

### 🍕 Intuitive Analogy — "The Mountain Spring at Sea"
Picture a thermos flask half-filled with warm seawater in which you suddenly create a strong vacuum. The water begins to boil even though it's only lukewarm — because low pressure lowers the boiling point. The vapor shoots upward, spins a turbine, and is then trapped against cold deep-sea pipes where it condenses as **fresh, drinkable water droplets** (no salt!). The remaining brine and condensed water are recycled or discharged.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Point:** In OC-OTEC, the steam operating pressures are extremely low (typically **1–3 kPa absolute**), meaning turbines must be **very large in diameter** to extract useful work at low pressure drops. This is a defining design challenge.

---

## 1.4 Environmental Impacts of OTEC — A Double-Edged Thermodynamic Sword

Although OTEC is a **near-zero-emission, renewable** technology, it physically interacts with massive volumes of seawater and the deep ocean ecosystem. The major environmental considerations are:

| Impact Category | Mechanism | Severity |
|---|---|---|
| **Cold-water plume discharge** | Deep cold nutrient-rich water is brought to the surface and discharged | Medium-High |
| **Entrainment & Impingement** | Marine organisms (plankton, larvae, small fish) sucked into intake pipes | High |
| **Nutrient redistribution** | Deep water is rich in nitrates, phosphates, silicates — upwelling alters local chemistry | Low-Medium |
| **Biofouling & Chemical Release** | Heat exchangers attract biofouling; anti-fouling chemicals (chlorine) used | Medium |
| **Algal blooms** | Nutrient upwelling may trigger localized eutrophication | Low |
| **Seabed & Deep ecosystem disturbance** | Cold-water pipe deployment can disturb benthic habitats | Low-Medium |
| **GHG emissions (working fluid leakage)** | Ammonia (if used) is mildly toxic; leaks harm atmosphere | Low |

> [!NOTE]
> **Net Environmental Balance:** Compared to fossil-fuel plants, OTEC avoids **~0.6–0.9 kg CO₂/kWh** equivalent emissions. The environmental footprint is **localized and reversible** upon plant decommissioning, making OTEC one of the cleanest baseload renewables.

---

## 1.5 Salinity Gradient Energy (SGE) — "The Power of Mixing"

### 📌 Formal Definition
**Salinity Gradient Energy** is the **Gibbs free energy of mixing** released when two aqueous solutions of different salt concentrations (e.g., **river fresh water** and **ocean salt water**) spontaneously mix. This mixing occurs at **river estuaries** globally and represents an enormous, untapped renewable resource.

### 🍕 Intuitive Analogy — "The Wine Mixer"
If you gently pour fresh water on top of salt water in a glass, you may think nothing happens. But thermodynamically, the system **loses free energy** in the form of an entropy increase — that lost energy *can* be harnessed as electricity using **membranes** that selectively pass either water molecules or ions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Tag:** Two principal SGE conversion technologies are: (1) **Pressure Retarded Osmosis (PRO)** and (2) **Reverse Electrodialysis (RED)**. Both are membrane-based and have no moving thermal parts.

The theoretical global potential of SGE is estimated at **~2.6 TW** (continuous), equivalent to roughly **~80% of present global electricity demand**.

---

## 1.6 Working Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded for Quick Recall)

- **Minimum OTEC ΔT ≈ 20 °C**
- **Carnot (ideal) OTEC efficiency η_C ≈ 6–7 %**
- **Warm surface water temperature T_w ≈ 25–28 °C**
- **Cold deep water temperature T_c ≈ 4–7 °C**
- **OC-OTEC flash chamber pressure ≈ 1–3 kPa (absolute)**
- **Common working fluid for CC-OTEC: NH₃, R-134a, R-22**
- **Theoretical global SGE potential ≈ 2.6 TW (continuous)**
- **Osmotic pressure of seawater (35 g/kg) ≈ 2.8 MPa (≈ 28 bar)**
- **van't Hoff constant for seawater: i ≈ 1.85**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Closed Cycle OTEC — Operational Logic Walkthrough

The **Closed Cycle OTEC** operates on a thermodynamic **Rankine cycle** with the following four internal processes:

1. **Process 1→2: Evaporation (Isobaric Heat Addition)**
   Warm surface seawater (T_w ≈ 26 °C) is pumped through the **evaporator**, transferring heat to the working fluid (typically ammonia) circulating inside tubes. The working fluid absorbs heat at constant pressure and **fully vaporizes** (saturated or slightly superheated vapor).

2. **Process 2→3: Expansion (Isentropic in Turbine)**
   High-pressure vapor expands through a **turbine**, producing mechanical shaft work. Because of low ΔT, the pressure ratio is modest (≈ 3–6 for ammonia), and the **isentropic efficiency** of the turbine (≈ 85–90 %) is critical.

3. **Process 3→4: Condensation (Isobaric Heat Rejection)**
   Cold deep seawater (T_c ≈ 5 °C) is pumped through the **condenser**, removing latent heat from the expanded vapor and returning it to a saturated liquid state.

4. **Process 4→1: Pumping (Isentropic Compression)**
   A **feed pump** raises the liquid working fluid back to the high-side pressure of the evaporator, closing the loop.

### 🎯 Why is CC-OTEC efficiency so low?
Because ΔT is small (~20 K) and the warm source is at low absolute temperature (~300 K), even a **perfect Carnot engine** has:
$$\eta_C = \frac{T_w - T_c}{T_w} \approx \frac{20}{299} \approx 6.7\%$$
A real CC-OTEC plant achieves only **3–4 % net thermal efficiency**. This is offset by the **massive, free thermal reservoir** of the ocean.

---

## 2.2 Open Cycle OTEC — Operational Logic Walkthrough

The **Open Cycle OTEC** is structurally simpler but operates with seawater itself as the working fluid:

1. **Warm seawater intake** — Surface seawater (~26 °C) is pumped into a large vacuum chamber (flash evaporator).
2. **Flash evaporation** — As pressure drops below saturation pressure (~2.5 kPa), water **boils at room temperature**, producing low-pressure steam.
3. **Steam turbine** — Steam (~0.02–0.03 kg/s per m²) expands through a **large-diameter low-pressure turbine** driving a generator.
4. **Direct-contact condenser** — Cold deep seawater is sprayed into the steam, condensing it into **fresh water** (desalinated product).
5. **Discharge** — Brine + condensate is discharged back to the ocean.

### 🎯 Critical Engineering Difference
Unlike CC-OTEC, OC-OTEC has **no metallic barrier** between working fluid and seawater in the evaporator and condenser — the heat exchangers are replaced by **direct-contact vessels**. This eliminates costly metal tubes but introduces **corrosion and biofouling** concerns at the seawater boundary.

---

## 2.3 Hybrid Cycle OTEC — Best of Both Worlds
A **Hybrid OTEC** uses a closed cycle for power generation AND an open cycle to produce fresh water, combining efficiency of CC-OTEC with the desalination benefit of OC-OTEC. Most modern demonstration plants (e.g., the **Sagamihara plant in Japan, 100 kW**) used hybrid configurations.

---

## 2.4 Environmental Impact Mechanism — Detailed

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Exam Focus:** Be prepared to enumerate *at least four* distinct environmental impacts and explain the *cause-effect* mechanism for each.

- **Cold Water Plume Ecology**: A 100 MW OTEC plant pumps ~**8 m³/s of cold deep water** to the surface. This cold, nutrient-rich water discharged near shore can lower local SST by 1–3 °C, affecting coral reef symbiosis (zooxanthellae bleaching) and cold-sensitive species.
- **Entrainment Mortality**: Small organisms (meroplankton, fish eggs) passing through the high-pressure pumps suffer shear-induced mortality rates of **10–30 %** per pass.
- **Nutrient Upwelling Effect**: Deep seawater is rich in nitrates (~30 μM) and phosphates. Surface discharge can trigger algal blooms if not managed.
- **Atmospheric Ammonia Releases**: Working fluid leakage (closed cycle) — although ammonia has **zero ODP and zero GWP**, it is toxic at concentrations above 35 ppm.

---

## 2.5 Salinity Gradient Energy — Theoretical Basis

The **Gibbs free energy of mixing** per unit volume for two solutions at constant T and P is:
$$\Delta G_{mix} = RT \sum_i c_i \left[\ln\left(\frac{c_i^{mix}}{c_i^0}\right)\right]$$
For seawater (≈ 0.6 M NaCl) and river water (≈ 0.01 M NaCl), the specific energy release is on the order of **0.5–1.0 kWh per cubic meter of fresh water** mixed.

### Two Conversion Routes:
- **Pressure Retarded Osmosis (PRO)**: Uses a **semi-permeable membrane** that lets water pass but blocks salts. Fresh water flows spontaneously to the salt side, pressurizing it; the pressurized brine passes through a **Pelton or Francis turbine**.
- **Reverse Electrodialysis (RED)**: Uses alternating stacks of **anion- and cation-exchange membranes**. Ion migration drives a direct current between electrodes; usable as DC power or inverted to AC via power electronics.

---

## 2.6 📊 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Markdown Table)

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta_{Carnot} = \dfrac{T_w - T_c}{T_w}$ | Upper-bound OTEC efficiency | dimensionless (%) |
| 2 | $\dot{Q}_{evap} = \dot{m}_w c_{p,w} (T_w - T_{evap})$ | Heat transferred from warm SW to working fluid | kW |
| 3 | $\dot{W}_{turb} = \dot{m}_f (h_2 - h_3)$ | Turbine shaft power output | kW |
| 4 | $\dot{m}_w = \dfrac{\rho_w Q_w}{\text{sec}}$ | Warm water mass flow rate | kg/s |
| 5 | $\dot{m}_{f} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{evap}}{h_{fg}}$ | Working fluid mass flow rate (CC-OTEC) | kg/s |
| 6 | $\pi = i C R T$ | van 't Hoff osmotic pressure (SGE) | Pa |
| 7 | $\Delta\pi = RT(C_s - C_f)$ | Osmotic pressure difference across PRO membrane | Pa |
| 8 | $P_{PRO} = \Delta\pi \cdot Q_{perm}$ | PRO power output (permeate flow × osmotic pressure) | W |
| 9 | $W_{mix} = 2RT\left[\sqrt{C_s C_f} - \dfrac{C_s + C_f}{2}\right] V$ | Mixing free energy of two salt solutions | J |
| 10 | $E_{SGE} \approx 0.8\,\text{kWh/m}^3$ | Practical recoverable energy per m³ fresh water | kWh/m³ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Markdown Note:** All equation delimiters use `\vert`, `\mid`, or `\dfrac` to prevent table-rendering breakage on the KTU e-learning portal.

### 🏭 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **CC-OTEC**: Suitable for **baseload tropical power** (Hawaii, Maldives, Caribbean).
- **OC-OTEC**: Ideal for **island nations needing both electricity AND freshwater** (e.g., the OTEC plant at Kona, Hawaii, 210 kW).
- **SGE (PRO/RED)**: Suitable for **river estuaries** globally; Netherlands, Norway, and Japan run RED pilot plants.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Carnot Efficiency Limit for OTEC

### Problem Statement:
Derive the maximum theoretical (Carnot) thermal efficiency of an OTEC plant given warm surface water at temperature $T_w$ (in K) and cold deep water at $T_c$ (in K).

### Step 1 — Start with the Carnot Heat Engine Definition
The Carnot efficiency of any heat engine operating between two thermal reservoirs at absolute temperatures $T_w$ (hot) and $T_c$ (cold) is defined as:

$$\eta_{Carnot} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}}$$

**Explanation:** The efficiency is the ratio of useful work output $W_{net}$ to the heat input $Q_{in}$ absorbed from the warm reservoir.

---

### Step 2 — Apply the Second Law (Reversible Engine Property)
For a reversible (Carnot) cycle, the ratio of heat rejected to heat absorbed equals the ratio of absolute reservoir temperatures:

$$\frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{T_c}{T_w}$$

**Explanation:** This follows directly from the Kelvin-Planck statement and the definition of entropy change in a reversible cycle ($\Delta S_{cycle} = 0$).

---

### Step 3 — Substitute into the Efficiency Expression
Substituting Step 2 into Step 1:

$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_c}{T_w}$$

This can be rewritten in the equivalent form:

$$\eta_{Carnot} = \frac{T_w - T_c}{T_w}$$

**Explanation:** This expresses the efficiency as the *temperature difference divided by the warm-source absolute temperature* — a powerful design heuristic showing why even large ΔT gives small η when T_w is low.

---

### Step 4 — Apply to a Typical Tropical OTEC Site
Insert $T_w = 299\,\text{K}$ (≈ 26 °C) and $T_c = 278\,\text{K}$ (≈ 5 °C):

$$\eta_{Carnot} = \frac{299 - 278}{299} = \frac{21}{299}$$

$$\eta_{Carnot} \approx 0.0702 \approx 7.02\%$$

**Conclusion:** Even an idealized, frictionless, perfectly reversible OTEC engine cannot exceed ~7 % thermal efficiency. Real-world plants achieve **3–4 %**, which is acceptable because the *fuel* (warm and cold seawater) is free and inexhaustible.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Working Fluid Mass Flow Rate in CC-OTEC

### Step 1 — Energy Balance Across the Evaporator
At steady state, the heat absorbed by the working fluid equals the heat given up by warm seawater:

$$\dot{Q}_{evap} = \dot{m}_w c_{p,w}(T_{w,in} - T_{w,out}) = \dot{m}_f (h_2 - h_1)$$

**Explanation:** Where $\dot{m}_w$ is warm seawater mass flow rate, $c_{p,w}$ ≈ 4.0 kJ/(kg·K) is the specific heat of seawater, and $(h_2 - h_1)$ is the working fluid enthalpy rise through the evaporator (typically the latent heat $h_{fg}$ for ammonia ≈ 1370 kJ/kg).

---

### Step 2 — Solve for Working Fluid Mass Flow Rate
Rearranging:

$$\dot{m}_f = \frac{\dot{m}_w c_{p,w}(T_{w,in} - T_{w,out})}{h_{fg}}$$

**Explanation:** This tells us how many kg of ammonia must circulate per second to absorb a given thermal power input from seawater.

---

### Step 3 — Numerical Example
Given: $\dot{m}_w = 500\,\text{kg/s}$, $c_{p,w} = 4.0\,\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$, $T_{w,in} - T_{w,out} = 3\,\text{K}$, $h_{fg}(\text{NH}_3) = 1370\,\text{kJ/kg}$.

Compute numerator:
$$\dot{m}_w c_{p,w}\Delta T_w = 500 \times 4.0 \times 3 = 6000\,\text{kW}$$

Compute denominator: $h_{fg} = 1370\,\text{kJ/kg}$

Therefore:
$$\dot{m}_f = \frac{6000}{1370} \approx 4.38\,\text{kg/s}$$

**Conclusion:** Roughly 4.4 kg of ammonia per second is required to capture 6 MW of thermal input from 500 kg/s of warm seawater cooling by 3 K.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Turbine Power Output in CC-OTEC

### Step 1 — Steady-Flow Energy Equation (SFEE) for Turbine
For an adiabatic turbine (no heat loss, no kinetic-energy change):

$$\dot{W}_{turb} = \dot{m}_f (h_2 - h_3)$$

**Explanation:** Where $h_2$ is the turbine-inlet enthalpy (saturated vapor at evaporator pressure) and $h_3$ is the turbine-exit enthalpy (after isentropic expansion to condenser pressure).

---

### Step 2 — Account for Turbine Isentropic Efficiency
For real turbines:

$$\dot{W}_{turb,real} = \eta_{turb} \cdot \dot{m}_f (h_2 - h_{3s})$$

Where $h_{3s}$ is the ideal (isentropic) exit enthalpy, found from $s_3 = s_2$ at condenser pressure.

---

### Step 3 — Numerical Evaluation
Suppose: $\eta_{turb} = 0.85$, $\dot{m}_f = 4.38\,\text{kg/s}$, $h_2 - h_{3s} = 200\,\text{kJ/kg}$.

Compute ideal power:
$$\dot{W}_{ideal} = 4.38 \times 200 = 876\,\text{kW}$$

Apply efficiency:
$$\dot{W}_{real} = 0.85 \times 876 \approx 744.6\,\text{kW}$$

**Conclusion:** The CC-OTEC turbine produces roughly **745 kW** of shaft power from a 6 MW thermal input — a turbine-stage efficiency of 12.4 %, which is consistent with low-pressure-ratio ammonia turbines.

---

## 3.4 Derivation — Osmotic Pressure for Salinity Gradient (van 't Hoff)

### Step 1 — van 't Hoff Equation for Dilute Solutions
For a dilute electrolytic solution:

$$\pi = i C R T$$

**Explanation:** Where $i$ is the van 't Hoff factor (≈ 2 for NaCl at moderate dilution; ≈ 1.85 for seawater), $C$ is molar concentration (mol/L), $R = 8.314\,\text{J/(mol·K)}$ is the gas constant, and $T$ is absolute temperature.

---

### Step 2 — Compute Osmotic Pressure of Seawater
For seawater at $C = 0.6\,\text{mol/L}$, $i = 1.85$, $T = 298\,\text{K}$:

$$\pi_{sw} = (1.85)(0.6)(8.314)(298)$$

Step-by-step multiplication:
$$\pi_{sw} = 1.85 \times 0.6 \times 8.314 \times 298$$

First, $1.85 \times 0.6 = 1.11$

Then, $1.11 \times 8.314 = 9.228$

Finally, $9.228 \times 298 \approx 2749.96\,\text{kPa}$

Therefore:
$$\pi_{sw} \approx 2.75\,\text{MPa} \approx 27.5\,\text{bar}$$

**Conclusion:** Seawater exerts an osmotic pressure of about **27 bar** — equivalent to a column of water 275 meters tall.

---

### Step 3 — Compute Osmotic Pressure Difference (PRO Power Source)
For fresh river water ($C_f = 0.01\,\text{mol/L}$, $i_f = 1$):

$$\pi_{fw} = (1)(0.01)(8.314)(298) \approx 24.8\,\text{kPa}$$

Therefore:
$$\Delta\pi = \pi_{sw} - \pi_{fw} = 2750 - 24.8 \approx 2725\,\text{kPa}$$

**Conclusion:** The maximum exploitable pressure difference in PRO is **~27 bar**, which can drive a hydroturbine when the pressurized permeate is discharged through it.

---

## 3.5 Derivation — PRO Power Density

### Step 1 — PRO Power Equation
Power from PRO equals the osmotic pressure difference times the volumetric permeate flow rate:

$$P_{PRO} = \Delta\pi \cdot Q_{perm}$$

**Explanation:** Where $Q_{perm}$ (m³/s) is the volumetric flow of water passing through the semi-permeable membrane per unit time.

---

### Step 2 — Numerical Evaluation
Given: $\Delta\pi = 2.725\,\text{MPa}$, $Q_{perm} = 0.05\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$:

$$P_{PRO} = (2.725 \times 10^6\,\text{Pa})(0.05\,\text{m}^3/\text{s})$$

$$P_{PRO} = 136{,}250\,\text{W} \approx 136\,\text{kW}$$

**Conclusion:** A modest 50 L/s membrane module produces **~136 kW** continuously — provided the membrane sustains flux without fouling. This is the engineering challenge limiting PRO commercialization.

---

## 3.6 Comparison Matrix — CC-OTEC vs OC-OTEC vs SGE

| Parameter | CC-OTEC | OC-OTEC | SGE (PRO/RED) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Working fluid | NH₃, R-134a | Seawater (steam) | Membrane-transported water/ions |
| Heat exchangers | Required (metal) | Not required | Membrane stacks |
| Fresh water byproduct | No | **Yes** (high value) | No |
| Net thermal efficiency | 3–4 % | 2–3 % | N/A (direct conversion) |
| Turbine type | Standard Rankine | Large low-pressure | Hydro turbine / DC electrodes |
| Plant size demonstrated | 50 kW – 1 MW | 210 kW (Kona) | 50 kW pilots |
| Environmental risk | NH₃ toxicity | Cold plume + entrainment | Membrane brine discharge |
| Best location | Tropical deep ocean | Tropical islands + islands needing water | Major river estuaries |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Closed Cycle OTEC — System Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Warm Surface SW Intake<br/>T = 26 deg C] --> B[Evaporator Heat Exchanger<br/>Vaporizes NH3 working fluid]
    B --> C[Turbine-Generator Unit<br/>Shaft work to electricity]
    C --> D[Condenser Heat Exchanger<br/>Cold deep SW at 5 deg C condenses NH3]
    D --> E[Working Fluid Feed Pump<br/>Pressurizes liquid NH3]
    E --> B
    F[Cold Deep SW Intake<br/>T = 5 deg C, 1000 m depth] --> D
    D --> G[Cold SW Discharge to Ocean]
    H[Warm SW Discharge to Ocean] --> I[Sea]
    B --> H
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Architecture Note:** The closed-loop boundary is the working-fluid circuit B → C → D → E → B. Seawater streams (A → B, F → D) are **once-through** open loops. This separation is the defining characteristic of CC-OTEC.

---

## 4.2 Open Cycle OTEC — System Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Warm Surface Seawater Pump<br/>T = 26 deg C] --> B[Flash Evaporator Vacuum Chamber<br/>Pressure approx 2.5 kPa]
    B --> C[Low-Pressure Steam Turbine<br/>Large diameter low rpm]
    C --> D[Direct Contact Condenser<br/>Cold deep SW sprayed in]
    D --> E[Fresh Water Collection Tank<br/>Desalinated product]
    E --> F[Fresh Water Output to Plant]
    F --> G[Potable Water Supply]
    D --> H[Brine Discharge to Ocean]
    I[Cold Deep Seawater Pump<br/>T = 5 deg C, 800 m] --> D
    B --> J[Non-evaporated Brine Recycle]
    J --> I2[Sea Discharge]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Architecture Note:** In OC-OTEC, the working fluid **is** seawater. No metallic heat exchangers separate working fluid from heat source/sink, eliminating the major cost of CC-OTEC. The vacuum chamber must be sized to handle enormous vapor volumes because of low steam density at 2.5 kPa.

---

## 4.3 Hybrid OTEC — Combined Power + Desalination

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Closed_Cycle_Loop
        A1[Evaporator] --> A2[Turbine-Generator]
        A2 --> A3[Condenser]
        A3 --> A4[Feed Pump]
        A4 --> A1
    end
    subgraph Open_Cycle_Desal
        B1[Flash Evaporator Vacuum] --> B2[Steam to Heat Exchanger]
        B2 --> A1
        A3 --> B3[Condensate as Fresh Water]
    end
    C1[Warm SW] --> A1
    C1 --> B1
    D1[Cold Deep SW] --> A3
    D1 --> B3
```

---

## 4.4 Salinity Gradient Energy — Pressure Retarded Osmosis (PRO) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fresh River Water<br/>Low salinity C_f] --> M[Semi-Permeable Membrane<br/>Water passes, salts blocked]
    M --> B[Pressurized Brine Side<br/>Pressure rises to approx 27 bar]
    B --> T[Pelton Hydro Turbine<br/>High-pressure water drives runner]
    T --> G[Electric Generator<br/>AC power output]
    G --> P[Power Grid]
    T --> D[Pressure Reduction Valve]
    D --> O[Spent Brine Discharge to Sea]
    S[Sea Water Inlet<br/>High salinity C_s] --> B
```

---

## 4.5 Reverse Electrodialysis (RED) — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sea Water Channel<br/>High salt] --> M1[Cation Exchange Membrane]
    M1 --> B[Concentrate Channel]
    B --> M2[Anion Exchange Membrane]
    M2 --> C[Fresh Water Channel]
    C --> M3[Next Cation Membrane]
    M3 --> D[Next Concentrate Channel]
    D --> M4[Next Anion Membrane]
    M4 --> E[End Fresh Water Channel]
    E --> EL[Cathode Electrode<br/>Negative terminal]
    B --> EA[Anode Electrode<br/>Positive terminal]
    EA --> EXT[DC Electrical Load]
    EXT --> EL
```

> [!NOTE]
> **RED Mechanism:** Cations (+ ions like Na⁺) migrate from salt to fresh channels through **CEM**; anions (– ions like Cl⁻) migrate through **AEM** in the opposite direction. Net ionic current flows between electrodes, harvested as **DC electricity**.

---

## ✏️ PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Closed Cycle and Open Cycle OTEC systems. Mention at least three points of distinction.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Closed Cycle OTEC | Open Cycle OTEC |
|---|---|---|
| Working fluid | Secondary fluid (ammonia) | Seawater itself |
| Heat exchangers | Required (evaporator + condenser) | Not required (direct contact) |
| Fresh water byproduct | No | **Yes** |
| Cycle type | Rankine cycle | Flash evaporation cycle |
| Turbine type | Conventional high-pressure | Large low-pressure |
| Desalination capability | Absent | Inherent |

*(Any three correct distinguishing points fetch full 3 marks.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is Salinity Gradient Energy? Name any two technologies used to harness it.**

**Model Answer:**
Salinity Gradient Energy is the **Gibbs free energy of mixing** released when two aqueous solutions of different salt concentrations (e.g., river water and seawater) are allowed to mix. This energy is harnessed using:
1. **Pressure Retarded Osmosis (PRO)** — uses a semi-permeable membrane.
2. **Reverse Electrodialysis (RED)** — uses alternating cation and anion exchange membranes.

*(3 marks for correct definition + naming both technologies.)*

## ✏️ PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With the help of a neat schematic diagram, explain the working principle of a Closed Cycle OTEC system. State its advantages and limitations.**

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle:**
A Closed Cycle OTEC system operates on the **Rankine cycle** using a low-boiling-point working fluid (typically **ammonia, NH₃**) confined within a sealed loop. The cycle consists of:

1. **Warm seawater intake** — Surface seawater at T_w ≈ 26 °C is pumped through the **evaporator**.
2. **Evaporation** — Working fluid absorbs heat from seawater at constant pressure and vaporizes (latent heat ~1370 kJ/kg for NH₃).
3. **Turbine expansion** — High-pressure vapor expands through a turbine coupled to a generator, producing electricity.
4. **Condensation** — Vapor at low pressure enters the **condenser** where cold deep seawater (T_c ≈ 5 °C) absorbs its latent heat, returning the working fluid to a saturated liquid.
5. **Pumping** — The feed pump raises liquid pressure back to evaporator level, closing the loop.

**Schematic (refer to SECTION 4.1 diagram).**

**Advantages:**
- Uses proven Rankine cycle technology.
- High-grade working fluid allows smaller turbine.
- No direct seawater contact → less corrosion.
- Suitable for continuous baseload generation.

**Limitations:**
- Requires **massive heat exchangers** (main CAPEX driver).
- Working fluid toxicity (NH₃) hazard.
- Low thermal efficiency (~3–4 %) due to small ΔT.
- Limited to tropical regions with deep cold water access.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A CC-OTEC plant uses ammonia as the working fluid. Warm seawater enters the evaporator at 28 °C and leaves at 24 °C at a mass flow rate of 400 kg/s. The evaporator transfers 5500 kW of heat to the ammonia, which vaporizes at a latent heat of 1370 kJ/kg. Calculate:**
**(i)** The working fluid mass flow rate
**(ii)** The turbine power output if the turbine isentropic efficiency is 80 % and the ideal enthalpy drop is 180 kJ/kg

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $\dot{m}_w = 400\,\text{kg/s}$
- $T_{w,in} = 28\,°\text{C}, T_{w,out} = 24\,°\text{C}$
- $\dot{Q}_{evap} = 5500\,\text{kW}$
- $h_{fg} = 1370\,\text{kJ/kg}$
- $\eta_{turb} = 0.80$
- $\Delta h_{ideal} = 180\,\text{kJ/kg}$

---

**(i) Working Fluid Mass Flow Rate:**

$$\dot{m}_f = \frac{\dot{Q}_{evap}}{h_{fg}} = \frac{5500\,\text{kW}}{1370\,\text{kJ/kg}}$$

$$\dot{m}_f \approx 4.015\,\text{kg/s}$$

---

**(ii) Turbine Power Output:**

Ideal power:
$$\dot{W}_{ideal} = \dot{m}_f \times \Delta h_{ideal} = 4.015 \times 180 = 722.7\,\text{kW}$$

Actual power (with efficiency):
$$\dot{W}_{actual} = \eta_{turb} \times \dot{W}_{ideal} = 0.80 \times 722.7$$

$$\dot{W}_{actual} \approx 578.2\,\text{kW}$$

**Final Answers:** (i) ≈ **4.02 kg/s**, (ii) ≈ **578 kW**

---

### **Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the working of an Open Cycle OTEC plant. Discuss its environmental impacts in detail.**

**Model Solution:**

**Open Cycle OTEC Working:**
1. Warm seawater (~26 °C) is pumped into a **vacuum flash chamber** (pressure ≈ 2.5 kPa).
2. Water flash-evaporates due to sudden pressure drop → low-pressure steam.
3. Steam expands through a **large low-pressure turbine** coupled to a generator.
4. Spent steam enters a **direct-contact condenser** sprayed with cold deep seawater.
5. Steam condenses to **fresh potable water** (desalination byproduct).
6. Non-evaporated brine and cold SW are discharged back to the ocean.

**Environmental Impacts:**

1. **Cold Water Plume:** Discharge of cold, nutrient-rich deep water lowers local SST, stressing coral reefs.
2. **Entrainment & Impingement:** Marine organisms (larvae, plankton) sucked into intake pipes suffer mortality.
3. **Nutrient Upwelling Algal Blooms:** Increased N/P in surface waters can trigger eutrophication.
4. **Biofouling & Anti-fouling Chemicals:** Chlorination damages non-target marine life.
5. **CO₂ Sequestration Bonus:** Deep ocean water is CO₂-rich; OTEC upwelling releases some CO₂ but offsets it by displacing fossil generation.
6. **Land Footprint:** Minimal (≈ 1–2 acres for 100 MW plant).
7. **GHG Emissions:** Only minor from pumping auxiliaries; essentially **zero-emission** baseline.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Compute the osmotic pressure difference between seawater (0.6 M NaCl, i = 1.85) and river water (0.01 M NaCl, i = 1.0) at T = 300 K. If a PRO membrane passes 0.04 m³/s of fresh water at this pressure difference, calculate the theoretical power output.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $C_s = 0.6\,\text{mol/L}, i_s = 1.85$
- $C_f = 0.01\,\text{mol/L}, i_f = 1.0$
- $T = 300\,\text{K}$
- $Q_{perm} = 0.04\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$
- $R = 8.314\,\text{J/(mol·K)}$

---

**Step 1: Compute Osmotic Pressure of Seawater**

$$\pi_{sw} = i_s C_s R T = 1.85 \times 0.6 \times 8.314 \times 300$$

Compute stepwise:
- $1.85 \times 0.6 = 1.11$
- $1.11 \times 8.314 = 9.2285$
- $9.2285 \times 300 = 2768.56\,\text{kPa}$

$$\pi_{sw} \approx 2768.6\,\text{kPa} = 2.769\,\text{MPa}$$

---

**Step 2: Compute Osmotic Pressure of River Water**

$$\pi_{fw} = i_f C_f R T = 1.0 \times 0.01 \times 8.314 \times 300$$

$$\pi_{fw} = 24.94\,\text{kPa} \approx 0.0249\,\text{MPa}$$

---

**Step 3: Compute Osmotic Pressure Difference**

$$\Delta\pi = \pi_{sw} - \pi_{fw} = 2768.6 - 24.94 = 2743.66\,\text{kPa}$$

$$\Delta\pi \approx 2.744\,\text{MPa}$$

---

**Step 4: Compute PRO Theoretical Power Output**

$$P_{PRO} = \Delta\pi \cdot Q_{perm} = (2.744 \times 10^6\,\text{Pa})(0.04\,\text{m}^3/\text{s})$$

$$P_{PRO} = 109{,}760\,\text{W} \approx 109.8\,\text{kW}$$

---

**Final Answers:**
- Osmotic pressure of seawater ≈ **2.77 MPa**
- Osmotic pressure of river water ≈ **25 kPa**
- Δπ ≈ **2.74 MPa**
- PRO power output ≈ **110 kW**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units**: Students frequently leave $R = 8.314$ but compute C in mol/m³ without converting from mol/L (×1000 factor). Always state: "C in mol/m³ for SI Pa result".
> 2. **Using T in °C instead of K**: This is the #1 silly mistake. van 't Hoff requires **absolute temperature**.
> 3. **Confusing $i$ values**: For NaCl, $i = 2$ ideally, but $i \approx 1.85$ empirically due to ion-ion interactions at seawater concentration. Use the value given in the problem.
> 4. **Neglecting turbine isentropic efficiency**: CC-OTEC problems *always* require application of η_turbine to convert ideal enthalpy drop to actual work.
> 5. **OC-OTEC vs CC-OTEC confusion in hybrid cycle**: When asked about *hybrid* plants, students mix up whether the desalination output comes from the open or closed loop.

---

## ✅ TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ⚡ **Closed Cycle OTEC** uses a **secondary working fluid** (NH₃, R-134a) in a sealed Rankine loop.
- ⚡ **Open Cycle OTEC** uses **seawater itself** as the working fluid; inherently produces **fresh water**.
- ⚡ **Hybrid OTEC** combines both — electricity + desalination simultaneously.
- ⚡ Minimum OTEC ΔT ≈ **20 °C**; restricted to **±20° latitude**.
- ⚡ **Carnot efficiency** formula: $\eta = \dfrac{T_w - T_c}{T_w}$ → yields ~6–7 % theoretical maximum.
- ⚡ Real plant efficiency: **3–4 %** (CC-OTEC), **2–3 %** (OC-OTEC).
- ⚡ Common working fluids: **NH₃** (preferred for low boiling point), R-134a, R-22.
- ⚡ Open cycle flash chamber pressure ≈ **1–3 kPa absolute**.
- ⚡ Major environmental impacts: **cold plume discharge, entrainment, biofouling, nutrient upwelling**.
- ⚡ OTEC avoids **~0.6–0.9 kg CO₂/kWh** versus fossil plants.
- ⚡ **Salinity Gradient Energy** exploits Gibbs free energy of mixing river water + seawater.
- ⚡ Two SGE technologies: **PRO (Pressure Retarded Osmosis)** and **RED (Reverse Electrodialysis)**.
- ⚡ **van 't Hoff equation**: $\pi = i C R T$ — applies for PRO membrane pressure.
- ⚡ Seawater osmotic pressure ≈ **27 bar** at 35 g/kg salinity.
- ⚡ Practical recoverable SGE energy ≈ **0.8 kWh per m³ of fresh water** mixed.
- ⚡ Theoretical global SGE potential ≈ **2.6 TW** (continuous).
- ⚡ Carnot limit importance: **absolute temperature** in denominator (in Kelvin) — small ΔT → small η.
- ⚡ Working fluid mass flow rate: $\dot{m}_f = \dot{Q}_{evap} / h_{fg}$.
- ⚡ Turbine power: $\dot{W} = \eta_{turb} \cdot \dot{m}_f (h_2 - h_{3s})$.
- ⚡ PRO power: $P = \Delta\pi \times Q_{perm}$.
- ⚡ OTEC needs **deep cold water** (800–1000 m) → land-based or offshore plant.
